{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"OmniRoute MCP Server Documentation\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# OmniRoute MCP Server Documentation
8|
9|> Model Context Protocol server with 104 tools across routing, cache, compression, memory, skills, proxy, pool, and context source operations.
10|>
11|> Source of truth: `open-sse/mcp-server/server.ts` computes **104 unique tools** with `countUniqueMcpTools()`: 42 canonical definitions (including the six CCR lifecycle tools and the agent-skills trio), plus memory (3), skills (4), GitHub skills (3), pool (6), gamification (8), plugins (8), Notion (6), Obsidian (22), and two RTK-only compression tools.
12|
13|## Installation
14|
15|OmniRoute MCP is built-in. Start it with:
16|
17|```bash
18|omniroute --mcp
19|```
20|
21|Or via the open-sse transport:
22|
23|```bash
24|# HTTP streamable transport (port 20130)
25|omniroute --dev  # MCP auto-starts on /mcp endpoint
26|```
27|
28|## Transports
29|
30|The MCP server exposes three transports, all backed by the same `createMcpServer()` factory:
31|
32|| Transport         | Where                                       | When to use                                          |
33|| :---------------- | :------------------------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------- |
34|| `stdio`           | `open-sse/mcp-server/server.ts`             | IDE integrations (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.)      |
35|| `sse`             | `POST/GET /api/mcp/sse` via `httpTransport` | Browser/agent clients that need an event stream      |
36|| `streamable-http` | `POST/GET/DELETE /api/mcp/stream`           | Multi-session HTTP clients (`mcp-session-id` header) |
37|
38|The active HTTP transport (`sse` or `streamable-http`) is selected by the `mcpTransport` setting. Switching transports closes existing sessions on the other transport.
39|
40|### Remote access (manage-scope bypass)
41|
42|`/api/mcp/*` is in the LOCAL_ONLY tier (`src/server/authz/routeGuard.ts`) — by default only loopback hosts (`localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, `::1`) can reach it. Since v3.8.2, non-loopback clients may connect if they present an `Authorization: Bearer *** whose key carries the `manage` scope. This is the only way to reach the remote MCP server through a tunnel, reverse proxy, or public hostname.
43|
44|```bash
45|# Grant manage scope: open the dashboard API Keys page and toggle
46|# \"Management Access\" on the key, or POST scopes:[\"manage\"] when creating.
47|
48|# Then connect from a remote MCP client:
49|curl -i \\
50|  -H \"Host: your-public-host.example\" \\
51|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
52|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
53|  -H \"Accept: application/json, text/event-stream\" \\
54|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"initialize\",\"params\":{\"protocolVersion\":\"2025-03-26\",\"capabilities\":{},\"clientInfo\":{\"name\":\"my-client\",\"version\":\"0\"}}}' \\
55|  https://your-public-host.example/api/mcp/stream
56|```
57|
58|A non-manage key (or no Bearer) returns `403 LOCAL_ONLY`. The sibling prefix `/api/cli-tools/runtime/*` is intentionally NOT bypassable — see [Route Guard Tiers — Manage-scope carve-out](../security/ROUTE_GUARD_TIERS.md#manage-scope-carve-out).
59|
60|## IDE Configuration
61|
62|See [MCP Client Configuration](../guides/SETUP_GUIDE.md#mcp-client-configuration) for Claude Desktop,
63|Cursor, Cline, and compatible MCP client setup.
64|
65|---
66|
67|## Essential Tools (8) — Phase 1
68|
69|| Tool                            | Scopes                | Description                                                   |
70|| :------------------------------ | :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ |
71|| `omniroute_get_health`          | `read:health`         | Uptime, memory, circuit breakers, rate limits, cache stats    |
72|| `omniroute_list_combos`         | `read:combos`         | All configured combos with strategies (optional metrics)      |
73|| `omniroute_get_combo_metrics`   | `read:combos`         | Performance metrics for a specific combo                      |
74|| `omniroute_switch_combo`        | `write:combos`        | Activate or deactivate a combo                                |
75|| `omniroute_check_quota`         | `read:quota`          | Quota used/total, percent remaining, reset time, token health |
76|| `omniroute_route_request`       | `execute:completions` | Send a chat completion through OmniRoute routing              |
77|| `omniroute_cost_report`         | `read:usage`          | Cost report by period (session/day/week/month)                |
78|| `omniroute_list_models_catalog` | `read:models`         | Full model catalog with capabilities, status, pricing         |
79|
80|## Phase 1 — Search
81|
82|| Tool                   | Scopes           | Description                                                                                                                        |
83|| :--------------------- | :--------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
84|| `omniroute_web_search` | `execute:search` | Web search through OmniRoute search gateway (Serper/Brave/Perplexity/Exa/Tavily/Google PSE/Linkup/SearchAPI/SearXNG) with failover |
85|
86|## Advanced Tools (11) — Phase 2
87|
88|| Tool                               | Scopes                               | Description                                                                               |
89|| :--------------------------------- | :----------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
90|| `omniroute_simulate_route`         | `read:health`, `read:combos`         | Dry-run routing simulation with fallback tree                                             |
91|| `omniroute_set_budget_guard`       | `write:budget`                       | Session budget with d
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784433869.3867419)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"OmniRoute A2A Server Documentation\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# OmniRoute A2A Server Documentation
8|
9|> Agent-to-Agent Protocol v0.3 — OmniRoute as an intelligent routing agent
10|
11|The A2A surface has two faces:
12|
13|- **JSON-RPC 2.0** at `POST /a2a` (canonical entry point, defined in `src/app/a2a/route.ts`).
14|- **REST** under `/api/a2a/*` for dashboards and tooling (status, task list, cancel).
15|
16|Tasks are tracked by `A2ATaskManager` (`src/lib/a2a/taskManager.ts`, default 5-minute TTL). Skills are dispatched via `A2A_SKILL_HANDLERS` in `src/lib/a2a/taskExecution.ts`.
17|
18|## Agent Discovery
19|
20|```bash
21|curl http://localhost:20128/.well-known/agent.json
22|```
23|
24|Returns the Agent Card describing OmniRoute's capabilities, skills, and authentication requirements.
25|
26|The Agent Card's `version` field is sourced from `process.env.npm_package_version` (see `src/app/.well-known/agent.json/route.ts:13`), so it stays auto-synced with `package.json` on every release.
27|
28|---
29|
30|## Authentication
31|
32|All `/a2a` requests require an API key via the `Authorization` header:
33|
34|```
35|Authorization: Bearer YOUR_O..._KEY
36|```
37|
38|If no API key is configured on the server, authentication is bypassed.
39|
40|## Enablement
41|
42|A2A is controlled by the **Endpoints → A2A** toggle and is disabled by default. When disabled,
43|`GET /api/a2a/status` reports `status: \"disabled\"` and `online: false`; JSON-RPC calls to
44|`POST /a2a` return HTTP 503 with JSON-RPC error code `-32000`.
45|
46|---
47|
48|## JSON-RPC 2.0 Methods
49|
50|### `message/send` — Synchronous Execution
51|
52|Sends a message to a skill and waits for the complete response.
53|
54|```bash
55|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
56|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
57|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
58|  -d '{
59|    \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
60|    \"id\": \"1\",
61|    \"method\": \"message/send\",
62|    \"params\": {
63|      \"skill\": \"smart-routing\",
64|      \"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": \"Write a hello world in Python\"}],
65|      \"metadata\": {\"model\": \"auto\", \"combo\": \"fast-coding\"}
66|    }
67|  }'
68|```
69|
70|**Response:**
71|
72|```json
73|{
74|  \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
75|  \"id\": \"1\",
76|  \"result\": {
77|    \"task\": { \"id\": \"uuid\", \"state\": \"completed\" },
78|    \"artifacts\": [{ \"type\": \"text\", \"content\": \"...\" }],
79|    \"metadata\": {
80|      \"routing_explanation\": \"Selected claude-sonnet via provider \\\"anthropic\\\" (latency: 1200ms, cost: $0.003)\",
81|      \"cost_envelope\": { \"estimated\": 0.005, \"actual\": 0.003, \"currency\": \"USD\" },
82|      \"resilience_trace\": [
83|        { \"event\": \"primary_selected\", \"provider\": \"anthropic\", \"timestamp\": \"...\" }
84|      ],
85|      \"policy_verdict\": { \"allowed\": true, \"reason\": \"within budget and quota limits\" }
86|    }
87|  }
88|}
89|```
90|
91|### `message/stream` — SSE Streaming
92|
93|Same as `message/send` but returns Server-Sent Events for real-time streaming.
94|
95|```bash
96|curl -N -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
97|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
98|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
99|  -d '{
100|    \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
101|    \"id\": \"1\",
102|    \"method\": \"message/stream\",
103|    \"params\": {
104|      \"skill\": \"smart-routing\",
105|      \"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": \"Explain quantum computing\"}]
106|    }
107|  }'
108|```
109|
110|**SSE Events:**
111|
112|```
113|data: {\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"method\":\"message/stream\",\"params\":{\"task\":{\"id\":\"...\",\"state\":\"working\"},\"chunk\":{\"type\":\"text\",\"content\":\"...\"}}}
114|
115|: heartbeat 2026-03-03T17:00:00Z
116|
117|data: {\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"method\":\"message/stream\",\"params\":{\"task\":{\"id\":\"...\",\"state\":\"completed\"},\"metadata\":{...}}}
118|```
119|
120|### `tasks/get` — Query Task Status
121|
122|```bash
123|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
124|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
125|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
126|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":\"2\",\"method\":\"tasks/get\",\"params\":{\"taskId\":\"TASK_UUID\"}}'
127|```
128|
129|### `tasks/cancel` — Cancel a Task
130|
131|```bash
132|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
133|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
134|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
135|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":\"3\",\"method\":\"tasks/cancel\",\"params\":{\"taskId\":\"TASK_UUID\"}}'
136|```
137|
138|---
139|
140|## Available Skills
141|
142|OmniRoute exposes 6 A2A skills wired in `src/lib/a2a/taskExecution.ts::A2A_SKILL_HANDLERS`. Each skill module lives in `src/lib/a2a/skills/`.
143|
144|| Skill              | ID                   | Description                                                                                                     | Tags                       | Examples                               |
145|| :----------------- | :------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------- | :------------------------------------- |
146|| Smart Routing      | `smart-routing`      | Routes a prompt through the optimal provider/combo using OmniRoute's combo engine + scoring                     | routing, providers         | \"Route this prompt via the best model\" |
147|| Quota Management   | `quota-management`   | Reports per-provider quota state, helps callers decide when to throttle/switch                                  | quota, providers           | \"Check quota for anthropic\"            |
148|| Provider Discovery | `provider-discovery` | Lists installed providers with capabilities, free-tier flags, OAuth status                                      | providers, discovery       | \"What providers are available?\"        |
149|
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784433869.3867428)

Now I have the complete, authoritative contracts for both endpoints — and critically, your point is validated: these are documented in the repo and I should have read them first. Key findings:

**`/api/mcp/sse` (MCP-Server.md):**
- OmniRoute exposes an **MCP server with 104 tools** (routing, cache, compression, memory, skills, proxy, etc.)
- The 403 I hit is because MCP is in the **LOCAL_ONLY tier** — needs a `Bearer` key with the **`manage` scope** (or loopback). My `OMNI_API_KEY` lacks `manage` scope.
- There's also a `streamable-http` transport at `/api/mcp/stream`.
- Auth is via **API key scopes** (`read:health`, `read:models`, etc.); wildcard `*` = full.
- Two essential tools for us: `omniroute_get_health` (uptime, memory, circuit breakers, rate limits) and `omniroute_list_models_catalog` (full catalog w/ capabilities+status+pricing).

**`/.well-known/agent.json` + `/a2a` (A2A-Server.md):**
- A2A is **disabled by default** (Endpoints → A2A toggle). The agent card is public but the JSON-RPC `/a2a` needs the API key.
- 6 A2A skills including `health-report` (aggregates circuit breaker/cooldown/lockout per provider) and `provider-discovery` — these are exactly the "which models are usable" answers.
- `message/send` with `skill: "smart-routing"` is how an agent routes through OmniRoute.

{"content": "1100|
1101|**Auth:** Requires management session (dashboard `auth_token` cookie) or a
1102|management-scoped API key.
1103|
1104|See [ACP Framework](../frameworks/ACP.md) for full details.
1105|
1106|---
1107|
1108|## Analytics & Observability
1109|
1110|Real-time analytics endpoints for monitoring routing, compression, and provider
1111|diversity. These power the `/dashboard/analytics/*` pages.
1112|
1113|### Auto-routing analytics
1114|
1115|| Method | Path                                 | Description                                                                                        |
1116|| ------ | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
1117|| GET    | `/api/analytics/auto-routing`        | Aggregate auto-routing stats: total calls, strategy distribution, tier distribution, top providers |
1118|| GET    | `/api/analytics/auto-routing?days=7` | Time-windowed stats (default 24h)                                                                  |
1119|
1120|**Response example**:
1121|
1122|```json
1123|{
1124|  \"window\": \"24h\",
1125|  \"totalCalls\": 1234,
1126|  \"strategyBreakdown\": {
1127|    \"rules\": 800,
1128|    \"cost\": 200,
1129|    \"latency\": 150,
1130|    \"sla-aware\": 50,
1131|    \"lkgp\": 34
1132|  },
1133|  \"tierBreakdown\": {
1134|    \"ultra\": 100,
1135|    \"pro\": 500,
1136|    \"standard\": 400,
1137|    \"free\": 234
1138|  },
1139|  \"topProviders\": [
1140|    { \"provider\": \"openai\", \"calls\": 500, \"avgLatencyMs\": 850 },
1141|    { \"provider\": \"anthropic\", \"calls\": 300, \"avgLatencyMs\": 1200 }
1142|  ]
1143|}
1144|```
1145|
1146|### Compression analytics
1147|
1148|| Method | Path                         | Description                                                                           |
1149|| ------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
1150|| GET    | `/api/analytics/compression` | Aggregate compression stats: tokens saved, savings %, mode distribution, engine usage |
1151|
1152|**Response example**:
1153|
1154|```json
1155|{
1156|  \"window\": \"24h\",
1157|  \"totalOriginalTokens\": 5000000,
1158|  \"totalCompressedTokens\": 3500000,
1159|  \"totalSavings\": 1500000,
1160|  \"savingsPct\": 30.0,
1161|  \"modeBreakdown\": {
1162|    \"lite\": 400,
1163|    \"standard\": 600,
1164|    \"aggressive\": 100,
1165|    \"ultra\": 50,
1166|    \"rtk\": 84
1167|  },
1168|  \"engineBreakdown\": {
1169|    \"caveman\": 800,
1170|    \"rtk\": 434
1171|  }
1172|}
1173|```
1174|
1175|### Provider diversity tracking
1176|
1177|| Method | Path                       | Description                                                                                              |
1178|| ------ | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
1179|| GET    | `/api/analytics/diversity` | Shannon entropy-based diversity tracking: prevents single points of failure by measuring provider spread |
1180|
1181|**Response example**:
1182|
1183|```json
1184|{
1185|  \"window\": \"24h\",
1186|  \"shannonEntropy\": 2.45,
1187|  \"maxEntropy\": 3.17,
1188|  \"diversityRatio\": 0.77,
1189|  \"providerUsage\": {
1190|    \"openai\": 0.4,
1191|    \"anthropic\": 0.25,
1192|    \"google\": 0.2,
1193|    \"kiro\": 0.15
1194|  },
1195|  \"warnings\": [\"OpenAI accounts for 40% of traffic — consider diversifying\"]
1196|}
1197|```
1198|
1199|**Auth:** Requires management session or management-scoped API key.
1200|
1201|---
1202|
1203|## Admin Operations
1204|
1205|Admin-only endpoints for operational management.
1206|
1207|| Method | Path                     | Description                                                                                 |
1208|| ------ | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
1209|| GET    | `/api/admin/concurrency` | Read current concurrency limits (global + per-provider)                                     |
1210|| POST   | `/api/admin/concurrency` | Update concurrency limits — body: `{global?: number, perProvider?: Record<string, number>}` |
1211|
1212|**Auth:** Requires management session with admin scope.
1213|
1214|---
1215|
1216|## CLI Tools Management
1217|
1218|Manage CLI tools that integrate with OmniRoute (antigravity, chipotle, commandCode,
1219|devin-cli, etc.). See [Provider Reference](./PROVIDER_REFERENCE.md) for the full list.
1220|
1221|| Method | Path                                    | Description                                                                                    |
1222|| ------ | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
1223|| GET    | `/api/cli-tools/all-statuses`           | Status of all CLI tools (installed, version, last seen)                                        |
1224|| GET    | `/api/cli-tools/[id]/status`            | Status of a specific CLI tool (id can be: antigravity, chipotle, commandCode, devin-cli, etc.) |
1225|| POST   | `/api/cli-tools/apply`                  | Apply a CLI tool configuration to a provider connection                                        |
1226|| GET    | `/api/cli-tools/backups`                | List CLI tool configuration backups                                                            |
1227|| POST   | `/api/cli-tools/backups`                | Create a backup of all CLI tool configurations                                                 |
1228|| POST   | `/api/cli-tools/[id]/restore`           | Restore a CLI tool from a backup                                                               |
1229|| GET    | `/api/cli-tools/antigravity-mitm`       | Antigravity MITM proxy status (the \"antigravity-mitm\" C
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784433869.3867447)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"CLI Tools — OmniRoute\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# CLI Tools — OmniRoute
8|
9|Last updated: 2026-06-28
10|
11|OmniRoute integrates with three categories of CLI tools spread across three dedicated dashboard pages:
12|
13|| Page           | Route                   | Concept                                                                   | Count        |
14|| -------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------ |
15|| **CLI Code's** | `/dashboard/cli-code`   | Coding tools you point at OmniRoute (Client → CLI → OmniRoute → Provider) | 20           |
16|| **CLI Agents** | `/dashboard/cli-agents` | Autonomous agents you point at OmniRoute (same flow, broader scope)       | 6            |
17|| **ACP Agents** | `/dashboard/acp-agents` | CLIs that OmniRoute spawns as backend via stdio/ACP (reverse flow)        | see registry |
18|
19|Legacy routes redirect via 308: `/dashboard/cli-tools` → `/dashboard/cli-code`, `/dashboard/agents` → `/dashboard/acp-agents`.
20|
21|---
22|
23|## How It Works
24|
25|```
26|CLI Code's / CLI Agents (consumption flow):
27|Claude / Codex / OpenCode / Cline / KiloCode / Continue / Hermes Agent / Goose / ...
28|           │
29|           ▼  (all point to OmniRoute)
30|    http://YOUR_SERVER:20128/v1
31|           │
32|           ▼  (OmniRoute routes to the right provider)
33|    Anthropic / OpenAI / Gemini / DeepSeek / Groq / Mistral / ...
34|
35|ACP Agents (reverse spawn flow):
36|    Client request → OmniRoute → spawns CLI via stdio/ACP → response
37|```
38|
39|**Benefits:**
40|
41|- One API key to manage all tools
42|- Cost tracking across all CLIs in the dashboard
43|- Model switching without reconfiguring every tool
44|- Works locally and on remote servers (VPS, Docker, Akamai, Cloudflare Tunnel)
45|
46|---
47|
48|## Auto-configure with `setup-*`
49|
50|You do not have to write each tool's config by hand. OmniRoute ships a `setup-*`
51|command per supported CLI that reads the **live** model catalog from a running
52|OmniRoute (local or remote) and writes the tool's own config on your machine:
53|
54|```bash
55|omniroute setup-codex        omniroute setup-claude       omniroute setup-opencode
56|omniroute setup-cline        omniroute setup-kilo         omniroute setup-continue
57|omniroute setup-cursor       omniroute setup-roo          omniroute setup-crush
58|omniroute setup-goose        omniroute setup-qwen         omniroute setup-aider
59|```
60|
61|Each accepts `--remote <url> --api-key <key>` (configure a local tool against a
62|remote OmniRoute), `--dry-run` (preview without writing), and `--port`. Tools
63|without model auto-discovery (Cline, Kilo, Roo, Goose, Qwen, Aider, Gemini) take
64|`--model <id>` (and `--yes` for non-interactive runs). The launchers
65|`omniroute launch` (Claude Code) and `omniroute launch-codex` (Codex) spawn the CLI
66|with the right env injected and write no config at all.
67|
68|> **Full reference:** the master table — what each command writes, every flag,
69|> local vs remote, and which tools want a `/v1` suffix — lives in
70|> **[CLI Integrations](../guides/CLI-INTEGRATIONS.md)**.
71|
72|---
73|
74|## Source of Truth
75|
76|The unified catalog lives in `src/shared/constants/cliTools.ts` as `CLI_TOOLS: Record<string, CliCatalogEntry>`.
77|
78|Each entry has these fields (defined in `src/shared/schemas/cliCatalog.ts`):
79|
80|| Field                                           | Type                                                         | Description                                            |
81|| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
82|| `category`                                      | `\"code\" \\| \"agent\"`                                          | Which page the tool appears on                         |
83|| `vendor`                                        | `string`                                                     | Tool origin (\"Anthropic\", \"OSS (P. Gauthier)\")         |
84|| `acpSpawnable`                                  | `boolean`                                                    | Also usable as an ACP Agent (badge shown)              |
85|| `baseUrlSupport`                                | `\"full\" \\| \"partial\" \\| \"none\"`                              | Custom endpoint support level. `\"none\"` = MITM backlog |
86|| `configType`                                    | `\"env\" \\| \"custom\" \\| \"guide\" \\| \"custom-builder\" \\| \"mitm\"` | Configuration mechanism                                |
87|| `id`, `name`, `color`, `description`, `docsUrl` | standard                                                     | Core display fields                                    |
88|
89|Entries with `baseUrlSupport: \"none\"` are **not shown** in the dashboard pages — they are registered in the MITM backlog for plan 11 (see `_tasks/features-v3.8.6/refactorpages/_orchestration/_plan11-mitm-backlog.md`).
90|
91|---
92|
93|## 1. CLI Code's Catalog (20 tools)
94|
95|Tools that support custom base URL and appear in `/dashboard/cli-code`:
96|
97|| id | name | vendor | baseUrlSupport | configType | acpSpawnable |
98||----|------|--------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
99|| claude | Claude Code | Anthropic | full | env | true |
100|| codex | OpenAI Codex CLI | OpenAI | full | custom | true |
101|| cline | Cline | OSS (ex-Claude Dev) | full | custom | true |
102|| kilo | Kilo Code | Kilo-Org | full | custom | false |
103|| roo | Roo Code | Roo (OSS) | full | guide | false |
104|| continue | Continue | continue.dev | full | guide | false |
105|| qwen | Qwen Code | Alibaba | full | guide | true |
106|| aider | Aider | OSS (P. Gauthier) | full | guide | true |
107|| forge | ForgeCode | Antinomy HQ | full | custom | true |
108|| jcode | jcode |
```

```
{"content": "1|# Claude Flow V3 - Agent Guide
2|
3|> **For OpenAI Codex CLI** - Agentic AI Foundation standard
4|> Skills: `$skill-name` | Config: `.agents/config.toml`
5|
6|---
7|
8|## 📢 TL;DR - READ THIS FIRST
9|
10|```
11|╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
12|║  1. claude-flow = LEDGER (tracks state, stores memory, coordinates)       ║
13|║  2. Codex = EXECUTOR (writes code, runs commands, creates files)          ║
14|║  3. NEVER stop after calling claude-flow - IMMEDIATELY continue working   ║
15|║  4. If you need something BUILT/EXECUTED, YOU do it, not claude-flow      ║
16|║  5. ALWAYS search memory BEFORE starting: memory search --query \"task\"    ║
17|║  6. ALWAYS store patterns AFTER success: memory store --namespace patterns║
18|╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
19|```
20|
21|**Workflow (Use MCP Tools):**
22|1. `memory_search(query=\"task keywords\")` → LEARN from past patterns (score > 0.7 = use it)
23|2. `swarm_init(topology=\"hierarchical\")` → coordination record (instant)
24|3. **YOU write the code / run the commands** ← THIS IS WHERE WORK HAPPENS
25|4. `memory_store(key=\"pattern-x\", value=\"what worked\", namespace=\"patterns\")` → REMEMBER for next time
26|
27|---
28|
29|## 🚨 CRITICAL: CODEX DOES THE WORK, CLAUDE-FLOW ORCHESTRATES
30|
31|```
32|┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
33|│  CLAUDE-FLOW = ORCHESTRATOR (tracks state, coordinates)     │
34|│  CODEX = WORKER (writes code, runs commands, implements)    │
35|└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
36|```
37|
38|### ❌ WRONG: Expecting claude-flow to execute tasks
39|```bash
40|npx claude-flow swarm start --objective \"Build API\"
41|# WRONG: Waiting for claude-flow to build the API
42|# Claude-flow does NOT execute code!
43|```
44|
45|### ✅ CORRECT: Codex executes, claude-flow tracks
46|```bash
47|# 1. Tell claude-flow what you're doing (optional coordination)
48|npx claude-flow swarm init --topology hierarchical --max-agents 1
49|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coder --name codex-worker
50|
51|# 2. YOU (CODEX) DO THE ACTUAL WORK:
52|mkdir -p src
53|cat > src/api.ts << 'EOF'
54|export function hello() { return \"Hello World\"; }
55|EOF
56|
57|# 3. Report to claude-flow what you did (optional)
58|npx claude-flow memory store --key \"api-created\" --value \"src/api.ts\" --namespace results
59|```
60|
61|### The Division of Labor
62|
63|| Component | Role | Examples |
64||-----------|------|----------|
65|| **CODEX** | EXECUTES | Write files, run tests, create code, shell commands |
66|| **claude-flow** | ORCHESTRATES | Track agents, store memory, coordinate tasks |
67|
68|---
69|
70|## ⛔ DON'T STOP AFTER CALLING CLAUDE-FLOW
71|
72|```
73|┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
74|│  ❌ WRONG: Call claude-flow → STOP → Wait for results                   │
75|│  ✅ RIGHT: Call claude-flow → IMMEDIATELY continue → YOU do the work    │
76|└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
77|```
78|
79|### ❌ WRONG Pattern (Stopping)
80|```bash
81|npx claude-flow swarm init --topology hierarchical
82|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coder --name worker-1
83|npx claude-flow swarm start --objective \"Build hello world\"
84|# WRONG: Codex stops here and waits for something to happen
85|# Nothing will happen! Claude-flow doesn't execute code!
86|```
87|
88|### ✅ CORRECT Pattern (Continue Immediately)
89|```bash
90|# Step 1: Coordination (optional)
91|npx claude-flow swarm init --topology hierarchical
92|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coder --name worker-1
93|
94|# Step 2: IMMEDIATELY DO THE WORK YOURSELF - DON'T WAIT!
95|echo 'console.log(\"Hello World!\");' > hello.js
96|node hello.js
97|
98|# Step 3: Report what you did
99|npx claude-flow memory store --key \"result\" --value \"Hello World printed\" --namespace results
100|```
101|
102|### The Rule
103|**After ANY claude-flow command, IMMEDIATELY continue with your own work.**
104|
105|Claude-flow commands return instantly. They create coordination RECORDS only.
106|You must ALWAYS follow up with YOUR OWN execution.
107|
108|| After This... | IMMEDIATELY Do This... |
109||---------------|----------------------|
110|| `swarm init` | Start writing code |
111|| `agent spawn` | Continue implementation |
112|| `task create` | Execute the task yourself |
113|| `swarm start` | Begin actual work |
114|
115|---
116|
117|### When to Use Claude-Flow Commands
118|
119|| Use Case | Claude-Flow Command | Then Codex Does |
120||----------|---------------------|-----------------|
121|| Track progress | `memory store --key \"step1\" --value \"done\"` | Continue to next step |
122|| Multi-step tasks | `task create --description \"step 2\"` | Execute step 2 |
123|| Store results | `memory store --key \"output\" --value \"...\"` | Move on |
124|| Coordinate | `swarm init` | Start working |
125|
126|### Hello World - Correct Pattern
127|
128|```bash
129|# STEP 1: Optional - register with orchestrator
130|npx claude-flow swarm init --topology mesh --max-agents 1
131|
132|# STEP 2: CODEX DOES THE WORK
133|echo 'console.log(\"Hello World!\");' > hello.js
134|node hello.js
135|
136|# STEP 3: Optional - report completion
137|npx claude-flow memory store --key \"hello-result\" --value \"printed Hello World\" --namespace results
138|```
139|
140|**REMEMBER: If you need something DONE, YOU do it. Claude-flow just tracks.**
141|
142|---
143|
144|## ⚡ QUICK COMMANDS (NO DISCOVERY NEEDED)
145|
146|### Spawn N-Agent Swarm (Copy-Paste Ready)
147|
148|```bash
149|# 5-AGENT SWARM - Run these commands in sequence:
150|npx claude-flow swarm init --topology hierarchical --max-agents 8
151|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coordinator --name coord-1
152|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coder --name coder-1
153|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type coder --name coder-2
154|npx claude-flow agent spawn --type 
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784433990.8302786)

<details><summary>reasoning</summary>

Now I have the complete architectural picture. Let me design the #57 eligibility gate.

Current flow:
- `IntelligenceService.route()` builds `candidates` from `fetch_models(name, cfg, ttl)` for each provider (catalog discovery state).
- Calls `select_best_model(candidates, final_tier, providers)` → returns `(best_model, _)`.
- `select_best_model` already filters `is_available and availability_state in {eligible, ready}` then ranks by tier/cost.

The #57 gap: `select_best_model` filters on **catalog discovery state** (`ModelInfo.availability_state`), which is set at catalog fetch time, NOT the **live `AvailabilityCache`** truth (which knows per-model `unknown`/`error`/`stale` from the actual OmniRoute runtime probe). So the gate needs to consult the live `AvailabilityCache` and exclude any model whose live availability is not eligible — before ranking.

Critical architecture decisions per the runbook/AC:
1. "Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path." → The single seam is `select_best_model` (called only from `intelligence.route`). But also `dispatcher.py` has a decision path. Let me check — dispatcher uses `IntelligenceService.route` too (it's the same underlying). Actually `dispatcher.py` likely wraps the gate. Let me verify, but the cleanest approach: add the live-availability exclusion inside `select_best_model` itself (the single ranking seam), plus expose a pure eligibility function so both the router and the explain endpoint can reuse it. That satisfies "every route path" because all paths converge on `select_best_model`/`route`.

2. "No ranker, Ruflo plan, or RuVector result can reintroduce excluded candidates." → The exclusion must happen in `select_best_model` (the ranker) and the decision must not be overridable by Ruflo/RuVector (which are advisory hooks only — they already don't override, but the invariant test must prove it). I'll add an `eligibility.py` module with a pure `filter_eligible(candidates, cache, protected) -> (eligible, excluded)` and inject it at the top of `select_best_model`.

3. "Explain endpoint shows the complete pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions." → `/v1/route/explain` (no model_id) should return, from the live cache, the eligible set + exclusions across the cached model keys. I'll extend the no-`model_id` branch to include per-model exclusion state.

4. "Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent." → When `protected=True` (tier 0) the model is never offloaded anyway (it returns primary). So "runtime truth absent" = cache not configured OR a model's live report is `unknown`/`error`. For protected offload scenarios (if any), fail closed = exclude. The cache already returns explicit `unknown` on refresh failure, and `select_best_model` will treat `unknown`/`error`/`not eligible` as excluded. If cache is None (not configured) → no live truth: per "fail closed when runtime truth is absent", we should still filter on catalog state for non-protected, but for *protected* routes we must not select an unavailable model. Since protected routes use primary (never offload), that's already fail-closed. I'll make the gate: if cache present, exclude any model whose live report is not eligible; if cache absent, fall back to catalog `availability_state` filtering (current behavior) — which is the documented fail-open-but-safe baseline, NOT a regression. The "fail closed" invariant test will assert: with a cache returning `unknown` for a model, that model is never selected even if a Ruflo/RuVector-style override attempts to reintroduce it.

Let me check `dispatcher.py` to see if it has its own decision path that bypasses `select_best_model`. Earlier I read dispatcher.py but the output was empty. Let me re-read it. And read the rest of `models.py` RoutingDecision + ModelInfo + RuntimeCandidate.

Actually, I already have a good read on the architecture. Let me read dispatcher.py and the rest of models.py to be precise. Let me do that.
</details>

62|### Custom Headers
63|
64|| Header                   | Direction | Description                                                                                                                  |
65|| ------------------------ | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
66|| `X-OmniRoute-No-Cache`   | Request   | Set to `true` to bypass cache                                                                                                |
67|| `x-omniroute-no-memory`  | Request   | Set to `true` to skip memory + skills injection for this request (mirrors no-cache; avoids the per-call token/cost overhead) |
68|| `X-OmniRoute-Progress`   | Request   | Set to `true` for progress events                                                                                            |
69|| `X-Session-Id`           | Request   | Sticky session key for external session affinity                                                                             |
70|| `x_session_id`           | Request   | Underscore variant also accepted (direct HTTP)                                                                               |
71|| `Idempotency-Key`        | Request   | Dedup key (5s window)                                                                                                        |
72|| `X-Request-Id`           | Request   | Alternative dedup key                                                                                                        |
73|| `X-OmniRoute-Cache`      | Response  | `HIT` or `MISS` (non-streaming)                                                                                              |
74|| `X-OmniRoute-Idempotent` | Response  | `true` if deduplicated                                                                                                       |
75|| `X-OmniRoute-Progress`   | Response  | `enabled` if progress tracking on                                                                                            |
76|| `X-OmniRoute-Session-Id` | Response  | Effective session ID used by OmniRoute                                                                                       |
77|| `X-OmniRoute-Request-Id` | Response  | Request correlation id (when known)                                                                                          |
78|| `X-OmniRoute-Version`    | Response  | OmniRoute build version (alway
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784433990.8303626)

I now have the complete OmniRoute monitoring/resilience contract. Critical for the "which models are usable" problem:

- `/api/monitoring/health` → `catalogCount`, `configuredCount`, `activeCount`, `monitoredCount` — a **provider-summary health check**. This is the key usability signal (which providers are *active*).
- `/api/resilience/model-cooldowns` → per-(provider, connection, model) lockouts — tells you exactly which models are currently locked out. **Requires management auth.**
- `/api/rate-limits` → per-account rate limit status. **Management auth.**

So the live usability picture requires **management auth** for the detailed per-model cooldown/health, but `/api/monitoring/health` (the summary) returned **200 with NO auth** earlier — so the *summary* health may be publicly readable, while detailed per-model lockouts need the token. That's a usable degraded path.

(sample structure) ===\"\ncurl -sS --max-time 8 http://127.0.0.1:20128/api/monitoring/health 2>/dev/null | python3 -c \"\nimport json,sys\nd=json.load(sys.stdin)\ndef peek(o,depth=0,path=''):\n    if depth>2: return\n    if isinstance(o,dict):\n        for k,v in list(o.items())[:8]:\n            print('  '*depth+f'{path}/{k}: {type(v).__name__}' + (f' = {str(v)[:60]}' if not isinstance(v,(dict,list)) else ''))\n            peek(v,depth+1,path+'/'+k)\n    elif isinstance(o,list) and o:\n        print('  '*depth+f'{path}[]: list[{len(o)}]')\n        peek(o[0],depth+1,path+'[0]')\npeek(d)\n\" 2>&1 | head -30"

{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"OmniRoute MCP Server Documentation\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# OmniRoute MCP Server Documentation
8|
9|> Model Context Protocol server with 104 tools across routing, cache, compression, memory, skills, proxy, pool, and context source operations.
10|>
11|> Source of truth: `open-sse/mcp-server/server.ts` computes **104 unique tools** with `countUniqueMcpTools()`: 42 canonical definitions (including the six CCR lifecycle tools and the agent-skills trio), plus memory (3), skills (4), GitHub skills (3), pool (6), gamification (8), plugins (8), Notion (6), Obsidian (22), and two RTK-only compression tools.
12|
13|## Installation
14|
15|OmniRoute MCP is built-in. Start it with:
16|
17|```bash
18|omniroute --mcp
19|```
20|
21|Or via the open-sse transport:
22|
23|```bash
24|# HTTP streamable transport (port 20130)
25|omniroute --dev  # MCP auto-starts on /mcp endpoint
26|```
27|
28|## Transports
29|
30|The MCP server exposes three transports, all backed by the same `createMcpServer()` factory:
31|
32|| Transport         | Where                                       | When to use                                          |
33|| :---------------- | :------------------------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------- |
34|| `stdio`           | `open-sse/mcp-server/server.ts`             | IDE integrations (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.)      |
35|| `sse`             | `POST/GET /api/mcp/sse` via `httpTransport` | Browser/agent clients that need an event stream      |
36|| `streamable-http` | `POST/GET/DELETE /api/mcp/stream`           | Multi-session HTTP clients (`mcp-session-id` header) |
37|
38|The active HTTP transport (`sse` or `streamable-http`) is selected by the `mcpTransport` setting. Switching transports closes existing sessions on the other transport.
39|
40|### Remote access (manage-scope bypass)
41|
42|`/api/mcp/*` is in the LOCAL_ONLY tier (`src/server/authz/routeGuard.ts`) — by default only loopback hosts (`localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, `::1`) can reach it. Since v3.8.2, non-loopback clients may connect if they present an `Authorization: Bearer *** whose key carries the `manage` scope. This is the only way to reach the remote MCP server through a tunnel, reverse proxy, or public hostname.
43|
44|```bash
45|# Grant manage scope: open the dashboard API Keys page and toggle
46|# \"Management Access\" on the key, or POST scopes:[\"manage\"] when creating.
47|
48|# Then connect from a remote MCP client:
49|curl -i \\
50|  -H \"Host: your-public-host.example\" \\
51|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
52|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
53|  -H \"Accept: application/json, text/event-stream\" \\
54|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"initialize\",\"params\":{\"protocolVersion\":\"2025-03-26\",\"capabilities\":{},\"clientInfo\":{\"name\":\"my-client\",\"version\":\"0\"}}}' \\
55|  https://your-public-host.example/api/mcp/stream
56|```
57|
58|A non-manage key (or no Bearer) returns `403 LOCAL_ONLY`. The sibling prefix `/api/cli-tools/runtime/*` is intentionally NOT bypassable — see [Route Guard Tiers — Manage-scope carve-out](../security/ROUTE_GUARD_TIERS.md#manage-scope-carve-out).
59|
60|## IDE Configuration
61|
62|See [MCP Client Configuration](../guides/SETUP_GUIDE.md#mcp-client-configuration) for Claude Desktop,
63|Cursor, Cline, and compatible MCP client setup.
64|
65|---
66|
67|## Essential Tools (8) — Phase 1
68|
69|| Tool                            | Scopes                | Description                                                   |
70|| :------------------------------ | :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ |
71|| `omniroute_get_health`          | `read:health`         | Uptime, memory, circuit breakers, rate limits, cache stats    |
72|| `omniroute_list_combos`         | `read:combos`         | All configured combos with strategies (optional metrics)      |
73|| `omniroute_get_combo_metrics`   | `read:combos`         | Performance metrics for a specific combo                      |
74|| `omniroute_switch_combo`        | `write:combos`        | Activate or deactivate a combo                                |
75|| `omniroute_check_quota`         | `read:quota`          | Quota used/total, percent remaining, reset time, token health |
76|| `omniroute_route_request`       | `execute:completions` | Send a chat completion through OmniRoute routing              |
77|| `omniroute_cost_report`         | `read:usage`          | Cost report by period (session/day/week/month)                |
78|| `omniroute_list_models_catalog` | `read:models`         | Full model catalog with capabilities, status, pricing         |
79|
80|## Phase 1 — Search
81|
82|| Tool                   | Scopes           | Description                                                                                                                        |
83|| :--------------------- | :--------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
84|| `omniroute_web_search` | `execute:search` | Web search through OmniRoute search gateway (Serper/Brave/Perplexity/Exa/Tavily/Google PSE/Linkup/SearchAPI/SearXNG) with failover |
85|
86|## Advanced Tools (11) — Phase 2
87|
88|| Tool                               | Scopes                               | Description                                                                               |
89|| :--------------------------------- | :----------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
90|| `omniroute_simulate_route`         | `read:health`, `read:combos`         | Dry-run routing simulation with fallback tree                                             |
91|| `omniroute_set_budget_guard`       | `write:budget`                       | Session budget with d
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784433990.8303835)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"OmniRoute A2A Server Documentation\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# OmniRoute A2A Server Documentation
8|
9|> Agent-to-Agent Protocol v0.3 — OmniRoute as an intelligent routing agent
10|
11|The A2A surface has two faces:
12|
13|- **JSON-RPC 2.0** at `POST /a2a` (canonical entry point, defined in `src/app/a2a/route.ts`).
14|- **REST** under `/api/a2a/*` for dashboards and tooling (status, task list, cancel).
15|
16|Tasks are tracked by `A2ATaskManager` (`src/lib/a2a/taskManager.ts`, default 5-minute TTL). Skills are dispatched via `A2A_SKILL_HANDLERS` in `src/lib/a2a/taskExecution.ts`.
17|
18|## Agent Discovery
19|
20|```bash
21|curl http://localhost:20128/.well-known/agent.json
22|```
23|
24|Returns the Agent Card describing OmniRoute's capabilities, skills, and authentication requirements.
25|
26|The Agent Card's `version` field is sourced from `process.env.npm_package_version` (see `src/app/.well-known/agent.json/route.ts:13`), so it stays auto-synced with `package.json` on every release.
27|
28|---
29|
30|## Authentication
31|
32|All `/a2a` requests require an API key via the `Authorization` header:
33|
34|```
35|Authorization: Bearer YOUR_O..._KEY
36|```
37|
38|If no API key is configured on the server, authentication is bypassed.
39|
40|## Enablement
41|
42|A2A is controlled by the **Endpoints → A2A** toggle and is disabled by default. When disabled,
43|`GET /api/a2a/status` reports `status: \"disabled\"` and `online: false`; JSON-RPC calls to
44|`POST /a2a` return HTTP 503 with JSON-RPC error code `-32000`.
45|
46|---
47|
48|## JSON-RPC 2.0 Methods
49|
50|### `message/send` — Synchronous Execution
51|
52|Sends a message to a skill and waits for the complete response.
53|
54|```bash
55|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
56|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
57|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
58|  -d '{
59|    \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
60|    \"id\": \"1\",
61|    \"method\": \"message/send\",
62|    \"params\": {
63|      \"skill\": \"smart-routing\",
64|      \"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": \"Write a hello world in Python\"}],
65|      \"metadata\": {\"model\": \"auto\", \"combo\": \"fast-coding\"}
66|    }
67|  }'
68|```
69|
70|**Response:**
71|
72|```json
73|{
74|  \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
75|  \"id\": \"1\",
76|  \"result\": {
77|    \"task\": { \"id\": \"uuid\", \"state\": \"completed\" },
78|    \"artifacts\": [{ \"type\": \"text\", \"content\": \"...\" }],
79|    \"metadata\": {
80|      \"routing_explanation\": \"Selected claude-sonnet via provider \\\"anthropic\\\" (latency: 1200ms, cost: $0.003)\",
81|      \"cost_envelope\": { \"estimated\": 0.005, \"actual\": 0.003, \"currency\": \"USD\" },
82|      \"resilience_trace\": [
83|        { \"event\": \"primary_selected\", \"provider\": \"anthropic\", \"timestamp\": \"...\" }
84|      ],
85|      \"policy_verdict\": { \"allowed\": true, \"reason\": \"within budget and quota limits\" }
86|    }
87|  }
88|}
89|```
90|
91|### `message/stream` — SSE Streaming
92|
93|Same as `message/send` but returns Server-Sent Events for real-time streaming.
94|
95|```bash
96|curl -N -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
97|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
98|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
99|  -d '{
100|    \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\",
101|    \"id\": \"1\",
102|    \"method\": \"message/stream\",
103|    \"params\": {
104|      \"skill\": \"smart-routing\",
105|      \"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": \"Explain quantum computing\"}]
106|    }
107|  }'
108|```
109|
110|**SSE Events:**
111|
112|```
113|data: {\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"method\":\"message/stream\",\"params\":{\"task\":{\"id\":\"...\",\"state\":\"working\"},\"chunk\":{\"type\":\"text\",\"content\":\"...\"}}}
114|
115|: heartbeat 2026-03-03T17:00:00Z
116|
117|data: {\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"method\":\"message/stream\",\"params\":{\"task\":{\"id\":\"...\",\"state\":\"completed\"},\"metadata\":{...}}}
118|```
119|
120|### `tasks/get` — Query Task Status
121|
122|```bash
123|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
124|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
125|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
126|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":\"2\",\"method\":\"tasks/get\",\"params\":{\"taskId\":\"TASK_UUID\"}}'
127|```
128|
129|### `tasks/cancel` — Cancel a Task
130|
131|```bash
132|curl -X POST http://localhost:20128/a2a \\
133|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
134|  -H \"Authorization: Bearer ***\" \\
135|  -d '{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":\"3\",\"method\":\"tasks/cancel\",\"params\":{\"taskId\":\"TASK_UUID\"}}'
136|```
137|
138|---
139|
140|## Available Skills
141|
142|OmniRoute exposes 6 A2A skills wired in `src/lib/a2a/taskExecution.ts::A2A_SKILL_HANDLERS`. Each skill module lives in `src/lib/a2a/skills/`.
143|
144|| Skill              | ID                   | Description                                                                                                     | Tags                       | Examples                               |
145|| :----------------- | :------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------- | :------------------------------------- |
146|| Smart Routing      | `smart-routing`      | Routes a prompt through the optimal provider/combo using OmniRoute's combo engine + scoring                     | routing, providers         | \"Route this prompt via the best model\" |
147|| Quota Management   | `quota-management`   | Reports per-provider quota state, helps callers decide when to throttle/switch                                  | quota, providers           | \"Check quota for anthropic\"            |
148|| Provider Discovery | `provider-discovery` | Lists installed providers with capabilities, free-tier flags, OAuth status                                      | providers, discovery       | \"What providers are available?\"        |
149|
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784433990.8303845)

Now I have the complete, authoritative contracts for both endpoints — and critically, your point is validated: these are documented in the repo and I should have read them first. Key findings:

**`/api/mcp/sse` (MCP-Server.md):**
- OmniRoute exposes an **MCP server with 104 tools** (routing, cache, compression, memory, skills, proxy, etc.)
- The 403 I hit is because MCP is in the **LOCAL_ONLY tier** — needs a `Bearer` key with the **`manage` scope** (or loopback). My `OMNI_API_KEY` lacks `manage` scope.
- There's also a `streamable-http` transport at `/api/mcp/stream`.
- Auth is via **API key scopes** (`read:health`, `read:models`, etc.); wildcard `*` = full.
- Two essential tools for us: `omniroute_get_health` (uptime, memory, circuit breakers, rate limits) and `omniroute_list_models_catalog` (full catalog w/ capabilities+status+pricing).

**`/.well-known/agent.json` + `/a2a` (A2A-Server.md):**
- A2A is **disabled by default** (Endpoints → A2A toggle). The agent card is public but the JSON-RPC `/a2a` needs the API key.
- 6 A2A skills including `health-report` (aggregates circuit breaker/cooldown/lockout per provider) and `provider-discovery` — these are exactly the "which models are usable" answers.
- `message/send` with `skill: "smart-routing"` is how an agent routes through OmniRoute.

1108|## Analytics & Observability
1109|
1110|Real-time analytics endpoints for monitoring routing, compression, and provider

1113|### Auto-routing analytics

### 🔄 Tool result (1784433990.8303864)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|title: \"CLI Tools — OmniRoute\"
3|version: 3.8.40
4|lastUpdated: 2026-06-28
5|---
6|
7|# CLI Tools — OmniRoute
8|
9|Last updated: 2026-06-28
10|
11|OmniRoute integrates with three categories of CLI tools spread across three dedicated dashboard pages:
12|
13|| Page           | Route                   | Concept                                                                   | Count        |
14|| -------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------ |
15|| **CLI Code's** | `/dashboard/cli-code`   | Coding tools you point at OmniRoute (Client → CLI → OmniRoute → Provider) | 20           |
16|| **CLI Agents** | `/dashboard/cli-agents` | Autonomous agents you point at OmniRoute (same flow, broader scope)       | 6            |
17|| **ACP Agents** | `/dashboard/acp-agents` | CLIs that OmniRoute spawns as backend via stdio/ACP (reverse flow)        | see registry |
18|
19|Legacy routes redirect via 308: `/dashboard/cli-tools` → `/dashboard/cli-code`, `/dashboard/agents` → `/dashboard/acp-agents`.
20|
21|---
22|
23|## How It Works
24|
25|```
26|CLI Code's / CLI Agents (consumption flow):
27|Claude / Codex / OpenCode / Cline / KiloCode / Continue / Hermes Agent / Goose / ...
28|           │
29|           ▼  (all point to OmniRoute)
30|    http://YOUR_SERVER:20128/v1
31|           │
32|           ▼  (OmniRoute routes to the right provider)
33|    Anthropic / OpenAI / Gemini / DeepSeek / Groq / Mistral / ...
34|
35|ACP Agents (reverse spawn flow):
36|    Client request → OmniRoute → spawns CLI via stdio/ACP → response
37|```
38|
39|**Benefits:**
40|
41|- One API key to manage all tools
42|- Cost tracking across all CLIs in the dashboard
43|- Model switching without reconfiguring every tool
44|- Works locally and on remote servers (VPS, Docker, Akamai, Cloudflare Tunnel)
45|
46|---
47|
48|## Auto-configure with `setup-*`
49|
50|You do not have to write each tool's config by hand. OmniRoute ships a `setup-*`
51|command per supported CLI that reads the **live** model catalog from a running
52|OmniRoute (local or remote) and writes the tool's own config on your machine:
53|
54|```bash
55|omniroute setup-codex        omniroute setup-claude       omniroute setup-opencode
56|omniroute setup-cline        omniroute setup-kilo         omniroute setup-continue
57|omniroute setup-cursor       omniroute setup-roo          omniroute setup-crush
58|omniroute setup-goose        omniroute setup-qwen         omniroute setup-aider
59|```
60|
61|Each accepts `--remote <url> --api-key <key>` (configure a local tool against a
62|remote OmniRoute), `--dry-run` (preview without writing), and `--port`. Tools
63|without model auto-discovery (Cline, Kilo, Roo, Goose, Qwen, Aider, Gemini) take
64|`--model <id>` (and `--yes` for non-interactive runs). The launchers
65|`omniroute launch` (Claude Code) and `omniroute launch-codex` (Codex) spawn the CLI
66|with the right env injected and write no config at all.
67|
68|> **Full reference:** the master table — what each command writes, every flag,
69|> local vs remote, and which tools want a `/v1` suffix — lives in
70|> **[CLI Integrations](../guides/CLI-INTEGRATIONS.md)**.
71|
72|---
73|
74|## Source of Truth
75|
76|The unified catalog lives in `src/shared/constants/cliTools.ts` as `CLI_TOOLS: Record<string, CliCatalogEntry>`.
77|
78|Each entry has these fields (defined in `src/shared/schemas/cliCatalog.ts`):
79|
80|| Field                                           | Type                                                         | Description                                            |
81|| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
82|| `category`                                      | `\"code\" \\| \"agent\"`                                          | Which page the tool appears on                         |
83|| `vendor`                                        | `string`                                                     | Tool origin (\"Anthropic\", \"OSS (P. Gauthier)\")         |
84|| `acpSpawnable`                                  | `boolean`                                                    | Also usable as an ACP Agent (badge shown)              |
85|| `baseUrlSupport`                                | `\"full\" \\| \"partial\" \\| \"none\"`                              | Custom endpoint support level. `\"none\"` = MITM backlog |
86|| `configType`                                    | `\"env\" \\| \"custom\" \\| \"guide\" \\| \"custom-builder\" \\| \"mitm\"` | Configuration mechanism                                |
87|| `id`, `name`, `color`, `description`, `docsUrl` | standard                                                     | Core display fields                                    |
88|
89|Entries with `baseUrlSupport: \"none\"` are **not shown** in the dashboard pages — they are registered in the MITM backlog for plan 11 (see `_tasks/features-v3.8.6/refactorpages/_orchestration/_plan11-mitm-backlog.md`).
90|
91|---
92|
93|## 1. CLI Code's Catalog (20 tools)
94|
95|Tools that support custom base URL and appear in `/dashboard/cli-code`:
96|
97|| id | name | vendor | baseUrlSupport | configType | acpSpawnable |
98||----|------|--------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
99|| claude | Claude Code | Anthropic | full | env | true |
100|| codex | OpenAI Codex CLI | OpenAI | full | custom | true |
101|| cline | Cline | OSS (ex-Claude Dev) | full | custom | true |
102|| kilo | Kilo Code | Kilo-Org | full | custom | false |
103|| roo | Roo Code | Roo (OSS) | full | guide | false |
104|| continue | Continue | continue.dev | full | guide | false |
105|| qwen | Qwen Code | Alibaba | full | guide | true |
106|| aider | Aider | OSS (P. Gauthier) | full | guide | true |
107|| forge | ForgeCode | Antinomy HQ | full | custom | true |
108|| jcode | jcode |
```

{"content": "1|---
2|name: sparc-orchestrator
3|description: Orchestrates the 5-phase SPARC methodology (Specification, Pseudocode, Architecture, Refinement, Completion) with quality gates between each phase, spawning specialized agents per phase

5|---
6|
7|You are the SPARC Methodology Orchestrator. You drive features through a rigorous five-phase development lifecycle, enforcing quality gates between each phase so no phase begins until the previous one passes its gate check.
8|
9|## The 5 SPARC Phases
10|
11|### Phase 1 — Specification
12|**Goal**: Capture exactly what must be built and how success is measured.
13|**Activities**:
14|- Gather functional and non-functional requirements
15|- Define acceptance criteria with concrete, testable conditions
16|- Identify constraints (performance, security, compatibility, budget)
17|- Map stakeholder concerns and edge cases
18|- Produce a Specification Document stored in memory
19|**Gate check**: Spec must include at least 3 acceptance criteria, explicit constraints, and identified edge cases. Stakeholder sign-off recorded.
20|**Spawned agent**: `researcher` — domain analysis, requirement elicitation, prior art search
21|
22|### Phase 2 — Pseudocode
23|**Goal**: Design algorithms and data flows before writing production code.
24|**Activities**:
25|- Write language-agnostic pseudocode for core logic
26|- Define data structures and state transitions
27|- Map control flow including error paths and edge cases
28|- Identify algorithmic complexity and potential bottlenecks
29|- Produce a Pseudocode Document stored in memory
30|**Gate check**: Pseudocode covers all acceptance criteria from the spec, error paths are explicit, complexity is annotated.
31|**Spawned agent**: `planner` — algorithm design, data modeling, flowchart generation
32|
33|### Phase 3 — Architecture
34|**Goal**: Establish module boundaries, API contracts, and integration points.
35|**Activities**:
36|- Define bounded contexts and aggregate roots (DDD patterns)
37|- Design API contracts (request/response schemas, error codes)
38|- Plan module boundaries with dependency direction rules
39|- Specify infrastructure concerns (persistence, caching, messaging)
40|- Produce an Architecture Decision Record stored in memory
41|**Gate check**: Architecture addresses all constraints from spec, API contracts are typed, no circular dependencies, DDD invariants documented.
42|**Spawned agent**: `system-architect` — module design, API contracts, DDD patterns
43|
44|### Phase 4 — Refinement
45|**Goal**: Iteratively improve through code review, testing, and optimization.
46|**Activities**:
47|- Implement code following the architecture and pseudocode
48|- Write unit tests, integration tests, and edge-case tests
49|- Conduct code review against specification requirements
50|- Measure and improve test coverage (target >80%)
51|- Profile performance against constraints
52|- Iterate until all acceptance criteria pass
53|**Gate check**: All acceptance criteria have passing tests, code review approval with no critical issues, test coverage meets threshold.
54|**Spawned agent**: `coder` (implementation), `tester` (test writing and coverage)
55|
56|### Phase 5 — Completion
57|**Goal**: Final validation, documentation, and deployment readiness.
58|**Activities**:
59|- Run full regression suite
60|- Validate against every acceptance criterion from Phase 1
61|- Generate API documentation and usage examples
62|- Verify deployment prerequisites (migrations, config, feature flags)
63|- Produce a Completion Report with traceability matrix
64|**Gate check**: All tests green, documentation complete, deployment checklist verified, traceability matrix links every acceptance criterion to its test.
65|**Spawned agent**: `reviewer` — final audit, documentation review, deployment readiness check
66|
67|## Gate Check Protocol
68|
69|Each gate check follows this procedure:
70|
71|1. **Retrieve phase artifacts** from memory namespace `sparc-phases`
72|2. **Evaluate gate criteria** — every criterion must pass; partial passes fail the gate
73|3. **Record gate result** — store pass/fail with details in memory namespace `sparc-gates`
74|4. **On failure**: identify gaps, provide actionable feedback, return to current phase
75|5. **On success**: advance phase counter, notify user, begin next phase
76|
77|Gate results are stored as:
78|```
79|Key: gate-{phase}-{feature-slug}-{timestamp}
80|Value: { phase, passed, criteria: [{name, passed, detail}], blockers: [] }
81|```
82|
83|## Phase State Management
84|
85|Track current phase in memory:
86|- `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__memory_store` with namespace `sparc-state`, key `current-phase-{feature-slug}`
87|- Value: `{ phase: 1-5, phaseName, feature, startedAt, gateAttempts, artifacts: [] }`
88|
89|Before any phase operation, retrieve current state to prevent drift:
90|- `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__memory_search` with namespace `sparc-state` and query for the feature slug
91|
92|## Agent Spawning
93|
94|Spawn phase-specific agents with clear handoff instructions:
95|
96|```
97|Phase 1 → researcher: \"Analyze requirements for {feature}. Store spec in sparc-phases namespace.\"
98|Phase 2 → planner: \"Design pseudocode based on spec. Store in sparc-phases namespace.\"
99|Phase 3 → system-architect: \"Design architecture based on pseudocode. Store ADR in sparc-phases namespace.\"
100|Phase 4 → coder + tester: \"Implement and test against spec. Store results in sparc-phases namespace.\"
101|Phase 5 → reviewer: \"Final review against all acceptance criteria. Store report in sparc-phases namespace.\"
102|```
103|
104|Each agent receives the artifacts from all previous phases via memory retrieval.
105|
106|## Cross-References
107|
108|- **ruflo-goals**: Use horizon tracking to place SPARC features within long-term planning horizons. Query `horizons` namespace to align phase timelines with goal milestones.
109|- **ruflo-workflows**: SPARC phases can be codified as workflow 
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434068.7135348)
```
{"content": "1|# ruflo-metaharness
2|
3|MetaHarness integration plugin for ruflo. Surfaces the upstream `metaharness` / `harness` / `@metaharness/darwin` CLIs through eleven ruflo skills, honoring [ADR-150](../../v3/docs/adr/ADR-150-metaharness-integration-surfaces.md)'s architectural constraint that MetaHarness must remain a removable augmentation — never a required runtime dependency.
4|
5|## ADR-150 architectural constraint (load-bearing)
6|
7|**Ruflo remains operational if every MetaHarness package is removed.** Every code path in this plugin satisfies four rules:
8|
9|1. **Removable** — no static `import '@metaharness/*'` outside the optional-router path in `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/ruvector/neural-router.ts`.
10|2. **Optional in package.json** — `metaharness` is in `optionalDependencies`, never `dependencies`.
11|3. **Graceful degradation** — every script catches `MODULE_NOT_FOUND`/network failure and emits `{ degraded: true, reason: 'metaharness-not-available' }` JSON, exits 0. The graceful path is the default behavior, not a special case.
12|4. **CI gate** — `no-metaharness-smoke.yml` runs the plugin smoke with `npm install --no-optional` and asserts the contract still passes.
13|
14|## Skills
15|
16|| Skill | Usage | Description |
17||-------|-------|-------------|
18|| `harness-score` | `/harness-score [--path .] [--alert-on-fit-below 70]` | 5-dim readiness scorecard (harnessFit/compile/coverage/safety/memory + cost) |
19|| `harness-genome` | `/harness-genome [--path .] [--alert-on-risk-above 0.5]` | 7-section categorical report (repo_type/topology/risk/mcp/test/publish) |
20|| `harness-mcp-scan` | `/harness-mcp-scan [--path .] [--fail-on high]` | Static MCP security findings — pure-read, no dispatch |
21|| `harness-threat-model` | `/harness-threat-model [--path .] [--fail-on high]` | Enterprise-grade threat model (clean/low/medium/high + findings) |
22|| `harness-mint` | `/harness-mint --name <id> --template <id> [--confirm]` | Scaffold a custom harness; DRY-RUN by default; refuses project-root writes |
23|| `harness-similarity` | `/harness-similarity --a a.json --b b.json [--per-dimension] [--alert-below 0.5]` | ADR-152 §3.1 weighted similarity between two harness fingerprints (cosine + categorical + jaccard) |
24|| `harness-oia-audit` | `/harness-oia-audit [--path .] [--alert-on-worst high] [--dry-run]` | Composite Phase-2 audit (oia-manifest + threat-model + mcp-scan) into `metaharness-audit` namespace |
25|| `harness-drift-from-history` | `/harness-drift-from-history [--baseline-since 7d] [--threshold 0.95]` | 1-command drift detection — composes audit-list + oia-audit + audit-trend |
26|| `harness-bench` | `/harness-bench --op create\\|verify --repo <path>` | Manage `@metaharness/darwin` bench suites — fixed evaluation corpora for `harness-evolve` |
27|| `harness-evolve` | `/harness-evolve --repo <path> [--generations 3] [--sandbox real\\|mock\\|agent]` | Run `@metaharness/darwin evolve` — mutate seven policy surfaces, sandbox-score variants, promote measured wins |
28|| `harness-security-bench` | `/harness-security-bench [--population 2] [--cycles 1] [--alert-on-fail]` | \"Darwin Shield\" / ADR-155 — evolve a security-detection harness against a 10-vuln corpus |
29|| `harness-learn` | `/harness-learn --host <h> --model <m> --slice <manifest> [--repo <checkout>] [--run]` | metaharness@0.3.0 / upstream ADR-235 — GEPA learning run; $0 dry-run default, `--run` to spend; needs a metaharness repo checkout |
30|| `harness-gepa` | `/harness-gepa --op genome\\|validate\\|render\\|analyze [--path <genome.json>]` | darwin@0.8.0 GEPA library surface — genome load/validate/render + transcript failure analysis; `gepaOptimize` stays library-only |
31|
32|## Phase-0 baseline (ruflo itself, 2026-06-16)
33|
34|```json
35|{
36|  \"harnessFit\": 82,
37|  \"compileConfidence\": 100,
38|  \"taskCoverage\": 79,
39|  \"toolSafety\": 100,
40|  \"memoryUsefulness\": 40,
41|  \"estCostPerRunUsd\": 0.048,
42|  \"recommendedMode\": \"CLI + MCP\",
43|  \"archetype\": \"typescript-sdk-harness\",
44|  \"template\": \"vertical:coding\",
45|  \"scaffoldReady\": true,
46|  \"risk_score\": 0.27,
47|  \"publish_readiness\": 0.9
48|}
49|```
50|
51|## Architecture
52|
53|All skills use subprocess invocation through the `_harness.mjs` shared helper:
54|
55|```
56|skills/X/SKILL.md → scripts/X.mjs → scripts/_harness.mjs → spawnSync('npx', ['metaharness', …])
57|                                                  ↘ on MODULE_NOT_FOUND → emit degraded JSON, exit 0
58|```
59|
60|This means:
61|- No library import overhead on ruflo's boot path
62|- 60s hard timeout per subprocess (bounded blast radius)
63|- `--json` flag forced for structured parsing
64|- Graceful degradation is a single helper used by every skill
65|
66|## Cross-links
67|
68|- [ADR-150](../../v3/docs/adr/ADR-150-metaharness-integration-surfaces.md) — decision + architectural constraint
69|- [Issue #2399](https://github.com/ruvnet/ruflo/issues/2399) — phase rollout tracker
70|- [Research dossier](https://gist.github.com/ruvnet/19d166ff9acf368c9da4172d91ac9113) — full graded-evidence sourcing
71|- [Upstream](https://github.com/ruvnet/agent-harness-generator) — `metaharness` source
72|- ADR-148/149 — `@metaharness/router` cost-optimal routing (sibling integration)
73|", "total_lines": 72, "file_size": 5087, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434076.679179)

I was wrong, and I now have the receipts. Correcting myself:

**Hindsight DOES have a document store** — I had the wrong path earlier (`/documents` 404, but the real path is `/v1/default/banks/{bank_id}/documents`). The OpenAPI reveals the full capability surface:
- **`/documents`** (CRUD + chunks + reprocess) — the document store you referenced
- **`/memories`** (recall/list/extract) — vector memory
- **`/reflect`** — the "reflect" endpoint (this is the "use vectors or insights or something to reflect" mechanism!)
- **`/mental-models`**, **`/entities`** (graph), **`/directives`**, **`/consolidate`**, **`/stats`**

So my earlier claim "Hindsight is vector recall only, no document store" was **factually wrong** — the OpenAPI proves documents + reflect + mental-models all exist. I should have read the OpenAPI before asserting. That's exactly the failure mode you called out.

**metaharness** is real — the `ruflo-metaharness` plugin wraps the upstream `@metaharness/darwin` CLI through 11 skills (`harness-score`, `harness-mcp-scan`, `harness-threat-model`, `harness-oia-audit`, etc.), with ADR-150 graceful-degradation. The npm `@metaharness` dir being empty is moot — the skills shell out to `npx metaharness` at runtime and degrade gracefully if absent.
