{"content": "1|# ruflo-ruvector
2|
3|Self-learning vector database powered by [`ruvector@0.2.25`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ruvector) — HNSW, Adaptive LoRA embeddings, hooks-based intelligence, SONA self-optimizing patterns, brain (collective knowledge), and 91 MCP tools (verified via `ruvector mcp tools`).
4|
5|> **Pinned version:** this plugin targets `ruvector@0.2.25`. Earlier 0.1.x versions are missing several commands (`brain`, `route`, `sona`); some legacy docs referenced 2.x features that do not exist on npm. Always invoke with the pin.
6|
7|## Overview
8|
9|Wraps the `ruvector` npm package as a Ruflo plugin, providing vector embedding, semantic search, code-graph clustering, hyperbolic projection, self-learning hooks, and SONA / Brain diagnostics. ruvector's Rust backend delivers sub-millisecond queries and 52,000+ inserts/sec.
10|
11|## Prerequisites
12|
13|```bash
14|# Required
15|npm install ruvector@0.2.25
16|
17|# Optional add-ons (install as needed)
18|npm install ruvector-onnx-embeddings-wasm   # required for `embed text` to work
19|npm install @ruvector/pi-brain              # required for `brain` subcommands
20|npm install @ruvector/ruvllm                # required for `sona` subcommands (JS fallback)
21|```
22|
23|Run a health check:
24|```bash
25|npx -y ruvector@0.2.25 doctor
26|```
27|
28|## Installation
29|
30|```bash
31|claude --plugin-dir plugins/ruflo-ruvector
32|```
33|
34|## MCP Integration (91 Tools)
35|
36|Register with the pinned version:
37|```bash
38|claude mcp add ruvector -- npx -y ruvector@0.2.25 mcp start
39|```
40|
41|Key tool categories: hooks routing, AST analysis, diff classification, coverage routing, graph clustering, security scanning, RAG context, brain knowledge, SONA learning.
42|
43|## Agents
44|
45|| Agent | Model | Role |
46||-------|-------|------|
47|| `vector-engineer` | sonnet | Embedding, HNSW indexing, code-graph clustering, hyperbolic projection, hooks routing, brain/SONA |
48|
49|## Skills
50|
51|| Skill | Usage | Description |
52||-------|-------|-------------|
53|| `vector-setup` | `/vector-setup [--full]` | First-run installer: pins `ruvector@0.2.25`, adds ONNX/Brain/SONA/router add-ons, registers MCP, runs `doctor` |
54|| `vector-embed` | `/vector-embed <text>` | ONNX embeddings (384-dim) via `embed text` |
55|| `vector-cluster` | `/vector-cluster <files...>` | Spectral/Louvain code-graph clustering via `hooks graph-cluster` |
56|| `vector-hyperbolic` | `/vector-hyperbolic <text>` | Standard ONNX embed + Poincare projection in user code |
57|
58|## Commands (`/vector` slash command)
59|
60|The full surface is documented in `commands/vector.md` (80+ subcommands). Quick reference:
61|
62|```bash
63|# Embedding
64|/vector embed <text>                 # ruvector embed text \"<text>\"
65|/vector embed-adaptive <text>        # ruvector embed text \"<text>\" --adaptive --domain code
66|/vector embed-file <path>            # read file, pass content as text
67|/vector embed-benchmark              # ruvector embed benchmark
68|
69|# Database lifecycle
70|/vector db create <path>             # ruvector create <path> -d 384 -m cosine
71|/vector db stats <path>              # ruvector stats <path>
72|/vector insert <db> <json>           # ruvector insert <db> <json>
73|/vector search <db> <vector-json>    # ruvector search <db> -v <json> -k N
74|/vector export <db>                  # ruvector export <db> -o backup.json
75|/vector import <file>                # ruvector import <file> -d <database>
76|
77|# RVF cognitive containers (45 example stores)
78|/vector rvf create|ingest|query|status|segments|derive|compact|export|examples|download
79|
80|# GNN + attention (real native bindings)
81|/vector gnn info|layer|search|compress
82|/vector attention list|compute|benchmark|hyperbolic|info
83|
84|# Code intelligence (hooks)
85|/vector ast <file>                   # ruvector hooks ast-analyze <file>
86|/vector hooks ast-complexity <files...>
87|/vector hooks coverage-route <file> | coverage-suggest <files...>
88|/vector hooks rag-context <query>
89|/vector hooks route|route-enhanced|suggest-context
90|/vector cluster <files...>           # ruvector hooks graph-cluster <files>
91|/vector hooks security-scan <files...>
92|/vector hooks diff-analyze|diff-classify|diff-similar [commit]
93|/vector hooks remember|recall <query>
94|/vector hooks coedit-record|coedit-suggest|error-record|error-suggest
95|/vector hooks trajectory-begin|trajectory-step|trajectory-end
96|
97|# Native + workers
98|/vector native list|run <type>|benchmark|compare
99|/vector workers triggers|presets|phases|dispatch|status|...
100|
101|# Collective intelligence
102|/vector brain status|search|share|list|drift|partition|transfer|sync|page  (needs @ruvector/pi-brain)
103|/vector sona status|info|stats|patterns|train|export                       (needs @ruvector/ruvllm)
104|/vector llm models|embed|benchmark|info                                    (needs @ruvector/ruvllm)
105|
106|# Identity + edge compute (pi network)
107|/vector identity generate|show|export|import
108|/vector edge status|balance|tasks|join|dashboard
109|
110|# Server / decompile / demo / system
111|/vector server [-p 8080] [-g 50051]
112|/vector decompile <npm-pkg-or-file-or-url>
113|/vector demo --basic | --gnn | --graph
114|/vector doctor | info | benchmark | install | setup
115|```
116|
117|## End-to-End Example: store + search project files
118|
119|```bash
120|# 0. One-time setup
121|/vector-setup
122|
123|# 1. Create a database
124|npx -y ruvector@0.2.25 create project.db -d 384 -m cosine
125|
126|# 2. Embed every TypeScript source file (loop — no built-in --batch)
127|mkdir -p .vec
128|for f in $(find src -name '*.ts'); do
129|  npx -y ruvector@0.2.25 embed text \"$(cat \"$f\")\" -o \".vec/${f//\\//_}.json\"
130|done
131|
132|# 3. Insert all embeddings (assumes a JSON array of {id, vector, metadata})
133|jq -s '[.[] | {id: input_filename, vector: .vector}]' .vec/*.json > corpus.json
134|npx -y ruvect
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434076.7701619)
```
{"content": "1|# ruflo-intelligence
2|
3|User-facing surface for Ruflo's self-learning system. Wraps **29 intelligence-related MCP tools** across four families into discoverable skills, commands, and the canonical 4-step pipeline (RETRIEVE → JUDGE → DISTILL → CONSOLIDATE). Coordinates with `ruflo-agentdb` (namespace convention), `ruflo-ruvector` (trajectory recording substrate), and `ruflo-browser` (consumes trajectory hooks for session replay).
4|
5|> **Status:** ADR-0001 implemented. Plugin v0.3.0 targets `@claude-flow/cli` v3.6.x.
6|
7|## Install
8|
9|```
10|/plugin marketplace add ruvnet/ruflo
11|/plugin install ruflo-intelligence@ruflo
12|```
13|
14|## Compatibility
15|
16|- **CLI:** pinned to `@claude-flow/cli` v3.6 major+minor.
17|- **Verification:** `bash plugins/ruflo-intelligence/scripts/smoke.sh` is the contract.
18|
19|## Tool inventory
20|
21|| Family | Count | Source |
22||--------|------:|--------|
23|| `neural_*` | 6 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/neural-tools.ts:195, 312, 413, 539, 651, 706` |
24|| `hooks_intelligence_*` (incl. dispatcher + reset) | 10 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/hooks-tools.ts:2093, 2226, 2296, 2355, 2404, 2556, 2634, 2741, 2952, 3027` |
25|| Routing & meta hooks (`hooks_route`, `hooks_explain`, `hooks_pretrain`, `hooks_build-agents`, `hooks_metrics`, `hooks_transfer`) | 6 | `hooks-tools.ts:884, 1062, 1420, 1499, 1593, 1664` |
26|| `hooks_model-*` (3-tier routing) | 3 | `hooks-tools.ts:3797, 3844, 3879` |
27|| `ruvllm_sona_*` + `ruvllm_microlora_*` | 4 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/ruvllm-tools.ts:142, 169, 192, 222` |
28|| **Total** | **29** | — |
29|
30|## The 4-step intelligence pipeline
31|
32|CLAUDE.md describes the V3 intelligence loop as four discrete phases. This plugin operationalizes them:
33|
34|| Step | What happens | Tools |
35||------|--------------|-------|
36|| **RETRIEVE** | Pull relevant patterns + past trajectories from HNSW index | `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search`, `agentdb_pattern-search`, `agentdb_semantic-route` |
37|| **JUDGE** | Score retrieved candidates with verdicts (success / failure / partial) | `hooks_intelligence_attention`, `neural_predict`, `hooks_explain` |
38|| **DISTILL** | Extract the key learnings via LoRA / SONA adaptation | `ruvllm_sona_adapt`, `ruvllm_microlora_adapt`, `neural_train`, `hooks_intelligence_learn` |
39|| **CONSOLIDATE** | Prevent catastrophic forgetting via EWC++ | `agentdb_consolidate`, `ruvllm_microlora_adapt --consolidate`, `neural_compress` |
40|
41|For an end-to-end run:
42|
43|```
44|hooks_pretrain
45|  → hooks_intelligence_trajectory-start
46|    → (each step) hooks_intelligence_trajectory-step
47|  → hooks_intelligence_trajectory-end
48|  → hooks_intelligence_learn
49|  → ruvllm_sona_adapt    # DISTILL
50|  → agentdb_consolidate  # CONSOLIDATE
51|  → neural_compress      # storage efficiency
52|```
53|
54|## Cross-project pattern transfer (IPFS)
55|
56|`hooks_transfer` is the substrate plugin's most underused capability. It publishes learned patterns to IPFS (via Pinata) so a different project — or a different machine — can fetch and apply them. Use the `intelligence-transfer` skill or call directly:
57|
58|```bash
59|# Publish patterns from this project to IPFS
60|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"store\", \"patterns\": [...]}'
61|
62|# Fetch and apply patterns from a CID
63|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"load\", \"cid\": \"QmXyz...\"}'
64|
65|# Mirror an entire project's patterns
66|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"from-project\", \"source\": \"/path/to/project\"}'
67|```
68|
69|Prerequisite: `PINATA_API_JWT` (or the equivalent endpoint env vars) must be configured. Without it, `hooks_transfer` returns a structured `success: false` with the missing-config error.
70|
71|## Hook integration
72|
73|Several Claude Code hooks fire intelligence-side writes:
74|
75|| Hook | Tool invoked | Target |
76||------|--------------|--------|
77|| `pre-task` | `hooks_route` + `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search` | RETRIEVE phase |
78|| `post-task --train-neural` | `agentdb_pattern-store` (ReasoningBank) → falls back to `memory_store --namespace pattern` | DISTILL phase, writes to **`pattern`** namespace |
79|| `pretrain` (one-shot) | `hooks_pretrain` → seeds `memory_store --namespace patterns` | Bootstrap, writes to **`patterns`** namespace (plural) |
80|| Trajectory hooks (ruvector substrate) | `intelligence_trajectory-*` | Recorded by `ruflo-ruvector`; consumed by this plugin's pattern-store |
81|
82|> **Pluralization gotcha:** ReasoningBank fallback writes to `pattern` (singular). The `pretrain` hook writes to `patterns` (plural). They are *different* namespaces. See `ruflo-agentdb` ADR-0001 §\"Namespace convention\" for the canonical contract.
83|
84|## Namespace coordination with ruflo-agentdb
85|
86|This plugin defers to [ruflo-agentdb ADR-0001](../ruflo-agentdb/docs/adrs/0001-agentdb-optimization.md) for namespace conventions. Three reserved namespaces are read by the intelligence pipeline:
87|
88|| Namespace | Read by | Source |
89||-----------|---------|--------|
90|| `pattern` | `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search`, `agentdb_pattern-search` | ReasoningBank fallback target |
91|| `patterns` (plural) | `hooks_pretrain`, `neural_train` corpus | distinct from `pattern` |
92|| `claude-memories` | `memory_search_unified` (default include) | Claude Code auto-memory bridge |
93|
94|Do **not** invent new top-level namespaces for intelligence purposes — the convention is owned upstream.
95|
96|## EWC++ consolidation
97|
98|The plugin claims EWC++ consolidation; here's how to actually invoke it:
99|
100|1. **At trajectory end**, call `hooks_intelligence_learn` to register the outcome.
101|2. **Periodically** (or after N task completions), call `agentdb_consolidate` to fold patterns into the long-term store under EWC++ semantics.
102|3. **For SONA / MicroLoRA adapters specifically**, call `ruvllm_microlora_adapt` with 
```

<details><summary>reasoning</summary>

Let me carefully understand what the user is now proposing, because this is a significant design proposal that refines the #57 eligibility gate.

The user is describing a **live canary/health-check mechanism**: for each model returned by OmniRoute's catalog (`/v1/models`), send a single-token prompt to that model via OmniRoute. Based on the response:
- If the **provider is wrong** (e.g., auth failure, provider misconfigured, connection refused) → disable it / make it not visible.
- If the **usage is just exhausted** (quota/rate-limit) → keep the model but note it (it's not broken, just temporarily unavailable).
- If the **model provider is active AND the model has usage** (i.e., it responded successfully) → it's usable for our purposes.

And OmniRoute even exposes per-model details: tooling, capabilities, etc.

So this is essentially a **live probe / canary / circuit-breaker seeding** mechanism that populates the llm-gate availability cache with REAL usability data, rather than relying solely on the catalog's descriptive `availability_state` or the management-only health endpoints.

This connects directly to the OmniRoute findings:
- `/api/monitoring/health` (public, no-auth) gives provider-level health: `providerHealth`, `providerBreakers`, `circuitBreakers`, `lockouts`, `quotaMonitor`, `credentialHealth`.
- `/api/resilience/model-cooldowns` (mgmt) gives per-(provider, connection, model) lockouts.
- The A2A `health-report` skill aggregates circuit-breaker/cooldown/lockout per provider.

But the user wants something MORE granular: per-MODEL probing via actual inference. That's a real canary. This is exactly what `llm_gate`'s `AvailabilityCache` is for — but the cache currently wraps a `Callable[[], AvailabilityReport]` that is fed by the OmniRoute adapter (catalog + health). The user is now proposing the *content* of that report should be driven by actual single-token probes.

Let me think about how this maps onto the existing llm-gate architecture and what's the right design.

- `select_best_model` in router.py filters candidates on catalog `availability_state`, then ranks.

The user's proposal adds a **probe layer**: a canary that actually calls each model with a 1-token prompt through OmniRoute, classifies the outcome:

This is a "shadow probe" / "canary" pattern. It's a great idea and directly addresses the "3628 models but which actually work" problem. The catalog tells you what exists; a live probe tells you what responds.

Now, important: the user is describing this as the mechanism that should feed llm-gate's eligibility. This is essentially the design for #57's availability source — a live probe that produces the `AvailabilityReport`.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434161.0149617)

Now I have the real code surface. Key findings that shape the design:

1. **`OmniRouteHTTPTransport`** (omniroute.py:37) currently only implements `catalog()` → `GET /v1/models`. It does NOT do chat completions. So a "single-token canary" needs a new transport method (e.g. `probe(model_id)` → `POST /v1/chat/completions` with a 1-token message), or a new `OmniRouteTransport` operation.

2. **`availability.py`** already has the full normalized model: `AvailabilityState` enum (ELIGIBLE, DEGRADED, QUOTA_EXHAUSTED, RATE_LIMITED, UNAUTHORIZED, LOCKED_OUT, CIRCUIT_OPEN, TIMEOUT, etc.), `RuntimeObservation`, `AvailabilityCandidate`, `AvailabilityReport`, `CandidateRequirements`. This is exactly the classification vocabulary your design needs — "provider wrong → UNAVAILABLE/DENIED", "usage exhausted → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (keep but note)", "active + usage → ELIGIBLE".

3. **`router.py select_best_model`** (line 6) filters `candidates` on `model.availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` BEFORE ranking. So the gate already exists as a filter on `availability_state` — #57's job is to populate that field from live probing instead of leaving it `unknown`.

4. **Transport protocol** (`OmniRouteTransport`) is the injection seam: `catalog()`, `runtime()`, `discover_capabilities()`. A canary/probe is a natural new operation (`probe(model_id)` → returns success/error classification).

So the design your message describes is a **`ModelProbe` / canary subsystem** that:
- Takes the catalog (3628 models) from `catalog()`
- For each (batched, rate-limited, concurrent with caps), sends a single-token completion via a new `probe()` transport method
- Classifies the response into `AvailabilityState`:
  - auth/401/403 → UNAUTHORIZED or DENIED (provider key wrong/missing → "disable it")
  - 429 / quota → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (keep but note)
  - timeout/connection refused → TIMEOUT/UNAVAILABLE (disable)
  - 200 with content → ELIGIBLE (ACTIVE + USAGE)
  - capability mismatch (asked for tools but unsupported) → CAPABILITY_MISMATCH
- Feeds results into `AvailabilityCache` so `select_best_model`'s filter and `/v1/route/explain` reflect live state.

This is the right architecture and directly answers "which of 3628 models work." It also sidesteps the missing management token — probing uses only the API key.

Per your standing protocol (audit tools → read docs → ingest → then design/implement), I've done the audit + doc ingestion. Now I should design this concretely and store it, then implement #57 against it. But first, a few things I must confirm before writing code (don't guess):

**Critical unknowns to verify before implementation:**
1. Does `OmniRouteHTTPTransport` have a `runtime()` method yet, and what does it call? (catalog-only per grep — need to see lines 1-130 of omniroute.py)
2. How does `AvailabilityCache` currently get fed (the #56 singleton in api.py)? #57 wires the cache's `fetch` callable to the prober.
3. The catalog has 3628 models — probing all is expensive. Need a strategy: probe lazily/on-demand per routing request, or a background sweep with concurrency caps + per-provider rate limits. OmniRoute exposes `rate-limits` but we can't read it (no mgmt token) → must use conservative static caps.

{"content": "1|\"\"\"Core routing algorithm prioritizing quality, tier, and capability.\"\"\"
2|
3|from llm_gate.models import ModelInfo, ProviderConfig
4|
5|
6|def select_best_model(
7|    candidates: list[ModelInfo], tier: int, configs: dict[str, ProviderConfig]
8|) -> tuple[ModelInfo | None, list[str]]:
9|    \"\"\"Select the highest-quality eligible model out of candidates.
10|
11|    The selector is deterministic and only considers candidates that satisfy the
12|    requested tier and are currently available. Quality confidence wins first,
13|    then capability tier, provider priority, and stable model ID.
14|    \"\"\"
15|    valid = [
16|        model
17|        for model in candidates
18|        if model.capability_tier <= tier
19|        and model.is_available
20|        and model.availability_state in {\"eligible\", \"ready\"}
21|    ]
22|    if not valid:
23|        return None, [m.id for m in candidates]
24|
25|    valid.sort(
26|        key=lambda model: (
27|            -(
28|                model.quality_confidence
29|                if model.quality_confidence is not None
30|                else max(0.0, 1.0 - model.capability_tier / 3.0)
31|            ),
32|            model.capability_tier,
33|            -configs[model.provider].priority,
34|            model.id,
35|        )
36|    )
37|
38|    chosen = valid[0]
39|    alts = [m.id for m in valid[1:5]]
40|    return chosen, alts
41|", "total_lines": 40, "file_size": 1284, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434219.2219546)

Now I've read all of omniroute.py (602 lines) and understand the transport fully. Two critical facts:

1. **`OmniRouteHTTPTransport` only does GET requests** (`_get_json` uses `client.stream("GET", ...)`). It does NOT do POST/chat completions. So a "single-token canary" needs either: (a) a new POST method on the transport, or (b) a separate `ModelProbe` that uses its own httpx POST — but it must honor the same security model (`validate_upstream_url`, `host_is_allowed`, credential-safe). 

2. **`runtime()` reads `/api/monitoring/health`** (the `health` source) and merges `providerHealth`, `providerBreakers`, `lockouts`, `learnedLimits` into per-provider observations. **It currently calls `/api/monitoring/health` with NO token** (since `health` is not in `_MANAGEMENT_SOURCES`). And we proved earlier that `/api/monitoring/health` returns 200 publicly! So the runtime `health` source ALREADY works without a management token — my earlier concern was wrong. The code only requires `management_token` for `rate_limits`/`model_cooldowns`/`budget`/`token_limits`, not for `health`. So `OmniRouteHTTPTransport(base_url, api_key=...)` with `runtime_sources={"health"}` would already populate provider-level health.

So the gap is narrower than I thought:
- **Provider-level health**: already works via `runtime()` + `/api/monitoring/health` (no token needed).
- **Per-model live usability**: NOT covered — needs the canary/probe you described.
- **Per-model quota/lockout**: needs management token (rate_limits/cooldowns). The canary also captures this (429→quota_exhausted, lockout→locked_out).

Now let me get the actual code-review-graph results (correct commands) and finish reading availability.py (501–1276) — I must read ALL of it before designing. Batching.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434232.244765)

Major discovery: **the probe concept ALREADY EXISTS in availability.py.** Lines 620–715 define `_probe_state()` which consumes a `RuntimeObservation` with `source == "llm-gate:probe"` and classifies probe results into `AvailabilityState`:
- `ready` → ELIGIBLE (with `usage_available=True`, http 200, auth authorized)
- `usage_unavailable` / `completion_unavailable` → DEGRADED (provider active but usage exhausted)
- `timeout` → TIMEOUT or DENIED
- `failed` (error_class) → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED / RATE_LIMITED / UNAVAILABLE / DENIED / MALFORMED
- `skipped` (cooldown/quarantined) → RATE_LIMITED / DENIED

This is **exactly** the classification your message described:
- "provider is wrong → disable" = UNAUTHORIZED/DENIED
- "usage just exhausted → keep but note" = QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (DEGRADED)
- "active + usage → usable" = ELIGIBLE (ready)

So the infrastructure for the canary is already scaffolded in `availability.py`. What's MISSING (the #57 work) is:
1. The **actual probe executor** — something that sends a single-token completion to each model and produces these `RuntimeObservation`s with `source="llm-gate:probe"`. The `normalize_observation`/`_probe_state` code exists but nothing calls it with real probe data yet.
2. Wiring that probe output into the `AvailabilityCache` (the #56 singleton in api.py) so `select_best_model`'s `availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` filter and `/v1/route/explain` reflect it.

Also: code-review-graph CLI doesn't accept `--format json` (it's `--format` only, no flag), so my commands errored. Let me use the correct syntax to get real results. Let me finish reading availability.py (901–1276) and run code-review-graph correctly. Batching.

931|def _policy_reason(
932|    candidate: AvailabilityCandidate, requirements: CandidateRequirements
933|) -> str | None:
934|    model = candidate.model
935|    model_capabilities = frozenset(canonical_capability(value) for value in model.capabilities)
936|    missing = sorted(requirements.required - model_capabilities)
937|    if missing:
938|        return f\"missing capability: {missing[0]}\"
939|    if requirements.allow_models and model.id not in requirements.allow_models:
940|        return \"model not in allowlist\"
941|    if model.id in requirements.deny_models:
942|        return \"model denied by policy\"
943|    if requirements.allow_providers and model.provider not in requirements.allow_providers:
944|        return \"provider not in allowlist\"

947|    return None

950|def select_capable_candidates(

952|) -> list[AvailabilityCandidate]:

954|    result: list[AvailabilityCandidate] = []

956|        if _candidate_is_eligible(item, requirements):

1029|) -> Any:

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434244.227413)

I've now read **all of availability.py (1276 lines), router.py (40), omniroute.py (602), and ran code-review-graph for real results.** 

The critical finding that reshapes everything: **the llm-gate codebase ALREADY has the complete availability/eligibility engine** — `normalize_observation()`, `_probe_state()` (the exact canary classification your message describes), `select_capable_candidates()`, `explain_candidates()`, `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()`. The probe classification state machine (lines 620–715) is precisely:
- `ready` → ELIGIBLE (active + usage)
- `usage_unavailable`/`completion_unavailable` → DEGRADED (keep but note; usage exhausted)
- `timeout`/`failed(unauthorized)`/`failed(quota_exhausted)` → TIMEOUT/UNAUTHORIZED/QUOTA_EXHAUSTED/DENIED (disable)

So the gap is NOT a design problem — it's an **implementation/wiring gap**. The engine exists but:
1. Nothing **executes the actual probe** (sends a single-token completion per model) and feeds `RuntimeObservation(source="llm-gate:probe")` into `normalize_observation`.
2. The `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()` calls `runtime()` (health) but has **no probe integration** — it never produces `source="llm-gate:probe"` observations.
3. `select_best_model` (router.py) only checks `availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` — which stays `unknown` because no probe ever ran.

This means **#57 is mostly about adding the probe executor + wiring it into the adapter/cache**, NOT redesigning. The classification logic you described is already written and tested (there are surely tests for `_probe_state`).

Let me verify the code-review-graph callers result and check the existing tests for the probe path, plus confirm where `AvailabilityCache` is wired (api.py). This tells me exactly what #57 must add. Batching the final reads.

36|def _build_availability_cache() -> AvailabilityCache | None:
37|    \"\"\"Build the bounded availability cache backed by the native OmniRoute transport.
38|
39|    Returns ``None`` when no OmniRoute endpoint is configured, so the server
40|    still boots without availability explainability.  The transport is
41|    loopback-only and credential-safe; a misconfigured base URL fails closed
42|    to ``None`` rather than crashing startup.
43|    \"\"\"
44|    base_url = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_BASE_URL\") or os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL\")
45|    if not base_url or base_url.strip().lower() in {\"\", \"none\"}:
46|        return None
47|    api_key = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\")
48|    management_token = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_MANAGEMENT_TOKEN\")
49|    usage_api_key_id = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_USAGE_API_KEY_ID\")
50|    allow_private = _allowed_private_hosts() | {
51|        item.strip().lower()
52|        for item in os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_ALLOW_PRIVATE_HOSTS\", \"\").split(\",\")
53|        if item.strip()
54|    }
55|    try:
56|        transport = OmniRouteHTTPTransport(
57|            base_url,
58|            api_key=api_key,
59|            management_token=management_token,
60|            usage_api_key_id=usage_api_key_id,
61|            allow_private_hosts=allow_private,
62|        )
63|    except Exception:
64|        return None
65|    adapter = OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter(transport)
66|    return AvailabilityCache(
67|        source=adapter.evaluate,
68|        ttl_seconds=DEFAULT_AVAILABILITY_TTL_SECONDS,
69|        stale_window_seconds=DEFAULT_AVAILABILITY_STALE_WINDOW_SECONDS,
70|    )

76|
77|
78|def _allowed_private_hosts() -> set[str]:
79|    configured = os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_ALLOW_PRIVATE_HOSTS\", \"\")

88|    api_key = os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_API_KEY\") or os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\")

- `openai_probe_transport` — sends `POST /v1/chat/completions` with `PROBE_PROMPT`, `max_tokens=1`, `tools=[]`
- `ProbeObservation` → `.as_runtime_observation()` produces `source="llm-gate:probe"` with all the classification fields

I owe you a corrected, accurate status — my earlier framing was wrong on a key point:

**The canary mechanism already exists and is fully built + tested.** `llm_gate/probes.py` implements exactly what you described — `ProbeRunner` sends a single-token `POST /v1/chat/completions` (`PROBE_PROMPT`, `max_tokens=1`) per model and classifies the result:
- 200 + usage → `ready` → ELIGIBLE (active + usable)
- 200 but zero usage / empty completion → `degraded` → keep but note (QUOTA_EXHAUSTED-style)
- 401/403 → `unauthorized` → DENIED (provider wrong → disable)
- 429 → `rate_limited` → RATE_LIMITED
- 503/timeout → UNAVAILABLE/TIMEOUT (disable)
- repeated failures → quarantine (with secret redaction)

This feeds `normalize_observation(source="llm-gate:probe")` which already contains the full `_probe_state` classification state machine (availability.py:620–715). So the engine is 100% there.

**What's actually missing (the real #57 scope):** `probes.py` is imported *nowhere except its tests*. The `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()` (availability.py:1117) calls `catalog()` + `runtime()` but **never the probe runner**. So `route()` → `select_best_model()` filters on `availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` (router.py:20) but those fields stay `unknown` forever. **#57 = wire the existing `ProbeRunner` into the adapter so `evaluate()` returns probe-classified candidates, and feed those into `select_best_model` + `AvailabilityCache` + `/v1/route/explain`.** The hard classification work is done; only the integration is missing.

<details><summary>reasoning</summary>

The user is giving me important architectural direction about role separation:

1. **My role (Hermes/orchestrator)**: investigating, orchestrating, researching (or offloading research and interpreting results), designing, architecting, verifying, validating, etc. — the high-level cognitive work.

2. **Lesser models (workers via ruflo/OmniRoute/Codex)**: should do the actual lifting (the grunt implementation work).

3. **Open question**: who owns ticket creation, AC verification, work-item state tracking, etc.?

This is a meta-process question about the division of labor in our stack. The user is essentially saying: I've been doing too much of the execution myself; the lesser models should be doing the lifting. And they're asking me to think about the ownership model for:
- ticket creation

## Parent
Part of #30 and the flagship completion program in #28/#36.

## Depends on
#30.2, #29.4, and existing dispatcher/router code.

## Goal
Prove that all route/workflow paths filter candidates using live eligibility before any adaptive or cost ranking.

## Scope
- Wire normalized availability into Python router, dispatcher, API, and proxy decision paths.
- Preserve exclusions and availability snapshot in RoutingDecision.
- Add invariant tests that an ineligible candidate cannot be selected by a learned/adaptive ranker.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path.
- [ ] No ranker, Ruflo plan, or RuVector result can reintroduce excluded candidates.
- [ ] Explain endpoint shows the complete pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions.
- [ ] Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent.
- [ ] Integration tests cover all public route entry points.

## Swarm routing
Lower-tier integration agent; stronger adversarial review required for the invariant tests.", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

```json
{
 "command": "cd /home/nick/dev/llm-gate\nAK=$(grep -E '^export OMNI_API_KEY=' ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null | head -1 | sed 's/.*=//')\ncat > /tmp/probe_canary.py <<'PY'\nimport os, json, urllib.request\n# 1) grab a real model id from the live catalog\nreq = urllib.request.Request(\"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1/models\",\n    headers={\"Authorization\": f\"Bearer {os.environ['AK']}\"})\ncatalog = json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10).read())\nids = [m[\"id\"] for m in catalog.get(\"data\", []) if isinstance(m, dict) and m.get(\"id\")]\nprint(\"catalog size:\", len(ids))\n# pick a chat-capable model that likely works\nprobe_id = next((i for i in ids if i.startswith(\"anthropic/claude\")), ids[0])\nprint(\"probing model:\", probe_id)\n\n# 2) use the repo's existing probe transport + runner\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0, \"/home/nick/dev/llm-gate\")\nfrom llm_gate.probes import (ProbeRunner, ProbePolicy, openai_probe_transport,\n                             ProbeObservation)\ntransport = openai_probe_transport(\"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1\")\nrunner = ProbeRunner(ProbePolicy(max_models_per_run=1, cooldown_seconds=0, timeout_seconds=15))\nresult = runner.run([probe_id], transport)\nr = result[0]\nprint(\"status:\", r.status)\nprint(\"availability_state:\", r.availability_state)\nprint(\"error_class:\", r.error_class)\nprint(\"usage_available:\", r.usage_available)\nprint(\"http_status:\", r.http_status)\nprint(\"payload sent max_tokens:\", r.payload.get(\"max_tokens\") if hasattr(r,'payload') else 'n/a')\nobs = r.as_runtime_observation()\nprint(\"runtime obs source:\", obs.source)\nfrom llm_gate.availability import normalize_observation, AvailabilityState, ModelInfo\nst = normalize_observation(ModelInfo(id=probe_id, provider=probe_id.split('/')[0], capability_tier=2), obs, now=r.observed_at)\nprint(\"normalized state:\", st.state.value)\nPY\nAK=\"$AK\" .venv/bin/python /tmp/probe_canary.py 2>&1 | head -40",
 "timeout": 60
}
```

I was wrong, and I now have the receipts. Correcting myself:

**Hindsight DOES have a document store** — I had the wrong path earlier (`/documents` 404, but the real path is `/v1/default/banks/{bank_id}/documents`). The OpenAPI reveals the full capability surface:
- **`/documents`** (CRUD + chunks + reprocess) — the document store you referenced
- **`/memories`** (recall/list/extract) — vector memory
- **`/reflect`** — the "reflect" endpoint (this is the "use vectors or insights or something to reflect" mechanism!)
- **`/mental-models`**, **`/entities`** (graph), **`/directives`**, **`/consolidate`**, **`/stats`**

So my earlier claim "Hindsight is vector recall only, no document store" was **factually wrong** — the OpenAPI proves documents + reflect + mental-models all exist. I should have read the OpenAPI before asserting. That's exactly the failure mode you called out.

**metaharness** is real — the `ruflo-metaharness` plugin wraps the upstream `@metaharness/darwin` CLI through 11 skills (`harness-score`, `harness-mcp-scan`, `harness-threat-model`, `harness-oia-audit`, etc.), with ADR-150 graceful-degradation. The npm `@metaharness` dir being empty is moot — the skills shell out to `npx metaharness` at runtime and degrade gracefully if absent. So metaharness IS usable as a quality gate over #57 (harness-score, harness-mcp-scan, harness-threat-model) — pending `npx metaharness` being resolvable.

Now I have the real code surface. Key findings that shape the design:

1. **`OmniRouteHTTPTransport`** (omniroute.py:37) currently only implements `catalog()` → `GET /v1/models`. It does NOT do chat completions. So a "single-token canary" needs a new transport method (e.g. `probe(model_id)` → `POST /v1/chat/completions` with a 1-token message), or a new `OmniRouteTransport` operation.

2. **`availability.py`** already has the full normalized model: `AvailabilityState` enum (ELIGIBLE, DEGRADED, QUOTA_EXHAUSTED, RATE_LIMITED, UNAUTHORIZED, LOCKED_OUT, CIRCUIT_OPEN, TIMEOUT, etc.), `RuntimeObservation`, `AvailabilityCandidate`, `AvailabilityReport`, `CandidateRequirements`. This is exactly the classification vocabulary your design needs — "provider wrong → UNAVAILABLE/DENIED", "usage exhausted → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (keep but note)", "active + usage → ELIGIBLE".

3. **`router.py select_best_model`** (line 6) filters `candidates` on `model.availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` BEFORE ranking. So the gate already exists as a filter on `availability_state` — #57's job is to populate that field from live probing instead of leaving it `unknown`.

4. **Transport protocol** (`OmniRouteTransport`) is the injection seam: `catalog()`, `runtime()`, `discover_capabilities()`. A canary/probe is a natural new operation (`probe(model_id)` → returns success/error classification).

So the design your message describes is a **`ModelProbe` / canary subsystem** that:
- Takes the catalog (3628 models) from `catalog()`
- For each (batched, rate-limited, concurrent with caps), sends a single-token completion via a new `probe()` transport method
- Classifies the response into `AvailabilityState`:
  - auth/401/403 → UNAUTHORIZED or DENIED (provider key wrong/missing → "disable it")
  - 429 / quota → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (keep but note)
  - timeout/connection refused → TIMEOUT/UNAVAILABLE (disable)
  - 200 with content → ELIGIBLE (ACTIVE + USAGE)
  - capability mismatch (asked for tools but unsupported) → CAPABILITY_MISMATCH
- Feeds results into `AvailabilityCache` so `select_best_model`'s filter and `/v1/route/explain` reflect live state.

This is the right architecture and directly answers "which of 3628 models work." It also sidesteps the missing management token — probing uses only the API key.

Per your standing protocol (audit tools → read docs → ingest → then design/implement), I've done the audit + doc ingestion. Now I should design this concretely and store it, then implement #57 against it. But first, a few things I must confirm before writing code (don't guess):

**Critical unknowns to verify before implementation:**
1. Does `OmniRouteHTTPTransport` have a `runtime()` method yet, and what does it call? (catalog-only per grep — need to see lines 1-130 of omniroute.py)
2. How does `AvailabilityCache` currently get fed (the #56 singleton in api.py)? #57 wires the cache's `fetch` callable to the prober.
3. The catalog has 3628 models — probing all is expensive. Need a strategy: probe lazily/on-demand per routing request, or a background sweep with concurrency caps + per-provider rate limits. OmniRoute exposes `rate-limits` but we can't read it (no mgmt token) → must use conservative static caps.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434511.7812922)

Now I've read all of omniroute.py (602 lines) and understand the transport fully. Two critical facts:

1. **`OmniRouteHTTPTransport` only does GET requests** (`_get_json` uses `client.stream("GET", ...)`). It does NOT do POST/chat completions. So a "single-token canary" needs either: (a) a new POST method on the transport, or (b) a separate `ModelProbe` that uses its own httpx POST — but it must honor the same security model (`validate_upstream_url`, `host_is_allowed`, credential-safe). 

2. **`runtime()` reads `/api/monitoring/health`** (the `health` source) and merges `providerHealth`, `providerBreakers`, `lockouts`, `learnedLimits` into per-provider observations. **It currently calls `/api/monitoring/health` with NO token** (since `health` is not in `_MANAGEMENT_SOURCES`). And we proved earlier that `/api/monitoring/health` returns 200 publicly! So the runtime `health` source ALREADY works without a management token — my earlier concern was wrong. The code only requires `management_token` for `rate_limits`/`model_cooldowns`/`budget`/`token_limits`, not for `health`. So `OmniRouteHTTPTransport(base_url, api_key=...)` with `runtime_sources={"health"}` would already populate provider-level health.

So the gap is narrower than I thought:
- **Provider-level health**: already works via `runtime()` + `/api/monitoring/health` (no token needed).
- **Per-model live usability**: NOT covered — needs the canary/probe you described.
- **Per-model quota/lockout**: needs management token (rate_limits/cooldowns). The canary also captures this (429→quota_exhausted, lockout→locked_out).

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434511.781295)

Major discovery: **the probe concept ALREADY EXISTS in availability.py.** Lines 620–715 define `_probe_state()` which consumes a `RuntimeObservation` with `source == "llm-gate:probe"` and classifies probe results into `AvailabilityState`:
- `ready` → ELIGIBLE (with `usage_available=True`, http 200, auth authorized)
- `usage_unavailable` / `completion_unavailable` → DEGRADED (provider active but usage exhausted)
- `timeout` → TIMEOUT or DENIED
- `failed` (error_class) → QUOTA_EXHAUSTED / RATE_LIMITED / UNAVAILABLE / DENIED / MALFORMED
- `skipped` (cooldown/quarantined) → RATE_LIMITED / DENIED

This is **exactly** the classification your message described:
- "provider is wrong → disable" = UNAUTHORIZED/DENIED
- "usage just exhausted → keep but note" = QUOTA_EXHAUSTED (DEGRADED)
- "active + usage → usable" = ELIGIBLE (ready)

So the infrastructure for the canary is already scaffolded in `availability.py`. What's MISSING (the #57 work) is:
1. The **actual probe executor** — something that sends a single-token completion to each model and produces these `RuntimeObservation`s with `source="llm-gate:probe"`. The `normalize_observation`/`_probe_state` code exists but nothing calls it with real probe data yet.
2. Wiring that probe output into the `AvailabilityCache` (the #56 singleton in api.py) so `select_best_model`'s `availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` filter and `/v1/route/explain` reflect it.

Also: code-review-graph CLI doesn't accept `--format json` (it's `--format` only, no flag), so my commands errored. Let me use the correct syntax to get real results. Let me finish reading availability.py (901–1276) and run code-review-graph correctly. Batching.

931|def _policy_reason(
932|    candidate: AvailabilityCandidate, requirements: CandidateRequirements
933|) -> str | None:
934|    model = candidate.model
935|    model_capabilities = frozenset(canonical_capability(value) for value in model.capabilities)
936|    missing = sorted(requirements.required - model_capabilities)
937|    if missing:
938|        return f\"missing capability: {missing[0]}\"
939|    if requirements.allow_models and model.id not in requirements.allow_models:
940|        return \"model not in allowlist\"
941|    if model.id in requirements.deny_models:
942|        return \"model denied by policy\"
943|    if requirements.allow_providers and model.provider not in requirements.allow_providers:
944|        return \"provider not in allowlist\"
945|    if model.provider in requirements.deny_providers:
946|        return \"provider denied by policy\"
947|    return None
948|
949|
950|def select_capable_candidates(
951|    states: list[AvailabilityCandidate], requirements: CandidateRequirements
952|) -> list[AvailabilityCandidate]:
953|    \"\"\"Return only candidates that pass every hard availability and policy gate.\"\"\"
954|    result: list[AvailabilityCandidate] = []
955|    for item in states:
956|        if _candidate_is_eligible(item, requirements):
957|            result.append(item)
958|    return sorted(result, key=lambda x: (x.model.capability_tier, x.model.id))
959|
960|
961|def _candidate_is_eligible(
962|    item: AvailabilityCandidate, requirements: CandidateRequirements
963|) -> bool:

966|    return _availability_state_is_eligible(item, requirements)
967|

969|def _availability_state_is_eligible(
970|    item: AvailabilityCandidate, requirements: CandidateRequirements

974|    if (
975|        item.state is AvailabilityState.DEGRADED
976|        and requirements.allow_degraded
977|        and not requirements.protected

980|    return (

982|        and requirements.unknown_is_eligible
983|        and not requirements.protected
984|        and item.freshness_seconds is not None
985|        and item.reasons == (\"health unknown\",)
986|    )
987|

1029|) -> Any:

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434511.781298)

I've now read **all of availability.py (1276 lines), router.py (40), omniroute.py (602), and ran code-review-graph for real results.** 

The critical finding that reshapes everything: **the llm-gate codebase ALREADY has the complete availability/eligibility engine** — `normalize_observation()`, `_probe_state()` (the exact canary classification your message describes), `select_capable_candidates()`, `explain_candidates()`, `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()`. The probe classification state machine (lines 620–715) is precisely:
- `ready` → ELIGIBLE (active + usage)
- `usage_unavailable`/`completion_unavailable` → DEGRADED (keep but note; usage exhausted)
- `timeout`/`failed(unauthorized)`/`failed(quota_exhausted)` → TIMEOUT/UNAUTHORIZED/QUOTA_EXHAUSTED/DENIED (disable)

So the gap is NOT a design problem — it's an **implementation/wiring gap**. The engine exists but:
1. Nothing **executes the actual probe** (sends a single-token completion per model) and feeds `RuntimeObservation(source="llm-gate:probe")` into `normalize_observation`.
2. The `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()` calls `runtime()` (health) but has **no probe integration** — it never produces `source="llm-gate:probe"` observations.
3. `select_best_model` (router.py) only checks `availability_state in {"eligible","ready"}` — which stays `unknown` because no probe ever ran.

This means **#57 is mostly about adding the probe executor + wiring it into the adapter/cache**, NOT redesigning. The classification logic you described is already written and tested (there are surely tests for `_probe_state`).

Let me verify the code-review-graph callers result and check the existing tests for the probe path, plus confirm where `AvailabilityCache` is wired (api.py). This tells me exactly what #57 must add. Batching the final reads.

36|def _build_availability_cache() -> AvailabilityCache | None:
37|    \"\"\"Build the bounded availability cache backed by the native OmniRoute transport.
38|
39|    Returns ``None`` when no OmniRoute endpoint is configured, so the server
40|    still boots without availability explainability.  The transport is
41|    loopback-only and credential-safe; a misconfigured base URL fails closed
42|    to ``None`` rather than crashing startup.
43|    \"\"\"
44|    base_url = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_BASE_URL\") or os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL\")
45|    if not base_url or base_url.strip().lower() in {\"\", \"none\"}:
46|        return None
47|    api_key = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\")
48|    management_token = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_MANAGEMENT_TOKEN\")
49|    usage_api_key_id = os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_USAGE_API_KEY_ID\")
50|    allow_private = _allowed_private_hosts() | {
51|        item.strip().lower()
52|        for item in os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_ALLOW_PRIVATE_HOSTS\", \"\").split(\",\")
53|        if item.strip()
54|    }
55|    try:
56|        transport = OmniRouteHTTPTransport(
57|            base_url,
58|            api_key=api_key,
59|            management_token=management_token,
60|            usage_api_key_id=usage_api_key_id,
61|            allow_private_hosts=allow_private,
62|        )
63|    except Exception:
64|        return None
65|    adapter = OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter(transport)
66|    return AvailabilityCache(
67|        source=adapter.evaluate,
68|        ttl_seconds=DEFAULT_AVAILABILITY_TTL_SECONDS,
69|        stale_window_seconds=DEFAULT_AVAILABILITY_STALE_WINDOW_SECONDS,
70|    )
71|
72|
73|DEFAULT_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1\"

76|
77|
78|def _allowed_private_hosts() -> set[str]:
79|    configured = os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_ALLOW_PRIVATE_HOSTS\", \"\")
80|    return DEFAULT_ALLOWED_PRIVATE_HOSTS | {
81|        item.strip().lower() for item in configured.split(\",\") if item.strip()
82|    }

85|def _build_proxy() -> UpstreamProxy:
86|    \"\"\"Build the configured upstream transport without reading client fields.\"\"\"
87|    base_url = os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL\", DEFAULT_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL)
88|    api_key = os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_API_KEY\") or os.getenv(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\")
89|    timeout_ms = int(os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_TIMEOUT_MS\", \"30000\"))
90|    if timeout_ms <= 0:
91|        raise ValueError(\"LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_TIMEOUT_MS must be positive\")
92|    return UpstreamProxy(

96|        allow_private_hosts=_allowed_private_hosts(),
97|    )
98|

103|    timeout_ms = int(os.getenv(\"LLMGATE_INTELLIGENCE_TIMEOUT_MS\", str(DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS)))
104|    allow_client_model_override = os.getenv(

115|        if frontier_allowlist_raw
116|        else None

121|        providers=providers,
122|        profile=profile,

**The canary mechanism already exists and is fully built + tested.** `llm_gate/probes.py` implements exactly what you described — `ProbeRunner` sends a single-token `POST /v1/chat/completions` (`PROBE_PROMPT`, `max_tokens=1`) per model and classifies the result:
- 200 + usage → `ready` → ELIGIBLE (active + usable)
- 200 but zero usage / empty completion → `degraded` → keep but note (QUOTA_EXHAUSTED-style)
- 401/403 → `unauthorized` → DENIED (provider wrong → disable)
- 429 → `rate_limited` → RATE_LIMITED
- 503/timeout → UNAVAILABLE/TIMEOUT (disable)
- repeated failures → quarantine (with secret redaction)

This feeds `normalize_observation(source="llm-gate:probe")` which already contains the full `_probe_state` classification state machine (availability.py:620–715). So the engine is 100% there.

## Parent
Part of #30 and the flagship completion program in #28/#36.

## Depends on
#30.2, #29.4, and existing dispatcher/router code.

## Goal
Prove that all route/workflow paths filter candidates using live eligibility before any adaptive or cost ranking.

## Scope
- Wire normalized availability into Python router, dispatcher, API, and proxy decision paths.
- Preserve exclusions and availability snapshot in RoutingDecision.
- Add invariant tests that an ineligible candidate cannot be selected by a learned/adaptive ranker.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path.
- [ ] No ranker, Ruflo plan, or RuVector result can reintroduce excluded candidates.
- [ ] Explain endpoint shows the complete pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions.
- [ ] Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent.
- [ ] Integration tests cover all public route entry points.

## Swarm routing
Lower-tier integration agent; stronger adversarial review required for the invariant tests.", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

```json
{
 "command": "cd /home/nick/dev/llm-gate\nAK=$(grep -E '^export OMNI_API_KEY=' ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null | head -1 | sed 's/.*=//')\ncat > /tmp/probe_canary.py <<'PY'\nimport os, json, urllib.request\n# 1) grab a real model id from the live catalog\nreq = urllib.request.Request(\"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1/models\",\n    headers={\"Authorization\": f\"Bearer {os.environ['AK']}\"})\ncatalog = json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10).read())\nids = [m[\"id\"] for m in catalog.get(\"data\", []) if isinstance(m, dict) and m.get(\"id\")]\nprint(\"catalog size:\", len(ids))\n# pick a chat-capable model that likely works\nprobe_id = next((i for i in ids if i.startswith(\"anthropic/claude\")), ids[0])\nprint(\"probing model:\", probe_id)\n\n# 2) use the repo's existing probe transport + runner\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0, \"/home/nick/dev/llm-gate\")\nfrom llm_gate.probes import (ProbeRunner, ProbePolicy, openai_probe_transport,\n                             ProbeObservation)\ntransport = openai_probe_transport(\"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1\")\nrunner = ProbeRunner(ProbePolicy(max_models_per_run=1, cooldown_seconds=0, timeout_seconds=15))\nresult = runner.run([probe_id], transport)\nr = result[0]\nprint(\"status:\", r.status)\nprint(\"availability_state:\", r.availability_state)\nprint(\"error_class:\", r.error_class)\nprint(\"usage_available:\", r.usage_available)\nprint(\"http_status:\", r.http_status)\nprint(\"payload sent max_tokens:\", r.payload.get(\"max_tokens\") if hasattr(r,'payload') else 'n/a')\nobs = r.as_runtime_observation()\nprint(\"runtime obs source:\", obs.source)\nfrom llm_gate.availability import normalize_observation, AvailabilityState, ModelInfo\nst = normalize_observation(ModelInfo(id=probe_id, provider=probe_id.split('/')[0], capability_tier=2), obs, now=r.observed_at)\nprint(\"normalized state:\", st.state.value)\nPY\nAK=\"$AK\" .venv/bin/python /tmp/probe_canary.py 2>&1 | head -40",
 "timeout": 60
}
```

{"content": "1|# AI Agent Constraints & Architectural Context (.claude.md)
2|
3|**Target Ecosystem:** Express.js Middleware / Next.js Routing
4|**Language:** Python 3.10+
5|**Primary Directive:** LLM API Cost Optimization and Fallback Orchestration.
6|
7|## Directory Boundaries
8|- `/llm_gate/gate.py`: Core routing engine. Modifying heuristic logic here requires validating the execution tree in `tests/test_gate_unit.py`.
9|- `/llm_gate/discovery.py`: The integration connector. Only add OpenRouter/Ollama parsers here. 
10|- `/llm_gate/api.py`: FastAPI server layer. Must never be instantiated in global execution scopes.
11|
12|## RAG Memory & Constraints
13|If an agent edits the parsing logic in `models.py`, ensure data structures remain mapped as `dataclass(frozen=True)` to prevent mutation across asynchronous generation loops.
14|
15|## Deployment Gating
16|Do not update `pyproject.toml` dependencies natively without also checking `[server]` and `[ci]` optional blocks. `llm-gate` is headless by design.
17|", "total_lines": 16, "file_size": 966, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434746.28127)
```
{"content": "1|# Ruflo — Claude Code Configuration
2|
3|## Rules
4|
5|- Do what has been asked; nothing more, nothing less
6|- NEVER create files unless absolutely necessary — prefer editing existing files
7|- NEVER create documentation files unless explicitly requested
8|- NEVER save working files or tests to root — use `/src`, `/tests`, `/docs`, `/config`, `/scripts`
9|- ALWAYS read a file before editing it
10|- NEVER commit secrets, credentials, or .env files
11|- NEVER add a `Co-Authored-By` trailer to user commits unless this project's `.claude/settings.json` has `attribution.commit` set (#2078). The Claude Code Bash tool may suggest one in its default commit-message template — ignore it. `Co-Authored-By` is semantic authorship attribution under git/GitHub convention; the tool is the facilitator, not a co-author.
12|- Keep files under 500 lines
13|- Validate input at system boundaries
14|
15|## Agent Comms (SendMessage-First Coordination)
16|
17|Named agents coordinate via `SendMessage`, not polling or shared state.
18|
19|```
20|Lead (you) ←→ architect ←→ developer ←→ tester ←→ reviewer
21|              (named agents message each other directly)
22|```
23|
24|### Spawning a Coordinated Team
25|
26|```javascript
27|// ALL agents in ONE message, each knows WHO to message next
28|Agent({ prompt: \"Research the codebase. SendMessage findings to 'architect'.\",
29|  subagent_type: \"researcher\", name: \"researcher\", run_in_background: true })
30|Agent({ prompt: \"Wait for 'researcher'. Design solution. SendMessage to 'coder'.\",
31|  subagent_type: \"system-architect\", name: \"architect\", run_in_background: true })
32|Agent({ prompt: \"Wait for 'architect'. Implement it. SendMessage to 'tester'.\",
33|  subagent_type: \"coder\", name: \"coder\", run_in_background: true })
34|Agent({ prompt: \"Wait for 'coder'. Write tests. SendMessage results to 'reviewer'.\",
35|  subagent_type: \"tester\", name: \"tester\", run_in_background: true })
36|Agent({ prompt: \"Wait for 'tester'. Review code quality and security.\",
37|  subagent_type: \"reviewer\", name: \"reviewer\", run_in_background: true })
38|
39|// Kick off the pipeline
40|SendMessage({ to: \"researcher\", summary: \"Start\", message: \"[task context]\" })
41|```
42|
43|### Patterns
44|
45|| Pattern | Flow | Use When |
46||---------|------|----------|
47|| **Pipeline** | A → B → C → D | Sequential dependencies (feature dev) |
48|| **Fan-out** | Lead → A, B, C → Lead | Independent parallel work (research) |
49|| **Supervisor** | Lead ↔ workers | Ongoing coordination (complex refactor) |
50|
51|### Rules
52|
53|- ALWAYS name agents — `name: \"role\"` makes them addressable
54|- ALWAYS include comms instructions in prompts — who to message, what to send
55|- Spawn ALL agents in ONE message with `run_in_background: true`
56|- After spawning: STOP, tell user what's running, wait for results
57|- NEVER poll status — agents message back or complete automatically
58|
59|## Swarm & Routing
60|
61|### Config
62|- **Topology**: mesh (anti-drift)
63|- **Max Agents**: 5
64|- **Memory**: memory
65|- **HNSW**: Disabled
66|- **Neural**: Disabled
67|
68|```bash
69|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest swarm init --topology hierarchical --max-agents 8 --strategy specialized
70|```
71|
72|### Agent Routing
73|
74|| Task | Agents | Topology |
75||------|--------|----------|
76|| Bug Fix | researcher, coder, tester | hierarchical |
77|| Feature | architect, coder, tester, reviewer | hierarchical |
78|| Refactor | architect, coder, reviewer | hierarchical |
79|| Performance | perf-engineer, coder | hierarchical |
80|| Security | security-architect, auditor | hierarchical |
81|
82|### When to Swarm
83|- **YES**: 3+ files, new features, cross-module refactoring, API changes, security, performance
84|- **NO**: single file edits, 1-2 line fixes, docs updates, config changes, questions
85|
86|### 3-Tier Model Routing
87|
88|| Tier | Handler | Use Cases |
89||------|---------|-----------|
90|| 1 | Agent Booster (WASM) | Simple transforms — skip LLM, use Edit directly |
91|| 2 | Haiku | Simple tasks, low complexity |
92|| 3 | Sonnet/Opus | Architecture, security, complex reasoning |
93|
94|## Build & Test
95|
96|- ALWAYS run tests after code changes
97|- ALWAYS verify build succeeds before committing
98|
99|```bash
100|npm run build && npm test
101|```
102|
103|## CLI Quick Reference
104|
105|```bash
106|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest init --wizard           # Setup
107|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest swarm init --v3-mode     # Start swarm
108|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest memory search --query \"\" # Vector search
109|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest hooks route --task \"\"    # Route to agent
110|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest doctor --fix             # Diagnostics
111|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest security scan            # Security scan
112|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest performance benchmark    # Benchmarks
113|```
114|
115|26 commands, 140+ subcommands. Use `--help` on any command for details.
116|
117|## Setup
118|
119|```bash
120|claude mcp add claude-flow -- npx -y ruflo@latest mcp start
121|npx ruflo@latest doctor --fix
122|```
123|
124|> The background `daemon` is optional. It runs interval workers that each spawn
125|> a headless `claude` session, so it consumes tokens continuously. Start it only
126|> if you want those sweeps: `npx ruflo@latest daemon start` (self-stops after 12h
127|> by default; `--ttl 0` to disable, `daemon status --all` to audit running daemons).
128|
129|**Agent tool** handles execution (agents, files, code, git). **MCP tools** handle coordination (swarm, memory, hooks). **CLI** is the same via Bash.
130|", "total_lines": 129, "file_size": 5219, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434746.293086)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate Routing Policy
2|## Best-appropriate-model selection contract
3|
4|### 1. Objective
5|
6|Choose the highest expected-quality model that satisfies every hard requirement and is currently eligible. The selector is not a cheapest-model sorter. Cost may break ties or be used only when the user explicitly selects a cost-ceiling mode.
7|
8|Every request passes through the mandatory `IntelligenceService`. Its deterministic component evaluates policy, capabilities, availability, and explainability. In the production profile, its managed Ruflo/RuVector component supplies a bounded adaptive signal. Adaptive intelligence can rank eligible candidates, but it can never override hard policy.
9|
10|The decision must be deterministic for identical inputs, catalog state, policy version, and learned-policy snapshot.
11|
12|### 2. Candidate states
13|
14|Every discovered model is normalized into an explicit availability state:
15|
16|- `ready`: catalog metadata is valid and the last health/headroom signal permits use.
17|- `degraded`: elevated latency or low remaining quota; rejected by default and usable
18|  only for non-protected work with an explicit `allow_degraded` opt-in.
19|- `unknown`: present in the catalog but not verified. Unknown is not treated as ready by default.
20|- `denied`: removed by policy, unsupported capability, privacy restriction, or failed hard gate.
21|- `unavailable`, `quota_exhausted`, `rate_limited`, `unauthorized`, `locked_out`,
22|  `circuit_open`, `timeout`, and `malformed`: stable hard-exclusion states with
23|  redacted explanations.
24|
25|OmniRoute's `/v1/models` response is a synchronized catalog. It can contain models that are not currently usable. llm-gate MUST apply its own allowlist, denylist, metadata overrides, and optional bounded health probes. An OmniRoute API key's `allowed_models` restriction does not necessarily filter the catalog response, so local filtering is mandatory.
26|
27|### 3. Requirement vector
28|
29|The request classifier produces:
30|
31|```text
32|TaskRequirements {
33|  task_class: security | auth | payments | live_execution | production_deploy |
34|              architecture | debugging | implementation | tests | docs | formatting | unknown
35|  minimum_quality_confidence: 0..1
36|  reasoning_level: none | low | medium | high | frontier
37|  coding_level: none | low | medium | high
38|  context_tokens_required: integer | unknown
39|  tools_required: boolean
40|  structured_output_required: boolean
41|  vision_required: boolean
42|  streaming_required: boolean
43|  latency_budget_ms: integer | unknown
44|  estimated_tokens: non-negative integer
45|  estimated_cost_usd: finite non-negative number
46|  budget_remaining_usd: finite non-negative number | unknown
47|  privacy_class: local_only | trusted_upstream | any
48|  protected: boolean
49|}
50|```
51|
52|The classifier MUST combine explicit user policy, request shape, known tool fields, model/client hints, deterministic lexical signals, and the bounded IntelligenceService signal. A learned suggestion can increase confidence or rank candidates but cannot lower `protected`, required capability, or a configured quality floor.
53|
54|### 4. Hard gates
55|
56|Hard gates run before scoring:
57|
58|1. Protected task classes require a frontier allowlist or an explicitly validated model policy. A generic name match is not sufficient to lower the floor.
59|2. A model without required tool calling, structured output, vision, context, or streaming capability is rejected.
60|3. A denied provider/model, privacy-incompatible provider, stale catalog row, or failed health policy is rejected.
61|4. A request with unknown risk and a protected keyword is escalated, never downgraded.
62|5. An upstream response already started streaming cannot be retried or silently switched.
63|6. User-specified `model` is treated as a preference only when `allow_client_model_override=false`; direct override requires an explicit policy flag.
64|7. Estimated request tokens must fit the candidate's normalized `token_headroom`
65|   (or `tokens_remaining` alias). Estimated cost must fit the smallest known
66|   request-policy or runtime budget.
67|8. Missing token or cost headroom degrades the candidate and requires an explicit
68|   non-protected opt-in. Protected work never admits degraded capacity evidence.
69|9. A request that exceeds known token or budget headroom is denied before ranking.
70|
71|#### 4.1 Conservative capacity reservations
72|
73|The deterministic planner emits an explicit `deterministic_effort_reservation_v1`
74|admission reservation:
75|
76|| Effort | Reserved tokens | Reserved cost (USD) | Reserved latency (ms) |
77|| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
78|| low | 1,024 | 0.05 | 2,000 |
79|| medium | 8,192 | 1.00 | 15,000 |
80|| high | 32,768 | 5.00 | 60,000 |
81|
82|These values are conservative routing reservations, not tokenizer output,
83|observed usage, pricing estimates, or billing records. They provide deterministic
84|floors until a versioned model-aware estimator is introduced. A structured or
85|learned planner may raise a reservation but cannot lower these floors. Invalid,
86|negative, non-finite, fractional token, or boolean estimates are rejected at the
87|planning or availability boundary.
88|
89|Capacity reasons are stable and machine-readable: `token headroom unknown`,
90|`token headroom exceeded`, `budget headroom unknown`, and
91|`budget headroom exceeded`.
92|
93|When both token-headroom aliases are present, they must agree or the observation
94|is malformed. When planned and runtime cost estimates are both present, the
95|larger estimate is checked against the smallest known budget.
96|
97|### 5. Scoring
98|
99|For each eligible candidate, calculate a score with normalized components:
100|
101|```text
102|score =
103|  0.35 * quality_confidence
104|+ 0.20 * capability_fit
105|+ 0.15 * observed_reliability
106|+ 0.10 * context_fit
107|+ 0.08 * availability_headroom
108|+
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434746.3017385)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate Enforcement and Learning Integration
2|## Mandatory intelligence contract with a deterministic safety floor
3|
4|### 1. Design rule
5|
6|The public `llm-gate` package MUST expose a stable `IntelligenceService` contract independent of Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Jcode, Hermes, Cowork, or any agent framework. The production profile MUST use Ruflo/RuVector as its managed adaptive intelligence backend. A deterministic local backend is always present as the safety floor and cold-start path, but running without the managed backend is an explicitly named development-only degraded mode, not a production configuration.
7|
8|The integration MUST remain protocol-based. It MUST NOT couple the public package to private Ruflo, RuVector, OmniRoute, or agent-tool database tables.
9|
10|### 2. Core enforcement layer
11|
12|The core owns:
13|
14|- typed configuration and policy versions;
15|- request parsing and protocol validation;
16|- protected-task classification;
17|- model capability and availability gates;
18|- prompt redaction and privacy policy;
19|- retry/fallback legality;
20|- decision explanations;
21|- outcome event schema;
22|- local JSONL or SQLite persistence;
23|- deterministic regression tests.
24|
25|A policy decision is allowed to fail closed for protected operations and fail safe for non-protected routing. The failure mode must be visible in the decision event.
26|
27|### 3. Ruflo adapter
28|
29|Ruflo integration is a subprocess or plugin adapter with strict timeouts and structured output. It may call only documented surfaces:
30|
31|- `ruflo guidance gates --command ... --content ... --json` for command/content enforcement;
32|- `ruflo hooks model-route -t ... --prefer-quality --context ...` for advisory model-family routing;
33|- `ruflo hooks model-outcome -t ... -m ... -o ... -q ...` for coarse model-family feedback;
34|- `ruflo neural router decide ...` and `train-from-trajectories` for offline/inspection workflows;
35|- `ruflo hooks pretrain` and `guidance compile` only during an explicit setup or build step, never implicitly during a request.
36|
37|The Ruflo `model-route`/`model-outcome` CLI currently accepts Claude-family labels such as `haiku`, `sonnet`, and `opus`, not arbitrary OmniRoute IDs. The adapter MUST map arbitrary model IDs to a stable family label and preserve the original ID in llm-gate telemetry. If a safe mapping is impossible, skip the Ruflo call and continue with the local policy.
38|
39|### 4. RuVector intelligence adapter
40|
41|RuVector SONA is the required managed adaptive signal for the production profile. It is an asynchronous learning sink and advisory scorer:
42|
43|- `ruvector hooks trajectory-begin --context ... --agent ... --file ...`;
44|- `ruvector hooks trajectory-step --action ... --result ... --reward ...`;
45|- `ruvector hooks trajectory-end --success --quality ...`;
46|- `ruvector sona train <json>` only in an offline/batch worker;
47|- `ruvector sona patterns <query> --json` for bounded advisory retrieval.
48|
49|The adapter MUST NOT read `ruvector.db`, Ruflo databases, OmniRoute SQLite storage, or any undocumented table directly. It exchanges versioned JSON events. If the adapter is unavailable, production readiness MUST fail closed at the service boundary, while the deterministic backend remains available for explicit degraded development mode.
50|
51|### 5. Event schema
52|
53|```json
54|{
55|  \"event_version\": \"1\",
56|  \"request_id\": \"uuid\",
57|  \"task_fingerprint\": \"sha256:...\",
58|  \"task_class\": \"implementation\",
59|  \"model_id\": \"aug/gpt-5.4-medium\",
60|  \"model_family\": \"gpt\",
61|  \"provider\": \"aug\",
62|  \"policy_version\": \"policy-2026-07-13.1\",
63|  \"decision\": \"selected|fallback|escalated|denied\",
64|  \"transport_outcome\": \"success|timeout|rate_limited|upstream_error|parse_error\",
65|  \"quality_outcome\": \"unknown|success|failure|user_rejected|tests_failed\",
66|  \"quality_score\": null,
67|  \"latency_ms\": 0,
68|  \"input_tokens\": null,
69|  \"output_tokens\": null,
70|  \"safety_flags\": [],
71|  \"timestamp\": \"2026-07-13T00:00:00Z\"
72|}
73|```
74|
75|For training and offline inspection, the core also emits a versioned privacy-safe `task_workflow_outcome_episode` record. The episode stores only redacted summaries and stable fingerprints for task intent, workflow shape, and execution outcome. It MUST NOT persist raw prompts, credential material, or full context blobs by default. Instead it records fields such as task fingerprint and redacted preview, task class/privacy/risk, context key names, workflow step counts and verification checks, selected route metadata, retry/fallback counts, and redacted outcome details.
76|
77|### 6. Learning safety
78|
79|- No raw prompt or completion is sent to learning sinks by default.
80|- Redaction occurs before any external process invocation.
81|- Learning calls are asynchronous and bounded by a small latency budget.
82|- A failed learning process never corrupts or crashes an in-flight user request. It does flip managed-intelligence readiness to not-ready and records the failure. The active profile then either rejects protected work or explicitly continues in visible degraded development mode.
83|- A learned model cannot override a hard gate, denied model, quality floor, or privacy policy.
84|- New policies start with a cold-start deterministic mode.
85|- Training data is append-only, versioned, and exportable for audit.
86|- User opt-out disables new prompt-derived collection and training for the opted-out scope. Deterministic policy intelligence continues to run, and any adaptive snapshot used must already be approved for that privacy scope. A global opt-out that makes the required managed backend unavailable leaves the production profile not-ready rather than silently pretending that intelligence is still active.
87|
88|### 7. Ruflo guidance integration
89|
90|Project bylaws should be compiled into a policy bundle d
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434755.6548808)

These four docs are the authoritative contract. Critical facts I now have from actually reading them:

- **ROUTING_POLICY.md**: the selector picks "highest expected-quality model that satisfies every hard requirement AND is currently eligible" — not cheapest. Candidate states: `ready/degraded/unknown/denied/...`. "An OmniRoute API key's `allowed_models` restriction does not necessarily filter the catalog — local filtering is mandatory." The probe mechanism fits here as the "optional bounded health probes" mentioned in §2.
- **ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md**: llm-gate MUST NOT couple to Ruflo/RuVector/OmniRoute internals — protocol-based only. The Ruflo adapter uses documented CLI surfaces (`ruflo hooks model-route`, `ruflo neural router decide`). RuVector SONA is the "required managed adaptive signal for production profile" via documented `ruvector hooks ...` + `ruvector sona patterns`. **This is the documented pattern for dynamic model selection / neural routing** — exactly what you referenced. It also says the deterministic local backend is the always-present safety floor, and a missing managed backend → "explicitly named development-only degraded mode," NOT production.
- **CLAUDE.md (ruflo)**: the 3-tier model routing is explicit: Tier 1 = Agent Booster WASM (skip LLM), Tier 2 = Haiku (simple), Tier 3 = Sonnet/Opus (architecture/security). And the swarm pattern: spawn named agents, `SendMessage`-first coordination, all in one message.
- **AGENTS.md (llm-gate)**: directory boundaries — `gate.py` core engine, `discovery.py` connector, `api.py` FastAPI (never instantiate in global scope). `models.py` must stay `frozen=True` dataclasses.

This directly answers your "use what's documented" point: the dynamic-model-selection + neural/SORA pattern is **already specified in ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md §3–§6** via ruflo/ruvector CLI. I had NOT read that. Now I understand the intended wiring instead of inventing it.
