### 🔄 Tool result (1784434755.7452722)
```
{"content": "1|# Intelligence Adapter Protocol v1
2|## Normative design for llm-gate issue #7
3|
4|**Status:** Implementation-blocking specification
5|**Version:** `intelligence-adapter/v1`
6|**Scope:** Ruflo/RuVector managed intelligence integration behind the public `IntelligenceService`
7|
8|## 1. Design invariants
9|
10|1. `IntelligenceService` is the only component allowed to return a model-selection decision.
11|2. Deterministic policy, capability, availability, privacy, and explanation logic runs before and after any adaptive call.
12|3. The managed adapter is advisory for ranking and mandatory for production readiness. It cannot authorize a denied candidate, lower a protected floor, override privacy, or turn an unknown candidate into ready.
13|4. The local deterministic backend is always available as the safety floor and cold-start path.
14|5. Production mode fails readiness closed when the managed adapter is unavailable. Development degraded mode is an explicit operator setting and is visible in readiness and every decision event.
15|6. The public package communicates with Ruflo and RuVector through documented CLI or plugin surfaces only. It never reads their private databases or tables.
16|7. Raw prompts, completions, credentials, environment values, and provider response bodies are never sent to the adapter by default.
17|
18|## 2. Configuration contract
19|
20|The implementation MUST validate configuration at startup:
21|
22|```text
23|LLMGATE_INTELLIGENCE_MODE=production|development_degraded
24|LLMGATE_INTELLIGENCE_TIMEOUT_MS=250          # positive bounded integer
25|LLMGATE_INTELLIGENCE_ADAPTER=managed|local
26|LLMGATE_RUFLO_COMMAND=ruflo                 # executable name or absolute path
27|LLMGATE_RUVECTOR_COMMAND=ruvector            # executable name or absolute path
28|LLMGATE_POLICY_VERSION=policy-YYYY-MM-DD.N
29|LLMGATE_PRIVACY_MODE=redacted|local_only
30|LLMGATE_LEARNING_ENABLED=true|false
31|```
32|
33|- The default mode is `production` for a release build and `development_degraded` only when explicitly selected by the operator.
34|- A production configuration with `LLMGATE_INTELLIGENCE_ADAPTER=local` is invalid.
35|- Unknown configuration keys, non-positive timeouts, unsafe executable paths, and malformed policy versions are startup errors.
36|- The adapter command is passed as an argument vector, never through a shell string.
37|
38|## 3. Versioned request envelope
39|
40|Every adapter invocation receives a redacted JSON request with this shape:
41|
42|```json
43|{
44|  \"protocol\": \"intelligence-adapter/v1\",
45|  \"operation\": \"route|readiness|record_outcome\",
46|  \"request_id\": \"uuid\",
47|  \"policy_version\": \"policy-2026-07-13.1\",
48|  \"task\": {
49|    \"class\": \"implementation\",
50|    \"fingerprint\": \"sha256:...\",
51|    \"message_count\": 2,
52|    \"estimated_input_tokens\": 800,
53|    \"requirements\": {
54|      \"reasoning\": \"high\",
55|      \"coding\": \"high\",
56|      \"tools\": true,
57|      \"structured_output\": false,
58|      \"vision\": false,
59|      \"streaming\": true,
60|      \"privacy\": \"trusted_upstream\",
61|      \"protected\": false
62|    }
63|  },
64|  \"candidates\": [
65|    {
66|      \"id\": \"agy/gpt-5.4-medium\",
67|      \"family\": \"gpt\",
68|      \"provider\": \"agy\",
69|      \"availability\": \"ready\",
70|      \"capabilities\": [\"coding\", \"tools\", \"streaming\"],
71|      \"quality_floor\": 0.7
72|    }
73|  ],
74|  \"policy\": {
75|    \"protected_floor\": \"frontier\",
76|    \"allow_client_model_override\": false,
77|    \"learned_policy_max_adjustment\": 0.05
78|  },
79|  \"privacy\": {
80|    \"raw_prompt_sent\": false,
81|    \"raw_completion_sent\": false,
82|    \"learning_opt_out\": false
83|  }
84|}
85|```
86|
87|The adapter MUST reject unknown protocol versions with a structured error. The envelope is safe to persist as test evidence after removing request IDs if desired.
88|
89|## 4. Route response envelope
90|
91|A successful `route` operation returns:
92|
93|```json
94|{
95|  \"protocol\": \"intelligence-adapter/v1\",
96|  \"operation\": \"route\",
97|  \"request_id\": \"uuid\",
98|  \"status\": \"ok\",
99|  \"managed_backend\": \"ruflo+ruvector\",
100|  \"adaptive_snapshot\": \"snapshot-id-or-null\",
101|  \"ranking\": [
102|    {\"model_id\": \"agy/gpt-5.4-medium\", \"adjustment\": 0.02, \"confidence\": 0.61}
103|  ],
104|  \"observations\": [\"warmup\", \"no_quality_outcome_for_bucket\"],
105|  \"expires_at\": \"2026-07-13T22:20:00Z\"
106|}
107|```
108|
109|The service MUST treat `ranking` as an advisory ordering signal only. It MUST intersect the result with deterministic eligible candidates, clamp adjustments, and recompute the final decision explanation locally.
110|
111|A managed adapter cannot return a final `denied=false` or `protected=false` authority. Those fields do not exist in the protocol by design.
112|
113|## 5. Readiness response envelope
114|
115|A `readiness` operation returns:
116|
117|```json
118|{
119|  \"protocol\": \"intelligence-adapter/v1\",
120|  \"operation\": \"readiness\",
121|  \"status\": \"ready|not_ready|degraded\",
122|  \"managed_backend\": \"ruflo+ruvector\",
123|  \"checks\": {
124|    \"ruflo\": \"ready|not_ready|unknown\",
125|    \"ruvector\": \"ready|not_ready|unknown\",
126|    \"policy_bundle\": \"ready|not_ready|unknown\"
127|  },
128|  \"checked_at\": \"2026-07-13T22:20:00Z\",
129|  \"safe_reason\": \"managed adapter unavailable\"
130|}
131|```
132|
133|Production `/ready` requires `status=ready` and every required check `ready`. Development degraded mode may report `degraded`, never `ready`, while the local safety floor remains available.
134|
135|## 6. Outcome response envelope
136|
137|`record_outcome` is asynchronous and bounded. The immediate transport outcome and later validated quality outcome are distinct:
138|
139|```json
140|{
141|  \"protocol\": \"intelligence-adapter/v1\",
142|  \"operation\": \"record_outcome\"
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434755.7551956)
```
{"content": "1|# Portfolio Continuation Runbook
2|
3|This is the durable operating record for finishing the portfolio with
4|`llm-gate` as the flagship. It is intentionally secret-free. Git history,
5|GitHub issues, reviews, and exact-SHA Actions runs are the sources of truth.
6|Sanitized Hindsight memories are recall pointers that must be revalidated;
7|worker prose is never authority.
8|
9|The [ecosystem product vision](ECOSYSTEM_PRODUCT_VISION.md) defines the product
10|family and integration boundaries. The
11|[memory source index](MEMORY_SOURCE_INDEX.md) defines what documentation may be
12|retained, with which trust labels and refresh rules.
13|
14|## Objective and quality bar
15|
16|The objective is a release-ready portfolio led by an LLM routing project whose
17|engineering, documentation, install experience, demo, benchmarks, security
18|posture, and evidence are credible beside repositories with 20,000–40,000
19|stars. That is a quality target, not a popularity claim.
20|
21|For every public claim:
22|
23|- a clean install must reproduce it;
24|- a deterministic test, demo, benchmark, or captured workflow must support it;
25|- failures and alpha boundaries must be explicit;
26|- no adaptive component may bypass policy, privacy, budget, availability, or
27|  production-impact gates;
28|- no credential, raw provider error, private URL, or user/project prompt may
29|  enter probes, logs, fixtures, commits, issues, or retained memory.
30|
31|## Recommended operating topology
32|
33|Use the VPS as the durable control node:
34|
35|```text
36|phone / laptop / tablet
37|          │ SSH or private VPN
38|          ▼
39|host-native Hermes gateway/orchestrator
40|          ├── isolated Git worktrees
41|          ├── Codex workers through OmniRoute
42|          ├── Hindsight cloud recall/retain hooks
43|          └── GitHub Issues, Projects, PRs, and Actions
44|                         │
45|                         ▼
46|              llm-gate policy/intelligence
47|                         │
48|                         ▼
49|              OmniRoute model/provider plane
50|```
51|
52|Keep the primary Hermes orchestrator host-native for this workload. Host mode
53|has the least overhead and lets Git, SSH, `gh`, npm, Python, Codex, and their
54|existing user-level credentials work without duplicated configuration or broad
55|container mounts. Run its unattended gateway under a user-level supervisor,
56|keep public HTTP surfaces loopback-only, and reach them through SSH or a private
57|VPN.
58|
59|Docker remains useful as a separate Hermes profile for untrusted or highly
60|reproducible jobs. It is not the default parallel-writing environment: Hermes
61|uses one persistent terminal container shared across sessions and delegated
62|subagents, so concurrent writes to the same paths can collide unless each task
63|gets an isolated environment. If Hermes itself is containerized later, use one
64|supervised container with separate profiles and one persistent data mount;
65|never attach two live containers to the same Hermes data directory.
66|
67|Run OmniRoute as an independently supervised service with persistent storage
68|and a loopback endpoint. Keep model identifiers, provider health, quota, price,
69|and availability as runtime data. `llm-gate` consumes normalized evidence and
70|owns deterministic eligibility and explanation; it does not hard-code a
71|permanent model catalog.
72|
73|## Component responsibilities
74|
75|| Component | Responsibility | Must not do |
76||---|---|---|
77|| Hermes | Durable orchestration, session continuity, scheduling, bounded delegation | Decide routing eligibility or share one write target across workers |
78|| OmniRoute | Unified model endpoint, provider credentials, catalog, runtime transport | Become permanent product policy |
79|| `llm-gate` | Versioned contracts, hard gates, availability normalization, planning, explainability | Treat catalog presence as readiness or let ranking bypass a gate |
80|| Hindsight cloud | Secret-free recall and retain across Codex sessions | Store tokens, raw config, private URLs, prompts, or health authority |
81|| GitHub | Issues/Project control plane, code review, exact-SHA CI and release evidence | Substitute a stale PR check for current-main verification |
82|
83|## Repository order
84|
85|| Order | Repository | Current focus |
86||---:|---|---|
87|| 1 | `llm-gate` | P0 routing, capacity/planner, intelligence, retry, orchestration, then flagship polish |
88|| 2 | `llm-gate-node` | Honest Python-parity boundary, package publishing, open-PR reconciliation |
89|| 3 | `backtest-harness` | Repair baseline, refresh applicable PR, reproducibility and release evidence |
90|| 4 | `trade-risk-engine` | Repair baseline, refresh applicable PRs, risk-contract and release evidence |
91|| 5 | `edge-mining-framework` | Claims, tests, packaging, security, demo, CI |
92|| 6 | `trading-cockpit-ui` | Build/test quality, UX proof, deployment and CI |
93|| 7 | Nick-owned Hermes adapter/plugin surface | Create only after scope, ownership, provenance, and integration contract are explicit |
94|| 8 | `mrnicholasbcarter-code` | Portfolio narrative and links only after repository evidence is current |
95|
96|Do not polish downstream profile claims before their repositories are green.
97|The local `hermes-plugins` checkout is the third-party
98|`42-evey/hermes-plugins` repository. Treat it as an evaluation source, not a
99|Nick-owned push target.
100|
101|## Phases and estimates
102|
103|These are elapsed-time estimates, not promises. They assume one primary agent,
104|at most two non-overlapping implementation workers, responsive providers, and
105|ordinary CI latency.
106|
107|| Phase | Exit condition | Estimate |
108||---|---|---:|
109|| P0 flagship contracts | Issues covering capacity, planner, intelligence, retry, and bounded dispatch are integrated and green | 3–6 focused days |
110|| Flagship product proof | Name decision, installer/wizard, qu
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434755.7647216)
```
{"content": "1|# Ecosystem Product Overview and Vision
2|
3|Status: working product strategy, last reviewed 2026-07-18.
4|
5|This document defines the product family that `llm-gate` should lead, the
6|boundaries between the surrounding tools, and the evidence required before the
7|portfolio makes public claims. `llm-gate` is a working name until a separate
8|name, package, trademark, and migration study is complete.
9|
10|## Executive brief
11|
12|The flagship product is a policy-safe, availability-aware execution control
13|plane for heterogeneous LLM and agent stacks. It turns a request into a
14|versioned task specification, excludes candidates that cannot legally or
15|reliably run it, selects a model or bounded workflow, verifies the result, and
16|records privacy-safe evidence for later improvement.
17|
18|The product is not another provider proxy and it is not an unconstrained
19|autonomous-agent framework. OmniRoute already provides the unified model
20|transport. Hermes already provides durable user interaction and orchestration.
21|Ruflo can provide an optional workflow meta-harness, and RuVector can provide
22|an optional retrieval and graph substrate. The flagship earns its place by
23|being the deterministic authority between intent and execution:
24|
25|```text
26|request
27|  → structured plan
28|  → policy, capability, availability, privacy, and budget gates
29|  → model or bounded workflow assignment
30|  → execution and verification
31|  → explainable outcome evidence
32|  → advisory learning that can never bypass a hard gate
33|```
34|
35|The public ambition is to reach the engineering and product quality associated
36|with widely adopted 20,000–40,000-star open-source repositories. That is a
37|quality and adoption goal, never a claim that popularity can be engineered or
38|that unfinished software is production-ready.
39|
40|## One portfolio, two coherent product lines
41|
42|### Agent and LLM control plane
43|
44|`llm-gate` and `llm-gate-node` form the primary product line. They make model
45|and workflow execution predictable across providers, clients, and agent
46|frameworks.
47|
48|The north-star experience is:
49|
50|1. Install one package or use one container.
51|2. Run a wizard or provide a declarative configuration.
52|3. Connect an existing OpenAI-compatible client.
53|4. See which candidates were considered, which were excluded, why one was
54|   selected, what it cost, and whether the result passed verification.
55|5. Add Hermes, OmniRoute, Ruflo, RuVector, or Hindsight through documented,
56|   optional adapters rather than private database coupling.
57|
58|### Deterministic decision systems
59|
60|The trading repositories are a separate vertical united by the same design
61|philosophy: explicit contracts, fail-closed gates, deterministic calculations,
62|and evidence-backed claims.
63|
64|They are not runtime dependencies of `llm-gate`. They demonstrate how the same
65|engineering discipline applies to financial decisions:
66|
67|```text
68|market evidence
69|  → feature and expected-value gates
70|  → backtest and falsification evidence
71|  → capital-protection authority
72|  → human-observable trading cockpit
73|```
74|
75|Keeping the lines separate prevents a broad portfolio story from becoming a
76|coupled monolith.
77|
78|## System architecture and authority
79|
80|```text
81|phone / laptop / tablet
82|          │ SSH or private VPN
83|          ▼
84|Hermes: durable interaction, sessions, schedules, bounded delegation
85|          │
86|          ▼
87|llm-gate: contracts, hard gates, planning, selection, explanation ─────┐
88|    ▲                ▲                                                 │
89|    │ bounded plans  │ advisory evidence                               │
90|    ▼                │                                                 ▼
91|Ruflo: optional    RuVector: optional retrieval/graph        OmniRoute: unified
92|meta-harness       substrate                                provider/model plane
93|                                                                    │
94|                                                                    ▼
95|                                                             eligible providers
96|
97|Hindsight: secret-free operator/session continuation memory
98|GitHub: issues, review, exact-SHA CI, release, and provenance control plane
99|```
100|
101|| Component | Product responsibility | Authority boundary |
102||---|---|---|
103|| Hermes | Channels, sessions, scheduling, durable orchestration, delegated tool use | Does not decide whether a model or workflow is eligible |
104|| `llm-gate` | Versioned contracts, hard gates, planning, deterministic routing, explanation, verification policy | Sole authority for route eligibility; learned signals are advisory |
105|| OmniRoute | Credentials, provider transport, OpenAI-compatible model plane, catalog, and any explicitly documented runtime evidence | Does not become permanent product policy or imply that catalog presence proves readiness |
106|| Ruflo | Optional workflow compilation, bounded swarm coordination, replanning and verification hooks | Cannot expand task scope or bypass a gate |
107|| RuVector | Optional semantic, graph, trajectory, and outcome retrieval | Cannot directly change a production route |
108|| Hindsight | Cross-session operator recall and sanitized resume checkpoints | Is not live health, quota, price, or policy authority |
109|| GitHub | Planned work, human review, source provenance, exact-SHA automation and releases | Status is evidence-based; a label is not proof |
110|
111|There must be one routing authority. Integrations exchange versioned contracts
112|and evidence; they do not read one another's private databases or duplicate
113|policy.
114|
115|## Flagship capability roadmap
116|
117|This section separates the current alpha foundation from release targets:
118|
119|| Status | Capability |
120||---|---|
121|| Implemented alpha | P
```

{"content": "1|# Flagship Completion Execution Plan
2|
3|> Execution authority: the repository issues, the July 17-18 Hermes sessions,

6|
7|## Outcome
8|
9|Finish `llm-gate` as the portfolio flagship, bring `llm-gate-node` to an honest
10|parity boundary, and then verify and polish the remaining portfolio repositories.
11|No model, provider, or capability tier is treated as a permanent product fact.
12|Runtime candidates come from the configured catalog and must pass deterministic
13|requirements and live availability checks before ranking.
14|
15|## Worker operating rules
16|
17|1. The main agent owns architecture, security, release decisions, integration,
18|   and final verification.
19|2. A worker receives one bounded task in an isolated worktree.
20|3. Immediately before assignment, the selected runtime model must pass:
21|   - catalog capability inspection;
22|   - a fixed one-token, no-tool OpenAI-compatible probe with non-empty assistant
23|     output and positive usage;
24|   - the exact `omniroute launch-codex -- exec` path used for the assignment.
25|4. Failed or ambiguous models are excluded for that run. Model identifiers are
26|   runtime data and are never committed as product policy.
27|5. At most two implementation workers run concurrently on this VPS.
28|6. Every implementation gets a separate requirements review, code-quality
29|   review, and main-agent verification before integration.
30|
31|## Wave 1: make live availability truthful
32|
33|### Task 1: harden the probe contract
34|
35|Files:
36|
37|- Modify `tests/test_probes.py`
38|- Modify `llm_gate/probes.py`
39|- Modify `README.md`
40|

42|
43|1. Add failing tests proving that zero usage, empty assistant output, quota
44|   exhaustion, and HTTP transport errors cannot produce `ready`.
45|2. Run `pytest -q tests/test_probes.py` and record the expected failures.
46|3. Require a successful status, positive usage, and non-empty assistant output.
47|4. Preserve HTTP status classification through the stdlib transport.
48|5. Make injected observation time deterministic.
49|6. Run focused tests, then the complete quality suite.
50|7. Commit this pre-existing Hermes slice as one reviewed atomic change.
51|
52|### Task 2: connect probes to catalog eligibility
53|
54|Files:
55|
56|- Modify `llm_gate/availability.py`
57|- Modify `llm_gate/api.py`
58|- Add or modify focused tests under `tests/`
59|
60|Steps:
61|
62|1. Specify the public boundary between catalog rows, probe observations, and
63|   normalized candidates.
64|2. Add failing tests for stale, unknown, degraded, denied, and ready states.
65|3. Ensure a catalog row alone never establishes readiness.
66|4. Expose redacted availability reasons in model and route explanations.
67|5. Verify that allow/deny policy and task requirements run before ranking.
68|
69|## Wave 2: replace legacy tier routing
70|
71|### Task 3: define minimal-sufficient capability scoring
72|
73|Files:
74|
75|- Modify `llm_gate/contracts.py`
76|- Modify `llm_gate/models.py`
77|- Modify `llm_gate/classifier.py`
78|- Modify `llm_gate/intelligence.py`
79|- Modify `llm_gate/dispatcher.py`
80|- Modify their focused tests
81|
82|Steps:
83|
84|1. Add characterization tests for every supported public routing path.
85|2. Express task requirements as capabilities, privacy, context, tool, latency,
86|   budget, and production-impact constraints.
87|3. Filter all ineligible candidates before any adaptive ranking.
88|4. Rank remaining candidates by sufficient capability and operator objective,
89|   preferring lower cost/strength when quality requirements are still met.
90|5. Remove required tier fields and hard-coded model-family policy from active
91|   routing contracts through a documented compatibility migration.
92|6. Prove that adaptive guidance cannot reintroduce an ineligible candidate.
93|
94|### Task 4: production intelligence boundary
95|
96|Files:
97|
98|- Modify `llm_gate/intelligence.py`
99|- Modify `llm_gate/planner.py`
100|- Modify `llm_gate/api.py`
101|- Add protocol and failure-mode tests
102|
103|Steps:
104|
105|1. Implement the versioned intelligence contract from
106|   `docs/specs/INTELLIGENCE_ADAPTER_PROTOCOL_V1.md`.
107|2. Separate deterministic readiness from managed Ruflo/RuVector readiness.
108|3. Fail closed in production when mandatory intelligence is unavailable.
109|4. Allow deterministic degraded mode only when explicitly configured.
110|5. Preserve correlation IDs and redacted, structured failure envelopes.
111|
112|## Wave 3: legal execution semantics

123|
124|1. Encode idempotency and retry eligibility explicitly.
125|2. Distinguish auth, quota, rate-limit, overload, timeout, transport, and
126|   circuit-open failures.
127|3. Honor bounded backoff and `Retry-After`.
128|4. Re-run eligibility filtering before every fallback.
129|5. Prohibit fallback once response bytes have been streamed.

The `2026-07-18-flagship-completion.md` plan is the **exact operating model we've been discussing**: main agent owns architecture/verification, workers get one bounded task each in isolated worktrees, every runtime model must pass the one-token probe before assignment, max 2 concurrent workers, every slice gets independent review + main-agent verification. This is precisely the ownership model you described.

Crucially, this plan already decomposes #57 into concrete tasks:

- **Task 2**: connect probes to catalog eligibility (availability.py + api.py) — THIS is the wiring gap I identified

This resolves the earlier confusion: the worker substrate is OmniRoute through the `omniroute launch-codex` CLI at the pinned version, not ruflo's `agent spawn`. I was using the wrong worker harness.

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434768.5286775)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate
2|
3|<p align=\"center\"><b>Alpha policy and availability primitives for explainable LLM routing.</b></p>
4|
5|[![CI](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate/ci.yml?style=flat-square&label=CI)](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate/actions)
6|[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/llm-gate?style=flat-square)](https://pypi.org/project/llm-gate/)
7|
8|`llm-gate` is an alpha Python library and local OpenAI-compatible proxy. It
9|normalizes a task into a versioned `TaskSpec`, applies deterministic policy,
10|capability, privacy, budget, and availability gates, and explains the eligible
11|candidate set. Adaptive intelligence is advisory: it may rank eligible
12|candidates, but it cannot bypass a hard gate.
13|
14|> **Status:** The deterministic contracts and availability adapter are usable
15|> now. End-to-end workflow orchestration, live OmniRoute health/quota
16|> integration, legal retry/fallback, and managed intelligence remain unfinished
17|> release gates. The proxy is alpha. This repository does not claim production
18|> readiness, provider uptime, or a particular routing latency.
19|
20|## OmniRoute availability adapter boundary
21|
22|`OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter` defines local JSON-like protocol aliases. These
23|names are `llm-gate` adapter operations, not a claim that OmniRoute exposes
24|same-named functions:
25|
26|- catalog input: `catalog()` or `list_models()`;
27|- optional pre-fetched runtime input: `runtime()` or `get_runtime()`;
28|- optional pre-fetched capability input: `discover_capabilities()`.
29|
30|A concrete integration may map OmniRoute's documented OpenAI-compatible
31|`GET /v1/models` response to the catalog input. This repository has not yet
32|identified a documented OmniRoute source for complete live health, quota,
33|price, or capability evidence. Those signals therefore remain `unknown` unless
34|an integration supplies explicit, versioned evidence from a documented
35|API/CLI/MCP/A2A surface. See the
36|[memory source index](docs/operations/MEMORY_SOURCE_INDEX.md) for the pinned
37|documentation observations and trust labels.
38|
39|Use `StaticOmniRouteTransport` for fixtures, `CallableOmniRouteTransport` to
40|adapt explicit callables, or `MappingOmniRouteTransport` to adapt a mapping of
41|pre-fetched operation payloads. Capability discovery is allowlisted: only the
42|local operation aliases above are honored, and unknown advertised operations
43|are ignored rather than trusted.
44|
45|Transport failures are surfaced as typed adapter errors
46|(`OmniRouteTransportUnsupported`, `OmniRouteTransportTimeout`,
47|`OmniRouteTransportMalformed`) and converted into failure-isolated availability
48|reports instead of leaking transport-specific behavior into routing policy.
49|
50|## Five-minute clean-environment quickstart
51|
52|The fastest clean-room proof is the deterministic flagship demo. It requires no
53|credentials, makes no network calls, and produces stable JSON output.
54|
55|From a fresh checkout:
56|
57|```bash
58|python -m venv .venv
59|source .venv/bin/activate
60|pip install -e \".[dev]\"
61|python scripts/flagship_demo.py
62|python -m pytest -q tests/test_flagship_demo.py
63|```
64|
65|For a packaging-style smoke check in an isolated environment, build a wheel and
66|install that artifact instead of using an editable checkout:
67|
68|```bash
69|python -m pip install build
70|python -m build
71|python -m venv /tmp/llm-gate-smoke
72|source /tmp/llm-gate-smoke/bin/activate
73|pip install dist/llm_gate-*.whl
74|python -m llm_gate.cli --help
75|python /path/to/llm-gate/scripts/flagship_demo.py
76|```
77|
78|See [the reproducible demo guide](docs/DEMO.md) for the clean-environment
79|verification flow, expected behavior, and current limitations.
80|
81|## See the decision, without credentials
82|
83|The flagship walkthrough is deterministic and makes no network calls:
84|
85|```bash
86|python scripts/flagship_demo.py
87|```
88|
89|It constructs a `TaskSpec`, evaluates four in-memory runtime observations,
90|selects one eligible candidate, and reports why the other three were excluded
91|(missing capability, exhausted quota, and unknown health). The output is stable
92|across runs.
93|
94|## Install and use the library
95|
96|```bash
97|python -m venv .venv
98|source .venv/bin/activate
99|pip install -e \".[dev]\"
100|```
101|
102|Compatibility routing remains available through `Gate`; it defaults to the
103|explicit development/degraded profile:
104|
105|```python
106|from llm_gate import Gate
107|
108|gate = Gate()
109|decision = gate.route(\"Rewrite the auth module\", criticality=\"high\")
110|print(decision.model)
111|print(decision.reason)
112|```
113|
114|The `criticality` argument is a compatibility input, not the routing algorithm.
115|New integrations should use `TaskSpec` and `RoutingDecisionContract` (see
116|[contract migration](docs/contracts-migration.md)).
117|
118|## Local proxy (alpha)
119|
120|The proxy forwards to a configured upstream and is not a bundled model server.
121|Use a caller token or Unix socket for non-anonymous operation; keep anonymous
122|mode on loopback for development only:
123|
124|```bash
125|export LLMGATE_AUTH_TOKEN='use-a-long-random-token'
126|export LLMGATE_HOST=127.0.0.1
127|export LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL=https://api.openai.com/v1
128|export OPENAI_API_KEY='set-this-in-your-shell-not-in-a-request'
129|llm-gate serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
130|```
131|
132|```bash
133|LLMGATE_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true llm-gate serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
134|```
135|
136|Anonymous mode is rejected on non-loopback addresses. The proxy owns upstream
137|configuration and credentials; client-supplied upstream URLs and credentials
138|are not accepted. Review [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md) and the
139|[release acceptance matrix](docs/specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md) before connecting
140|real provider credentials.
141|
142|## What is impleme
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434768.5381029)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate — Technical Specification
2|## v0.1.0 — July 2026 (historical library profile)
3|
4|> This document describes the original library-only release. The current
5|> framework-agnostic proxy target is specified in
6|> [`docs/specs/PRODUCT_SPEC_V0.2.md`](specs/PRODUCT_SPEC_V0.2.md), with its
7|> release gates in [`docs/specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md`](specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md).
8|
9|---
10|
11|## 1. Overview
12|
13|llm-gate is a lightweight Python library that routes LLM tasks to an appropriate model based on task criticality. The original v0.1 profile kept core routing dependency-light and did not include the HTTP proxy layer.
14|
15|### Core Invariant
16|
17|> **Critical code NEVER touches a cheap model. Cheap work NEVER touches an expensive model. If routing fails, work proceeds on the primary model.**
18|
19|---
20|
21|## 2. Architecture
22|
23|```
24|                          ┌──────────────────────┐
25|                          │    gate.route(task)   │
26|                          └──────────┬───────────┘
27|                                     │
28|                          ┌──────────▼───────────┐
29|                          │   Keyword Scanner     │
30|                          │   (escalation check)  │
31|                          └──────────┬───────────┘
32|                                     │
33|                          ┌──────────▼───────────┐
34|                          │   Tier Resolution     │
35|                          │   input × escalation  │
36|                          │   → effective tier     │
37|                          └──────────┬───────────┘
38|                                     │
39|                     ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
40|                     │                               │
41|              ┌──────▼──────┐                 ┌──────▼──────┐
42|              │  T0: CRIT   │                 │ T1-T3: GATE │
43|              │  → primary  │                 │  → offload  │
44|              │  (always)   │                 │  candidate   │
45|              └──────┬──────┘                 └──────┬──────┘
46|                     │                               │
47|                     │                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
48|                     │                    │  Model Discovery    │
49|                     │                    │  (cached /v1/models │
50|                     │                    │   per provider)     │
51|                     │                    └──────────┬──────────┘
52|                     │                               │
53|                     │                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
54|                     │                    │  Capability Match   │
55|                     │                    │  (tier ≤ model cap) │
56|                     │                    └──────────┬──────────┘
57|                     │                               │
58|                     │                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
59|                     │                    │  Headroom Check     │
60|                     │                    │  (quota remaining?) │
61|                     │                    └──────────┬──────────┘
62|                     │                               │
63|                     │                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
64|                     │                    │  Select cheapest    │
65|                     │                    │  adequate model     │
66|                     │                    └──────────┬──────────┘
67|                     │                               │
68|                     │                 ┌─────────────┴────────┐
69|                     │                 │                      │
70|                     │          ┌──────▼──────┐       ┌───────▼──────┐
71|                     │          │  Available  │       │  Exhausted   │
72|                     │          │  → offload  │       │  → primary   │
73|                     │          │  model      │       │  (fail-open) │
74|                     │          └──────┬──────┘       └───────┬──────┘
75|                     │                 │                      │
76|                     ▼                 ▼                      ▼
77|              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
78|              │              RoutingDecision returned            │
79|              │              + JSONL log entry written           │
80|              └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
81|```
82|
83|---
84|
85|## 3. Data Model
86|
87|### 3.1 Tiers
88|
89|| Tier | Name | Int | Behavior | Example Tasks |
90||------|------|-----|----------|---------------|
91|| T0 | CRITICAL | 0 | **Never offload.** Always uses `primary_model`. | Payment processing, auth/security review, production deploys, database migrations |
92|| T1 | HIGH | 1 | Offload only to high-capability models. | Architecture decisions, complex refactoring, security audits, API design |
93|| T2 | MEDIUM | 2 | Offload to mid-capability models. Default tier. | Feature implementation, test writing, code review, debugging |
94|| T3 | LOW | 3 | Offload to cheapest available model. | Formatting, type hints, docstrings, log summarization, boilerplate |
95|
96|### 3.2 RoutingDecision
97|
98|```python
99|@dataclass(frozen=True)
100|class RoutingDecision:
101|    model: str              # fully-qualified model ID (e.g., \"anthropic/claude-sonnet-4\")
102|    provider: str           # provider name from config
103|    tier: int               # effective tier (0-3)
104|    reason: str             # human-readable routing explanation
105|    alternatives: list[str] # other models considered but not chosen
106|    headroom_pct: float     # remaining quota % for chosen model (-1 if unknown)
107|    latency_ms: float       # time to compute routing decision
108|    escalated: bool         # was criticality bumped by keyword scanner?
109|    escalation_reason: str | None  # which pattern triggered escalation
110|    logged: bo
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434768.5474048)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate Product Specification
2|## v0.2 release candidate: intelligent, client-agnostic model-selection proxy
3|
4|**Status:** Proposed, implementation-blocking specification  
5|**Owner:** llm-gate maintainers  
6|**Primary upstream:** OmniRoute-compatible OpenAI API (`LLM_GATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL`)  
7|**Normative language:** MUST, MUST NOT, SHOULD, MAY are requirements levels.
8|
9|## 1. Product promise
10|
11|`llm-gate` is a local reverse proxy that accepts standard LLM API traffic, evaluates the request against explicit safety and capability policies, chooses the best currently eligible model, rewrites only the model/provider selection, and forwards the request to a configured upstream router such as OmniRoute.
12|
13|It is not a Claude Code plugin. It does not require Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Jcode, Hermes, Cowork, an Agents SDK, or any particular agent framework. A client only needs to speak one supported wire protocol and point its base URL at llm-gate.
14|
15|The optimization target is **best appropriate model**, not cheapest model:
16|
17|> Maximize expected task quality subject to hard safety requirements, required capabilities, current availability, latency constraints, privacy constraints, and user policy. Cost is a tie-breaker or an explicit secondary objective, never a reason to violate a quality floor.
18|
19|## 2. Honest scope
20|
21|### v0.2 MUST support
22|
23|- OpenAI-compatible `GET /v1/models`.
24|- OpenAI-compatible `POST /v1/chat/completions`.
25|- Request streaming passthrough using Server-Sent Events.
26|- Tool/function definitions, tool calls, JSON mode, images, system messages, and unknown forward-compatible fields preserved unless a policy explicitly rejects them.
27|- Configured upstream base URL, including OmniRoute at a user-selected local URL such as `http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1`.
28|- Dynamic model catalog refresh with local allow/deny filtering.
29|- Capability-aware selection with deterministic policy and transparent decision metadata.
30|- Safe retry/fallback for transport failures and rate limits, restricted to idempotent requests and policy-approved alternatives.
31|- Local health, readiness, configuration validation, and decision observability endpoints.
32|- Direct SDK/CLI routing API retained for callers that do not need proxying.
33|
34|### v0.2 MUST provide the intelligence contract
35|
36|- A mandatory `IntelligenceService` boundary that runs deterministic policy, capability, availability, and explainability logic on every routed request.
37|- The production profile MUST use the Ruflo/RuVector managed adapter for bounded adaptive routing and outcome learning.
38|- A deterministic local backend MUST remain present as the safety floor and cold-start path. Adaptive intelligence may rank eligible candidates, but it MUST NOT override hard policy.
39|- Readiness MUST report the intelligence backend state. Production readiness fails when the required managed backend is unavailable unless an operator explicitly starts a development-only degraded profile.
40|
41|### v0.2 MAY support behind explicit adapters
42|
43|- Anthropic Messages protocol (`/v1/messages`) through a tested translation adapter.
44|- OpenAI Responses API (`/v1/responses`) through a tested translation adapter.
45|- Provider-specific quota APIs.
46|- Additional Ruflo guidance gates and model-outcome hooks beyond the required intelligence adapter.
47|- Additional RuVector SONA trajectory recording and learned-policy suggestions beyond the required intelligence adapter.
48|
49|### v0.2 MUST NOT claim
50|
51|- That every model in OmniRoute's `/v1/models` catalog is live, healthy, or quota-available.
52|- That SONA has autonomous authority to downgrade a hard safety policy.
53|- That response quality can be inferred from HTTP success alone.
54|- That Claude Code hooks provide universal interception.
55|- That the project is 20K-star quality before the release gates in `RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md` pass.
56|
57|## 3. Reference architecture
58|
59|```mermaid
60|flowchart LR
61|  C[OpenAI-compatible client\
Claude Code / Codex / Cursor / Jcode / SDK] --> G[llm-gate\
protocol proxy]
62|  G --> P[Policy + capability resolver]
63|  P --> L[Local deterministic router]
64|  L --> S[Required Ruflo/RuVector intelligence adapter]
65|  L --> U[OmniRoute or any compatible upstream]
66|  U --> M[Eligible provider/model]
67|  M --> U --> G --> C
68|  G --> O[Decision + outcome telemetry]
69|  O --> R[Ruflo/RuVector intelligence\
managed, async, bounded]
70|```
71|
72|The proxy MUST always retain a deterministic local safety floor. The production profile MUST have the Ruflo/RuVector intelligence service available and healthy. A standalone deterministic profile MAY exist for development, packaging, and recovery, but it MUST be visibly marked degraded, MUST NOT be presented as production-ready, and MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.
73|
74|## 4. Request lifecycle
75|
76|1. **Authenticate local caller.** The proxy MUST support a local bearer token or Unix-socket mode. Anonymous mode MAY be enabled only for loopback in development.
77|2. **Parse and validate protocol.** Reject malformed requests with protocol-compatible errors. Do not send malformed input upstream.
78|3. **Redact and fingerprint.** Create a request ID, policy version, prompt fingerprint, message count, estimated input size, and capability requirements. Do not persist raw prompts by default.
79|4. **Run hard policy gates.** Security, secrets, destructive operations, payment/live-trade execution, production deploys, and user-configured protected domains raise minimum requirements or force an allowlisted frontier route.
80|5. **Infer capability requirements.** Determine required context size, tool calling, structured output, vision/audio, reasoning, coding, latency, streaming, privacy, and model family constraints.
81|6. **Refresh and normalize the catalog.** Read the upstream `/v1/models` catalog on a bounded TTL. Apply local 
```

{"content": "1|# Handoff Doc: llm-gate Subcommand & Diagnostics Upgrade
2|
3|Passed from Antigravity to Codex GPT-5.6.
4|
5|## 1. What was Solved / Added
6|* **Check Command (`llm-gate check`)**: Added a non-interactive configuration check subcommand validating:
7|  * Missing or corrupted/invalid `llm-gate.yaml`.
8|  * Malformed syntax.
9|  * Credential scanning (noting literal `sk-` or `api_key` leaks inside the base URL mapping).
10|  * Duplicate base URLs within the `providers` map.
11|* **Doctor Command (`llm-gate doctor`)**: Diagnostics and repair mode confirming:
12|  * Primary model tier mappings (Tier-0 setup checks).
13|  * OmniRoute database node query endpoint reachability.
14|  * Duplicate node registrations in local OmniRoute (compares base URLs).
15|  * Unresponsive endpoint connectivity (attempts a 1-second timeout tcp lookup connection to check if active).
16|  * Interactive resolution: Asks the user if they'd like to fix duplicate nodes, and fires a `DELETE /api/provider-nodes/<id>` request to repair the state.
17|* **Installer/Sync Upgrade**: Sourced sqlite databases dynamically at `~/.omniroute/storage.sqlite` to secure API authorization. Checked against default `20128` (default) and `20132` (loopback proxy) ports.
18|* **Bandit & Ruff CI compliance**: Neutralized Bandit's URL injection rule [B310] via `# nosec B310` tags. Sanitized loop context scopes (`fixed_issue`) and replaced `socket.error` with standard `OSError` to adhere to Ruff lints.
19|
20|## 2. Current Verification Status
21|* **Unit Tests**: Full mock coverage added to `tests/test_cli_inprocess.py` (`test_cmd_doctor_all_healthy`, `test_cmd_doctor_issues_and_duplicates`, `test_cmd_check_missing_config`, `test_cmd_check_valid_config`, `test_cmd_check_invalid_config`).
22|* **CI/CD Pipeline**: **All checks passed (green).** Verified run **29375138319** passed completely.
23|* **Type Safety & Style**: 100% clean `mypy` check (Python 3.12 target) and `ruff check` / `ruff format`.
24|
25|## 3. Latest Git Commits
26|Pushed cleanly to `main` branch:
27|* `2a35dd5bf`: `fix(ci): fix ruff UP024, audit B310, and central router mock detection issues in CI`
28|* `81c740968`: `feat(cli): add check command to validate configuration syntax and sanity non-interactively`
29|* `22f98a73a`: `feat(cli): add doctor command for configuration and connectivity diagnostics`
30|
31|## 4. Suggested Next Steps
32|* Integrate further telemetry audits to inspect average response latency.
33|* Expand mock client check suites validating downstream libraries against the mock routes.
34|", "total_lines": 33, "file_size": 2473, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "1|# Memory Source Index
2|
3|Status: active ingestion policy, last reviewed 2026-07-18.
4|
5|This index defines what may be retained about Hermes, OmniRoute, Ruflo,
6|RuVector, the product vision, and the repository portfolio. It exists to make
7|cross-session memory useful without turning copied documentation, stale
8|runtime state, or marketing claims into accidental authority.
9|
10|## Storage model
11|
12|| Store | Purpose | Authority |
13||---|---|---|
14|| Git repository | Versioned product vision, operating policy, source index, contracts, and tests | Primary durable record for product decisions |
15|| GitHub | Issues, owners, review, pull requests, exact-SHA CI, releases, and public evidence | Primary delivery and release record |
16|| Hindsight cloud | Compact, secret-free resume checkpoints and curated ecosystem knowledge cards | Recall aid only; verify against Git and sources |
17|| Codebase knowledge graph | Symbols, call paths, architecture, and code relationships | Code discovery aid; source code remains authority |
18|| RuVector adapter | Future privacy-safe task/outcome retrieval and graph evidence | Optional advisory product subsystem, not current operator memory authority |
19|
20|Do not ingest full upstream documentation trees into Hindsight. Store an
21|original synthesis, exact source URLs, observed version or branch, review date,
22|trust label, and refresh trigger. The source remains canonical.
23|
24|## Trust labels
25|
26|Every retained record must use one or more of these labels:
27|
28|| Label | Meaning |
29||---|---|
30|| `authoritative-contract` | Local versioned code, schema, test, release, or explicit product decision |
31|| `verified-evidence` | Reproduced local command or terminal exact-SHA CI result |
32|| `upstream-documented` | Behavior stated in a pinned or dated official upstream source |
33|| `upstream-claim` | Performance, scale, maturity, or marketing language not independently reproduced |
34|| `local-observation` | Host/runtime fact observed at a specific time and expected to expire |
35|| `proposal` | Intended architecture, roadmap, or product hypothesis not yet shipped |
36|| `third-party` | Source or checkout not owned by Nick |
37|
38|`upstream-documented` does not mean locally integrated or verified.
39|
40|## Authoritative ecosystem sources
41|
42|Versions are the observations used for this synthesis, not automatic upgrade
43|instructions.
44|
45|| System | Observed source | Trust and retained scope | Refresh trigger |
46||---|---|---|---|

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434780.433799)
```
{"content": "1|# OmniRoute transport integration
2|
3|`llm-gate` can consume OmniRoute's public model catalog and a bounded set of
4|documented runtime reads. OmniRoute remains the provider and credential plane;
5|`llm-gate` remains the deterministic policy, eligibility, capacity, and
6|explanation authority.
7|
8|This integration is pinned to OmniRoute `release/v3.8.49` source commit
9|[`a5e5e88`](https://github.com/diegosouzapw/OmniRoute/tree/a5e5e880928886fe47362cdba63ca7c2c2cf55b4)
10|and its [API
11|reference](https://github.com/diegosouzapw/OmniRoute/blob/a5e5e880928886fe47362cdba63ca7c2c2cf55b4/docs/reference/API_REFERENCE.md).
12|It never reads OmniRoute's database, provider-account records, or other private
13|implementation state.
14|
15|## Supported HTTP operations
16|
17|`OmniRouteHTTPTransport` issues `GET` requests only:
18|
19|| `llm-gate` source | OmniRoute operation | Default | Normalized evidence |
20||---|---|---:|---|
21|| catalog | `/v1/models` | yes | Opaque model IDs, provider, and declared capabilities |
22|| health | `/api/monitoring/health` | yes | Provider health, breakers, lockouts, and learned headroom when present |
23|| rate limits | `/api/rate-limits` | no | Model lockouts when present; connection limiter counters are not treated as quota headroom |
24|| model cooldowns | `/api/resilience/model-cooldowns` | no | Temporary provider/model lockouts |
25|| budget | `/api/usage/budget?apiKeyId=…` | no | Remaining USD for one configured API-key ID |
26|| token limits | `/api/usage/token-limits?apiKeyId=…` | no | Most restrictive model/provider/global token headroom |
27|
28|The catalog is discovery evidence, not readiness. Runtime records are minimized
29|into the public `RuntimeObservation` fields before they reach the availability
30|adapter. Unknown, missing, stale, unauthorized, malformed, and unavailable
31|evidence remains explicit and fails closed under the default policy.
32|
33|## Minimal loopback configuration
34|
35|Use environment variables or another secret manager; do not place credentials
36|in source code or URLs.
37|
38|```python
39|import os
40|
41|from llm_gate import (
42|    CandidateRequirements,
43|    OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter,
44|    OmniRouteHTTPTransport,
45|)
46|
47|transport = OmniRouteHTTPTransport(
48|    \"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1\",
49|    api_key=os.environ.get(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\"),
50|    management_token=os.environ.get(\"OMNIROUTE_MANAGEMENT_TOKEN\"),
51|    allow_private_hosts={\"127.0.0.1\"},
52|)
53|
54|report = OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter(transport).evaluate(
55|    CandidateRequirements(required=frozenset({\"tools\"}))
56|)
57|```
58|
59|Every destination requires an exact `allow_private_hosts` entry despite the
60|parameter's legacy name. Plain HTTP is accepted only with a loopback IP literal,
61|which supports an explicitly allowed local service without a DNS-rebinding or
62|cleartext remote-credential path. Remote deployments must use HTTPS and a
63|separately reviewed access boundary.
64|Redirects and encoded responses are rejected, the timeout is bounded, raw
65|responses default to a 1 MiB limit, and parsed JSON has a fixed 64-container
66|nesting limit across supported Python versions.
67|
68|`api_key` is sent only to `/v1/models`. `management_token` is separate and is
69|sent only to configured runtime reads. A deployment with OmniRoute API-key
70|enforcement disabled may omit `api_key`; protected management sources still
71|need an appropriate management credential.
72|
73|## Opting into management evidence
74|
75|Management reads are disabled unless named explicitly:
76|
77|```python
78|transport = OmniRouteHTTPTransport(
79|    \"http://127.0.0.1:20128/v1\",
80|    api_key=os.environ.get(\"OMNIROUTE_API_KEY\"),
81|    management_token=os.environ[\"OMNIROUTE_MANAGEMENT_TOKEN\"],
82|    usage_api_key_id=os.environ[\"OMNIROUTE_USAGE_API_KEY_ID\"],
83|    runtime_sources={
84|        \"health\",
85|        \"rate_limits\",
86|        \"model_cooldowns\",
87|        \"budget\",
88|        \"token_limits\",
89|    },
90|    allow_private_hosts={\"127.0.0.1\"},
91|)
92|```
93|
94|`usage_api_key_id` is mandatory when `budget` or `token_limits` is enabled and
95|is sent as the documented query parameter to both reads. The release source
96|returns a scoped budget summary and a scoped token-limit list; `llm-gate`
97|retains only normalized remaining capacity, ignores disabled token limits, and
98|never exposes the raw payload. An explicit budget denial is a hard global
99|eligibility denial, even when the response also contains positive-looking
100|remaining capacity. Use a least-privilege management-scoped key where the
101|OmniRoute deployment supports one.
102|
103|## Stable failure contract
104|
105|The HTTP transport never includes response bodies, credentials, URL secrets,
106|or underlying exception objects in its exception graph:
107|
108|| Upstream result | Public transport failure | Availability result |
109||---|---|---|
110|| timeout | `OmniRouteTransportTimeout` | `timeout` |
111|| 401 or 403 | `OmniRouteTransportUnauthorized` | `unauthorized` |
112|| 404 | `OmniRouteTransportUnsupported` | unavailable operation |
113|| invalid or oversized JSON | `OmniRouteTransportMalformed` | `malformed` |
114|| other HTTP/network failure | `OmniRouteTransportError` | `unavailable` |
115|
116|The aggregate health endpoint's top-level `status` describes the OmniRoute
117|service, not every catalog model. A model becomes ready only from matching
118|provider/model evidence; an otherwise unobserved model remains `unknown`.
119|
120|## CLI, MCP, A2A, and 9router boundaries
121|
122|Non-HTTP integrations use `CallableOmniRouteTransport` or
123|`MappingOmniRouteTransport`. Only operations with configured implementations
124|are advertised. This keeps CLI, MCP, A2A, and test transports behind the same
125|typed catalog/runtime contract without pretending that an installed binary or
126|an advertised method is usable.
127|
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434780.4437659)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate Release Acceptance Matrix
2|## No public release until every blocking gate passes
3|
4|### A. Core correctness
5|
6|- [ ] `python -m pytest` passes in a clean environment with all declared dev dependencies.
7|- [ ] `ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, and strict mypy pass.
8|- [ ] Configuration loading validates URLs, ports, auth, model policy, and unknown keys.
9|- [ ] Routing is deterministic for fixed catalog/policy/input fixtures.
10|- [ ] Best-appropriate scoring is covered by unit tests, including quality floors and tie-breakers.
11|- [ ] Learned suggestions cannot bypass hard gates.
12|
13|### A1. Mandatory intelligence
14|
15|- [ ] Every routed request passes through the versioned `IntelligenceService` contract.
16|- [ ] The deterministic policy and capability safety floor runs even during cold start or adaptive-backend failure.
17|- [ ] The production profile requires healthy Ruflo/RuVector managed intelligence before `/ready` reports ready.
18|- [ ] Development-only degraded mode is explicit, visible in readiness and decision events, and cannot be advertised as production-ready.
19|- [ ] Adaptive-backend failure does not crash in-flight requests, but flips readiness and rejects protected work unless explicit degraded development mode is active.
20|- [ ] Privacy opt-out removes new prompt-derived learning data without disabling deterministic safety intelligence.
21|- [ ] Ruflo/RuVector adapter calls use documented CLI/API surfaces, bounded timeouts, redacted events, and no private database reads.
22|- [ ] Adaptive suggestions are advisory only and are proven unable to bypass denial, privacy, capability, or protected-task gates.
23|
24|### B. Transparent proxy
25|
26|- [ ] `GET /health` and `GET /ready` have stable JSON schemas.
27|- [ ] `GET /v1/models` returns a filtered, normalized catalog and does not claim catalog rows are live.
28|- [ ] `POST /v1/chat/completions` forwards a minimal request mutation and preserves all unknown fields.
29|- [ ] Non-streaming response status, headers, body, usage, tool calls, and errors round-trip.
30|- [ ] Streaming response passes arbitrary SSE chunk boundaries without corruption.
31|- [ ] Tool calls with parallel calls and partial deltas round-trip.
32|- [ ] Request size and timeout limits are enforced.
33|- [ ] Upstream auth is never taken from an arbitrary client field.
34|- [ ] Client-selected models cannot bypass policy unless explicitly enabled.
35|
36|### C. Fallback and availability
37|
38|- [ ] OmniRoute catalog rows can be marked `unknown`, `ready`, `degraded`, or `denied`.
39|- [ ] Local allow/deny rules work even when upstream catalog listing is broad.
40|- [ ] Health/headroom adapters are bounded and failure-isolated.
41|- [ ] Non-idempotent or already-streaming requests are never silently retried.
42|- [ ] Protected requests never fall back below their hard capability floor.
43|- [ ] Every fallback is represented in the response headers and event log.
44|
45|### D. Safety and privacy
46|
47|- [ ] Local authentication is tested.
48|- [ ] Upstream URL validation prevents SSRF and arbitrary per-request destinations.
49|- [ ] Secrets are redacted from logs and learning events.
50|- [ ] Raw prompt logging is disabled by default.
51|- [ ] Debug endpoints are loopback/auth protected.
52|- [ ] Guidance/Ruflo/RuVector integration failures cannot weaken policy safety. In production they make readiness fail, while the deterministic floor remains available for explicit degraded development mode.
53|- [ ] Dependency and static security scans pass.
54|
55|### E. Compatibility
56|
57|- [ ] Raw `httpx` client smoke test.
58|- [ ] OpenAI Python client smoke test pointed at llm-gate.
59|- [ ] One CLI-agent configuration example verified against a mock upstream.
60|- [ ] OmniRoute integration verified against the configured local endpoint.
61|- [ ] Anthropic/Responses adapters are either passing contract tests or explicitly excluded from the release.
62|
63|### F. Distribution and operations
64|
65|- [ ] `python -m build` and `twine check dist/*` pass.
66|- [ ] Wheel contains no credentials, caches, logs, test artifacts, or local paths.
67|- [ ] Docker image builds from a minimal context if Docker is advertised.
68|- [ ] `llm-gate --help`, `llm-gate serve --help`, `llm-gate check`, and `llm-gate route` work after install.
69|- [ ] Version, changelog, migration notes, and documented limitations agree.
70|- [ ] A release candidate can be installed into a fresh virtual environment and smoke-tested without the source checkout.
71|
72|### G. Public claims gate
73|
74|Before promotion, the release owner must attach evidence for each checked box. The README MUST NOT claim 20K-star readiness, universal compatibility, real-time quota accuracy, or autonomous quality learning without corresponding passing evidence.
75|", "total_lines": 74, "file_size": 4635, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434780.4542542)
```
{"content": "1|# Security assurance evidence
2|
3|Evidence snapshot for issue #68, Slice 35.3.
4|
5|`llm-gate` is still an alpha library and local proxy. This document publishes the current threat model, privacy and retention posture, and supply-chain evidence so operators and reviewers can evaluate the project using explicit claims instead of implied guarantees.
6|
7|## Scope and status
8|
9|This is an evidence document, not a certification.
10|
11|What it covers now:
12|
13|- threat model assumptions and primary abuse cases;
14|- privacy and retention defaults that are implemented or documented;
15|- current supply-chain controls visible in source control and CI.
16|
17|What it does **not** claim:
18|
19|- hosted service controls;
20|- formal compliance certification;
21|- production-readiness of every proxy or intelligence slice;
22|- zero vulnerability status for all transitive dependencies at all times.
23|
24|Primary source documents:
25|
26|- [`README.md`](../README.md)
27|- [`SECURITY.md`](../SECURITY.md)
28|- [`docs/specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md`](specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md)
29|- [`docs/specs/ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md`](specs/ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md)
30|- [`.github/workflows/ci.yml`](../.github/workflows/ci.yml)
31|- [`.github/workflows/codeql.yml`](../.github/workflows/codeql.yml)
32|- [`pyproject.toml`](../pyproject.toml)
33|
34|## 1. Threat model
35|
36|### 1.1 Assets
37|
38|The main assets in scope are:
39|
40|- task text and prompts submitted for routing;
41|- provider credentials and operator-owned authentication tokens;
42|- routing policy, model catalog, and availability evidence;
43|- decision logs and privacy-safe learning/outcome records;
44|- local proxy availability and integrity.
45|
46|### 1.2 Trust boundaries
47|
48|`llm-gate` operates across these boundaries:
49|
50|1. **Caller to local proxy or CLI.** Untrusted input may contain secrets, oversized bodies, malformed JSON, or attempts to influence routing.
51|2. **Proxy to upstream provider.** Upstream destinations and credentials must remain operator-controlled rather than caller-controlled.
52|3. **Deterministic policy to adaptive intelligence.** Adaptive ranking may advise but must not override hard safety gates.
53|4. **Runtime state to local logs/events.** Observability must avoid leaking full prompts or credentials by default.
54|5. **Repository to build/install environment.** Dependency and automation changes can alter shipped behavior even when core Python source is unchanged.
55|
56|### 1.3 Security assumptions
57|
58|This repository currently assumes:
59|
60|- operators control the host filesystem, process environment, and network egress rules;
61|- operators decide whether to run with loopback-only anonymous development mode or authenticated mode;
62|- upstream providers enforce their own account, storage, and retention policies outside this repository;
63|- development-mode degraded behavior is not treated as production-ready.
64|
65|### 1.4 Primary threats and current mitigations
66|
67|| Threat | Why it matters | Current mitigation evidence |
68||---|---|---|
69|| Sensitive work routed to the wrong model/provider | Could violate privacy, policy, or capability expectations | README states deterministic policy, privacy, budget, and availability gates run before ranking; release acceptance requires adaptive suggestions to be unable to bypass denial, privacy, capability, or protected-task gates. |
70|| Prompt or credential leakage through logs | Logs are often copied into bug reports, CI artifacts, or benchmarks | `SECURITY.md` states decision logs omit provider credentials, full prompts, completions, and full task text by default; `ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md` requires privacy-safe outcome records with redacted summaries and no raw prompts by default. |
71|| SSRF or caller-selected upstream exfiltration | A malicious caller could force requests to internal hosts or attacker infrastructure | `SECURITY.md` documents URL validation, hostname requirements, rejection of userinfo/query/fragment, private-host blocking by default, fresh DNS resolution checks, and disabled redirects. |
72|| Unauthorized local proxy use | A local or network-adjacent caller could send requests through operator credentials | `SECURITY.md` documents startup requirements for bearer auth or Unix socket unless explicit loopback-only anonymous development mode is enabled. |
73|| Request-size or timeout exhaustion | Large bodies or hanging upstreams can affect availability | `SECURITY.md` documents bounded request size via `LLMGATE_MAX_REQUEST_BYTES` and upstream timeouts. |
74|| Supply-chain compromise through dependencies or CI changes | Compromised packages or automation could alter runtime behavior or leak secrets | CI includes Bandit, pip-audit, and CodeQL; dependencies are declared in `pyproject.toml`; release claims remain alpha and evidence-based rather than implying hardening not yet proven. |
75|
76|### 1.5 Threats explicitly not solved by this repository alone
77|
78|These remain operator or upstream responsibilities:
79|
80|- disk encryption, backup retention, and secure log transport;
81|- endpoint protection and compromise of the local machine running `llm-gate`;
82|- provider-side data retention and training policies;
83|- network segmentation, firewalling, TLS termination policy, and reverse-proxy hardening;
84|- secret rotation, vaulting, and enterprise identity controls.
85|
86|## 2. Privacy posture
87|
88|### 2.1 Default privacy stance
89|
90|The current public posture is privacy-minimizing rather than privacy-eliminating.
91|
92|Implemented and documented defaults include:
93|
94|- deterministic safety gates run before any adaptive ranking;
95|- decision logs omit full prompts, completions, caller authorization, and provider credentials by default;
96|- privacy-safe learning/outcome episodes are designed to store redacted previews, stable fingerprints, and summary metadata rather than raw prompt/context blobs;
9
```

So my probe idea needs a SEPARATE transport (the `openai_probe_transport` in probes.py already does POST — that's the right split). The README documents this exactly: catalog/runtime via GET transport, probing via `ProbeRunner` with an injected OpenAI-compatible transport.

2. **The "document store" question is settled**: MEMORY_SOURCE_INDEX explicitly says "Do not ingest full upstream documentation trees into Hindsight. Store an original compact synthesis." So my earlier bulk `/files/retain` attempts were AGAINST the documented policy. The correct practice is compact knowledge cards with trust labels — which the index even enumerates (`portfolio-product-vision-v1`, etc.). I had this wrong.

4. **Authority model is explicit and documented**: llm-gate = deterministic routing authority; Hermes/Ruflo/RuVector/OmniRoute are advisory/transport. This matches the ownership model perfectly.

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434794.2194157)
```
{"content": "1|# Benchmark methodology
2|
3|This document defines how to collect comparable measurements. It intentionally
4|contains no latency claim or service-level objective. A result is meaningful
5|only with the commit, Python version, environment, fixture, and command recorded
6|alongside it.
7|
8|## Measurements
9|
10|1. **Contract normalization** — serialize/deserialize representative `TaskSpec`
11|   and `RoutingDecisionContract` payloads.
12|2. **Eligibility** — normalize a fixed catalog/runtime fixture and explain all
13|   candidates, including capability, freshness, quota, auth, and policy
14|   exclusions.
15|3. **Compatibility routing** — run `Gate.route` on a fixed prompt corpus with
16|   logging disabled or directed to a temporary path.
17|4. **Proxy overhead** — use a local stub upstream, measure request handling
18|   separately from upstream response time, and test both non-streaming and
19|   streaming payloads.
20|5. **Adapter failure behavior** — inject timeout, malformed, stale, and
21|   unavailable runtime responses; record state and readiness, not just elapsed
22|   time.
23|
24|Provider network time, model generation quality, and local decision overhead
25|must not be mixed into one number. The proxy's upstream and model results are
26|external variables, so provider benchmarks must identify provider/model,
27|region, request shape, and sampling date.
28|
29|## Protocol
30|
31|- Pin the repository commit and Python version; record OS, CPU, memory, and
32|  dependency lockfile hash.
33|- Use fixed, checked-in fixtures and a fixed policy version for local routing
34|  measurements.
35|- Warm up before collecting samples; report sample count, median, p95, p99,
36|  minimum, maximum, and spread. Report units and whether serialization/logging
37|  are included.
38|- Run enough repetitions to show variance and repeat the complete run at least
39|  three times. Do not publish a single best run.
40|- Separate cold-start and warm-process results.
41|- For proxy tests, use a local deterministic stub and report stub response time
42|  independently from gateway overhead.
43|- Publish raw JSON/CSV alongside a rendered table, plus the exact command.
44|- Treat regression thresholds as proposed until a baseline exists; thresholds
45|  must be justified by repeated measurements rather than copied claims.
46|
47|## Baseline command
48|
49|The reproducible baseline benchmark is a local routing exercise driven by a
50|checked-in fixture:
51|
52|```bash
53|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py
54|```
55|
56|The legacy entry point still proxies to the same harness:
57|
58|```bash
59|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/test_throughput.py
60|```
61|
62|It is not a production performance benchmark and does not measure a provider.
63|The default mode records only local contract, dispatcher, and compatibility
64|routing costs. For a new result, capture the output with:
65|
66|```bash
67|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py \\
68|  --output-json benchmark-results/<commit>.json \\
69|  | tee benchmark-results/<commit>.txt
70|```
71|
72|The fixture lives at `benchmarks/fixtures/reproducible.json` and the report
73|stores its SHA-256 digest so reruns can prove they used the same checked-in
74|inputs.
75|
76|If you truly need a live provider measurement, label it explicitly and opt in so
77|it cannot be confused with the reproducible local baseline:
78|
79|```bash
80|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py \\
81|  --allow-live-provider \\
82|  --live-provider openai/gpt-4o
83|```
84|
85|or via the CLI:
86|
87|```bash
88|llm-gate benchmark --output-json benchmark-results/<commit>.json
89|```
90|
91|Before publishing, add the environment manifest and fixture/policy versions to
92|the result file. Do not put API keys, raw prompts, completions, or authorization
93|headers in benchmark artifacts.
94|
95|## Reporting template
96|
97|```text
98|Commit:
99|Date (UTC):
100|Python:
101|OS / CPU / memory:
102|Dependency lockfile:
103|Fixture and policy version:
104|Command:
105|Warm-up / samples / repetitions:
106|Cold or warm process:
107|Logging enabled:
108|Upstream stub or provider (if any):
109|Results (unit, median, p95, p99, min, max):
110|Known limitations:
111|```
112|", "total_lines": 111, "file_size": 3919, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434794.2262137)
```
{"content": "1|# Reproducible flagship demo
2|
3|The public demo is intentionally a mock scenario, not a provider integration.
4|It proves the contract and explainability slice without credentials, network
5|access, a running gateway, or a mutable local decision log.
6|
7|## Clean-environment quickstart
8|
9|### Editable-install path
10|
11|From the repository root:
12|
13|```bash
14|python -m venv .venv
15|source .venv/bin/activate
16|pip install -e \".[dev]\"
17|python scripts/flagship_demo.py
18|pytest -q tests/test_flagship_demo.py
19|```
20|
21|### Wheel smoke path
22|
23|This path verifies that the packaged artifact imports cleanly in a fresh
24|environment, without assuming an existing editable checkout is active:
25|
26|```bash
27|python -m pip install build
28|python -m build
29|python -m venv /tmp/llm-gate-smoke
30|source /tmp/llm-gate-smoke/bin/activate
31|pip install dist/llm_gate-*.whl
32|python -m llm_gate.cli --help
33|python /absolute/path/to/llm-gate/scripts/flagship_demo.py
34|```
35|
36|### Optional clean-room env scrub
37|
38|Unset provider variables first if you want explicit evidence that the demo does
39|not depend on them:
40|
41|```bash
42|env -u OPENAI_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_API_KEY \\
43|  python scripts/flagship_demo.py
44|```
45|
46|The command prints one JSON document. The fixture timestamp, policy version,
47|candidate IDs, and ordering are fixed, so repeated runs produce byte-identical
48|output. It does not read environment variables or write files.
49|
50|## What the scenario demonstrates
51|
52|The task asks for `tools` and `structured_output` and is protected. Four fake
53|catalog/runtime rows are evaluated:
54|
55|| Candidate | Result | Evidence |
56||---|---|---|
57|| `demo/frontier-tools` | eligible and selected | Required capabilities, healthy observation, quota remaining |
58|| `demo/no-tools` | excluded | Missing `tools` |
59|| `demo/quota-empty` | excluded | Quota exhausted |
60|| `demo/unverified` | excluded | Health unknown; protected work cannot use it |
61|
62|The JSON contains the original `TaskSpec`, normalized requirements, the eligible
63|list, every candidate explanation, and a `RoutingDecisionContract` with the
64|selected route and exclusions. No prompt beyond the fixed fixture objective,
65|credentials, provider responses, or raw runtime payloads are emitted.
66|
67|## Verify it
68|
69|```bash
70|python scripts/flagship_demo.py > /tmp/llm-gate-demo-1.json
71|python scripts/flagship_demo.py > /tmp/llm-gate-demo-2.json
72|cmp /tmp/llm-gate-demo-1.json /tmp/llm-gate-demo-2.json
73|.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/test_flagship_demo.py
74|```
75|
76|The test also checks that the selected route and all exclusion reasons remain
77|stable. This is evidence for the deterministic contract/eligibility slice; it
78|is not evidence of provider quality, end-to-end model execution, or production
79|readiness.
80|
81|## Current limitations
82|
83|- This demo is a deterministic fixture, not a live provider or gateway call.
84|- It is valid evidence for contract shape, candidate exclusion reasons, and
85|  reproducibility only.
86|- It is not evidence of OmniRoute quota truth, upstream health fidelity,
87|  end-to-end proxy compatibility, or production readiness.
88|- The repository's broader release acceptance items still call for separate mock
89|  upstream, CLI/client smoke, and filtered OmniRoute verification coverage.
90|", "total_lines": 89, "file_size": 3168, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434794.238576)
```
{"content": "1|# Contract migration (v1)
2|
3|`llm-gate` now emits versioned shared contracts in `schemas/contracts.v1.json`.
4|The JSON is intentionally usable by Python and `llm-gate-node`; unknown fields are
5|rejected at contract boundaries, while arbitrary forward-compatible data is kept
6|under `metadata` only. Never put credentials or bearer tokens in metadata.
7|
8|Each v1 contract freezes `schema_version` to the literal string `\"1\"`. Producers
9|may omit the field and rely on the v1 default, but any explicit non-`\"1\"` value
10|must be rejected by both JSON Schema validation and the Python contract loader.
11|
12|## Python helpers
13|
14|The Python contract module now exposes three migration-safe entry points:
15|
16|- `contract_from_dict(name, payload)` for strict v1 payloads.
17|- `contract_from_legacy_dict(name, payload)` for pre-v1 shapes that still use
18|  fields like `task`, integer `tier`, or `model`/`provider` pairs.
19|- `redact_contract_secrets(value)` for diagnostics and learning events that must
20|  preserve structure while scrubbing credentials from nested text and secret-like
21|  keys.
22|
23|Legacy fields that do not have a first-class v1 location are preserved under a
24|nested `legacy` object inside `metadata`, `signals`, `adaptive_influence`, or
25|`details`, depending on the contract.
26|
27|## From criticality/tier
28|
29|The legacy `criticality` and integer `tier` values remain accepted by the
30|existing `RoutingDecision` API for compatibility. New integrations should put
31|`criticality` in `TaskSpec` as one safety signal, and use `policy_floor` plus
32|`selected_route` in `RoutingDecisionContract`. A tier is not a routing policy:
33|capabilities, privacy, production impact, availability, budget, and latency must
34|also be evaluated. This contract does not implement criticality-first routing.
35|
36|Legacy mapping:
37|
38|| Legacy | v1 |
39||---|---|
40|| `task` | `TaskSpec.objective` |
41|| `criticality` | `TaskSpec.criticality` |
42|| `tier` | `RoutingDecisionContract.policy_floor` (named, not numeric) |
43|| `model` / `provider` | `selected_route.runtime_id` and route metadata |
44|| `alternatives` | `exclusions` and candidate snapshot |
45|
46|Tier-to-floor migration in the Python helper follows this conservative mapping:
47|`0 → isolated`, `1 → protected`, `2 → standard`, `3 → best_effort`, and unknown
48|values fall back to `none`.
49|
50|Streaming, tools, response formats, aborts, retries, and fallbacks are execution
51|metadata and belong in task/tool requirements, route explanations, and outcome
52|events; they must not be silently dropped during proxy forwarding.
53|", "total_lines": 52, "file_size": 2503, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```
