{"content": "1|# Security assurance evidence
2|
3|Evidence snapshot for issue #68, Slice 35.3.
4|
5|`llm-gate` is still an alpha library and local proxy. This document publishes the current threat model, privacy and retention posture, and supply-chain evidence so operators and reviewers can evaluate the project using explicit claims instead of implied guarantees.
6|
7|## Scope and status
8|
9|This is an evidence document, not a certification.
10|
11|What it covers now:
12|
13|- threat model assumptions and primary abuse cases;
14|- privacy and retention defaults that are implemented or documented;
15|- current supply-chain controls visible in source control and CI.
16|
17|What it does **not** claim:
18|
19|- hosted service controls;
20|- formal compliance certification;
21|- production-readiness of every proxy or intelligence slice;
22|- zero vulnerability status for all transitive dependencies at all times.
23|
24|Primary source documents:
25|
26|- [`README.md`](../README.md)
27|- [`SECURITY.md`](../SECURITY.md)
28|- [`docs/specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md`](specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md)
29|- [`docs/specs/ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md`](specs/ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md)
30|- [`.github/workflows/ci.yml`](../.github/workflows/ci.yml)
31|- [`.github/workflows/codeql.yml`](../.github/workflows/codeql.yml)
32|- [`pyproject.toml`](../pyproject.toml)
33|
34|## 1. Threat model
35|
36|### 1.1 Assets
37|
38|The main assets in scope are:
39|
40|- task text and prompts submitted for routing;
41|- provider credentials and operator-owned authentication tokens;
42|- routing policy, model catalog, and availability evidence;
43|- decision logs and privacy-safe learning/outcome records;
44|- local proxy availability and integrity.
45|
46|### 1.2 Trust boundaries
47|
48|`llm-gate` operates across these boundaries:
49|
50|1. **Caller to local proxy or CLI.** Untrusted input may contain secrets, oversized bodies, malformed JSON, or attempts to influence routing.
51|2. **Proxy to upstream provider.** Upstream destinations and credentials must remain operator-controlled rather than caller-controlled.
52|3. **Deterministic policy to adaptive intelligence.** Adaptive ranking may advise but must not override hard safety gates.
53|4. **Runtime state to local logs/events.** Observability must avoid leaking full prompts or credentials by default.
54|5. **Repository to build/install environment.** Dependency and automation changes can alter shipped behavior even when core Python source is unchanged.
55|
56|### 1.3 Security assumptions
57|
58|This repository currently assumes:
59|
60|- operators control the host filesystem, process environment, and network egress rules;
61|- operators decide whether to run with loopback-only anonymous development mode or authenticated mode;
62|- upstream providers enforce their own account, storage, and retention policies outside this repository;
63|- development-mode degraded behavior is not treated as production-ready.
64|
65|### 1.4 Primary threats and current mitigations
66|
67|| Threat | Why it matters | Current mitigation evidence |
68||---|---|---|
69|| Sensitive work routed to the wrong model/provider | Could violate privacy, policy, or capability expectations | README states deterministic policy, privacy, budget, and availability gates run before ranking; release acceptance requires adaptive suggestions to be unable to bypass denial, privacy, capability, or protected-task gates. |
70|| Prompt or credential leakage through logs | Logs are often copied into bug reports, CI artifacts, or benchmarks | `SECURITY.md` states decision logs omit provider credentials, full prompts, completions, and full task text by default; `ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md` requires privacy-safe outcome records with redacted summaries and no raw prompts by default. |
71|| SSRF or caller-selected upstream exfiltration | A malicious caller could force requests to internal hosts or attacker infrastructure | `SECURITY.md` documents URL validation, hostname requirements, rejection of userinfo/query/fragment, private-host blocking by default, fresh DNS resolution checks, and disabled redirects. |
72|| Unauthorized local proxy use | A local or network-adjacent caller could send requests through operator credentials | `SECURITY.md` documents startup requirements for bearer auth or Unix socket unless explicit loopback-only anonymous development mode is enabled. |
73|| Request-size or timeout exhaustion | Large bodies or hanging upstreams can affect availability | `SECURITY.md` documents bounded request size via `LLMGATE_MAX_REQUEST_BYTES` and upstream timeouts. |
74|| Supply-chain compromise through dependencies or CI changes | Compromised packages or automation could alter runtime behavior or leak secrets | CI includes Bandit, pip-audit, and CodeQL; dependencies are declared in `pyproject.toml`; release claims remain alpha and evidence-based rather than implying hardening not yet proven. |
75|
76|### 1.5 Threats explicitly not solved by this repository alone
77|
78|These remain operator or upstream responsibilities:
79|
80|- disk encryption, backup retention, and secure log transport;
81|- endpoint protection and compromise of the local machine running `llm-gate`;
82|- provider-side data retention and training policies;
83|- network segmentation, firewalling, TLS termination policy, and reverse-proxy hardening;
84|- secret rotation, vaulting, and enterprise identity controls.
85|
86|## 2. Privacy posture
87|
88|### 2.1 Default privacy stance
89|
90|The current public posture is privacy-minimizing rather than privacy-eliminating.
91|
92|Implemented and documented defaults include:
93|
94|- deterministic safety gates run before any adaptive ranking;
95|- decision logs omit full prompts, completions, caller authorization, and provider credentials by default;
96|- privacy-safe learning/outcome episodes are designed to store redacted previews, stable fingerprints, and summary metadata rather than raw prompt/context blobs;
9
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022469)
```
{"content": "1|# Benchmark methodology
2|
3|This document defines how to collect comparable measurements. It intentionally
4|contains no latency claim or service-level objective. A result is meaningful
5|only with the commit, Python version, environment, fixture, and command recorded
6|alongside it.
7|
8|## Measurements
9|
10|1. **Contract normalization** — serialize/deserialize representative `TaskSpec`
11|   and `RoutingDecisionContract` payloads.
12|2. **Eligibility** — normalize a fixed catalog/runtime fixture and explain all
13|   candidates, including capability, freshness, quota, auth, and policy
14|   exclusions.
15|3. **Compatibility routing** — run `Gate.route` on a fixed prompt corpus with
16|   logging disabled or directed to a temporary path.
17|4. **Proxy overhead** — use a local stub upstream, measure request handling
18|   separately from upstream response time, and test both non-streaming and
19|   streaming payloads.
20|5. **Adapter failure behavior** — inject timeout, malformed, stale, and
21|   unavailable runtime responses; record state and readiness, not just elapsed
22|   time.
23|
24|Provider network time, model generation quality, and local decision overhead
25|must not be mixed into one number. The proxy's upstream and model results are
26|external variables, so provider benchmarks must identify provider/model,
27|region, request shape, and sampling date.
28|
29|## Protocol
30|
31|- Pin the repository commit and Python version; record OS, CPU, memory, and
32|  dependency lockfile hash.
33|- Use fixed, checked-in fixtures and a fixed policy version for local routing
34|  measurements.
35|- Warm up before collecting samples; report sample count, median, p95, p99,
36|  minimum, maximum, and spread. Report units and whether serialization/logging
37|  are included.
38|- Run enough repetitions to show variance and repeat the complete run at least
39|  three times. Do not publish a single best run.
40|- Separate cold-start and warm-process results.
41|- For proxy tests, use a local deterministic stub and report stub response time
42|  independently from gateway overhead.
43|- Publish raw JSON/CSV alongside a rendered table, plus the exact command.
44|- Treat regression thresholds as proposed until a baseline exists; thresholds
45|  must be justified by repeated measurements rather than copied claims.
46|
47|## Baseline command
48|
49|The reproducible baseline benchmark is a local routing exercise driven by a
50|checked-in fixture:
51|
52|```bash
53|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py
54|```
55|
56|The legacy entry point still proxies to the same harness:
57|
58|```bash
59|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/test_throughput.py
60|```
61|
62|It is not a production performance benchmark and does not measure a provider.
63|The default mode records only local contract, dispatcher, and compatibility
64|routing costs. For a new result, capture the output with:
65|
66|```bash
67|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py \\
68|  --output-json benchmark-results/<commit>.json \\
69|  | tee benchmark-results/<commit>.txt
70|```
71|
72|The fixture lives at `benchmarks/fixtures/reproducible.json` and the report
73|stores its SHA-256 digest so reruns can prove they used the same checked-in
74|inputs.
75|
76|If you truly need a live provider measurement, label it explicitly and opt in so
77|it cannot be confused with the reproducible local baseline:
78|
79|```bash
80|.venv/bin/python benchmarks/run_reproducible.py \\
81|  --allow-live-provider \\
82|  --live-provider openai/gpt-4o
83|```
84|
85|or via the CLI:
86|
87|```bash
88|llm-gate benchmark --output-json benchmark-results/<commit>.json
89|```
90|
91|Before publishing, add the environment manifest and fixture/policy versions to
92|the result file. Do not put API keys, raw prompts, completions, or authorization
93|headers in benchmark artifacts.
94|
95|## Reporting template
96|
97|```text
98|Commit:
99|Date (UTC):
100|Python:
101|OS / CPU / memory:
102|Dependency lockfile:
103|Fixture and policy version:
104|Command:
105|Warm-up / samples / repetitions:
106|Cold or warm process:
107|Logging enabled:
108|Upstream stub or provider (if any):
109|Results (unit, median, p95, p99, min, max):
110|Known limitations:
111|```
112|", "total_lines": 111, "file_size": 3919, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022478)
```
{"content": "1|# Reproducible flagship demo
2|
3|The public demo is intentionally a mock scenario, not a provider integration.
4|It proves the contract and explainability slice without credentials, network
5|access, a running gateway, or a mutable local decision log.
6|
7|## Clean-environment quickstart
8|
9|### Editable-install path
10|
11|From the repository root:
12|
13|```bash
14|python -m venv .venv
15|source .venv/bin/activate
16|pip install -e \".[dev]\"
17|python scripts/flagship_demo.py
18|pytest -q tests/test_flagship_demo.py
19|```
20|
21|### Wheel smoke path
22|
23|This path verifies that the packaged artifact imports cleanly in a fresh
24|environment, without assuming an existing editable checkout is active:
25|
26|```bash
27|python -m pip install build
28|python -m build
29|python -m venv /tmp/llm-gate-smoke
30|source /tmp/llm-gate-smoke/bin/activate
31|pip install dist/llm_gate-*.whl
32|python -m llm_gate.cli --help
33|python /absolute/path/to/llm-gate/scripts/flagship_demo.py
34|```
35|
36|### Optional clean-room env scrub
37|
38|Unset provider variables first if you want explicit evidence that the demo does
39|not depend on them:
40|
41|```bash
42|env -u OPENAI_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_API_KEY \\
43|  python scripts/flagship_demo.py
44|```
45|
46|The command prints one JSON document. The fixture timestamp, policy version,
47|candidate IDs, and ordering are fixed, so repeated runs produce byte-identical
48|output. It does not read environment variables or write files.
49|
50|## What the scenario demonstrates
51|
52|The task asks for `tools` and `structured_output` and is protected. Four fake
53|catalog/runtime rows are evaluated:
54|
55|| Candidate | Result | Evidence |
56||---|---|---|
57|| `demo/frontier-tools` | eligible and selected | Required capabilities, healthy observation, quota remaining |
58|| `demo/no-tools` | excluded | Missing `tools` |
59|| `demo/quota-empty` | excluded | Quota exhausted |
60|| `demo/unverified` | excluded | Health unknown; protected work cannot use it |
61|
62|The JSON contains the original `TaskSpec`, normalized requirements, the eligible
63|list, every candidate explanation, and a `RoutingDecisionContract` with the
64|selected route and exclusions. No prompt beyond the fixed fixture objective,
65|credentials, provider responses, or raw runtime payloads are emitted.
66|
67|## Verify it
68|
69|```bash
70|python scripts/flagship_demo.py > /tmp/llm-gate-demo-1.json
71|python scripts/flagship_demo.py > /tmp/llm-gate-demo-2.json
72|cmp /tmp/llm-gate-demo-1.json /tmp/llm-gate-demo-2.json
73|.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/test_flagship_demo.py
74|```
75|
76|The test also checks that the selected route and all exclusion reasons remain
77|stable. This is evidence for the deterministic contract/eligibility slice; it
78|is not evidence of provider quality, end-to-end model execution, or production
79|readiness.
80|
81|## Current limitations
82|
83|- This demo is a deterministic fixture, not a live provider or gateway call.
84|- It is valid evidence for contract shape, candidate exclusion reasons, and
85|  reproducibility only.
86|- It is not evidence of OmniRoute quota truth, upstream health fidelity,
87|  end-to-end proxy compatibility, or production readiness.
88|- The repository's broader release acceptance items still call for separate mock
89|  upstream, CLI/client smoke, and filtered OmniRoute verification coverage.
90|", "total_lines": 89, "file_size": 3168, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022488)
```
{"content": "1|# Contract migration (v1)
2|
3|`llm-gate` now emits versioned shared contracts in `schemas/contracts.v1.json`.
4|The JSON is intentionally usable by Python and `llm-gate-node`; unknown fields are
5|rejected at contract boundaries, while arbitrary forward-compatible data is kept
6|under `metadata` only. Never put credentials or bearer tokens in metadata.
7|
8|Each v1 contract freezes `schema_version` to the literal string `\"1\"`. Producers
9|may omit the field and rely on the v1 default, but any explicit non-`\"1\"` value
10|must be rejected by both JSON Schema validation and the Python contract loader.
11|
12|## Python helpers
13|
14|The Python contract module now exposes three migration-safe entry points:
15|
16|- `contract_from_dict(name, payload)` for strict v1 payloads.
17|- `contract_from_legacy_dict(name, payload)` for pre-v1 shapes that still use
18|  fields like `task`, integer `tier`, or `model`/`provider` pairs.
19|- `redact_contract_secrets(value)` for diagnostics and learning events that must
20|  preserve structure while scrubbing credentials from nested text and secret-like
21|  keys.
22|
23|Legacy fields that do not have a first-class v1 location are preserved under a
24|nested `legacy` object inside `metadata`, `signals`, `adaptive_influence`, or
25|`details`, depending on the contract.
26|
27|## From criticality/tier
28|
29|The legacy `criticality` and integer `tier` values remain accepted by the
30|existing `RoutingDecision` API for compatibility. New integrations should put
31|`criticality` in `TaskSpec` as one safety signal, and use `policy_floor` plus
32|`selected_route` in `RoutingDecisionContract`. A tier is not a routing policy:
33|capabilities, privacy, production impact, availability, budget, and latency must
34|also be evaluated. This contract does not implement criticality-first routing.
35|
36|Legacy mapping:
37|
38|| Legacy | v1 |
39||---|---|
40|| `task` | `TaskSpec.objective` |
41|| `criticality` | `TaskSpec.criticality` |
42|| `tier` | `RoutingDecisionContract.policy_floor` (named, not numeric) |
43|| `model` / `provider` | `selected_route.runtime_id` and route metadata |
44|| `alternatives` | `exclusions` and candidate snapshot |
45|
46|Tier-to-floor migration in the Python helper follows this conservative mapping:
47|`0 → isolated`, `1 → protected`, `2 → standard`, `3 → best_effort`, and unknown
48|values fall back to `none`.
49|
50|Streaming, tools, response formats, aborts, retries, and fallbacks are execution
51|metadata and belong in task/tool requirements, route explanations, and outcome
52|events; they must not be silently dropped during proxy forwarding.
53|", "total_lines": 52, "file_size": 2503, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022516)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate Flagship 20K-Star Readiness Audit
2|## Evidence snapshot: 2026-07-13
3|
4|**Decision:** Do not promote this repository yet. The concept is differentiated and the current proxy/catalog slice is credible, but the implementation is still alpha and does not yet prove the core product promise: a framework-agnostic, policy-safe, best-appropriate-model gateway with mandatory production intelligence.
5|
6|This document is an implementation planning artifact, not a claim that the project is likely to reach 20K stars. Star count depends on product quality, timing, distribution, and community adoption. The objective here is to remove objectively verifiable reasons a serious user, maintainer, or reviewer would reject the repository on first contact.
7|
8|## 1. Evidence-based current state
9|
10|### Verified strengths
11|
12|- Clean environment checkpoint: 71 tests passing.
13|- Ruff lint, Ruff format, strict mypy, wheel/sdist build, and `twine check` pass in the declared validation environment.
14|- `/v1/models` normalizes rows, applies local allow/deny filters, and labels catalog-only rows as `unknown` rather than claiming they are live.
15|- `/v1/chat/completions` preserves arbitrary JSON fields, tools, response format, usage, errors, and arbitrary streaming byte boundaries in the current mock contract tests.
16|- Upstream authentication is process-owned rather than copied from an arbitrary client field.
17|- OmniRoute is the current upstream abstraction, routed through the loopback filtered boundary where configured.
18|- Public documentation explicitly discloses that managed intelligence, authentication, safe fallback, live availability, and end-to-end evidence remain open.
19|
20|### Verified limitations
21|
22|The following are facts from the current source, not inferred weaknesses:
23|
24|- `llm_gate/api.py` constructs `Gate` directly and calls `gate_instance.route(...)` from the proxy path. There is no `IntelligenceService` boundary.
25|- `llm_gate/neural.py` still contains a historical `~/.9router/db/data.sqlite` path and direct SQLite access. It does not implement the documented Ruflo/RuVector protocol.
26|- `/ready` checks only process initialization and upstream `/models`. It does not check managed intelligence readiness, policy bundle state, or degraded-mode visibility.
27|- Chat requests are always classified with `criticality=\"medium\"`; request policy, protected-task detection, capability requirements, and client override policy are not enforced on the proxy path.
28|- `UpstreamProxy` forwards to a configured URL but does not yet validate SSRF-sensitive URL configuration, authenticate the local caller, implement legal retry/fallback, or attach outcome records.
29|- Discovery swallows all failures and returns an empty list. The model data type defaults `is_available=True`, which is not a safe representation of an unverified catalog row.
30|- Explainability is currently a `RoutingDecision` with a reason string and limited fields. It does not expose policy version, candidate rejection reasons, score components, request fingerprint, safety flags, or outcome identity.
31|- The current test suite does not prove local authentication, SSRF protection, idempotency-aware fallback, protected-task rejection when intelligence is unavailable, redaction before adapters, Ruflo/RuVector command contracts, delayed quality outcomes, or clean install outside the source checkout.
32|
33|## 2. What mature high-star repositories set as the bar
34|
35|The comparison set used by the portfolio benchmark is: FastAPI, Pydantic, HTTPX, LiteLLM, Portkey Gateway, Express, Vercel AI SDK, OpenAI Node, Qlib, Backtesting.py, NautilusTrader, CCXT, Next.js, and Zustand. The exact star counts and snapshot date are recorded in the central portfolio benchmark, not repeated as permanent claims here.
36|
37|A 20K-star attempt must meet the following practical bar, regardless of eventual star count:
38|
39|| Dimension | Mature-repository expectation | llm-gate implication |
40|| --- | --- | --- |
41|| Product wedge | One sentence explains why the project exists and who uses it | Keep “best appropriate model through one stable gateway” as the wedge. Remove competing legacy language about only criticality tiers. |
42|| Installation | Fresh install works without source checkout assumptions | Add a release-candidate install harness, CLI smoke, mock upstream, and a documented OmniRoute path. |
43|| Protocol fidelity | Contract tests cover the real wire protocol, not only happy-path unit tests | Expand OpenAI-compatible models, chat, SSE, errors, tools, JSON mode, images, headers, cancellation, and retry boundaries. |
44|| Safety | Explicit auth, SSRF, secret handling, resource limits, and fail-closed behavior | Make local auth and upstream URL validation mandatory. Make protected requests reject when required intelligence is unavailable. |
45|| Correctness | Deterministic fixtures, property tests, and reproducible failure cases | Add policy and candidate-state fixtures, hard-gate property tests, and redacted evidence artifacts. |
46|| Operations | Health, readiness, metrics, logs, tracing, and clear degraded states | Readiness must include managed intelligence and policy state. Decision and outcome schemas must be stable. |
47|| Performance | Published benchmark methodology and regression thresholds | Benchmark classification, deterministic selection, proxy overhead, streaming throughput, and bounded adapter latency. |
48|| Extensibility | Stable interfaces and adapters instead of private coupling | Define versioned `IntelligenceService`, catalog, availability, outcome, and protocol adapter interfaces. |
49|| Documentation | README, examples, architecture, security, limits, and contributor path agree | Add a five-minute quickstart, production profile, degraded development profile, compatibility matrix, threat model, and demo transcript. |
50|| Community surface | Issues, labels, contribution rules, chang
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022526)
```
{"content": "1|# Codex / Hermes Integration
2|
3|For agents like **Codex Companion** and **Hermes**, which rely on heavily partitioned AgentDB memory state namespaces (e.g., `codex-kalshi-trader` vs `hermes-kalshi-trader`), enforcing strict model boundaries is critical so cheap models don't pollute your Vector store with low-quality insights.
4|
5|### Global Intercept Hook
6|
7|Because these tools are often invoked from inside other programmatic loops, the safest integration is a global executable wrapper.
8|
9|Create `/usr/local/bin/codex-routed`:
10|
11|```bash
12|#!/usr/bin/env bash
13|TASK=\"$1\"
14|
15|# LLM-Gate strict evaluation
16|TARGET=$(llm-gate route \"$TASK\" | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
17|
18|if [[ \"$TARGET\" != \"\" ]]; then
19|    export CODEX_MODEL_NAME=\"$TARGET\"
20|fi
21|
22|exec codex \"$@\"
23|```
24|
25|By hooking this layer, your agentic databases remain populated only by Tier-0 or Tier-1 models when handling critical code reasoning, while formatting tasks silently fall back to Tier-3 models, saving massive schema costs.
26|", "total_lines": 25, "file_size": 1009, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022535)
```
{"content": "1|# Terminal & CLI Agents 
2|*(Kilo Code, pi / Oh-My-Pi, Codebuff, Poolside, Aider)*
3|
4|Terminal-based coding agents execute hundreds of prompts reading configurations, listing directories, and formatting files. Using `opus-4.8` for this is a massive waste of API quota.
5|
6|Wrap these tools in your `.bashrc` or `.zshrc` using `llm-gate route --terse`.
7|
8|**Kilo Code:**
9|```bash
10|kilocode_routed() {
11|    export KILO_TARGET=$(llm-gate route \"$*\" --terse)
12|    kilo \"$@\"
13|}
14|```
15|
16|**pi & Oh-My-Pi (omp.sh):**
17|```bash
18|pi_routed() {
19|    export PI_MODEL=$(llm-gate route \"$*\" --terse)
20|    pi \"$@\"
21|}
22|```
23|
24|**Poolside (pool):**
25|```bash
26|pool_routed() {
27|    pool --model $(llm-gate route \"$*\" --terse) \"$@\"
28|}
29|```
30|
31|**Codebuff:**
32|```bash
33|codebuff_routed() {
34|    codebuff --model $(llm-gate route \"$*\" --terse) \"$@\"
35|}
36|```
37|", "total_lines": 36, "file_size": 794, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022545)
```
{"content": "1|# LiteLLM Integration
2|
3|[LiteLLM](https://litellm.ai/) is the industry standard open-source API proxy. If you run a centralized LiteLLM proxy for your team, you can use `llm-gate` as a custom Python router directly inside it.
4|
5|Because `llm-gate` is a pure Python library, you can import it in your LiteLLM custom routing script to evaluate incoming traffic:
6|
7|```python
8|# custom_router.py for LiteLLM
9|from litellm import Router
10|from llm_gate import Gate
11|
12|gate = Gate(primary_model=\"anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229\")
13|
14|async def custom_routing_logic(kwargs):
15|    prompt_text = str(kwargs.get(\"messages\", \"\"))
16|    
17|    # Zero-network overhead evaluation
18|    decision = gate.route(prompt_text)
19|    
20|    # Overwrite the model before LiteLLM proxies the request
21|    kwargs[\"model\"] = decision.model
22|    return kwargs
23|```
24|", "total_lines": 23, "file_size": 814, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784434937.5022554)

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022564)
```
{"content": "1|# Enterprise Gateways & Proxies (Portkey, OmniRoute, Peezy)
2|
3|`llm-gate` can be used in two modes:
4|
5|1. **Explain-only routing:** call `POST /v1/route` to obtain a model decision, then let another gateway execute the request.
6|2. **Development proxy:** point an OpenAI-compatible client at `llm-gate` and use `/v1/chat/completions`. The proxy rewrites only the selected model, keeps the remaining request fields, forwards server-owned upstream auth, and passes streamed bytes through unchanged.
7|
8|The second mode is an implemented alpha slice, not a production-readiness claim. Local auth, live health/headroom, legal retry/fallback, required managed intelligence, and a configured OmniRoute smoke test remain release gates.
9|
10|## OmniRoute-compatible upstream
11|
12|Configure the upstream explicitly. The default is the local OmniRoute-compatible address `http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1`.
13|
14|```bash
15|export LLMGATE_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1
16|export OMNIROUTE_API_KEY=your-local-key
17|export LLMGATE_PRIMARY=cx/gpt-5.4-mini
18|llm-gate serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
19|```
20|
21|The upstream URL and credential are process configuration. They are never taken from arbitrary client request fields. The client should target:
22|
23|```text
24|http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1
25|```
26|
27|Useful checks:
28|
29|```bash
30|curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/health
31|curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/ready
32|curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/models
33|```
34|
35|`/v1/models` applies the optional exact-match local filters `LLMGATE_MODEL_ALLOWLIST` and `LLMGATE_MODEL_DENYLIST`. Rows include `llm_gate.availability_state: \"unknown\"` unless a future bounded health/headroom adapter establishes stronger evidence. A catalog row is not proof that a provider is live, healthy, or quota-available.
36|
37|## Portkey AI
38|
39|[Portkey](https://portkey.ai) can remain the execution and observability gateway. Use `POST /v1/route` as a policy decision hook when you need a model choice without having `llm-gate` forward request bytes.
40|
41|## Peezy Gateway
42|
43|For Peezy or another OpenAI-compatible gateway, use the same explain-only hook pattern or configure it to send compatible chat-completion traffic to the development proxy endpoint. Verify the exact upstream contract with a mock server before using production credentials.
44|
45|## Scope and verification
46|
47|The normative requirements are in:
48|
49|- [`docs/specs/PRODUCT_SPEC_V0.2.md`](../specs/PRODUCT_SPEC_V0.2.md)
50|- [`docs/specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md`](../specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md)
51|
52|The current proxy contract tests cover unknown request fields, tools, server-owned auth, non-streaming response passthrough, arbitrary SSE boundaries, malformed/oversized bodies, catalog filtering, readiness, and stale content-length handling. They do not constitute live OmniRoute validation.
53|", "total_lines": 52, "file_size": 2729, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022573)

{"content": "1|# IDE Integration (Cursor / Zed / Cline / Roo Code)
2|
3|To integrate `llm-gate` directly into your IDE (Cursor, Zed, Cline, or Roo Code), you want the Fast-API microservice mode.
4|
5|### 1. Launch the Server
6|Run the built-in FastAPI microservice:
7|```bash
8|llm-gate serve --port 8000
9|```
10|
11|### 2. Configure Your IDE
12|
13|`llm-gate` now has an alpha `POST /v1/chat/completions` proxy that preserves request fields and streamed bytes. For production IDE use, continue pairing it with LiteLLM or another execution proxy until local auth, live availability, retry/fallback, and compatibility gates pass.
14|
15|In your IDE AI configuration (e.g., `config.json` for Continue.dev):
16|
17|```json
18|{
19|  \"models\": [
20|    {
21|      \"title\": \"llm-gate Auto-Router\",
22|      \"provider\": \"openai\",
23|      \"model\": \"dynamic\",
24|      \"apiBase\": \"http://localhost:8000/v1\"
25|    }
26|  ]
27|}
28|```
29|
30|*(Note: the native proxy path is an alpha development slice, not yet a 100% production drop-in replacement. See the [release acceptance matrix](../specs/RELEASE_ACCEPTANCE.md) before using it with real credentials.)*
31|", "total_lines": 30, "file_size": 1059, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022583)
```
{"content": "1|# OpenCode Integration
2|
3|[OpenCode] is a powerful open-source coding agent that relies heavily on contextual awareness. To prevent OpenCode from burning your frontier model quota on simple file-reads or git-diff summaries, you can wrap it with `llm-gate`.
4|
5|### Configuration
6|
7|OpenCode supports standard environment variable configuration for its target model.
8|
9|Create an alias in your `.bashrc` or `.zshrc`:
10|
11|```bash
12|opencode_routed() {
13|    local task=\"$*\"
14|    # llm-gate evaluates the first prompt 
15|    TARGET=$(llm-gate route \"$task\" | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
16|    
17|    # Inject into OpenCode's environment
18|    OPENCODE_MODEL=\"$TARGET\" opencode \"$@\"
19|}
20|```
21|
22|This ensures OpenCode dynamically degrades its model for repetitive file operations, but snaps back to `opus-4.8` when the task contains \"architecture\" or \"security\" keywords.
23|", "total_lines": 22, "file_size": 873, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022593)
```
{"content": "1|# Aider Integration
2|
3|[Aider](https://aider.chat/) is one of the most popular terminal-based AI coding agents. While Aider is incredibly powerful, it can consume massive amounts of tokens reading files.
4|
5|You can safely wrap Aider so that it dynamically switches to a cheaper model for simple queries and escalates for complex architecture refactors.
6|
7|### Bash Wrapper
8|
9|```bash
10|# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
11|aider_routed() {
12|    local task=\"$*\"
13|    # Evaluate criticality
14|    TARGET=$(llm-gate route \"$task\" | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
15|    
16|    # Execute Aider with the dynamic model override
17|    aider --model \"$TARGET\" --message \"$task\"
18|}
19|```
20|", "total_lines": 19, "file_size": 682, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022602)
```
{"content": "1|# Anthropic Claude & Agent SDKs
2|
3|If you are writing raw Python or TypeScript using the official Anthropic SDKs (or Anthropic's newer Agent/Tool-Use Frameworks), you can use `llm-gate` directly in your code as a policy function. 
4|
5|This prevents your agent loops from burning `opus` tokens on simple tool iterations.
6|
7|### Python SDK Integration
8|
9|```python
10|import anthropic
11|from llm_gate import Gate
12|
13|# Initialize your Anthropic client and the Router
14|client = anthropic.Anthropic()
15|gate = Gate(primary_model=\"claude-3-opus-20240229\")
16|
17|def execute_agent_loop(prompt: str):
18|    # 1. Ask llm-gate what model is safe to use for this specific step
19|    decision = gate.route(prompt)
20|    
21|    # 2. Extract just the model name (stripping 'anthropic/' if necessary)
22|    target_model = decision.model.replace(\"anthropic/\", \"\")
23|    
24|    # 3. Execute the official SDK call
25|    response = client.messages.create(
26|        model=target_model,
27|        max_tokens=1000,
28|        messages=[{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": prompt}]
29|    )
30|    
31|    return response
32|
33|# This triggers the T0 gate (keywords: 'payment') -> runs on Opus
34|execute_agent_loop(\"Implement the payment confirmation webhook\")
35|
36|# This is a safe task -> offloads to Haiku/Sonnet based on your config
37|execute_agent_loop(\"Format these text strings into a Python List\")
38|```
39|
40|This pattern works identically for the **TypeScript SDK** by querying the `llm-gate serve` HTTP endpoint before building the `anthropic.messages.create()` payload.
41|", "total_lines": 40, "file_size": 1476, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.502262)
```
{"content": "1|# Universal / Agnostic Integration
2|
3|If you need to inject `llm-gate` into a completely unknown, proprietary, or brand-new AI tool, you have two universal fallbacks. 
4|
5|Because `llm-gate` determines the routing policy without needing to proxy the actual bytes, you can inject it anywhere.
6|
7|### 1. The Terse CLI Pipe (For Bash/CLI tools)
8|
9|Every CLI tool on earth accepts environment variables or configuration flags. You can use the `--terse` flag to make `llm-gate` output **only** the model name evaluated for the prompt.
10|
11|```bash
12|# Returns literally just: \"anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229\"
13|TARGET_MODEL=$(llm-gate route \"Review my database schema\" --terse)
14|
15|# Pipe this into ANY unknown CLI tool
16|unknown-ai-agent --model \"$TARGET_MODEL\" --execute
17|```
18|
19|### 2. The Microservice Webhook (For web/backend tools)
20|
21|If the tool doesn't live in a terminal (e.g., a SaaS platform, a Ruby on Rails backend, or a proprietary C++ trading system), run the `llm-gate` microservice.
22|
23|```bash
24|llm-gate serve --port 8000
25|```
26|
27|From any language, fire a standard HTTP POST request before you make your LLM call:
28|
29|```bash
30|curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/route \\
31|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
32|  -d '{\"task\": \"Analyze this CSV file\", \"criticality\": \"medium\"}'
33|
34|# Response:
35|# {\"model\": \"groq/llama-3-8b\", \"tier\": 3, \"escalated\": false, ...}
36|```
37|Extract `.model` from the JSON and pass it to your LLM API request.
38|", "total_lines": 37, "file_size": 1407, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.502263)
```
{"content": "1|# Claude Code Integration (OmniRoute/9router)
2|
3|Want to stop burning your precious `Claude Max` API quota on formatting files and finding regex matches? 
4|
5|You can bind `llm-gate` directly into the terminal tooling of Claude Code by using the `userPromptSubmit` lifecycle hook. Every time you submit a prompt, `llm-gate` evaluates the criticality and intercepts the `CLAUDE_MODEL` environment variable.
6|
7|### 1. Install llm-gate
8|Ensure it is installed in your system path or virtual environment:
9|```bash
10|pipx install llm-gate
11|```
12|
13|### 2. Add the Hook
14|Create the `.claude/hooks/` directory in your project if you haven't already. Create an executable bash script named `userPromptSubmit.sh`.
15|
16|```bash
17|mkdir -p .claude/hooks
18|touch .claude/hooks/userPromptSubmit.sh
19|chmod +x .claude/hooks/userPromptSubmit.sh
20|```
21|
22|### 3. The Hook Script
23|Copy this into `.claude/hooks/userPromptSubmit.sh`:
24|
25|```bash
26|#!/usr/bin/env bash
27|# Claude Code Intercept Hook via llm-gate
28|
29|PROMPT=\"$1\"
30|
31|# 1. Ask llm-gate to evaluate the prompt (returns JSON)
32|# We default to \"medium\" criticality. llm-gate will bump it to \"critical\" automatically
33|# if it detects words like \"auth\", \"payment\", or \"production_api\" in the prompt text.
34|DECISION_JSON=$(llm-gate route \"$PROMPT\" --criticality medium 2>/dev/null | sed -n '/^{/,$p')
35|
36|# 2. Extract the safe model target
37|TARGET_MODEL=$(echo \"$DECISION_JSON\" | grep -o '\"model\": \"[^\"]*' | cut -d'\"' -f4)
38|
39|# 3. Export the target override for OmniRoute / claude-code
40|if [ -n \"$TARGET_MODEL\" ]; then
41|    export CLAUDE_MODEL=\"$TARGET_MODEL\"
42|    echo \"[llm-gate] Routing task to: $TARGET_MODEL\"
43|fi
44|
45|# Pass control back to Claude Code
46|exit 0
47|```
48|
49|### Expected Behavior
50|Now, when you type a prompt in Claude Code:
51|1. `\"Write a new JWT validation function\"` -> `llm-gate` detects the security keyword, sets `CLAUDE_MODEL=\"claude-3-opus-20240229\"`.
52|2. `\"Can you add docstrings to this file?\"` -> `llm-gate` checks headroom and sets `CLAUDE_MODEL=\"claude-3-5-haiku-20241022\"` (saving you roughly 95% of the token cost).
53|", "total_lines": 52, "file_size": 2016, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

{"content": "1|# Mimo Integration
2|
3|When integrating `llm-gate` with Mimo, you want to enforce strict budgetary boundaries for automated development workflows. 
4|
5|### The Wrapper Approach
6|
7|Run Mimo commands seamlessly through our shell interceptor:
8|
9|```bash
10|# Add to ~/.bash_profile
11|mimo_routed() {
12|    local prompt=\"$1\"
13|    MODEL_OVERRIDE=$(llm-gate route \"$prompt\" --criticality medium | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
14|    
15|    MIMO_TARGET_MODEL=\"$MODEL_OVERRIDE\" mimo \"$@\"
16|}
17|```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.502266)
```
{"content": "1|# Antigravity (agy) Integration
2|
3|Google DeepMind's Antigravity (`agy`) is a powerful agentic execution environment. By default, it runs on Gemini or your configured default model. You can seamlessly inject `llm-gate` into the `agy` execution path using a simple bash wrapper or environment variable injection.
4|
5|### The Bash Wrapper Method
6|
7|Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:
8|
9|```bash
10|# llm-gate wrapper for Antigravity
11|agy_routed() {
12|    local task=\"$*\"
13|    
14|    # 1. Ask llm-gate for the best model based on the task description
15|    echo \"⚙️ Evaluating Antigravity task criticality...\"
16|    local target_model=$(llm-gate route \"$task\" --criticality high | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
17|    
18|    echo \"🚀 llm-gate selected: $target_model\"
19|    
20|    # 2. Execute Antigravity with the dynamically routed model
21|    AGY_MODEL_OVERRIDE=\"$target_model\" agy \"$task\"
22|}
23|```
24|
25|**Usage:**
26|Instead of typing `agy \"build a fast API\"`, type `agy_routed \"build a fast API\"`. 
27|`llm-gate` intercepts the prompt, evaluates rate limits and regex gates, and forces `agy` to use the optimal model!
28|", "total_lines": 27, "file_size": 1123, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.502267)
```
{"content": "1|# OpenClaw & NemoClaw Integration
2|
3|For autonomous agent loops like **OpenClaw** and **NemoClaw**, routing is critical. Because these frameworks loop repeatedly without human intervention, a single runaway process on an expensive model can drain hundreds of dollars.
4|
5|`llm-gate` acts as a fail-safe firewall for these agents.
6|
7|### Integration via OmniRoute / Proxy
8|
9|Because OpenClaw and NemoClaw operate via continuous API HTTP requests, the recommended integration is to put `llm-gate` behind an API Gateway like [OmniRoute](https://github.com/diegosouzapw/OmniRoute).
10|
11|1. OmniRoute intercepts the OpenClaw / NemoClaw `/v1/chat/completions` request.
12|2. OmniRoute triggers a fast-webhook to your `llm-gate serve --port 8000` instance.
13|3. `llm-gate` reads the `messages[-1].content` in the payload.
14|4. If `llm-gate` detects safe/trivial polling, it returns a T3 model (e.g., `llama-3.1-8b`). OmniRoute proxies the traffic there.
15|5. If OpenClaw attempts a destructive action or secure refactor, `llm-gate` forces a T0 model (e.g., `opus-4.8`).
16|
17|This creates a bulletproof autonomous execution boundary.
18|", "total_lines": 17, "file_size": 1100, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022678)
```
{"content": "1|# Jcode Integration
2|
3|Jcode is an advanced AI coding assistant that supports recursive multi-agent swarm coordination. 
4|
5|When configuring a Jcode swarm (via `~/.jcode/swarm-prompt.md` or `.jcode/swarm-prompt.md`), you are encouraged to pass a `model` argument when spawning subagents. You can dynamically route these assignments by intercepting the CLI invocation.
6|
7|### The Dynamic Alias
8|
9|Add this to your `~/.bashrc`:
10|
11|```bash
12|jcode_routed() {
13|    local task=\"$1\"
14|    
15|    # Determine the safest model for this specific jcode task
16|    local model_id=$(llm-gate route \"$task\" | grep -o '• Model: .*' | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/\\[.*\\]//g')
17|    
18|    # Run jcode, forcing the model selection
19|    JCODE_MODEL=\"$model_id\" jcode \"$task\"
20|}
21|```
22|
23|Now, typing `jcode_routed \"audit the authentication middleware\"` will instantly trigger an escalation in `llm-gate` (because of the keyword \"authentication\"), returning Tier-0 (e.g. `claude-3-opus`), and binding it to your Jcode session.
24|", "total_lines": 23, "file_size": 978, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022697)
```
{"content": "1|# llm-gate-node
2|
3|<div align=\"center\">
4|  <p><strong>TypeScript and Express middleware for capability-aware LLM request routing</strong></p>
5|  <p>
6|    <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/status-alpha-orange\" alt=\"Alpha\" />
7|    <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/typescript-strict-blue\" alt=\"TypeScript strict\" />
8|    <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/tests-139%20passing-blue\" alt=\"Tests passing\" />
9|    <a href=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate-node/blob/master/LICENSE\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-green\" alt=\"MIT\" /></a>
10|  </p>
11|</div>
12|
13|`llm-gate-node` is the TypeScript middleware implementation in the llm-gate portfolio. It accepts an Express request, classifies criticality, discovers models from a configured OpenAI-compatible upstream, rewrites the selected model, and can forward non-streaming or SSE responses through that upstream.
14|
15|The default local upstream is the filtered OmniRoute proxy at `http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1`. The client framework is not special. Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Jcode, Hermes, an Agents SDK, or a raw OpenAI-compatible client can use the gateway if they can send HTTP requests.
16|
17|## Current status
18|
19|This package is alpha and is not yet a production-ready transparent gateway. The current implementation provides:
20|
21|- heuristic criticality classification;
22|- Zod request and response schemas;
23|- model catalog discovery from the configured upstream;
24|- a bounded fallback ladder for selected HTTP/network failures;
25|- non-streaming and SSE forwarding code paths;
26|- an in-memory score cache used only within the process.
27|
28|It does **not** yet provide a verified Ruflo/RuVector IntelligenceService, persistent learning, reliable live quota/headroom data, full OpenAI field preservation, or a complete adversarial streaming/fallback contract. Those are tracked in the portfolio release board and must pass before publication claims are made.
29|
30|## Architecture
31|
32|```mermaid
33|graph LR
34|    Client[OpenAI-compatible client] --> Middleware[llm-gate-node]
35|    Middleware --> Policy[Criticality policy]
36|    Policy --> Catalog[Configured upstream catalog]
37|    Catalog --> Proxy[Forward and bounded fallback]
38|    Proxy --> Omni[OmniRoute or another compatible upstream]
39|    Omni --> Provider[Eligible provider/model]
40|```
41|
42|The package does not read private `9router` or OmniRoute databases. Quota and headroom integrations must use documented APIs or remain explicitly unknown.
43|
44|## Installation
45|
46|Install from npm once a published release exists:
47|
48|```bash
49|npm install @nickhq/llm-gate-node
50|```
51|
52|For local development from source:
53|
54|```bash
55|git clone https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate-node.git
56|cd llm-gate-node
57|npm ci
58|npm test -- --runInBand
59|npm run build
60|```
61|
62|Publication evidence for the package boundary is gathered with
63|`npm run verify:package`. The command builds and packs into a temporary
64|directory, rejects unintended files, installs the tarball into a clean
65|consumer, imports both public entry points, and type-checks the shipped
66|declarations. Only the built runtime (`dist/**`) plus package metadata and
67|top-level release documents (`README.md`, `LICENSE`, `CHANGELOG.md`) should
68|ship. Development files, coverage, logs, tests, source, workflows, and
69|generated tarballs must not ship.
70|
71|## Quickstart
72|
73|```typescript
74|import express from 'express';
75|import { LlmGateNode } from '@nickhq/llm-gate-node';
76|
77|const app = express();
78|app.use(express.json());
79|
80|const gateway = new LlmGateNode({
81|  baseUrl: process.env.OMNIROUTE_BASE_URL ?? 'http://127.0.0.1:20132/v1',
82|  usageUrl: process.env.OMNIROUTE_API_BASE_URL ?? 'http://127.0.0.1:20132/api',
83|  apiKey: proces...KEY,
84|});
85|
86|app.post('/v1/chat/completions', gateway.middleware(), gateway.proxy());
87|app.listen(3000, () => console.log('llm-gate-node listening on :3000'));
88|```
89|
90|For local development, set `OMNIROUTE_BASE_URL` and `OMNIROUTE_API_KEY` explicitly. The gateway never invents a credential when none is configured.
91|
92|## Public API
93|
94|- `LlmGateNode` and the `LLMGateway` compatibility alias.
95|- `middleware()` for attaching a redacted routing decision to an Express request.
96|- `proxy()` for forwarding the request to the configured upstream.
97|- `OpenAIChatCompletionRequestSchema` and response schemas.
98|- `RoutingDecisionSchema` and its inferred TypeScript type.
99|- Validation helpers under `src/middleware/validator.ts`.
100|
101|## Known limitations
102|
103|- The criticality classifier is heuristic and is not a quality evaluator.
104|- The in-memory score table is not persistent learning and has no Ruflo/RuVector adapter yet.
105|- The upstream model catalog is not proof of live quota or health.
106|- Usage/headroom is fail-open when no documented provider usage adapter is configured.
107|- Streaming forwarding needs broader chunk-boundary, cancellation, and retry-safety tests.
108|- Unknown request fields and tool-call variants need a complete compatibility matrix.
109|- The package has no verified npm release yet.
110|
111|## Verification
112|
113|Run the local release boundary with:
114|
115|```bash
116|npm ci
117|npm run typecheck
118|npm test -- --runInBand
119|npm run verify:package
120|```
121|
122|The current baseline is 139 Jest tests, a passing TypeScript build, and a clean
123|consumer package smoke. The release contract additionally requires:
124|
125|- raw HTTP and OpenAI client compatibility tests;
126|- non-streaming and arbitrary-boundary SSE fixtures;
127|- abort, timeout, retry legality, and upstream error tests;
128|- clean package installation outside the source tree;
129|- security and dependency checks;
130|- independent review of every public claim.
131|
132|Publishing remains blocked until the 
```

10|- **Dependency Sandboxing:** This SDK must remain framework-indepedent. Do not tightly couple code to `NextRequest/NextResponse` unless mapping via an agnostic wrapper, as this is used primarily in Express.

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022726)
```
{"content": "1|<div align=\"center\">
2|  <h1>Backtest Harness</h1>
3|  <p><strong>Monte Carlo Simulation Engine for Prediction Market Strategies</strong></p>
4|
5|  <a href=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness/actions/workflows/ci.yml\"><img src=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg\" alt=\"CI\" /></a>
6|  <a href=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness/actions/workflows/lint.yml\"><img src=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness/actions/workflows/lint.yml/badge.svg\" alt=\"Lint\" /></a>
7|  <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.10+-3776ab?logo=python&logoColor=white\" alt=\"Python 3.10+\" />
8|  <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/numpy-vectorized-013243?logo=numpy\" alt=\"NumPy\" />
9|  <img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/numba-JIT-00A3E0?logo=numba\" alt=\"Numba JIT\" />
10|  <a href=\"LICENSE\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-green\" alt=\"License: MIT\" /></a>
11|</div>
12|
13|---
14|
15|## Why This Exists
16|
17|Traditional backtesters run a single linear equity curve. That tells you what *did* happen, not what *could* happen. This harness uses Numba-accelerated Monte Carlo simulation to generate thousands of parallel equity paths from the same return distribution, answering the questions that matter:
18|
19|- **What is my probability of ruin?** Not a guess. A distribution.
20|- **What does the P5/P50/P95 equity path look like after 250 trades?**
21|- **Does my edge survive after Kalshi's 7% bounded-profit fee or Polymarket's maker-taker spread?**
22|
23|## Features
24|
25|| Feature | Description |
26||---------|-------------|
27|| **Monte Carlo Engine** | Numba `@njit(parallel=True)` for millions of equity paths per second |
28|| **Fee Models** | Pluggable `FeeModel` protocol. Ships with Kalshi bounded-profit and Polymarket flat maker-taker |
29|| **Tearsheet Analytics** | Sharpe, Sortino, Calmar, max drawdown, win rate, total return in one call |
30|| **Walk-Forward Splits** | `split_walk_forward()` yields train/test index pairs for out-of-sample validation |
31|| **Equity Cone Visualization** | P5/P25/P50/P75/P95 percentile bands via matplotlib |
32|
33|## Installation
34|
35|```bash
36|pip install backtest-harness
37|```
38|
39|For development:
40|
41|```bash
42|git clone https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness.git
43|cd backtest-harness
44|pip install -e '.[dev]'
45|```
46|
47|## Quick Start
48|
49|### Simulate 5,000 Equity Paths
50|
51|```python
52|import numpy as np
53|from backtest_harness import MonteCarloSimulator
54|
55|# Historical trade returns (fractions: 0.05 = +5%)
56|returns = np.array([0.05, -0.10, 0.02, 0.15, -0.05, 0.08, -0.03, 0.12])
57|
58|stats = MonteCarloSimulator.simulate_equity_paths(
59|    trade_returns_pct=returns,
60|    starting_equity=1000.0,
61|    num_simulations=5000,
62|    trades_per_sim=250
63|)
64|
65|print(f\"Probability of Ruin: {stats['prob_ruin'] * 100:.1f}%\")
66|print(f\"Median Final Equity: ${stats['median_final_equity']:.2f}\")
67|print(f\"P5 (worst case):     ${stats['p5_final_equity']:.2f}\")
68|print(f\"P95 (best case):     ${stats['p95_final_equity']:.2f}\")
69|```
70|
71|### Calculate Prediction Market Fees
72|
73|```python
74|from backtest_harness import BoundedProfitFeeModel, FlatFeeModel
75|
76|# Kalshi: 7% of gross profit, capped at 5 cents
77|kalshi = BoundedProfitFeeModel(percent_of_profit=0.07, maximum_fee_cents=0.05)
78|fee = kalshi.calculate_fee(entry_cents=50.0, payout_cents=100.0)
79|print(f\"Kalshi fee: {fee:.2f} cents\")  # 3.50 cents
80|
81|# Polymarket: flat maker-taker
82|poly = FlatFeeModel(maker_fee_bps=0, taker_fee_bps=15)
83|fee = poly.calculate_fee(entry_cents=50.0, payout_cents=100.0)
84|print(f\"Polymarket fee: {fee:.2f} cents\")
85|```
86|
87|### Generate a Tearsheet
88|
89|```python
90|from backtest_harness import tearsheet
91|
92|returns = [0.05, -0.10, 0.02, 0.15, -0.05, 0.08, -0.03, 0.12, 0.01, -0.02]
93|stats = tearsheet(returns, periods_per_year=252)
94|
95|print(f\"Sharpe Ratio:    {stats['sharpe']:.2f}\")
96|print(f\"Sortino Ratio:   {stats['sortino']:.2f}\")
97|print(f\"Max Drawdown:    {stats['max_drawdown']:.2%}\")
98|print(f\"Calmar Ratio:    {stats['calmar']:.2f}\")
99|print(f\"Win Rate:        {stats['win_rate']:.2%}\")
100|print(f\"Total Return:    {stats['total_return']:.2%}\")
101|```
102|
103|### Walk-Forward Validation
104|
105|```python
106|from backtest_harness import split_walk_forward
107|
108|returns = list(range(100))  # 100 periods
109|for train_idx, test_idx in split_walk_forward(returns, n_splits=5, train_ratio=0.6):
110|    print(f\"Train: periods {train_idx[0]}-{train_idx[-1]}, \"
111|          f\"Test: periods {test_idx[0]}-{test_idx[-1]}\")
112|```
113|
114|## Architecture
115|
116|```mermaid
117|graph LR
118|    A[Historical Returns] --> B[Monte Carlo Engine]
119|    B --> C[Numba @njit parallel]
120|    C --> D[N Equity Paths]
121|    D --> E[Percentile Aggregator]
122|    E --> F[P5 / P50 / P95]
123|    E --> G[Prob of Ruin]
124|    
125|    H[Fee Model] --> I{Protocol}
126|    I --> J[BoundedProfitFeeModel]
127|    I --> K[FlatFeeModel]
128|    I --> L[Custom: YourFeeModel]
129|    
130|    M[Returns Array] --> N[tearsheet]
131|    N --> O[Sharpe / Sortino / Calmar / MaxDD]
132|    
133|    M --> P[split_walk_forward]
134|    P --> Q[Train/Test Index Pairs]
135|```
136|
137|## API Reference
138|
139|### `MonteCarloSimulator.simulate_equity_paths()`
140|
141|| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
142||-----------|------|---------|-------------|
143|| `trade_returns_pct` | `np.ndarray` | required | 1D array of historical trade returns |
144|| `starting_equity` | `float` | required | Initial portfolio value |
145|| `num_simulations` | `int` | required | Number of parallel equity paths |
146|| `trades_per_sim` | `int` | required | Trades per simulation path |
147|
148|Returns a dict with `
```

{"content": "1|# AI Agent Constraints & Architectural Context (.claude.md)
2|
3|**Target Ecosystem:** Statistical Validation & Portfolio Math
4|**Language:** Python 3.10+
5|**Primary Directive:** Mathematical rigor and Numpy optimization.
6|
7|## Design Philosophy
8|- **NO PANDAS.** We use raw `numpy` arrays (`np.ndarray`) for Monte Carlo simulations. The overhead of DataFrame indexing kills simulation throughput at 10,000+ paths.
9|- **Pure Functions.** The `FeeModel` classes must remain pure.
10|
11|## Safe Evaluation Parameters
12|When generating new simulated distributions or updating the `simulate_equity_paths` method, agents must ensure the returning dict strictly contains `prob_ruin`, `p05_equity`, `p50_equity`, and `p95_equity`. 
13|
14|If generating PRs, add tests utilizing `pytest.approx()` to `tests/test_monte_carlo.py` to bound floating-point instability.
15|", "total_lines": 14, "file_size": 834, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022755)
```
{"content": "1|# Trade Risk Engine
2|
3|[![Build Status](https://img.shields.io/badge/build-passing-brightgreen)](#)
4|[![Fuzzing](https://img.shields.io/badge/fuzzing-hypothesis-blue)](#)
5|[![Coverage](https://img.shields.io/badge/coverage-100%25-brightgreen)](#)
6|[![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.10%20%7C%203.11-blue)](#)
7|
8|A **deterministic, pure-functional capital protection evaluator** for quantitative trading. Designed to run directly inside order-routing hot paths, `trade-risk-engine` verifies trading signals against complex risk parameters in memory, guaranteeing sub-millisecond latencies under strict execution constraints.
9|
10|---
11|
12|## Table of Contents
13|
14|- [Core Philosophy](#core-philosophy)
15|- [Key Features](#key-features)
16|- [Architecture](#architecture)
17|- [Installation](#installation)
18|- [API Reference](#api-reference)
19|  - [State Models](#state-models)
20|  - [Stateless Risk Gates](#stateless-risk-gates)
21|  - [Stateful Desk Controls](#stateful-desk-controls)
22|- [Usage Examples](#usage-examples)
23|  - [Falsification Example (Drawdown Gate rejection)](#falsification-example-drawdown-gate-rejection)
24|  - [Stateful Desk Gates Example](#stateful-desk-gates-example)
25|  - [Telemetry Integration](#telemetry-integration)
26|  - [State Persistence](#state-persistence)
27|  - [Paper Execution Guardrails](#paper-execution-guardrails)
28|  - [Benchmark Report](#benchmark-report)
29|- [Testing & Mathematical Fuzzing](#testing--mathematical-fuzzing)
30|- [Performance Optimization](#performance-optimization)
31|- [Contributing](#contributing)
32|- [License](#license)
33|
34|---
35|
36|## Core Philosophy
37|
38|Traditional trade risk systems suffer from execution drift, concurrency race conditions, and network-induced latency spikes. The `trade-risk-engine` solves these problems by splitting risk evaluation into a pure, mathematical computation layer separate from state management and network I/O:
39|
40|*   **Deterministic Evaluation:** Given the exact same inputs (risk context, portfolios, proposed trade), the evaluation output will be identical.
41|*   **Pure Functions:** The core gates do not mutate any parameters, make network requests, or touch database systems. All validations are done in-memory.
42|*   **Zero-Allocation Design:** Utilizes compiled `msgspec.Struct` formats and turns off garbage collector tracing (`gc=False`) for critical data structures, ensuring minimal overhead and consistent tail latencies.
43|
44|---
45|
46|## Key Features
47|
48|1.  **Sub-Millisecond Execution:** The stateless evaluation path is fully optimized to execute in under a microsecond (typically `~500ns`), staying well within the most demanding execution loops.
49|2.  **In-Memory Continuous EV Checks:** Evaluates proposed trade Expected Value (EV) against minimum thresholds continuously without allocating service objects.
50|3.  **Property-Based Fuzzing Proofs:** Verified using `hypothesis` against 1,000+ randomized property parameters, asserting that no inputs (including `NaN`, `Infinity`, or subnormal floats) can crash the evaluator.
51|4.  **Telemetry & Webhooks:** Built-in support for OpenTelemetry tracing and out-of-band asynchronous webhook emissions to alert trading desks of gate violations immediately.
52|
53|---
54|
55|## Architecture
56|
57|The engine runs a sequential pipeline of risk gates. Crucially, the evaluator separates **stateful desk gates** (which handle manual kill switches, cooldowns, and trade history) from the **pure stateless gates** (which compute mathematical drawdown, concentration, and EV bounds).
58|
59|```mermaid
60|flowchart TD
61|    A[Proposed Trade] --> SG{Stateful Desk Gates?}
62|    SG -->|Enabled| KS{KillSwitch Tripped?}
63|    KS -->|Yes| R[Reject: ERR_KILL_SWITCH_MANUAL]
64|    KS -->|No| TCB{TimedCircuitBreaker Tripped?}
65|    TCB -->|Yes| R
66|    TCB -->|No| CLG{ConsecutiveLossGate Tripped?}
67|    CLG -->|Yes| R
68|    CLG -->|No| PE[Stateless Pipeline]
69|    SG -->|Disabled| PE
70|
71|    subgraph Pure Stateless Pipeline
72|        PE --> EVG{evaluate_expected_value}
73|        EVG -->|EV < Limit| R[Reject: ERR_EXPECTED_VALUE]
74|        EVG -->|EV OK| DDG{evaluate_drawdown}
75|        DDG -->|Drawdown > Limit| R[Reject: ERR_DAILY_DRAWDOWN]
76|        DDG -->|Drawdown OK| CLW{evaluate_consecutive_losses}
77|        CLW -->|Window Limit Met| R[Reject: ERR_CONSECUTIVE_LOSS_LIMIT]
78|        CLW -->|Streak OK| CCG{evaluate_concentration}
79|        CCG -->|Exposure > Limit| R[Reject: ERR_CONCENTRATION]
80|    end
81|
82|    CCG -->|Exposure OK| LBC{Latency Budget Check}
83|    LBC -->|Over Budget| R[Reject: ERR_LATENCY_BUDGET]
84|    LBC -->|Under Budget| APP[Approved: OK]
85|```
86|
87|---
88|
89|## Installation
90|
91|`trade-risk-engine` requires Python 3.10 or 3.11.
92|
93|```bash
94|# Clone the repository
95|git clone https://github.com/quant-desk/trade-risk-engine.git
96|cd trade-risk-engine
97|
98|# Install production dependencies
99|pip install .
100|
101|# Install development/testing dependencies
102|pip install -e \".[dev]\"
103|```
104|
105|---
106|
107|## API Reference
108|
109|### State Models
110|
111|Immutable data structures built for high-performance serialization and evaluation.
112|
113|#### `RiskContext`
114|Captures capital protection policies and latency budgets. Implemented as an immutable `msgspec.Struct` (`frozen=True`).
115|| Field | Type | Default | Description |
116|| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
117|| `max_daily_drawdown_pct` | `float` | `0.10` | Daily drawdown threshold (e.g. `0.10` for 10% maximum loss). |
118|| `max_weekly_drawdown_pct` | `float` | `0.20` | Weekly drawdown threshold. |
119|| `max_correlated_exposure` | `float` | `2500.0` | Maximum currency exposure allowed per contract family. |
120|| `min_expected_value` | `float` | `0.0` | Minimum Expected Value required for a trade to be approved. |
121|| `latency_budget_us` | `int` | `500` | Microsecond threshold for
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022764)
```
{"content": "1|# AI Agent Constraints & Architectural Context (.claude.md)
2|
3|**Target Ecosystem:** Execution Guardrails & Capital Protection
4|**Language:** Python 3.10+
5|**Primary Directive:** Mathematically deterministic risk evaluation.
6|
7|## Execution Rules
8|- Under no circumstances should an AI agent implement a network request or I/O operation inside `/src/trade_risk_engine/engine.py`.
9|- The engine must remain computationally pure. State -> Evaluator -> Boolean.
10|
11|## Floating Point Drift
12|When modifying the kill switch boundaries in `gates.py`, utilize `pytest.approx()` inside the test harness to account for IEEE 754 precision drift. Do not write raw `==` assertions for float evaluations.
13|", "total_lines": 12, "file_size": 680, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022774)
```
{"content": "1|<div align=\"center\">
2|  <h1>Edge Mining Framework</h1>
3|  <p><strong>Agnostic Signal Feature Evaluator and EV Payout Gating Engine</strong></p>
4|  <p>
5|    <a href=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/edge-mining-framework/actions\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/build-passing-brightgreen\" alt=\"Build Status\" /></a>
6|    <a href=\"https://codecov.io/gh/mrnicholasbcarter-code/edge-mining-framework\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/tests-39-passing-blue\" alt=\"Code Coverage\" /></a>
7|    <a href=\"https://pypi.org/project/edge-mining-framework\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/edge-mining-framework\" alt=\"Python Versions\" /></a>
8|    <a href=\"https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/edge-mining-framework/blob/master/LICENSE\"><img src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-blue\" alt=\"License\" /></a>
9|  </p>
10|</div>
11|
12|---
13|
14|The **Edge Mining Framework** is a lightweight, low-latency, mathematical engine designed for prediction market alpha pipelines. It enables algorithmic traders and quant researchers to decouple data ingestion from execution logic. By running live features against compound logical filters and evaluating profitability under friction constraints, the framework strictly gates deployment to maximize Expected Value (EV).
15|
16|Unlike generic backtesting frameworks, the Edge Mining Framework is built from the ground up to support prediction market contract types (such as binary contracts trading in the $[0, 100]$ cents interval) and natively handles transaction costs, exchange fee structures, and Kelly criterion-based capital allocation.
17|
18|---
19|
20|## Table of Contents
21|
22|- [Architecture & Execution Flow](#architecture--execution-flow)
23|- [Scope and Boundaries](#scope-and-boundaries)
24|- [Key Features](#key-features)
25|- [Mathematical Model](#mathematical-model)
26|  - [Expected Value (EV) Formulation](#expected-value-ev-formulation)
27|  - [Kelly Criterion & Position Sizing](#kelly-criterion--position-sizing)
28|- [Installation](#installation)
29|- [Quickstart Guide](#quickstart-guide)
30|  - [1. Scalar Gating](#1-scalar-gating)
31|  - [2. NumPy-Powered Series Logic](#2-numpy-powered-series-logic)
32|  - [3. EV Gate & Kelly Capital Allocation](#3-ev-gate--kelly-capital-allocation)
33|- [API Reference](#api-reference)
34|  - [`FeatureEvaluator`](#featureevaluator)
35|  - [`ExpectedValueGate`](#expectedvaluegate)
36|  - [`EVMetrics`](#evmetrics)
37|- [Anti-Lookahead Safeguard Contract](#anti-lookahead-safeguard-contract)
38|- [Development and Testing](#development-and-testing)
39|- [Contributing](#contributing)
40|- [License](#license)
41|
42|---
43|
44|## Architecture & Execution Flow
45|
46|The framework operates at the execution boundary of your pipeline. The `FeatureEvaluator` first analyzes raw features against static rules. If all rules are satisfied, the output is routed into the `ExpectedValueGate` for financial validation.
47|
48|```mermaid
49|sequenceDiagram
50|    autonumber
51|    participant Pipeline as Trading Pipeline
52|    participant FeatureEvaluator as Feature Evaluator
53|    participant ExpectedValueGate as EV Payout Gate
54|
55|    Pipeline->>FeatureEvaluator: evaluate_compound(features, rules)
56|    Note over FeatureEvaluator: Safe O(1) operator dispatch &<br/>fail-fast compound evaluation
57|    
58|    alt Signal Rules Reject (Short-circuit)
59|        FeatureEvaluator-->>Pipeline: False (Abort execution immediately)
60|    else Signal Rules Pass (Signal Match)
61|        FeatureEvaluator-->>Pipeline: True (Proceed to Risk Check)
62|        Pipeline->>ExpectedValueGate: calculate_ev_metrics(prob=0.55, price=45, fee_pct=0.07)
63|        Note over ExpectedValueGate: Evaluate 55% win probability<br/>vs 7% fee at 45c contract cost
64|        
65|        alt expected_value <= 0 (Negative EV / Friction too high)
66|            ExpectedValueGate-->>Pipeline: EVMetrics(profitable=False, ...)
67|            Pipeline->>Pipeline: Abort Order Placement
68|        else expected_value > 0 (Positive EV / Profitable)
69|            ExpectedValueGate-->>Pipeline: EVMetrics(profitable=True, expected_value=5.83, kelly_fraction=0.12, ...)
70|            Pipeline->>Pipeline: Execute Order (Size bet based on recommended_size)
71|        end
72|    end
73|```
74|
75|---
76|
77|## Scope and Boundaries
78|
79|This package is a pure evaluator and expected-value gate. It accepts features
80|and rules from the caller, computes deterministic decisions, and does not
81|ingest market data, place orders, provide a complete feature library, or claim
82|to be a backtesting engine. Integrations with those systems are explicit
83|adapters so the boundary and provenance remain visible.
84|
85|### Installed backtest-harness adapter
86|
87|`examples/backtest_adapter.py` demonstrates the supported cross-repository
88|boundary using the public `backtest_harness` package API. It deliberately uses
89|an ordinary installed import and never mutates `sys.path` or imports sibling
90|source directories:
91|
92|```bash
93|pip install -e /path/to/backtest-harness
94|pip install -e .
95|python examples/backtest_adapter.py
96|```
97|
98|The example is optional because `backtest-harness` is a separate package.
99|
100|---
101|
102|## Key Features
103|
104|1. **Product Definition (Agnostic Design)**: The codebase is fully decoupled from exchange-specific SDKs (e.g., Kalshi, Polymarket) or market API wrappers. It processes raw dictionary inputs and performs pure-math evaluations, making it highly portable.
105|2. **O(1) Safe Operator Routing**: Implements dynamic function dispatch using Python's native `operator` module (such as `operator.gt`, `operator.eq`). It avoids slow and highly insecure `eval()` or `exec()` invocations.
106|3. **Fail-Fast Short-Circuiting**: Compound rules are evaluated in-order. The engine returns `False` immediately upon the first failed condition, preventing unnecessary com
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.5022783)
```
{"content": "1|# AI Agent Constraints & Architectural Context (.claude.md)
2|
3|**Target Ecosystem:** Alpha Discovery & Signal Evaluation
4|**Language:** Python 3.10+
5|**Primary Directive:** Modular, uncoupled feature gating.
6|
7|## Core Directives
8|- **Feature Evaluator Isolation:** `FeatureEvaluator` is strictly agnostic to where data comes from. Do not import `KalshiClient` or any exchange SDKs into this repository. It evaluates raw dictionaries.
9|- **Fail-Fast Loops:** When evaluating compound rulesets, ensure internal algorithms break and return `False` immediately upon the first failed condition to preserve CPU cycles on downstream feature resolution.
10|
11|## Edge Testing
12|When an AI agent modifies `ExpectedValueGate`, it must create an exact mathematical unit test in `tests/test_ev_gate.py` bounding the theoretical loss limit of the execution against variable exchange fees.
13|", "total_lines": 12, "file_size": 862, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

8|- **Atomic State:** Avoids React context re-render thrashing on fast market moves by using Zustand slices.
9|- **Risk Authority UI:** Real-time visualization of the Python `trade-risk-engine` telemetry.

{"content": "1|# Evey's Hermes Plugins
2|
3|> **Support Evey** — runs 24/7 on free models, hardware costs ~$69/mo: **[Donate](https://evey.cc/donate.html)** (BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP, DOGE)
4|
5|
6|23 custom plugins for [hermes-agent](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent) — built for autonomous operation, observability, cost control, and self-improvement.
7|
8|## Install
9|
10|```bash
11|git clone https://github.com/42-evey/hermes-plugins.git
12|cp -r hermes-plugins/evey-* ~/.hermes/plugins/
13|cp hermes-plugins/evey_utils.py ~/.hermes/plugins/
14|# Restart hermes-agent
15|```
16|
17|## Plugins
18|
19|### Autonomy & Decision-Making
20|| Plugin | What It Does |
21||--------|-------------|
22|| **evey-autonomy** | Core autonomy engine: autonomous_decide, autonomous_plan, autonomous_reflect tools. The brain of self-directed behavior. |
23|| **evey-council** | 3-model debate for hard decisions. Multiple models argue, then consensus is extracted. |
24|| **evey-delegate-model** | Smart model routing with auto task-type detection, 3-retry per model, 4-model fallback chain. Sensitivity filter keeps private data local. |
25|
26|### Observability & Telemetry
27|| Plugin | What It Does |
28||--------|-------------|
29|| **evey-telemetry** | Structured JSON logging of every tool call, delegation, and error. Auto-rotating event log. `telemetry_query` tool for self-inspection. |
30|| **evey-status** | Unified status check — ONE tool call returns bridge + MQTT + costs + cron + goals + time context. |
31|| **evey-mqtt** | Auto-connecting MQTT real-time event stream. Subscribes to bridge/events/health/mother topics on plugin load. |
32|| **evey-cost-guard** | Budget enforcement via Langfuse. Tracks daily/per-task costs. Warns at 80%, alerts at 100%. |
33|
34|### Quality & Safety
35|| Plugin | What It Does |
36||--------|-------------|
37|| **evey-reflect** | Self-correction loop. Critiques outputs with a cheap model before sending. |
38|| **evey-validate** | Hallucination detection. Regex patterns + LLM scoring (0-10). |
39|| **evey-email-guard** | Prompt injection screening. Dual-layer: 20+ regex patterns + local AI classifier. |
40|| **evey-sandbox** | Sandboxed code execution with resource limits. |
41|| **evey-session-guard** | Session lifecycle management and cleanup. |
42|
43|### Learning & Memory
44|| Plugin | What It Does |
45||--------|-------------|
46|| **evey-learner** | Experiential learning — learns from interactions, applies learnings next time. |
47|| **evey-memory-adaptive** | Importance scoring + decay for memories. High-value memories persist, low-value fade. |
48|| **evey-memory-consolidate** | Nightly memory consolidation — extracts facts into MEMORY.md + Qdrant vectors. |
49|| **evey-cache** | Delegation caching — avoid re-asking the same questions to models. |
50|| **evey-identity** | Self-updating SOUL.md — evolves personality based on interactions. |
51|
52|### Communication & Integration
53|| Plugin | What It Does |
54||--------|-------------|
55|| **evey-bridge** | Bidirectional communication with Claude Code. File-based inbox/outbox + MCP server. |
56|| **evey-goals** | Autonomous goal management. Set, track, complete, review goals. |
57|| **evey-telegram-ux** | Telegram UX improvements — message formatting, interruption management. |
58|| **evey-research** | Automated research pipeline with web search and note-taking. |
59|| **evey-digest** | Daily digest compilation from multiple data sources. |
60|| **evey-delegation-score** | Score and rank delegation results for quality tracking. |
61|
62|### Shared
63|| File | What It Does |
64||------|-------------|
65|| **evey_utils.py** | Shared retry logic (call_llm with exponential backoff), HTTP helpers. |
66|
67|## Stack
68|
69|These plugins are part of Evey's autonomous AI agent stack:
70|- Brain: MiMo-V2-Pro (1T params, FREE via OpenClaw)
71|- Free models via LiteLLM (OpenRouter + Ollama + Nous Portal)
72|- 18 Docker services, all monitored
73|- $0/day operating cost
74|
75|## License
76|
77|MIT", "total_lines": 76, "file_size": 3811, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

{"content": "1|# evey-rag
2|
3|Semantic search over Evey's knowledge base via Qdrant.
4|
5|## Requirements
6|
7|- **Qdrant** running at `hermes-qdrant:6333` with collection `evey-knowledge`
8|- **Ollama** running at `hermes-ollama:11434` with `snowflake-arctic-embed2` model
9|- Index populated via `scripts/rag-index.py` (run once, then `--incremental` after changes)
10|
11|## Tools
12|
13|- `knowledge_search` — Semantic search with optional type filter (plugin, skill, config, research, memory, goals, personality, hook, docs)
14|- `knowledge_stats` — Collection stats: total vectors, type distribution, unique sources
15|
16|## Setup
17|
18|```bash
19|# Pull embedding model
20|docker exec hermes-ollama ollama pull snowflake-arctic-embed2
21|
22|# Create collection (if not exists)
23|curl -X PUT http://localhost:6333/collections/evey-knowledge \\
24|  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \\
25|  -d '{\"vectors\": {\"size\": 1024, \"distance\": \"Cosine\"}}'
26|
27|# Index documents
28|python3 scripts/rag-index.py
29|
30|# Incremental re-index after changes
31|python3 scripts/rag-index.py --incremental
32|```
33|", "total_lines": 32, "file_size": 1015, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784434937.502285)
```
{"content": "1|# evey-autonomy configuration
2|
3|The autonomy plugin reads optional instance-specific config from:
4|
5|- `$HERMES_AUTONOMY_CONFIG`, if set
6|- otherwise `$HERMES_HOME/evey-autonomy.json`
7|
8|With no config file it runs on neutral defaults (operator `operator`, timezone
9|`UTC`, no tracked projects). Override any field via the config file or the
10|matching environment variable.
11|
12|```json
13|{
14|  \"operator_name\": \"operator\",
15|  \"timezone\": \"UTC\",
16|  \"heavy_model\": \"claude-sonnet-4-6\",
17|  \"cheap_model\": \"claude-haiku-4-5\",
18|  \"bridge_peer_names\": [\"claude-code\", \"pr-agent\"],
19|  \"projects\": [
20|    {\"name\": \"my-app\", \"path\": \"/path/to/my-app\", \"base_branch\": \"main\", \"importance\": 8}
21|  ],
22|  \"disabled_sources\": []
23|}
24|```
25|
26|## Environment overrides
27|
28|These win over the config file:
29|
30|- `HERMES_OPERATOR_NAME`
31|- `HERMES_TIMEZONE`
32|- `HERMES_AUTONOMY_HEAVY_MODEL`
33|- `HERMES_AUTONOMY_CHEAP_MODEL`
34|- `HERMES_AUTONOMY_CONFIG` (path to the config file)
35|
36|## Project signals
37|
38|Add local git repositories to `projects` and the decide loop surfaces
39|uncommitted changes, non-default branches, and unpushed commits as low-cost
40|signals. Each entry takes `name`, `path`, `base_branch`, and `importance`.
41|
42|## Install locally
43|
44|```bash
45|cp -r evey-autonomy ~/.hermes/plugins/
46|# restart gateway or start a new Hermes session
47|```
48|
49|## Disable noisy sources
50|
51|```json
52|{
53|  \"disabled_sources\": [\"time\", \"projects\"]
54|}
55|```
56|
57|Available sources: `bridge`, `goals`, `projects`, `memory`, `cron`, `time`.
58|", "total_lines": 57, "file_size": 1445, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

{"content": "1|# Nicholas Carter
2|
3|**Senior Software Engineer | Python · TypeScript · Real-Time Systems**
4|North Port, FL (Remote) · [linkedin.com/in/nicholas-carter-dev](https://linkedin.com/in/nicholas-carter-dev)
5|
6|I independently design and ship production systems — from a 57,000-line algorithmic
7|trading platform with sub-second WebSocket execution to LLM routing middleware that
8|dynamically dispatches work across 15+ model providers with tier-based criticality gates.
9|
10|## Portfolio
11|
12|A set of cleanly extractable, production-grade libraries that showcase specific engineering skills.
13|
14|| Repo | What it is |
15||------|------------|
16|| [**llm-gate**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate) | AI criticality router. Mathematically prevents expensive models (Opus) from doing cheap formatting work — saves ~60% API cost. |
17|| [**llm-gate-node**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/llm-gate-node) | TypeScript/Express equivalent of the router with strict `Zod` schemas. Proves enterprise backend-JS competence. |
18|| [**trading-cockpit-ui**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/trading-cockpit-ui) | Next.js dashboard using `Zustand` to surgically render WebSockets at 60fps. |
19|| [**prediction-market-sdk**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/prediction-market-sdk) | HFT Python SDK using zero-copy `msgspec` structs for Kalshi + Polymarket. |
20|| [**trade-risk-engine**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/trade-risk-engine) | Pure-functional drawdown / circuit-breaker engine with a sub-millisecond kill-switch. |
21|| [**edge-mining-framework**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/edge-mining-framework) | YAML-driven evaluation harness accounting for brutal exchange fee-ceilings. |
22|| [**backtest-harness**](https://github.com/mrnicholasbcarter-code/backtest-harness) | High-fidelity Monte Carlo simulation engine with tick replay. |
23|
24|## Core Competencies
25|
26|- **Languages:** Python 3.11+, TypeScript/JavaScript (ES2022+), C#/.NET, Java, Rust (learning), SQL

29|- **APIs:** REST, WebSocket, RSA-PSS auth, OAuth2/OIDC, rate limiting, OpenAPI
30|- **Infra:** SQLite (WAL), systemd, Linux admin, Docker, Azure
31|- **AI/LLM:** Multi-provider routing (15+ providers), Claude Code, 9router integration
32|

34|
