### 🔄 Tool result (1784435599.263687)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|sidebar_position: 11
3|title: Model Catalog
4|description: Remotely-hosted manifest driving curated model picker lists for OpenRouter and Nous Portal.
5|---
6|
7|# Model Catalog
8|
9|Hermes fetches curated model lists for **OpenRouter** and **Nous Portal** from a JSON manifest hosted alongside the docs site. This lets maintainers update picker lists without shipping a new `hermes-agent` release.
10|
11|When the manifest is unreachable (offline, network blocked, hosting failure), Hermes silently falls back to the in-repo snapshot that ships with the CLI. The manifest never breaks the picker — worst case you see whatever list was bundled with your installed version.
12|
13|## Live manifest URL
14|
15|```
16|https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/api/model-catalog.json
17|```
18|
19|Published on every merge to `main` via the existing `deploy-site.yml` GitHub Pages pipeline. The source of truth lives in the repo at `website/static/api/model-catalog.json`.
20|
21|## Schema
22|
23|```json
24|{
25|  \"version\": 1,
26|  \"updated_at\": \"2026-04-25T22:00:00Z\",
27|  \"metadata\": {},
28|  \"providers\": {
29|    \"openrouter\": {
30|      \"metadata\": {},
31|      \"models\": [
32|        {\"id\": \"z-ai/glm-5.2\",         \"description\": \"default\", \"default\": true},
33|        {\"id\": \"moonshotai/kimi-k3\",   \"description\": \"recommended\", \"metadata\": {}},
34|        {\"id\": \"openai/gpt-5.4\",       \"description\": \"\"}
35|      ]
36|    },
37|    \"nous\": {
38|      \"metadata\": {},
39|      \"models\": [
40|        {\"id\": \"z-ai/glm-5.2\", \"default\": true},
41|        {\"id\": \"anthropic/claude-opus-4.7\"},
42|        {\"id\": \"moonshotai/kimi-k3\"}
43|      ]
44|    }
45|  }
46|}
47|```
48|
49|Field notes:
50|
51|- **`version`** — integer schema version. Future schemas bump this; Hermes refuses manifests with versions it doesn't understand and falls back to the hardcoded snapshot.
52|- **`metadata`** — free-form dict at the manifest, provider, and model level. Any keys. Hermes ignores unknown fields, so you can annotate entries (`\"tier\": \"paid\"`, `\"tags\": [...]`, etc.) without coordinating a schema change.
53|- **`description`** — OpenRouter-only. Drives picker badge text (`\"recommended\"`, `\"free\"`, `\"default\"`, or empty). Nous Portal doesn't use this — free-tier gating is determined live from the Portal's pricing endpoint.
54|- **`default`** — exactly one entry per provider may carry `\"default\": true`. That model is the **silent default**: what Hermes lands on when the user never selected a model (GUI onboarding confirm card, `provider` configured with no `model`, empty `model.default`). Read cache-only at runtime (`get_default_model_from_cache`) so hot resolution paths never hit the network; when no cached manifest exists, Hermes falls back to the in-repo `PREFERRED_SILENT_DEFAULT_MODEL` constant, which must match the labeled entry. This lets maintainers rotate the silent default without shipping a release. It is deliberately a capable low-cost model, never the priciest flagship.
55|- **Pricing and context length** are NOT in the manifest. Those come from live provider APIs (`/v1/models` endpoints, models.dev) at fetch time.
56|
57|## Fetch behavior
58|
59|| When | What happens |
60||---|---|
61|| `/model` or `hermes model` | Fetches if disk cache is stale, else uses cache |
62|| Disk cache fresh (< TTL) | No network hit |
63|| Network failure with cache | Silent fallback to cache, one log line |
64|| Network failure, no cache | Silent fallback to in-repo snapshot |
65|| Manifest fails schema validation | Treated as unreachable |
66|
67|Cache location: `~/.hermes/cache/model_catalog.json`.
68|
69|## Config
70|
71|```yaml
72|model_catalog:
73|  enabled: true
74|  url: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/api/model-catalog.json
75|  ttl_hours: 1
76|  providers: {}
77|```
78|
79|Set `enabled: false` to disable remote fetch entirely and always use the in-repo snapshot.
80|
81|### Per-provider override URLs
82|
83|Third parties can self-host their own curation list using the same schema. Point a provider at a custom URL:
84|
85|```yaml
86|model_catalog:
87|  providers:
88|    openrouter:
89|      url: https://example.com/my-openrouter-curation.json
90|```
91|
92|The overriding manifest only needs to populate the provider block(s) it cares about. Other providers continue to resolve against the master URL.
93|
94|## Updating the manifest
95|
96|Maintainers:
97|
98|```bash
99|# Re-generate from the in-repo hardcoded lists (keeps manifest in sync after
100|# editing OPENROUTER_MODELS or _PROVIDER_MODELS[\"nous\"] in hermes_cli/models.py).
101|python scripts/build_model_catalog.py
102|```
103|
104|Then PR the resulting change to `website/static/api/model-catalog.json` to `main`. The docs site auto-deploys on merge and the new manifest is live within a few minutes.
105|
106|You can also hand-edit the JSON directly for fine-grained metadata changes that don't belong in the in-repo snapshot — the generator script is a convenience, not the single source of truth.
107|", "total_lines": 106, "file_size": 4718, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784435608.6798506)

Important correction nailed down on the auto-routing claim:

**`classifier.py` tiers the ENTIRE dynamic catalog, not a fixed 3-model allowlist.** Every model fetched from OmniRoute/OpenRouter's `/v1/models` gets `classify(model_id)` applied. The regex table (T0–T3) is the mechanism — and crucially, anything that doesn't match a pattern **defaults to tier 2 (medium)**, so new/exotic models aren't dropped; they're treated as medium. So the *claim* "only uses Claude opus/haiku/sonnet" is **not** how llm-gate is built — it classifies all of them.

But the *spirit* of your concern is real and I'll say it plainly: **the tier table itself is a hand-maintained static regex list.** When OpenRouter/OmniRoute adds a brand-new capable model with an unfamiliar slug, it silently lands in tier 2 until someone edits `classifier.py`. That's the actual gap — not a hardcoded allowlist, but a stale classifier that needs manual upkeep. OpenRouter's "Custom Classifiers" / routing features are the natural fix, and OmniRoute's smart-routing already does dynamic selection server-side. I'll come back to this in the recommendations.

Also confirmed: **Hermes itself pulls a curated, maintainer-published model-catalog manifest** (OpenRouter + Nous), NOT a live full catalog. So Hermes's picker is curated/snapshot-based — a relevant contrast to llm-gate's full-catalog tiering.

The OpenRouter sub-paths 404'd (their doc URLs use `.md` extensions; quickstart gave me the nav tree). Let me grab the routing-relevant pages using the correct `.md` paths from the index, and read ruflo's intelligence/router docs + OmniRoute smart-routing doc. Batch:

45:### Thompson sampling model router (alpha.5)
47:The 3-tier model selector (Haiku / Sonnet / Opus) is now a **cost-adjusted multi-armed bandit** instead of static thresholds. `hooks_model-outcome` calls update Beta(α, β) priors per tier; `hooks_model-route` samples θ ~ Beta(α, β) and picks argmax. After ~50 outcomes the routing distribution self-corrects against tier overuse — no manual threshold tuning. Cost: 45 µs per route call.
49:### `@claude-flow/neural@3.0.0-alpha.8` — substrate upgrades
51:Six concrete additions to the neural package:
53:1. **Persistence** — `serialize()` / `deserialize()` on `SONAManager`, `ReasoningBank`, `PatternLearner`. Process restarts no longer wipe state.

```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435641.5651343)

{"content": "1|# ruflo-intelligence
2|
3|User-facing surface for Ruflo's self-learning system. Wraps **29 intelligence-related MCP tools** across four families into discoverable skills, commands, and the canonical 4-step pipeline (RETRIEVE → JUDGE → DISTILL → CONSOLIDATE). Coordinates with `ruflo-agentdb` (namespace convention), `ruflo-ruvector` (trajectory recording substrate), and `ruflo-browser` (consumes trajectory hooks for session replay).
4|
5|> **Status:** ADR-0001 implemented. Plugin v0.3.0 targets `@claude-flow/cli` v3.6.x.
6|
7|## Install
8|
9|```
10|/plugin marketplace add ruvnet/ruflo
11|/plugin install ruflo-intelligence@ruflo
12|```
13|
14|## Compatibility
15|
16|- **CLI:** pinned to `@claude-flow/cli` v3.6 major+minor.
17|- **Verification:** `bash plugins/ruflo-intelligence/scripts/smoke.sh` is the contract.
18|
19|## Tool inventory
20|
21|| Family | Count | Source |
22||--------|------:|--------|
23|| `neural_*` | 6 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/neural-tools.ts:195, 312, 413, 539, 651, 706` |
24|| `hooks_intelligence_*` (incl. dispatcher + reset) | 10 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/hooks-tools.ts:2093, 2226, 2296, 2355, 2404, 2556, 2634, 2741, 2952, 3027` |
25|| Routing & meta hooks (`hooks_route`, `hooks_explain`, `hooks_pretrain`, `hooks_build-agents`, `hooks_metrics`, `hooks_transfer`) | 6 | `hooks-tools.ts:884, 1062, 1420, 1499, 1593, 1664` |
26|| `hooks_model-*` (3-tier routing) | 3 | `hooks-tools.ts:3797, 3844, 3879` |
27|| `ruvllm_sona_*` + `ruvllm_microlora_*` | 4 | `v3/@claude-flow/cli/src/mcp-tools/ruvllm-tools.ts:142, 169, 192, 222` |
28|| **Total** | **29** | — |
29|
30|## The 4-step intelligence pipeline
31|
32|CLAUDE.md describes the V3 intelligence loop as four discrete phases. This plugin operationalizes them:
33|
34|| Step | What happens | Tools |
35||------|--------------|-------|
36|| **RETRIEVE** | Pull relevant patterns + past trajectories from HNSW index | `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search`, `agentdb_pattern-search`, `agentdb_semantic-route` |
37|| **JUDGE** | Score retrieved candidates with verdicts (success / failure / partial) | `hooks_intelligence_attention`, `neural_predict`, `hooks_explain` |
38|| **DISTILL** | Extract the key learnings via LoRA / SONA adaptation | `ruvllm_sona_adapt`, `ruvllm_microlora_adapt`, `neural_train`, `hooks_intelligence_learn` |
39|| **CONSOLIDATE** | Prevent catastrophic forgetting via EWC++ | `agentdb_consolidate`, `ruvllm_microlora_adapt --consolidate`, `neural_compress` |
40|
41|For an end-to-end run:
42|
43|```
44|hooks_pretrain
45|  → hooks_intelligence_trajectory-start
46|    → (each step) hooks_intelligence_trajectory-step
47|  → hooks_intelligence_trajectory-end
48|  → hooks_intelligence_learn
49|  → ruvllm_sona_adapt    # DISTILL
50|  → agentdb_consolidate  # CONSOLIDATE
51|  → neural_compress      # storage efficiency
52|```
53|
54|## Cross-project pattern transfer (IPFS)
55|
56|`hooks_transfer` is the substrate plugin's most underused capability. It publishes learned patterns to IPFS (via Pinata) so a different project — or a different machine — can fetch and apply them. Use the `intelligence-transfer` skill or call directly:
57|
58|```bash
59|# Publish patterns from this project to IPFS
60|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"store\", \"patterns\": [...]}'
61|
62|# Fetch and apply patterns from a CID
63|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"load\", \"cid\": \"QmXyz...\"}'
64|
65|# Mirror an entire project's patterns
66|mcp tool call hooks_transfer --json -- '{\"action\": \"from-project\", \"source\": \"/path/to/project\"}'
67|```
68|
69|Prerequisite: `PINATA_API_JWT` (or the equivalent endpoint env vars) must be configured. Without it, `hooks_transfer` returns a structured `success: false` with the missing-config error.
70|
71|## Hook integration
72|
73|Several Claude Code hooks fire intelligence-side writes:
74|
75|| Hook | Tool invoked | Target |
76||------|--------------|--------|
77|| `pre-task` | `hooks_route` + `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search` | RETRIEVE phase |
78|| `post-task --train-neural` | `agentdb_pattern-store` (ReasoningBank) → falls back to `memory_store --namespace pattern` | DISTILL phase, writes to **`pattern`** namespace |
79|| `pretrain` (one-shot) | `hooks_pretrain` → seeds `memory_store --namespace patterns` | Bootstrap, writes to **`patterns`** namespace (plural) |
80|| Trajectory hooks (ruvector substrate) | `intelligence_trajectory-*` | Recorded by `ruflo-ruvector`; consumed by this plugin's pattern-store |
81|
82|> **Pluralization gotcha:** ReasoningBank fallback writes to `pattern` (singular). The `pretrain` hook writes to `patterns` (plural). They are *different* namespaces. See `ruflo-agentdb` ADR-0001 §\"Namespace convention\" for the canonical contract.
83|
84|## Namespace coordination with ruflo-agentdb
85|
86|This plugin defers to [ruflo-agentdb ADR-0001](../ruflo-agentdb/docs/adrs/0001-agentdb-optimization.md) for namespace conventions. Three reserved namespaces are read by the intelligence pipeline:
87|
88|| Namespace | Read by | Source |
89||-----------|---------|--------|
90|| `pattern` | `hooks_intelligence_pattern-search`, `agentdb_pattern-search` | ReasoningBank fallback target |
91|| `patterns` (plural) | `hooks_pretrain`, `neural_train` corpus | distinct from `pattern` |
92|| `claude-memories` | `memory_search_unified` (default include) | Claude Code auto-memory bridge |
93|
94|Do **not** invent new top-level namespaces for intelligence purposes — the convention is owned upstream.
95|
96|## EWC++ consolidation
97|
98|The plugin claims EWC++ consolidation; here's how to actually invoke it:
99|
100|1. **At trajectory end**, call `hooks_intelligence_learn` to register the outcome.
101|2. **Periodically** (or after N task completions), call `agentdb_consolidate` to fold patterns into the long-term store under EWC++ semantics.
102|3. **For SONA / MicroLoRA adapters specifically**, call `ruvllm_microlora_adapt` with 
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435641.575715)
```
{"content": "1|---
2|name: intelligence-route
3|description: Route tasks via the 3-tier model selector and learned patterns; emits a routing rationale via hooks_explain
4|argument-hint: \"<task-description> [--why]\"
5|allowed-tools: mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_route mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_explain mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_model-route mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_model-stats mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_model-outcome mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_intelligence_pattern-search mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_intelligence_attention mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_intelligence_stats mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__neural_predict mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_pre-task Bash
6|---
7|
8|# Intelligence Routing
9|
10|Pick the optimal agent + model tier for a task using learned patterns + the 3-tier router. Emits a `hooks_explain` rationale so the choice is auditable.
11|
12|## When to use
13|
14|Before starting any non-trivial task. Replaces manual agent selection with data-driven decisions.
15|
16|## Steps
17|
18|1. **Get an agent recommendation** — `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_route` with the task description. Returns `{ recommended, confidence, reasoning }`.
19|2. **Get a model tier recommendation** — `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_model-route` for Haiku/Sonnet/Opus selection.
20|3. **Search for similar past patterns** — `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_intelligence_pattern-search` to find prior successes.
21|4. **Predict outcome** — `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__neural_predict` with the task description for a confidence-scored prediction.
22|5. **Spawn the recommended agent** at the recommended model tier.
23|6. **(If `--why` was passed)** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_explain` to surface the routing rationale to the user.
24|7. **After task completes** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_model-outcome` with `success: true|false` to train the router.
25|
26|## 3-Tier Model Routing
27|
28|| Tier | Handler | Latency | Cost | When |
29||------|---------|---------|------|------|
30|| 1 | Deterministic codemod (TS compiler) | ~1ms | $0 | Structural transforms with no LLM: `var-to-const`, `remove-console`, `add-logging` |
31|| 2 | Haiku | ~500ms | ~$0.0002 | Low complexity (<30%), bug fixes, quick patches |
32|| 3 | Sonnet/Opus | 2–5s | $0.003–$0.015 | Complex reasoning, architecture, security, multi-file refactors |
33|
34|When `hooks_route` returns `[CODEMOD_AVAILABLE]` for a deterministic intent (`var-to-const`, `remove-console`, `add-logging`), call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__hooks_codemod` with the intent + file — it applies the transform via the TypeScript compiler at $0, no LLM. Note: `add-types`, `add-error-handling`, `async-await` require judgement and route to a model (Tier 2/3) per ADR-143; they are NOT $0 codemods. Agent Booster is a fast-apply merge engine for LLM-produced edits, not the Tier-1 path.
35|
36|## Recording outcomes
37|
38|Closing the routing loop is mandatory:
39|
40|```bash
41|# Success
42|mcp tool call hooks_model-outcome --json -- '{\"taskId\": \"T123\", \"success\": true, \"model\": \"haiku\"}'
43|
44|# Failure with reason
45|mcp tool call hooks_model-outcome --json -- '{\"taskId\": \"T123\", \"success\": false, \"model\": \"haiku\", \"reason\": \"complexity-misjudged\"}'
46|```
47|
48|The router learns from these calls. Skipping them = no learning.
49|
50|## CLI alternative
51|
52|```bash
53|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest hooks route --task \"description\"
54|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest hooks pre-task --description \"description\"
55|npx @claude-flow/cli@latest hooks explain --topic \"routing decision\"
56|```
57|", "total_lines": 56, "file_size": 3514, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

{"content": "1|---
2|name: cognitive-pattern
3|description: Define and manage cognitive patterns for agent reasoning and decision-making
4|argument-hint: \"<pattern-name>\"
5|allowed-tools: mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_cognitive_pattern mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_workflow_create mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_workflow_execute mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_knowledge_share mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_learning_status Bash
6|---
7|
8|# Cognitive Pattern
9|
10|Define cognitive patterns that guide agent reasoning and decision-making.
11|
12|## When to use
13|
14|When you want to codify reasoning strategies, decision trees, or problem-solving approaches that agents can use and improve over time.
15|
16|## Steps
17|
18|1. **Define pattern** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_cognitive_pattern` with the pattern structure and triggers
19|2. **Create workflow** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_workflow_create` to embed the pattern in an executable workflow
20|3. **Execute** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_workflow_execute` to run the cognitive workflow
21|4. **Evaluate** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_learning_status` to see how the pattern performs
22|5. **Share** — call `mcp__plugin_ruflo-core_ruflo__daa_knowledge_share` to propagate successful patterns
23|
24|## Pattern types
25|
26|- **Decision trees** — structured if/then reasoning
27|- **Chain of thought** — step-by-step problem decomposition
28|- **Analogy mapping** — solve new problems by referencing similar solved ones
29|- **Consensus patterns** — multi-perspective evaluation before deciding
30|", "total_lines": 29, "file_size": 1554, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435641.597748)
```
{"content": "1|# ADR-149 — Per-Model Cost-Optimal Routing (drop the 3-tier abstraction)
2|
3|**Status**: Proposed
4|**Date**: 2026-06-15
5|**Related**: ADR-026 (3-tier model routing), ADR-074 (observable-not-inferred), ADR-086 (graceful-degradation), ADR-124 (optional native dependencies), ADR-142 (per-task bandit priors), ADR-148 (cost-optimal router lifecycle via `@metaharness/router`), #2334 (Option B), #2329
6|**External reference**: [`ruvnet/agent-harness-generator` ADR-040 — DRACO routing finding](https://github.com/ruvnet/agent-harness-generator) — the productized methodology that `@metaharness/router` exposes.
7|
8|## Context
9|
10|ADR-148 wired the cost-optimal router (`@metaharness/router` + optional `@ruvector/tiny-dancer` FastGRNN backend) behind a 3-tier abstraction (`haiku` / `sonnet` / `opus`) and a per-tier OpenRouter alternate. That was the right Phase A — small, reversible, byte-identical default — but it forecloses real Pareto wins that the bench evidence now makes visible.
11|
12|### What the measurements showed
13|
14|Two real benches landed in this branch (`feat/2334-metaharness-router-integration`):
15|
16|**Cheap-tier, N=45 (variance-corrected), measured 2026-06-15** (`docs/benchmarks/runs/cheap-models-2026-06-15-20-3*.json`):
17|
18|| Model | Pass | Latency | $/1k passes |
19||---|---|---|---|
20|| `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash` | 100% | 684 ± 104 ms | $0.001 |
21|| `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` (control) | 100% | 1022 ± 226 ms | **$0.151** |
22|
23|→ **Haiku is 151× more expensive than Ling for the same pass rate**, and 1.5× slower.
24|
25|**Mid-tier, LLM-judged 12-row corpus, measured 2026-06-15** (`docs/benchmarks/runs/midtier-models-2026-06-15-20-53-55Z.json`):
26|
27|| Model | Avg score | $/run | $/quality |
28||---|---|---|---|
29|| `openai/gpt-4.1` | **81.0%** | $0.030 | $0.037 |
30|| `google/gemini-2.5-flash` | 76.7% | $0.014 | $0.018 |
31|| `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` (control) | 76.7% | $0.112 | $0.145 |
32|| `meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` | 69.6% | $0.001 | **$0.002** |
33|
34|→ **GPT-4.1 outscores Sonnet 4.6 at 4× less cost.** Llama 3.3 70B is the $/quality Pareto leader by **70×** — it delivers 91% of Sonnet's quality at 0.9% of Sonnet's price.
35|
36|### Why the 3-tier abstraction wastes these wins
37|
38|ADR-148's `openrouter-alts.json` maps each Claude tier to **one** OpenRouter alternate. The routing math (bandit + hybrid neural prior) operates on three tier labels. This has three structural problems:
39|
40|1. **Tier-internal alternates are invisible to the router.** A user who wants the $/quality leader (Llama 3.3 70B) for sonnet-routed traffic has to hand-edit the JSON — the router itself has no way to consider Llama as a sonnet alternate vs. GPT-4.1 at runtime.
41|2. **Cross-tier alternates are foreclosed.** For a task at the cheap/sonnet boundary, the optimal pick might be Llama 3.3 70B ($0.001/run, 70% quality on mid-tier). The bandit-then-tier-map routes that task to either `haiku→Ling` (potentially under-capable on a real mid-tier query) or `sonnet→GPT-4.1` (3× the cost). There is no path that picks Llama-the-cross-tier-option.
42|3. **The bandit cannot distinguish models within a tier.** Beta(α, β) per `ClaudeModel` aggregates outcomes for GPT-4.1 and Claude Sonnet under one `sonnet` prior. Online learning can never separate them — the structure throws away the signal.
43|
44|### The DRACO finding (external reference)
45|
46|`ruvnet/agent-harness-generator` ADR-040 found that for cross-domain agent work, **structure/fusion does not beat a strong model on quality, but routing each query to the *right, cheapest* model is a measured Pareto win**. A learned embedding router with `(query embedding → quality each model achieved)` examples beats the best fixed model on the DRACO benchmark, and its accuracy rises monotonically with training-data size (the DRACO \"learning curve\").
47|
48|`@metaharness/router` is the productized form of that finding. Its `Router.fromExamples(rows, prices, { qualityBar })` is *literally* a per-model cost-optimal selector — given measured `{embedding, scores: {model_id: quality}}` rows and a per-model price table, it returns the cheapest model predicted to clear the bar. We are currently using a fraction of its capability.
49|
50|### The Phase 1 seed corpus is unmeasured
51|
52|`v3/@claude-flow/cli/assets/model-router/seed-rows.json` carries `scores: { haiku: 0.94, sonnet: 0.92, opus: 0.93 }` — **hand-coded, not measured.** The bundled KRR artifact was fit to these assumptions, not to real model behavior. The 100% accuracy on `scripts/benchmark-router.mjs` is a property of the synthetic corpus, not real-world routing fidelity.
53|
54|## Decision
55|
56|Adopt **per-model cost-optimal routing** end-to-end:
57|
58|### 1. Drop `ClaudeModel` from the routing-math interior
59|
60|The public `ModelRoutingResult.model` field stays `ClaudeModel` for backwards compatibility with all existing consumers, but the routing computation operates on `ModelId = string`. A canonical-model registry maps the tier label to the underlying string id when callers need it, but the bandit, the neural prior, the seed-corpus scores, and `@metaharness/router`'s `Router` all operate on the string id throughout.
61|
62|`ModelRoutingResult` gains:
63|- `modelId: string` — the *concrete* picked model (e.g., `openai/gpt-4.1`, `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash`, `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`)
64|- Existing `model: ClaudeModel` is derived from `modelId` via the registry (the closest tier — preserves Anthropic-API consumers that still expect `'haiku' | 'sonnet' | 'opus'`)
65|
66|### 2. Candidate registry replaces per-tier alts
67|
68|`assets/model-router/openrouter-alts.json` is replaced by `assets/model-router/candidates.json` — a flat list of candidate models with their measured stats, costs, and provider mapping:
69|
70|```json
71|{
72|  \"candidates\": [
73|    { \"id\": \"anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5\",
74|      \"tier_label\": \"haiku\",
75|      \"p
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435641.607484)
```
{"content": "1|# LLM Router
2|
3|Chat UI includes an intelligent routing system that automatically selects the best model for each request. When enabled, users see a virtual \"Omni\" model that routes to specialized models based on the conversation context.
4|
5|The router uses [katanemo/Arch-Router-1.5B](https://huggingface.co/katanemo/Arch-Router-1.5B) for route selection.
6|
7|## Configuration
8|
9|### Basic Setup
10|
11|```ini
12|# Arch router endpoint (OpenAI-compatible)
13|LLM_ROUTER_ARCH_BASE_URL=https://router.huggingface.co/v1
14|LLM_ROUTER_ARCH_MODEL=katanemo/Arch-Router-1.5B
15|
16|# Path to your routes policy JSON
17|LLM_ROUTER_ROUTES_PATH=./config/routes.json
18|```
19|
20|### Routes Policy
21|
22|Create a JSON file defining your routes. Each route specifies:
23|
24|```json
25|[
26|	{
27|		\"name\": \"coding\",
28|		\"description\": \"Programming, debugging, code review\",
29|		\"primary_model\": \"Qwen/Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct\",
30|		\"fallback_models\": [\"meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct\"]
31|	},
32|	{
33|		\"name\": \"casual_conversation\",
34|		\"description\": \"General chat, questions, explanations\",
35|		\"primary_model\": \"meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct\"
36|	}
37|]
38|```
39|
40|### Fallback Behavior
41|
42|```ini
43|# Route to use when Arch returns \"other\"
44|LLM_ROUTER_OTHER_ROUTE=casual_conversation
45|
46|# Model to use if Arch selection fails entirely
47|LLM_ROUTER_FALLBACK_MODEL=meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
48|
49|# Selection timeout (milliseconds)
50|LLM_ROUTER_ARCH_TIMEOUT_MS=10000
51|```
52|
53|## Multimodal Routing
54|
55|When a user sends an image, the router can bypass Arch and route directly to a vision model:
56|
57|```ini
58|LLM_ROUTER_ENABLE_MULTIMODAL=true
59|LLM_ROUTER_MULTIMODAL_MODEL=meta-llama/Llama-3.2-90B-Vision-Instruct
60|```
61|
62|## Tools Routing
63|
64|When a user has MCP servers enabled, the router can automatically select a tools-capable model:
65|
66|```ini
67|LLM_ROUTER_ENABLE_TOOLS=true
68|LLM_ROUTER_TOOLS_MODEL=meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
69|```
70|
71|## UI Customization
72|
73|Customize how the router appears in the model selector:
74|
75|```ini
76|PUBLIC_LLM_ROUTER_ALIAS_ID=omni
77|PUBLIC_LLM_ROUTER_DISPLAY_NAME=Omni
78|PUBLIC_LLM_ROUTER_LOGO_URL=https://example.com/logo.png
79|```
80|
81|## How It Works
82|
83|When a user selects Omni:
84|
85|1. Chat UI sends the conversation context to the Arch router
86|2. Arch analyzes the content and returns a route name
87|3. Chat UI maps the route to the corresponding model
88|4. The request streams from the selected model
89|5. On errors, fallback models are tried in order
90|
91|The route selection is displayed in the UI so users can see which model was chosen.
92|
93|## Message Length Limits
94|
95|To optimize router performance, message content is trimmed before sending to Arch:
96|
97|```ini
98|# Max characters for assistant messages (default: 500)
99|LLM_ROUTER_MAX_ASSISTANT_LENGTH=500
100|
101|# Max characters for previous user messages (default: 400)
102|LLM_ROUTER_MAX_PREV_USER_LENGTH=400
103|```
104|
105|The latest user message is never trimmed.
106|", "total_lines": 105, "file_size": 2782, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

62|### Custom Headers
63|
64|| Header                   | Direction | Description                                                                                                                  |
65|| ------------------------ | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
66|| `X-OmniRoute-No-Cache`   | Request   | Set to `true` to bypass cache                                                                                                |
67|| `x-omniroute-no-memory`  | Request   | Set to `true` to skip memory + skills injection for this request (mirrors no-cache; avoids the per-call token/cost overhead) |
68|| `X-OmniRoute-Progress`   | Request   | Set to `true` for progress events                                                                                            |
69|| `X-Session-Id`           | Request   | Sticky session key for external session affinity                                                                             |
70|| `x_session_id`           | Request   | Underscore variant also accepted (direct HTTP)                                                                               |
71|| `Idempotency-Key`        | Request   | Dedup key (5s window)                                                                                                        |
72|| `X-Request-Id`           | Request   | Alternative dedup key                                                                                                        |

74|| `X-OmniRoute-Idempotent` | Response  | `true` if deduplicated                                                                                                       |
75|| `X-OmniRoute-Progress`   | Response  | `enabled` if progress tracking on                                                                                            |
76|| `X-OmniRoute-Session-Id` | Response  | Effective session ID used by OmniRoute                                                                                       |
77|| `X-OmniRoute-Request-Id` | Response  | Request correlation id (when known)                                                                                          |
78|| `X-OmniRoute-Version`    | Response  | OmniRoute build version (alway

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435649.7346141)

{"content": "1|# SONA Integration - Quick Start
2|
3|## Installation
4|
5|Already installed: `@ruvector/sona@0.1.5`
6|
7|## Basic Usage (30 seconds)
8|
9|```typescript
10|import { createSONALearningEngine, getModeConfig } from '@claude-flow/neural';
11|
12|// 1. Create engine
13|const sona = createSONALearningEngine('balanced', getModeConfig('balanced'));
14|
15|// 2. Learn from trajectory
16|await sona.learn({
17|  trajectoryId: 'traj-001',
18|  context: 'Implement authentication',
19|  domain: 'code',
20|  steps: [/* ... */],
21|  qualityScore: 0.88,
22|  isComplete: true,
23|  startTime: Date.now(),
24|});
25|
26|// 3. Adapt to context
27|const adapted = await sona.adapt({
28|  domain: 'code',
29|  queryEmbedding: new Float32Array(768).fill(0.1),
30|});
31|
32|console.log(`Suggested: ${adapted.suggestedRoute}`);
33|console.log(`Confidence: ${adapted.confidence}`);
34|```
35|
36|## Key Methods
37|
38|```typescript
39|// Learning
40|await sona.learn(trajectory);              // Learn from trajectory (<0.05ms)
41|console.log(sona.getLearningTime());       // Get learning time
42|
43|// Adaptation
44|const result = await sona.adapt(context);  // Adapt behavior (<0.1ms)
45|console.log(sona.getAdaptationTime());     // Get adaptation time
46|
47|// Patterns
48|const patterns = sona.findPatterns(emb, 5); // Find similar patterns
49|
50|// Statistics
51|const stats = sona.getStats();             // Get engine stats
52|console.log(`Patterns: ${stats.patternsLearned}`);
53|
54|// Control
55|sona.forceLearning();                      // Force learning cycle
56|sona.tick();                               // Background learning
57|sona.setEnabled(false);                    // Disable learning
58|```
59|
60|## Learning Modes
61|
62|```typescript
63|// Real-time: Fastest (<0.05ms)
64|createSONALearningEngine('real-time', getModeConfig('real-time'));
65|
66|// Balanced: Default (1ms)
67|createSONALearningEngine('balanced', getModeConfig('balanced'));
68|
69|// Research: Highest quality (10ms)
70|createSONALearningEngine('research', getModeConfig('research'));
71|
72|// Edge: Resource-limited (50MB)
73|createSONALearningEngine('edge', getModeConfig('edge'));
74|
75|// Batch: Large-scale (1GB)
76|createSONALearningEngine('batch', getModeConfig('batch'));
77|```
78|
79|## Performance Targets
80|
81|| Operation | Target | Achieved |
82||-----------|--------|----------|
83|| Learning  | <0.05ms | ~0.03ms ✓ |
84|| Adaptation | <0.1ms | ~0.06ms ✓ |
85|| Pattern search | <1ms | ~0.05ms ✓ |
86|
87|## Examples
88|
89|Run comprehensive examples:
90|```bash
91|cd v3/@claude-flow/neural
92|npx tsx examples/sona-usage.ts
93|```
94|
95|## Documentation
96|
97|- **Full Guide**: `/docs/SONA_INTEGRATION.md`
98|- **Summary**: `/SONA_INTEGRATION_SUMMARY.md`
99|- **Examples**: `/examples/sona-usage.ts`
100|
101|## Common Patterns
102|
103|### Pattern 1: Learn and Adapt
104|```typescript
105|// Learn from multiple trajectories
106|for (const traj of trajectories) {
107|  await sona.learn(traj);
108|}
109|
110|// Adapt to new context
111|const adapted = await sona.adapt(context);
112|```
113|
114|### Pattern 2: Performance Monitoring
115|```typescript
116|await sona.learn(trajectory);
117|console.log(`Learning: ${sona.getLearningTime()}ms`);
118|
119|const adapted = await sona.adapt(context);
120|console.log(`Adaptation: ${sona.getAdaptationTime()}ms`);
121|```
122|
123|### Pattern 3: Pattern Discovery
124|```typescript
125|// Force learning
126|sona.forceLearning();
127|
128|// Find patterns
129|const patterns = sona.findPatterns(query, 5);
130|patterns.forEach(p => {
131|  console.log(`Quality: ${p.avgQuality}`);
132|});
133|```
134|
135|## Quick Tips
136|
137|1. Use `'real-time'` mode for interactive apps
138|2. Use `'balanced'` mode for general purpose
139|3. Use `'research'` mode for high quality
140|4. Use `'edge'` mode for resource-limited devices
141|5. Call `tick()` periodically for background learning
142|6. Monitor `getStats()` for performance insights
143|
144|## Files Created
145|
146|```
147|v3/@claude-flow/neural/
148|├── src/sona-integration.ts          (432 lines)
149|├── docs/SONA_INTEGRATION.md         (460 lines)
150|├── examples/sona-usage.ts           (318 lines)
151|└── SONA_INTEGRATION_SUMMARY.md      (summary)
152|```
153|
154|## Next Steps
155|
156|1. Read full documentation: `/docs/SONA_INTEGRATION.md`
157|2. Run examples: `npx tsx examples/sona-usage.ts`
158|3. Integrate into your code
159|4. Monitor performance with `getStats()`
160|5. Tune mode based on your needs
161|
162|---
163|
164|**Location**: `/workspaces/claude-flow/v3/@claude-flow/neural/`
165|
166|**Package**: `@ruvector/sona@0.1.5`
167|
168|**Performance**: <0.05ms learning target achieved
169|", "total_lines": 168, "file_size": 4185, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435656.6960564)
```
{"content": "1|# Auto-Combo: Let OmniRoute Pick the Best AI for You
2|
3|> **TL;DR**: Set your model to `auto` and OmniRoute automatically picks the best AI provider for each request. No configuration needed.
4|
5|---
6|
7|## What It Does
8|
9|Instead of choosing a specific AI model (like GPT-4o or Claude), you can let OmniRoute **automatically pick the best one** for each request. It considers:
10|
11|- **Health** — Is the provider working right now?
12|- **Speed** — How fast is it?
13|- **Cost** — How much does it cost?
14|- **Quality** — Is it good at this type of task?
15|- **Capacity** — Does it have quota remaining?
16|
17|OmniRoute scores all your connected providers and picks the best one. If it fails, it automatically tries the next one.
18|
19|---
20|
21|## Quick Start
22|
23|**Step 1**: Set your model to `auto` in your IDE or CLI:
24|
25|```
26|model: \"auto\"
27|```
28|
29|**Step 2**: That's it! OmniRoute handles the rest.
30|
31|**Step 3** (optional): Use a variant for specific tasks:
32|
33|```
34|model: \"auto/coding\"    # Best for code
35|model: \"auto/fast\"      # Fastest response
36|model: \"auto/cheap\"     # Cheapest option
37|```
38|
39|---
40|
41|## Which \"auto\" Should I Use?
42|
43|| If you want... | Use this | Best for | How it works |
44||----------------|----------|----------|--------------|
45|| **Best overall** | `auto` | General questions, chat | Balances speed, cost, and quality |
46|| **Best code** | `auto/coding` | Writing code, debugging | Picks models good at coding tasks |
47|| **Fastest response** | `auto/fast` | Quick answers, low latency | Prioritizes speed over everything |
48|| **Cheapest option** | `auto/cheap` | Saving money | Picks the cheapest provider |
49|| **Smartest model** | `auto/smart` | Complex tasks | Quality-first + explores new models |
50|| **Most available** | `auto/offline` | When providers are busy | Picks providers with most capacity |
51|
52|### Examples
53|
54|```bash
55|# General chat — balanced
56|curl http://localhost:20128/v1/chat/completions \\
57|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
58|  -d '{\"model\":\"auto\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hello!\"}]}'
59|
60|# Code generation — quality-first
61|curl http://localhost:20128/v1/chat/completions \\
62|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
63|  -d '{\"model\":\"auto/coding\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Write a Python function\"}]}'
64|
65|# Quick answer — speed-first
66|curl http://localhost:20128/v1/chat/completions \\
67|  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\
68|  -d '{\"model\":\"auto/fast\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"What is 2+2?\"}]}'
69|```
70|
71|---
72|
73|## How It Works (Simple Version)
74|
75|When you send a request with `model: \"auto\"`, OmniRoute:
76|
77|1. **Looks at all your connected providers** — Every provider you've added (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.)
78|2. **Scores each one** on 5 factors:
79|   - Is it working? (health)
80|   - Does it have capacity? (quota)
81|   - How much does it cost? (price)
82|   - How fast is it? (speed)
83|   - Is it good at this task? (quality)
84|3. **Picks the best one** — The highest-scoring provider gets your request
85|4. **Auto-recovers** — If it fails, OmniRoute tries the next one automatically
86|
87|### The Scoring System
88|
89|Each provider gets a score from 0 to 1. The higher the score, the better the fit.
90|
91|| Factor | Weight | What it means |
92||--------|--------|---------------|
93|| Health | 20% | Is the provider working? (circuit breaker state) |
94|| Quota | 15% | Does it have capacity remaining? |
95|| Cost | 15% | How expensive is it? (cheaper = higher score) |
96|| Speed | 12% | How fast is it? (lower latency = higher score) |
97|| Task Fit | 8% | Is it good at this type of task? |
98|| Stability | 5% | Is it consistent? (low error rate) |
99|| Tier | 5% | Account tier (Ultra > Pro > Free) |
100|| Other | 20% | Context affinity, connection density, etc. |
101|
102|### How Variants Change the Scoring
103|
104|Each variant uses different weights:
105|
106|| Variant | Prioritizes | Key Weights |
107||---------|-------------|-------------|
108|| `auto` | Balanced | health=20%, quota=15%, cost=15% |
109|| `auto/coding` | Quality | taskFit=37%, stability=15% |
110|| `auto/fast` | Speed | latency=32%, health=28% |
111|| `auto/cheap` | Cost | cost=37% |
112|| `auto/smart` | Quality + Explore | taskFit=37%, exploration=10% |
113|| `auto/offline` | Capacity | quota=37%, health=28% |
114|
115|---
116|
117|## How It Handles Failures
118|
119|OmniRoute has **three layers of protection**:
120|
121|### 1. Auto-Fallback
122|If the best provider fails, OmniRoute automatically tries the next one. You don't need to do anything.
123|
124|### 2. Self-Healing
125|If a provider keeps failing:
126|- **Score < 0.2** → Excluded for 5 minutes
127|- **Circuit breaker open** → Auto-excluded
128|- **More than 50% providers down** → Incident mode (no exploration)
129|
130|### 3. Emergency Fallback
131|If all providers fail, OmniRoute routes to stable free providers (like Kiro or Qoder) as a last resort.
132|
133|---
134|
135|## Multi-Account Support
136|
137|If you have multiple accounts for the same provider (e.g., two OpenAI keys), OmniRoute treats each as a **separate candidate**. This means:
138|
139|- Account A has quota remaining → use it
140|- Account B is rate-limited → skip it
141|- Account C is cheaper → prefer it
142|
143|Each account is scored independently based on its own health, quota, and speed.
144|
145|---
146|
147|## Bandit Exploration
148|
149|OmniRoute occasionally **explores** new providers to discover better options:
150|
151|- **Default**: 5% of requests go to random providers
152|- **Auto/smart**: 10% exploration rate
153|- **Disabled** when more than 50% of providers are unhealthy
154|
155|This helps OmniRoute learn which providers work best for your usage patterns.
156|
157|---
158|
159|## Common Questions
160|
161|### \"Will i
```

FULL SIMULTANEOUS UNDERSTANDING — what I've now actually read (first-party, excluding node_modules noise):

REPOS (all read end-to-end):
- llm-gate (Python flagship) — AGENTS/CLAUDE/README/SPEC + all docs/specs/integrations/ops, plus the actual source: classifier.py, router.py, availability.py, omniroute.py, probes.py, api.py, intelligence.py, gate.py, catalog.py

- ruvector — README + @ruvector/* READMEs from the installed package, plus /tmp/ruvector Rust crate tree
- omniroute — /tmp/OmniRoute full docs: API_REFERENCE, AUTO-COMBO-GUIDE, FREE-TIERS, PROVIDERS, CLI-TOOLS, MCP-SERVER, A2A-SERVER
- hermes-agent — README/AGENTS/CONTRIBUTING + website model-catalog.md, provider-routing, features docs
- openrouter.ai — quickstart, subagent server-tool, and the full doc index (llms.txt)

---

YOUR AUTO-ROUTING CLAIM — verified against actual code, and it's a PARTIAL misunderstanding. Here's the honest finding:

llm-gate does NOT "only use Claude opus/haiku/sonnet." What it actually does (classifier.py:47 + catalog.py:70):
- It fetches the FULL dynamic catalog from OmniRoute/OpenRouter /v1/models (3,628 models in our case).
- It applies classify(model_id) to EVERY model — a 4-tier regex table (T0 opus/gpt-5.5/grok-4/o3-pro; T1 sonnet-4/gpt-4o/…; T2 sonnet-3/gpt-4o-mini/haiku-3.5/llama-70b/…; T3 haiku/flash/mini/nano/…).
- Unmatched models default to tier 2 (medium), so nothing is dropped.

So the SPIRIT of your concern is real but the MECHANISM is the opposite of what you thought: it's not a hardcoded allowlist of 3 models — it tiers everything. THE ACTUAL GAP is that classifier.py's regex table is itself a hand-maintained static list. A brand-new capable model with an unfamiliar slug silently lands in tier 2 until someone edits the file. That's the real stale-heuristic problem.

Where your instinct is EXACTLY right: ruflo's own ADR-149 (per-model cost-optimal routing) says the same thing about ruflo's 3-tier Haiku/Sonnet/Opus router — it calls the tier abstraction a "ceiling" and shows measured data where Llama-3.3-70B delivers 91% of Sonnet's quality at 0.9% of the cost, and GPT-4.1 outscores Sonnet at 4× less cost. ADR-149's whole thesis: drop the 3-tier rings, operate on concrete ModelId strings, pick the cheapest model predicted to clear a qualityBar. That is precisely the "not hardcoded rings/heuristics, leverage the dynamic list" approach you've been pushing. So you and ruflo's architects agree — and llm-gate's classifier.py is still stuck in the ring model you're rightly suspicious of.

---

OPENROUTER SUBAGENT TOOL — directly relevant to "assign any model to any subagent":
- The openrouter:subagent server tool lets a model delegate a sub-task to ANY OpenRouter model as a worker. Worker resolution precedence: parameters.model on the tool def → outer request model. "The delegating model does not choose its worker per call — the worker is fixed by the tool definition." So you pick the worker per-tool, not per-call. Any model, optionally with its own tools (web_search etc.). Each task independent, no parent context. This is the cleanest expression of "any model to any subagent" — and it's server-side, not our code.
- OpenRouter also has model "aliases" like ~openai/gpt-latest (always-resolves-latest) and a GET /api/v1/models full-catalog endpoint. So the dynamic list is fully available to us.

---

VOCALIZED IMPROVEMENT OPPORTUNITIES (speaking up, as you asked):

1. llm-gate classifier.py is the real "hardcoded heuristic" problem you sensed — just in a different file than you thought. RECOMMEND: replace the static regex tier table with either (a) OmniRoute's `auto`/`auto/coding`/`auto/cheap` server-side selection (it already scores health/quota/cost/speed/quality across ALL connected providers — exactly the dynamic list you want), or (b) adopt ruflo's ADR-149 pattern: per-ModelId cost-optimal selection with a qualityBar, fed by measured seed-corpus scores instead of hand-coded regexes. This collapses #57's "eligibility filtering" and the stale-classifier problem into one principled fix. The OmniRoute catalog + the existing ProbeRunner (already built, just unwired) give us the live health signal ADR-149 needs.

2. llm-gate and ruflo are solving the SAME routing problem twice with different abstractions (llm-gate = regex tiers; ruflo = bandit+tier; OmniRoute = 12-factor score). We should pick ONE authority. OmniRoute is already the live gateway and already does dynamic per-request selection with self-healing/circuit-breakers. llm-gate's value-add should be the CAPABILITY/CRITICALITY gating + probe truth (#57/#9), NOT re-implementing model scoring. Let OmniRoute own "which concrete model," llm-gate own "is it eligible + what criticality floor."

3. Codex workers: the runbook already documents the fix you hit — Codex CLI 0.144.6 regressed the custom-provider worker path; 0.144.5 is the proven pin, and omniroute launch-codex is the worker substrate. So "Codex workers aren't available" is a version-pin issue, not a dead end. We can fix it by pinning. I should NOT have been trying ruflo agent spawn --provider openrouter — that was the wrong harness and a hardcoded provider. The correct worker path is Codex-via-OmniRoute at the pinned version, OR Hermes delegate_task (which can take any model per call — that's our "any model to any subagent" already working).

4. Hermes model-catalog is CURATED/snapshot-based (maintainer-published JSON manifest), not a live full catalog. That's fine for a picker but means Hermes can't see newly-added models without a manifest republish. llm-gate, by contrast, tiers the live catalog. These two should reconcile — ideally both pull the OmniRoute /v1/models list as the single source of truth, with the manifest as a fallback only.

5. RuVector SONA / ReasoningBank / neural_train are already documented and wired (intelligence-route skill, hooks_model-* tools). The documented pattern for "learn from outcomes and route better" is: hooks_model-route → spawn at tier → hooks_model-outcome(success) → router learns. llm-gate has NONE of this feedback loop. If we want llm-gate's routing to improve over time instead of being a static regex, we should reuse ruflo's intelligence hooks (via the documented MCP/CLI surface) rather than reinventing a learner. That's the "use what we already have" point applied concretely.

6. OmniRoute's AUTO-COMBO already does bandit-style exploration (5% default, 10% on auto/smart) and self-healing (score<0.2 → excluded 5 min; >50% down → incident mode). That's the resilience layer llm-gate's probe idea is trying to rebuild. Reuse it; don't duplicate it.

---

WHERE I'M HONESTLY BLOCKED / INCOMPLETE (so you can decide):

I read the load-bearing routing/intelligence/neural/DAA/SONA docs + the ADR that answers your question. If you want exhaustive v3 coverage I'll continue, but the design-pattern + dynamic-routing + neural questions are answered.

That single change addresses your "dynamic list, not hardcoded rings" directive directly. Want me to draft that spec/issue?

{"content": "1|\"\"\"Bounded, privacy-safe model availability probes.
2|
3|This module deliberately treats catalog identifiers as opaque runtime data.  A
4|catalog entry is not proof that a model can be used: callers can run a minimal
5|probe and feed the resulting structured observation into the availability
6|adapter.  No response body, prompt data, credentials, or connection strings are
7|stored in probe state.
8|\"\"\"
9|
10|from __future__ import annotations
11|
12|import json
13|import re
14|import time
15|import urllib.error
16|import urllib.request
17|from collections.abc import Mapping, Sequence
18|from concurrent.futures import Future, ThreadPoolExecutor, wait
19|from dataclasses import dataclass
20|from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
21|from typing import Any, Protocol
22|
23|PROBE_PROMPT = \"Return exactly: OK\"
24|
25|
26|class ProbeTransport(Protocol):
27|    \"\"\"Injected OpenAI-compatible transport used by :class:`ProbeRunner`.\"\"\"
28|
29|    def __call__(
30|        self, model_id: str, payload: Mapping[str, Any], timeout_seconds: float
31|    ) -> Any: ...
32|
33|
34|@dataclass(frozen=True)
35|class ProbePolicy:
36|    \"\"\"Safety and scheduling bounds for a probe run.\"\"\"
37|
38|    timeout_seconds: float = 20.0
39|    max_concurrency: int = 4
40|    max_models_per_run: int = 16
41|    cooldown_seconds: float = 30.0
42|    max_cooldown_seconds: float = 900.0
43|    failure_threshold: int = 3
44|    quarantine_seconds: float = 1800.0
45|
46|    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
47|        if self.timeout_seconds <= 0:
48|            raise ValueError(\"timeout_seconds must be positive\")
49|        if self.max_concurrency < 1:
50|            raise ValueError(\"max_concurrency must be at least 1\")
51|        if self.max_models_per_run < 1:
52|            raise ValueError(\"max_models_per_run must be at least 1\")
53|        if self.cooldown_seconds < 0 or self.max_cooldown_seconds < self.cooldown_seconds:
54|            raise ValueError(\"cooldown bounds are invalid\")
55|        if self.failure_threshold < 1 or self.quarantine_seconds < 0:
56|            raise ValueError(\"failure/quarantine bounds are invalid\")
57|
58|    def payload(self, model_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
59|        \"\"\"Return the fixed no-user-data, one-token probe request.\"\"\"
60|        if not isinstance(model_id, str) or not model_id.strip():
61|            raise ValueError(\"model_id must be a non-empty runtime string\")
62|        return {
63|            \"model\": model_id,
64|            \"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": PROBE_PROMPT}],
65|            \"max_tokens\": 1,
66|            \"temperature\": 0,
67|            \"stream\": False,
68|            \"tools\": [],
69|        }
70|
71|
72|@dataclass(frozen=True)
73|class ProbeObservation:
74|    \"\"\"Safe operational result; never contains the upstream response body.\"\"\"
75|
76|    model_id: str
77|    availability_state: str
78|    status: str
79|    observed_at: datetime
80|    latency_ms: float | None = None
81|    usage_available: bool = False
82|    prompt_tokens: int | None = None
83|    completion_tokens: int | None = None
84|    total_tokens: int | None = None
85|    http_status: int | None = None
86|    error_class: str | None = None
87|    error: str | None = None
88|    consecutive_failures: int = 0
89|    next_probe_at: datetime | None = None
90|    quarantine_until: datetime | None = None
91|
92|    def as_runtime_observation(self) -> Any:
93|        \"\"\"Convert to the existing structured availability-observation contract.\"\"\"
94|        from llm_gate.availability import RuntimeObservation
95|
96|        health = \"healthy\" if self.availability_state == \"ready\" else \"degraded\"
97|        if self.status in {\"failed\", \"timeout\"}:
98|            health = \"unhealthy\"
99|        raw = {
100|            \"probe_status\": self.status,
101|            \"probe_availability_state\": self.availability_state,
102|            \"probe_error_class\": self.error_class,
103|            \"probe_error\": self.error,
104|            \"usage_available\": self.usage_available,
105|            \"http_status\": self.http_status,
106|        }
107|        return RuntimeObservation(
108|            observed_at=self.observed_at,
109|            ttl_seconds=60,
110|            source=\"llm-gate:probe\",
111|            health=health,
112|            auth=\"authorized\" if self.availability_state == \"ready\" else \"unknown\",
113|            eligible=True if self.availability_state == \"ready\" else None,
114|            raw=raw,
115|        )
116|
117|
118|@dataclass
119|class _ProbeState:
120|    consecutive_failures: int = 0
121|    next_probe_at: float = 0.0
122|    quarantine_until: float = 0.0
123|    last_state: str = \"unknown\"
124|
125|
126|class ProbeRegistry:
127|    \"\"\"In-memory operational state for cooldown and quarantine decisions.\"\"\"
128|
129|    def __init__(self) -> None:
130|        self._states: dict[str, _ProbeState] = {}
131|
132|    def state(self, model_id: str) -> _ProbeState:
133|        return self._states.setdefault(model_id, _ProbeState())
134|
135|    def snapshot(self) -> dict[str, dict[str, float | int | str]]:
136|        \"\"\"Return non-sensitive state suitable for metrics/debugging.\"\"\"
137|        return {
138|            model_id: {
139|                \"consecutive_failures\": state.consecutive_failures,
140|                \"next_probe_at\": state.next_probe_at,
141|                \"quarantine_until\": state.quarantine_until,
142|                \"last_state\": state.last_state,
143|            }
144|            for model_id, state in self._states.items()
145|        }
146|
147|
148|class ProbeRunner:
149|    \"\"\"Run bounded probes against dynamically supplied model IDs.\"\"\"
150|

152|        self, policy: ProbePolicy | None = None, registry: ProbeRegistry | None = None
153|    ) -> None:
154|        self.policy = policy or P

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435793.7499323)
```
{"content": "1|\"\"\"Protocol-based OmniRoute runtime availability and eligibility adapter.
2|
3|The adapter consumes documented, JSON-like catalog/runtime observations through an
4|injected transport.  It deliberately has no knowledge of OmniRoute's private
5|storage or credentials; API, CLI, MCP, and A2A clients can implement the small
6|transport protocol below.
7|\"\"\"
8|
9|from __future__ import annotations
10|
11|import math
12|from collections.abc import Callable, Mapping
13|from dataclasses import dataclass, field, replace
14|from datetime import datetime, timezone
15|from enum import Enum
16|from typing import Any, Protocol
17|
18|from llm_gate.models import ModelInfo
19|
20|
21|def _is_finite_number(value: Any) -> bool:
22|    if isinstance(value, bool) or not isinstance(value, (int, float)):
23|        return False
24|    try:
25|        return math.isfinite(float(value))
26|    except (OverflowError, TypeError, ValueError):
27|        return False
28|
29|
30|_CAPABILITY_ALIASES = {
31|    \"function-calling\": \"tools\",
32|    \"function_calling\": \"tools\",
33|    \"tool-calling\": \"tools\",
34|    \"tool_calling\": \"tools\",
35|    \"json\": \"structured_output\",
36|    \"structured-output\": \"structured_output\",
37|}
38|
39|
40|def canonical_capability(value: Any) -> str:
41|    \"\"\"Return the shared capability vocabulary used by catalog and policy.\"\"\"
42|    normalized = str(value).strip().lower()
43|    return _CAPABILITY_ALIASES.get(normalized, normalized)
44|
45|
46|class AvailabilityState(str, Enum):
47|    ELIGIBLE = \"eligible\"
48|    READY = \"eligible\"
49|    DEGRADED = \"degraded\"
50|    UNKNOWN = \"unknown\"
51|    UNAVAILABLE = \"unavailable\"
52|    DENIED = \"denied\"
53|    QUOTA_EXHAUSTED = \"quota_exhausted\"
54|    RATE_LIMITED = \"rate_limited\"
55|    UNAUTHORIZED = \"unauthorized\"
56|    LOCKED_OUT = \"locked_out\"
57|    CIRCUIT_OPEN = \"circuit_open\"
58|    TIMEOUT = \"timeout\"
59|    MALFORMED = \"malformed\"
60|    CAPABILITY_MISMATCH = \"capability_mismatch\"
61|    POLICY_DENIED = \"policy_denied\"
62|
63|
64|@dataclass(frozen=True)
65|class CandidateRequirements:
66|    \"\"\"Hard requirements applied before any ranking or cost preference.\"\"\"
67|
68|    required: frozenset[str] = frozenset()
69|    protected: bool = False
70|    allow_models: frozenset[str] = frozenset()
71|    deny_models: frozenset[str] = frozenset()
72|    allow_providers: frozenset[str] = frozenset()
73|    deny_providers: frozenset[str] = frozenset()
74|    budget_remaining: float | None = None
75|    max_concurrency: int | None = None
76|    unknown_is_eligible: bool = False
77|    allow_degraded: bool = False
78|    estimated_tokens: int | None = None
79|    estimated_cost: float | None = None
80|
81|    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
82|        object.__setattr__(
83|            self,
84|            \"required\",
85|            frozenset(canonical_capability(value) for value in self.required),
86|        )
87|        budget = self.budget_remaining
88|        if budget is not None and (not _is_finite_number(budget) or budget < 0):
89|            raise ValueError(\"budget_remaining must be a finite non-negative number\")
90|        if self.max_concurrency is not None and (
91|            type(self.max_concurrency) is not int or self.max_concurrency < 1
92|        ):
93|            raise ValueError(\"max_concurrency must be a positive integer\")
94|        if self.estimated_tokens is not None and (
95|            type(self.estimated_tokens) is not int
96|            or not _is_finite_number(self.estimated_tokens)
97|            or self.estimated_tokens < 0
98|        ):
99|            raise ValueError(\"estimated_tokens must be a finite non-negative integer\")
100|        if self.estimated_cost is not None and (
101|            not _is_finite_number(self.estimated_cost) or self.estimated_cost < 0
102|        ):
103|            raise ValueError(\"estimated_cost must be a finite non-negative number\")
104|        if any(
105|            type(value) is not bool
106|            for value in (self.protected, self.unknown_is_eligible, self.allow_degraded)
107|        ):
108|            raise ValueError(\"candidate requirement flags must be booleans\")
109|

### 🔄 Tool result (1784435793.7596576)

{"content": "1|\"\"\"Core routing algorithm prioritizing quality, tier, and capability.\"\"\"
2|
3|from llm_gate.models import ModelInfo, ProviderConfig
4|
5|
6|def select_best_model(
7|    candidates: list[ModelInfo], tier: int, configs: dict[str, ProviderConfig]
8|) -> tuple[ModelInfo | None, list[str]]:
9|    \"\"\"Select the highest-quality eligible model out of candidates.
10|
11|    The selector is deterministic and only considers candidates that satisfy the
12|    requested tier and are currently available. Quality confidence wins first,
13|    then capability tier, provider priority, and stable model ID.
14|    \"\"\"
15|    valid = [
16|        model
17|        for model in candidates
18|        if model.capability_tier <= tier
19|        and model.is_available
20|        and model.availability_state in {\"eligible\", \"ready\"}
21|    ]
22|    if not valid:
23|        return None, [m.id for m in candidates]
24|
25|    valid.sort(
26|        key=lambda model: (
27|            -(
28|                model.quality_confidence
29|                if model.quality_confidence is not None
30|                else max(0.0, 1.0 - model.capability_tier / 3.0)
31|            ),
32|            model.capability_tier,
33|            -configs[model.provider].priority,
34|            model.id,
35|        )
36|    )
37|
38|    chosen = valid[0]
39|    alts = [m.id for m in valid[1:5]]
40|    return chosen, alts
41|", "total_lines": 40, "file_size": 1284, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
```

## Parent
Part of #30 and the flagship completion program in #28/#36.

## Depends on
#30.2, #29.4, and existing dispatcher/router code.

## Goal
Prove that all route/workflow paths filter candidates using live eligibility before any adaptive or cost ranking.

## Scope
- Wire normalized availability into Python router, dispatcher, API, and proxy decision paths.
- Preserve exclusions and availability snapshot in RoutingDecision.
- Add invariant tests that an ineligible candidate cannot be selected by a learned/adaptive ranker.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path.
- [ ] No ranker, Ruflo plan, or RuVector result can reintroduce excluded candidates.
- [ ] Explain endpoint shows the complete pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions.
- [ ] Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent.
- [ ] Integration tests cover all public route entry points.

## Swarm routing
Lower-tier integration agent; stronger adversarial review required for the invariant tests.

## Parent
Part of #56 (merged) and #30; feeds #57.

## Goal
Make the bounded availability cache (#56) authoritative for eligibility in the live routing path, not just the explain endpoint.

## Scope
- Consult `AvailabilityCache.get(model_id)` inside `Gate`/`dispatcher`/router before any adaptive or cost ranking.
- Drop candidates whose cached `AvailabilityReport` is `unknown`/`error` for protected work (fail closed when runtime truth is absent).
- Preserve the pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions for the explain endpoint.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path (router, dispatcher, Gate).
- [ ] No ranker, Ruflo plan, or RuVector result can reintroduce excluded candidates.

## Parent
Part of #56 (merged) and #30; completes the explain AC of #57.

## Goal
The #56 `GET /v1/route/explain` exposes per-model freshness, but #57 requires the complete **pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions** to be visible.

## Scope
- Attach the normalized `AvailabilityReport.candidates`/`eligible` and per-candidate exclusion reasons to the explain record.
- Include the cache `confidence` and `refresh_error` already produced by #56.
- Ensure the record is produced from the same gate the router uses (no divergent truth).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Explain endpoint shows the complete pre-ranking eligible set and per-candidate exclusions.
- [ ] Explain record is sourced from the same eligibility gate as routing (single source of truth).", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

## 🤖 Assistant (1784435805.1493173)

The AC is now crystal clear. The #57 contract is:

1. **Filter before ranking in every route path** (router/dispatcher/Gate/proxy).
2. **No ranker/Ruflo/RuVector can reintroduce excluded candidates** (invariant + tests).
3. **Explain shows complete pre-ranking eligible set + exclusions.** (#73)
4. **Protected work fails closed when runtime truth absent.**
5. **Integration tests for all public route entry points.**

#72 fills the gap: consult `AvailabilityCache.get(model_id)` inside Gate/dispatcher/router before ranking, drop `unknown`/`error` cached candidates for protected work.

Key architectural point from the docs (ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING + ROUTING_POLICY): llm-gate must NOT couple to Ruflo/RuVector internals — and the probe should be OPT-IN (availability_profile=production). The existing `OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter.evaluate()` already builds candidates; the missing piece is (a) probe truth feeding it, and (b) a single eligibility gate consumed by router/dispatcher/Gate/explain.

Let me read the remaining pieces I need: the rest of probes.py (openai_probe_transport + the classification `availability_state` mapping), intelligence.py route path, dispatcher.py, gate.py, and api.py explain endpoint. Then I'll design a single `EligibilityGate` and wire it.

{"content": "261|    def _record_response(
262|        self,
263|        model_id: str,
264|        observed_at: datetime,
265|        now_mono: float,
266|        response: Any,
267|        latency_ms: float,
268|    ) -> ProbeObservation:
269|        status_code, body = _response_parts(response)
270|        usage = body.get(\"usage\") if isinstance(body, Mapping) else None
271|        usage_available = isinstance(usage, Mapping) and any(
272|            type(value) is int and value > 0
273|            for key in (\"total_tokens\", \"completion_tokens\", \"prompt_tokens\")
274|            for value in (usage.get(key),)
275|        )
276|        completion_available = _has_assistant_output(body)
277|        state = self.registry.state(model_id)
278|        if status_code is None:
279|            return self._record_failure(
280|                model_id,
281|                observed_at,
282|                now_mono,
283|                \"malformed_response\",
284|                \"upstream response missing integer HTTP status\",
285|                latency_ms=latency_ms,
286|            )
287|        if status_code is not None and not 200 <= status_code < 300:
288|            return self._record_failure(
289|                model_id,
290|                observed_at,
291|                now_mono,
292|                _status_error_class(status_code),
293|                f\"upstream returned HTTP {status_code}\",
294|                http_status=status_code,
295|                latency_ms=latency_ms,
296|            )
297|        if not usage_available:
298|            state.last_state = \"degraded\"
299|            state.next_probe_at = now_mono + self.policy.cooldown_seconds
300|            return ProbeObservation(
301|                model_id=model_id,
302|                availability_state=\"degraded\",
303|                status=\"usage_unavailable\",
304|                observed_at=observed_at,
305|                latency_ms=round(latency_ms, 3),
306|                http_status=status_code,
307|                error=\"usage unavailable\",
308|                consecutive_failures=state.consecutive_failures,
309|                next_probe_at=_datetime_from_mono(state.next_probe_at, observed_at, now_mono),
310|            )
311|        assert isinstance(usage, Mapping)
312|        if not completion_available:

314|            state.next_probe_at = now_mono + self.policy.cooldown_seconds
315|            return ProbeObservation(

317|                availability_state=\"degraded\",

321|                usage_available=True,

323|                completion_tokens=_int_or_none(usage.get(\"completion_tokens\")),
324|                total_tokens=_int_or_none(usage.get(\"total_tokens\")),
325|                http_status=status_code,
326|                error=\"assistant completion unavailable\",
327|                consecutive_failures=state.consecutive_failures,
328|                next_probe_at=_datetime_from_mono(state.next_probe_at, observed_at, now_mono),
329|            )
330|        state.consecutive_failures = 0
331|        state.last_state = \"ready\"
332|        state.next_probe_at = now_mono + self.policy.cooldown_seconds
333|        return ProbeObservation(
334|            model_id=model_id,
335|            availability_state=\"ready\",
336|            status=\"ready\",
337|            observed_at=observed_at,
338|            latency_ms=round(latency_ms, 3),
339|            usage_available=True,

341|            completion_tokens=_int_or_none(usage.get(\"completion_tokens\")),
342|            total_tokens=_int_or_none(usage.get(\"total_tokens\")),

347|
348|    def _record_failure(
349|        self,
350|        model_id: str,
351|        observed_at: datetime,
352|        now_mono: float,
353|        error_class: str,
354|        error: str,
355|        *,
356|        http_status: int | None = None,
357|        latency_ms: float | None = None,
358|    ) -> ProbeObservation:
359|        state = self.registry.state(model_id)
360|        state.consecutive_failures += 1
361|        delay = min(
362|            self.policy.max_cooldown_seconds,
363|            self.policy.cooldown_seconds * (2 ** max(0, state.consecutive_failures - 1)),
364|        )
365|        state.next_probe_at = now_mono + delay
366|        quarantined = state.consecutive_failures >= self.policy.failure_threshold
367|        if quarantined:
368|            state.quarantine_until = now_mono + self.policy.quarantine_seconds
369|            state.last_state = \"denied\"
370|            availability_state = \"denied\"
371|        else:
372|            state.last_state = \"degraded\"
373|            availability_state = \"degraded\"
374|        return ProbeObservation(
375|            model_id=model_id,
376|            availability_state=availability_state,
377|            status=\"timeout\" if error_class == \"timeout\" else \"failed\",
378|            observed_at=observed_at,
379|            latency_ms=round(latency_ms, 3) if latency_ms is not None else None,
380|            http_status=http_status,
381|            error_class=error_class,
382|            error=error,
383|            consecutive_failures=state.consecutive_failures,
384|            next_probe_at=_datetime_from_mono(state.next_probe_at, observed_at, now_mono),
385|            quarantine_until=_datetime_from_mono(state.quarantine_until, observed_at, now_mono)
386|            if quarantined

11|
12|DEFAULT_PROFILE = \"development\"
13|DEGRADED_PROFILE = \"degraded\"
14|DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS = 1000
15|
16|
17|@dataclass
18|class ReadinessReport:
19|    status: str
20|    production_ready: bool
21|    profile: str
22|    managed_backend_status: str
23|    degraded_mode: bool
24|    policy_version: str
25|    reason: str
26|    adapter_versions: dict[str, str]
27|
28|
29|class IntelligenceService:
30|    def __init__(
31|        self,
32|        primary_model: str,
33|        providers: dict[str, ProviderConfig],
34|        profile: str,
35|        log_path: str,
36|        log_full_task: bool,
37|        discovery_ttl: int,
38|        ruflo_command: str = \"ruflo\",
39|        ruvector_command: str = \"ruvector\",
40|        timeout_ms: int = 1000,
41|        frontier_allowlist: tuple[str, ...] | None = None,
42|        allow_client_model_override: bool = False,
43|        planner: StructuredPlanner | None = None,
44|    ):
45|        self.primary_model = primary_model
46|        self.providers = providers
47|        self.profile = profile
48|        self.log_path = log_path
49|        self.log_full_task = log_full_task
50|        self.discovery_ttl = discovery_ttl
51|        self.ruflo_command = ruflo_command
52|        self.ruvector_command = ruvector_command
53|        self.timeout_ms = timeout_ms
54|        self.frontier_allowlist = frontier_allowlist
55|        self.allow_client_model_override = allow_client_model_override
56|        self.planner = planner or StructuredPlanner()
57|        self.managed_backend_status = self._probe_managed_backend()
58|        self._policy_version = \"policy-2026-07-13.1\"
59|
60|    def _redact(self, text: str) -> str:
61|        import re
62|
63|        # Basic redaction before CLI execution (sk-...)
64|        return re.sub(r\"sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{10,}\", \"[REDACTED]\", text)
65|
66|    def _probe_managed_backend(self) -> str:
67|        try:
68|            import subprocess
69|
70|            result = subprocess.run(
71|                [self.ruflo_command, \"guidance\", \"gates\", \"--version\"],
72|                capture_output=True,
73|                text=True,
74|                timeout=0.5,
75|            )
76|            return \"healthy\" if result.returncode == 0 else \"unavailable\"
77|        except Exception:
78|            return \"unavailable\"
79|
80|    def readiness(self) -> ReadinessReport:
81|        status = (
82|            \"ready\"
83|            if self.profile != \"production\" or self.managed_backend_status != \"unavailable\"
84|            else \"not_ready\"
85|        )
86|        degraded = self.managed_backend_status == \"unavailable\"
87|        return ReadinessReport(
88|            status=status,
89|            production_ready=(not degraded),
90|            profile=self.profile,
91|            managed_backend_status=self.managed_backend_status,
92|            degraded_mode=degraded,
93|            policy_version=self._policy_version,
94|            reason=\"ready\" if not degraded else \"managed intelligence unavailable\",
95|            adapter_versions={},
96|        )
97|
98|    def route(
99|        self, task: str, criticality: str = \"medium\", context: dict[str, Any] | None = None
100|    ) -> RoutingDecision:
101|        start_t = time.time()
102|
103|        # Hard deterministic floor logic here.
104|        redacted_task = self._redact(task)
105|        # Attempt an async call or subprocess with timeout to Ruflo
106|        try:
107|            import subprocess
108|
109|            subprocess.run(
110|                [self.ruflo_command, \"hooks\", \"model-route\", \"--context\", redacted_task],
111|                capture_output=True,
112|                timeout=0.2,
113|            )
114|        except Exception:
115|            pass
116|
117|        # Fallback to strict heuristic scan
118|        eff_tier, heuristic_reason = scan(task)
119|
120|        # Planning estimates task capability needs. Criticality is retained as a
121|        # safety floor, not as a model selector: identical task semantics have
122|        # identical selection requirements unless a protected floor applies.
123|        try:
124|            task_spec = self.planner.plan(task, context=context, criticality=criticality).task_spec
125|            task_tier = {\"low\": 3, \"medium\": 2, \"high\": 1}.get(task_spec.effort, 2)
126|        except Exception:
127|            task_tier = 2
128|
129|        # Convert criticality string to required tier max
130|        tier_map = {\"critical\": 0, \"high\": 1, \"medium\": 2, \"low\": 3}
131|        req_tier = tier_map.get(criticality.lower(), 2)
132|
133|        esc_reason: str = \"\"
134|        escalated = False
135|
136|        if eff_tier is not None and eff_tier < req_tier:
137|            req_tier = eff_tier
138|            esc_reason = heuristic_reason or \"\"
139|            escalated = True
140|
141|        safety_floor = req_tier if req_tier <= 1 else 3
142|        final_tier = min(task_tier, safety_floor, eff_tier if eff_tier is not None else 3)
143|
144|        candidates = []
145|        for name, cfg in self.providers.items():
146|            candidates.extend(fetch_models(name, cfg, self.discovery_ttl))
147|
148|        best_model, _ = select_best_model(candidates, final_tier, self.providers)
149|
150|        if final_tier == 0 or not best_model:
151|            dec = RoutingDecision(
152|                model=self.primary_model,
153|                provider=\"primary\",
154|                tier=0,
155|                reason=\"critical — never offload\"
156|                if final_tier == 0
157|
