```
{"content": "1|\"\"\"Bounded availability cache with documented TTL and stale-while-revalidate.
2|
3|The cache wraps an :class:`OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter` (or any callable that
4|returns an :class:`AvailabilityReport`).  It deliberately never trusts a stale
5|entry for protected routing unless an explicit policy opt-in allows degraded
6|mode, and it isolates entries per provider/model/policy-version so one
7|source can never poison another.
8|
9|Design constraints (from issue #56 acceptance criteria):
10|- Cache keys include provider/model and the relevant policy/contract version.
11|- Expired or refresh-failed states are explicit ``unknown``/``error`` states.
12|- Concurrent refreshes are bounded and deduplicated.
13|- ``explain`` output identifies freshness and source.
14|\"\"\"
15|
16|from __future__ import annotations
17|
18|import threading
19|from collections.abc import Callable
20|from dataclasses import dataclass, field, replace
21|from datetime import datetime, timedelta
22|from typing import Any
23|
24|from llm_gate.availability import AvailabilityReport, _now
25|
26|
27|@dataclass(frozen=True)
28|class CacheKey:
29|    \"\"\"Identity for a cached availability observation.
30|
31|    Includes provider/model and the policy/contract version so a contract
32|    change invalidates stale entries instead of leaking them across versions.
33|    \"\"\"
34|
35|    provider: str
36|    model: str
37|    policy_version: str = \"policy-2026-07-13.1\"
38|
39|    @classmethod
40|    def for_candidate(cls, model_id: str, policy_version: str | None = None) -> CacheKey:
41|        provider = model_id.split(\"/\", 1)[0] if \"/\" in model_id else \"unknown\"
42|        return cls(
43|            provider=provider,
44|            model=model_id,
45|            policy_version=policy_version or cls.policy_version,
46|        )
47|
48|
49|@dataclass
50|class _Entry:
51|    \"\"\"Mutable cache entry holding the last good report and refresh bookkeeping.\"\"\"
52|
53|    report: AvailabilityReport
54|    stored_at: datetime
55|    ttl_seconds: int
56|    # Refresh coordination: only one in-flight refresh per key at a time.
57|    refreshing: bool = False
58|    refresh_error: str | None = None
59|    last_refresh_attempt: datetime | None = None
60|
61|    def age_seconds(self, now: datetime) -> float:
62|        return (now - self.stored_at).total_seconds()
63|
64|    def is_fresh(self, now: datetime, *, stale_window_seconds: int) -> bool:
65|        # Freshness is purely TTL-based; the stale window governs revalidation.
66|        return self.age_seconds(now) <= self.ttl_seconds
67|
68|    def is_within_stale_window(self, now: datetime, *, stale_window_seconds: int) -> bool:
69|        age = self.age_seconds(now)
70|        return self.ttl_seconds < age <= (self.ttl_seconds + stale_window_seconds)
71|
72|
73|def _default_clock() -> datetime:
74|    return _now()
75|
76|
77|def _confidence_for(report: AvailabilityReport) -> float:
78|    \"\"\"Deterministic, secret-free confidence in [0, 1] for an explain record.
79|
80|    Errors collapse confidence to zero. The cache/replay source (``\"cache\"``)
81|    is treated as low-trust. Otherwise confidence tracks the eligible share of
82|    observed candidates.
83|    \"\"\"
84|    if report.errors:
85|        return 0.0
86|    if not report.candidates:
87|        return 0.0 if report.source in (\"unknown\", \"cache\") else 0.5
88|    ratio = len(report.eligible) / len(report.candidates)
89|    if report.source in (\"unknown\", \"cache\"):
90|        return round(0.3 * ratio, 3)
91|    return round(ratio, 3)
92|
93|
94|@dataclass
95|class AvailabilityCache:
96|    \"\"\"Bounded, per-key availability cache with stale-while-revalidate.
97|
98|    ``get`` returns a cached report when fresh.  When the entry is within
99|    the stale window it returns the stale report (so callers keep working)
100|    but triggers a deduplicated refresh for next time.  When the entry is
101|    past the stale window (or absent) it performs a synchronous refresh and,
102|    on failure, returns an explicit ``unknown`` report rather than silently
103|    serving poisoned data.
104|    \"\"\"
105|
106|    source: Callable[[], AvailabilityReport]
107|    ttl_seconds: int = 60
108|    stale_window_seconds: int = 30
109|    policy_version: str = \"policy-2026-07-13.1\"
110|    clock: Callable[[], datetime] | None = None
111|    max_entries: int = 4096
112|    # Reentrant lock: ``get`` may call ``_refresh_locked`` while already holding
113|    # the lock (nested acquisition), so a plain ``Lock`` would deadlock.
114|    _entries: dict[CacheKey, _Entry] = field(default_factory=dict)
115|    _lock: threading.RLock = field(default_factory=threading.RLock)
116|
117|    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
118|        if not isinstance(self.ttl_seconds, int) or self.ttl_seconds <= 0:
119|            raise ValueError(\"ttl_seconds must be a positive integer\")
120|        if not isinstance(self.stale_window_seconds, int) or self.stale_window_seconds < 0:
121|            raise ValueError(\"stale_window_seconds must be a non-negative integer\")
122|        if not isinstance(self.max_entries, int) or self.max_entries < 1:
123|            raise ValueError(\"max_entries must be a positive integer\")
124|        # Reject a non-callable source early; the adapter is required.
125|        if not callable(self.source):
126|            raise TypeError(\"source must be callable and return an AvailabilityReport\")
127|
128|    def _key(self, model_id: str) -> CacheKey:
129|        return CacheKey.for_candidate(model_id, self.policy_version)
130|
131|    def get(self, model_id: str) -> AvailabilityReport:
132|        \"\"\"Return a cached-or-refreshed report for ``model_id``.
133|
134|        Fresh -> cached.  Stale-but-within-window -> cached + schedule refresh.
135|        Expired/missing -> refresh now; on refresh failure return explicit
136|        ``unknown`` rather than the stale entry.
137|        \
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784436320.4672062)

I now have complete command of the code. The honest finding: **`intelligence.route()` (the live routing path) never consults availability at all** — it picks `best_model = select_best_model(candidates, final_tier, ...)` purely from the catalog, and `select_best_model` only filters on `capability_tier` + `is_available` (a static catalog flag). The `AvailabilityCache` from #56 is wired only into the explain endpoint. That is exactly the #57/#72 gap: the routing path does not filter by live eligibility before ranking.

The minimal, doc-compliant fix (reusing existing pieces, no reinvention):

1. Add an `EligibilityGate` that, given candidate `ModelInfo` + the `AvailabilityCache`, (a) drops any candidate whose cached report state is not eligible/ready, (b) for **protected** work fails closed when the cached state is `unknown`/`error`/missing. This is the single source of truth both router and explain use.
2. Wire `EligibilityGate` into `IntelligenceService.route()` BEFORE `select_best_model` — satisfying "filter before ranking in every route path." Also reflect the gate's `eligible`/`exclusions` into the `RoutingDecision` (preserving exclusions per AC).
3. Extend `/v1/route/explain` (#73) to return the full candidate set, pre-ranking eligible set, per-candidate exclusion reasons, and cache confidence/refresh_error — from the same gate.
4. Probe wiring (#57 root cause): make `_build_availability_cache` optionally enrich the adapter with a `ProbeRunner` when `LLMGATE_AVAILABILITY_PROFILE=production` and a probe base URL/key are set — reusing `ProbeRunner`/`openai_probe_transport`, no new transport code.

Let me create a new `eligibility.py` module (the gate) and a probe-enriched adapter in `availability.py`, then wire them. I'll start the isolated worktree per the runbook to avoid conflicts, implement, and run the full test suite + ruff + mypy before reporting. Let me set up the worktree and write the gate module.

```
[Subdirectory context discovered: llm-gate-57/AGENTS.md]
# AI Agent Constraints & Architectural Context (.claude.md)

**Target Ecosystem:** Express.js Middleware / Next.js Routing
**Language:** Python 3.10+
**Primary Directive:** LLM API Cost Optimization and Fallback Orchestration.

## Directory Boundaries
- `/llm_gate/gate.py`: Core routing engine. Modifying heuristic logic here requires validating the execution tree in `tests/test_gate_unit.py`.
- `/llm_gate/discovery.py`: The integration connector. Only add OpenRouter/Ollama parsers here. 
- `/llm_gate/api.py`: FastAPI server layer. Must never be instantiated in global execution scopes.

## RAG Memory & Constraints
If an agent edits the parsing logic in `models.py`, ensure data structures remain mapped as `dataclass(frozen=True)` to prevent mutation across asynchronous generation loops.

## Deployment Gating
Do not update `pyproject.toml` dependencies natively without also checking `[server]` and `[ci]` optional blocks. `llm-gate` is headless by design.
```

```json
{
 "path": "/home/nick/dev/llm-gate-57/llm_gate/eligibility.py",
 "content": "\"\"\"Single-source-of-truth eligibility gate for pre-ranking filtering.\n\nThis module implements the issue #57 / #72 invariant: candidate filtering\nhappens *before* any adaptive or cost ranking, and no ranker, Ruflo plan, or\nRuVector result can reintroduce a candidate that the gate excluded.\n\nThe gate consults the already-merged :class:`AvailabilityCache` (issue #56),\nwhich wraps the :class:`OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter`.  It is deliberately\nprotocol-based: it takes a ``Callable[[str], AvailabilityReport]`` so the live\nrouting path and the explain endpoint share identical truth.\n\nFail-closed semantics (per ROUTING_POLICY + #57 AC): when a request is\n*protected* and the live availability truth is absent (``unknown`` / ``error``\n/ missing), the candidate is excluded rather than optimistically admitted.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom enum import Enum\nfrom typing import Any, Callable, Mapping\n\nfrom llm_gate.availability import AvailabilityState, AvailabilityReport\nfrom llm_gate.models import ModelInfo\n\n\nclass EligibilityVerdict(str, Enum):\n    \"\"\"Why a candidate was kept or excluded by the gate.\"\"\"\n\n    ELIGIBLE = \"eligible\"\n    NOT_LIVE_ELIGIBLE = \"not_live_eligible\"\n    RUNTIME_TRUTH_ABSENT = \"runtime_truth_absent\"\n    NOT_REQUESTED_TIER = \"not_requested_tier\"\n\n\n# States that admit a candidate into the pre-ranking eligible set.\n_ADMITTED_STATES = frozenset(\n    {\n        AvailabilityState.ELIGIBLE,\n        AvailabilityState.READY,\n        AvailabilityState.DEGRADED,\n    }\n)\n\n\n@dataclass(frozen=True)\nclass EligibilityRecord:\n    \"\"\"Per-candidate gate outcome, preserved for the explain endpoint (#73).\"\"\"\n\n    model_id: str\n    provider: str\n    admitted: bool\n    verdict: str\n    state: str\n    source: str\n    reason: str | None = None\n\n    def to_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:\n        return {\n            \"model_id\": self.model_id,\n            \"provider\": self.provider,\n            \"admitted\": self.admitted,\n            \"verdict\": self.verdict.value if isinstance(self.verdict, EligibilityVerdict) else self.verdict,\n            \"state\": self.state,\n            \"source\": self.source,\n            \"reason\": self.reason,\n        }\n\n\n@dataclass\nclass EligibilityResult:\n    \"\"\"Outcome of filtering a candidate set before ranking.\"\"\"\n\n    admitted: list[ModelInfo] = field(default_factory=list)\n    records: list[EligibilityRecord] = field(default_factory=list)\n\n    @property\n    def exclusions(self) -> list[EligibilityRecord]:\n        return [r for r in self.records if not r.admitted]\n\n    @property\n    def eligible(self) -> list[ModelInfo]:\n        return self.admitted\n\n    def to_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:\n        return {\n            \"admitted\": [m.id for m in self.admitted],\n            \"records\": [r.to_dict() for r in self.records],\n            \"exclusions\": [r.to_dict() for r in self.exclusions],\n        }\n\n\ndef _state_for(report: AvailabilityReport | None, model_id: str) -> tuple[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"Return (state, source) for a model from its cached report.\"\"\"\n    if report is None:\n        return (\"unknown\", \"cache\")\n    for candidate in report.candidates:\n        if candidate.model.id == model_id or candidate.model.id.split(\"/\", 1)[-1] == model_id.split(\"/\", 1)[-1]:\n            return (candidate.state.value, candidate.source)\n    # Candidate absent from the report entirely -> treat as unknown (fail-closed\n    # for protected work; the router may still admit unverified in dev mode).\n    return (\"unknown\", report.source)\n\n\nclass EligibilityGate:\n    \"\"\"Filter candidates by live eligibility before any ranking.\n\n    The gate is the single authority consulted by the router, dispatcher, and\n    the explain endpoint, so no downstream
```

```
240|class MappingOmniRouteTransport:
241|    \"\"\"Adapter exposing documented OmniRoute operations from a mapping payload.\"\"\"
242|
243|    def __init__(self, operations: Mapping[str, Any]) -> None:
244|        self._operations = dict(operations)
245|        unsupported = sorted(set(self._operations) - set(SUPPORTED_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS))
246|        if unsupported:
247|            raise OmniRouteTransportUnsupported(
248|                unsupported[0],
249|                f\"unsupported operation; expected one of {', '.join(SUPPORTED_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS)}\",
250|            )
251|        self.configured_operations = frozenset(
252|            operation
253|            for operation, names in (
254|                (\"catalog\", CATALOG_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS),
255|                (\"runtime\", RUNTIME_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS),
256|                (\"discover_capabilities\", CAPABILITY_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS),
257|            )
258|            if any(name in self._operations for name in names)
259|        )
260|
261|    def _resolve(self, *names: str) -> Any:
262|        for name in names:
263|            if name not in self._operations:
264|                continue
265|            value = self._operations[name]
266|            return value() if callable(value) else value
267|        raise OmniRouteTransportUnsupported(names[0], f\"expected one of {', '.join(names)}\")
268|
269|    def catalog(self) -> Any:
270|        return self._resolve(*CATALOG_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS)
271|
272|    def runtime(self) -> Any:
273|        return self._resolve(*RUNTIME_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS)
274|
275|    def discover_capabilities(self) -> Any:
276|        return self._resolve(*CAPABILITY_TRANSPORT_OPERATIONS)
```

```
717|
718|def normalize_observation(
719|    model: ModelInfo, observation: RuntimeObservation, *, now: datetime | None = None
720|) -> AvailabilityCandidate:
721|    \"\"\"Apply conservative precedence to contradictory runtime signals.\"\"\"
722|    current = _now(now)
723|    obs = _raw_observation(observation)
724|    seen = _timestamp(obs.observed_at)
725|    age = (current - seen).total_seconds() if seen else None
726|    reasons: list[str] = []
727|    if (
728|        obs.error
729|        or not _observation_is_well_formed(obs)
```

```json
 "new_string": "    check = evaluate\n\n\nclass ProbeEnrichedAdapter:\n    \"\"\"Adapter that adds bounded live probes on top of catalog/runtime truth.\n\n    This is the issue #57 root-cause wiring: the existing\n    :class:`OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter` only consults catalog + runtime\n    metadata, never proving a model is actually usable.      check = evaluate
```
 
 
```
+class ProbeEnrichedAdapter:
+    \"\"\"Adapter that adds bounded live probes on top of catalog/runtime truth.
+
+    This is the issue #57 root-cause wiring: the existing
+    :class:`OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter` only consults catalog + runtime
+    metadata, never proving a model is actually usable.  This adapter reuses the
+    already-built, already-tested :class:`~llm_gate.probes.ProbeRunner` and
+    merges single-token probe observations into the same candidate states the
+    router consumes.  No new transport code is introduced: the probe uses the
+    documented ``openai_probe_transport`` (POST /v1/chat/completions), separate
+    from the GET-only catalog/runtime transport.
+
+    Probing is opt-in via ``enabled`` so the development/default profile stays
+    pure catalog/runtime (per ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING: probing is a
+    production-profile concern).  When disabled the adapter is a thin pass-through
+    to the wrapped adapter.
+    \"\"\"
+
+    def __init__(
+        self,
+        base: OmniRouteAvailabilityAdapter,
+        *,
+        probe_transport: Any | None = None,
+        enabled: bool = False,
+        policy: Any | None = None,
+        registry: Any | None = None,
+        max_probed: int = 16,
+        clock: Any = None,
+    ) -> None:
+        self.base = base
+        self.probe_transport = probe_transport
+        self.enabled = enabled
+        self._runner = ProbeRunner(policy=policy, registry=registry) if enabled else None
+        self.max_probed = max_probed
+        self.clock = clock
+
+    def evaluate(
+        self,
+        requirements: CandidateRequirements = CandidateRequirements(),
+        *,
+        now: datetime | None = None,
+    ) -> AvailabilityReport:
+        report = self.base.evaluate(requirements, now=now)
+        if not self.enabled or self._runner is None or self.probe_transport is None:
+            return report
+        # Bound the probe fan-out to the most relevant candidates to keep the
+        # one-token probe cheap and within ProbePolicy.max_models_per_run.
+        model_ids = [c.model.id for c in report.candidates][: self.max_probed]
+        if not model_ids:
+            return report
+        observations = self._runner.run(model_ids, self.probe_transport, now=now)
+        probe_by_id: dict[str, RuntimeObservation] = {}
+        for obs in observations:
+            try:
+                probe_by_id[obs.model_id] = obs.as_runtime_observation()
+            except Exception:
+                # A malformed probe result must never poison the report.
+                continue
+        if not probe_by_id:
+            return report
+        current = _now(now or (self.clock() if self.clock else None))
+        merged = [
+            self._merge_probe(candidate, probe_by_id, current)
+            for candidate in report.candidates
+        ]
+        eligible = select_capable_candidates(merged, requirements)
+        return AvailabilityReport(
+            tuple(merged),
+            tuple(eligible),
+            report.source,
+            report.freshness_seconds,
+            report.errors,
+        )
+
+    @staticmethod
+    def _merge_probe(
+        candidate: AvailabilityCandidate,
+        probe_by_id: dict[str, RuntimeObservation],
+        now: datetime,
+    ) -> AvailabilityCandidate:
+        probe = probe_by_id.get(candidate.model.id)
+        if probe is None:
+            return candidate
+        # Probe truth is authoritative when it contradicts catalog/runtime.
+        probe_candidate = normalize_observation(candidate.model, probe, now=now)
+        if probe_candidate.state is AvailabilityState.MALFORMED:
+            # Keep the catalog/runtime verdict rather than degrading on a probe
+            # metadata glitch.
+            return candidate
+        return probe_candidate
+
```

> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://openrouter.ai/docs/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Client SDKs

> Lightweight, type-safe clients for the OpenRouter API

The Client SDKs give you a thin, type-safe layer over the OpenRouter REST API. It handles authentication, request validation, and response typing so you can call any of 400+ models with a single function call — no boilerplate, no provider-specific quirks.

## Installation

| Language   | Package                                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| TypeScript | [`@openrouter/sdk`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@openrouter/sdk) |
| Python     | [`openrouter`](https://pypi.org/project/openrouter/)               |
| Go         | [`go-sdk`](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/OpenRouterTeam/go-sdk)    |

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash title="npm" lines theme={null}
  npm install @openrouter/sdk
  ```

  ```bash title="pnpm" lines theme={null}
  pnpm add @openrouter/sdk
  ```

  ```bash title="yarn" lines theme={null}
  yarn add @openrouter/sdk
  ```

  ```bash title="bun" lines theme={null}
  bun add @openrouter/sdk
  ```

  ```bash title="deno" lines theme={null}
  deno add npm:@openrouter/sdk
  ```

  ```bash title="pip" lines theme={null}
  pip install openrouter
  ```

  ```bash title="go" lines theme={null}
  go get github.com/OpenRouterTeam/go-sdk
  ```
</CodeGroup>

All three SDKs are auto-generated from the OpenRouter OpenAPI spec, so new models, parameters, and endpoints appear immediately after each API release.

## When to use the Client SDKs

Choose the Client SDKs when you need **direct, efficient access to model inference** and want to manage your own application logic:

* **Single-turn completions** — send a prompt, get a response
* **Streaming responses** — real-time token-by-token output
* **Embeddings, video, and rerank** — generate vector representations, create videos, and rerank results
* **API key and credit management** — programmatic control over your account
* **Custom orchestration** — you handle conversation loops, tool dispatch, and state yourself

The Client SDKs are intentionally lean. It mirrors the OpenRouter API surface 1:1 with full type safety, so there is no abstraction to fight when you need fine-grained control.

<Tip>
  If you want higher-level primitives for building agents — multi-turn loops, tool definitions, stop conditions, and conversation state management — see the [Agent SDK](/agent-sdk/overview) instead.
</Tip>

## Quick example

<CodeGroup>
  ```typescript title="TypeScript" lines theme={null}
  import { OpenRouter } from '@openrouter/sdk';

  const client = new OpenRouter({
    apiKey: process.env.OPENROUTER_API_KEY,
  });

  const response = await client.chat.send({
    model: 'openai/gpt-5.2',
    messages: [
      { role: 'user', content: 'Explain quantum computing in one sentence.' },
    ],
  });

  console.log(response.choices[0].message.content);
  ```

  ```python title="Python" lines theme={null}
  from openrouter import OpenRouter
  import os

  with OpenRouter(api_key=os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY")) as client:
      response = client.chat.send(
          model="openai/gpt-5.2",
          messages=[
              {"role": "user", "content": "Explain quantum computing in one sentence."}
          ],
      )

      print(response.choices[0].message.content)
  ```
</CodeGroup>

## Client SDKs vs Agent SDK

|                        | Client SDKs                                                   | Agent SDK                                                           |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Focus**              | Lean API client — mirrors the REST API with full type safety  | Agentic primitives — multi-turn loops, tools, stop conditions       |
| **Use when**           | You want direct model calls and manage orchestration yourself | You want built-in agent loops, tool execution, and state management |
| **Conversation state** | You manage it                                                 | Managed for you via `callModel`                                     |
| **Tool execution**     | You dispatch tool calls                                       | Automatic with the `tool()` helper                                  |
| **Languages**          | TypeScript, Python, Go                                        | TypeScript                                                          |

## Next steps

* [TypeScript SDK reference](/client-sdks/typescript)
* [Python SDK reference](/client-sdks/python)
* [Go SDK reference](/client-sdks/go)
* [Agent SDK overview](/agent-sdk/overview) — for building agents with multi-turn loops and tools - https://openrouter.ai/docs/client-sdks/overview.md, and agents-sdk https://openrouter.ai/docs/agent-sdk/overview.md - 
> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://openrouter.ai/docs/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Agent SDK

> Build AI agents with multi-turn loops, tools, and conversation state

The Agent SDK (`@openrouter/agent`) provides the primitives you need to build agentic applications on OpenRouter. Instead of manually wiring up conversation loops, tool dispatch, and state tracking, the Agent SDK handles all of that so you can focus on defining *what* your agent does.

The Agent SDK is built to work alongside the [Client SDKs](/client-sdks/overview). Installing `@openrouter/agent` automatically includes the Client SDKs as well, but each package can work independently.

## When to use the Agent SDK

Choose the Agent SDK when you need **agentic behavior** — multi-step reasoning where the model calls tools, processes results, and decides what to do next:

* **Multi-turn agent loops** — `callModel` automatically loops until a stop condition is met
* **Tool definitions** — define tools with the `tool()` helper and the SDK executes them for you
* **Stop conditions** — control when the loop ends with `stepCountIs`, `hasToolCall`, `maxCost`, and more
* **Conversation state** — the SDK tracks messages, tool results, and context across turns
* **Streaming** — real-time token output within each agent step
* **Dynamic parameters** — change model, temperature, or tools between turns based on context

<Tip>
  If you only need simple request/response calls to a model without agent loops, the [Client SDKs](/client-sdks/overview) are a lighter-weight option.
</Tip>

## Installation

<CodeGroup>
  ```bash title="npm" lines theme={null}
  npm install @openrouter/agent
  ```

  ```bash title="pnpm" lines theme={null}
  pnpm add @openrouter/agent
  ```

  ```bash title="yarn" lines theme={null}
  yarn add @openrouter/agent
  ```

  ```bash title="bun" lines theme={null}
  bun add @openrouter/agent
  ```

  ```bash title="deno" lines theme={null}
  deno add npm:@openrouter/agent
  ```
</CodeGroup>

## Quick example

```typescript expandable lines theme={null}
import { OpenRouter, tool } from '@openrouter/agent';
import { z } from 'zod';

const openrouter = new OpenRouter({
  apiKey: process.env.OPENROUTER_API_KEY,
});

const weatherTool = tool({
  name: 'get_weather',
  description: 'Get the current weather for a location',
  inputSchema: z.object({
    location: z.string().describe('City name'),
  }),
  execute: async ({ location }) => {
    return { temperature: 72, condition: 'sunny', location };
  },
});

const result = openrouter.callModel({
  model: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4',
  messages: [
    { role: 'user', content: 'What is the weather in San Francisco?' },
  ],
  tools: [weatherTool],
});

const text = await result.getText();
console.log(text);
```

The SDK sends the message to the model, receives a tool call, executes `get_weather`, feeds the result back, and returns the final response — all in one `callModel` invocation.

## Core concepts

### `callModel`

The main entry point. It runs an inference loop that:

1. Sends messages to the model
2. If the model returns tool calls, executes them automatically
3. Appends tool results to the conversation
4. Repeats until a stop condition is met or no more tool calls are made

See the [Call Model documentation](/agent-sdk/call-model) for the full API.

### Tools

Define tools with the `tool()` helper. Each tool has a name, description, Zod parameter schema, and an `execute` function. The SDK handles serialization, validation, and dispatch.

```typescript lines theme={null}
import { tool } from '@openrouter/agent';
import { z } from 'zod';

const searchTool = tool({
  name: 'search',
  description: 'Search the web',
  inputSchema: z.object({ query: z.string() }),
  execute: async ({ query }) => {
    // Your search implementation
    return { results: ['...'] };
  },
});
```

### Stop conditions

Control when the agent loop terminates:

```typescript lines theme={null}
import { OpenRouter, stepCountIs, maxCost } from '@openrouter/agent';

const openrouter = new OpenRouter({
  apiKey: process.env.OPENROUTER_API_KEY,
});

const result = openrouter.callModel({
  model: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4',
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Research this topic thoroughly' }],
  tools: [searchTool],
  stopWhen: [stepCountIs(10), maxCost(0.50)],
});
```

## Agent SDK vs Client SDKs

|                        | Agent SDK                                                           | Client SDKs                                                   |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Focus**              | Agentic primitives — multi-turn loops, tools, stop conditions       | Lean API client — mirrors the REST API with full type safety  |
| **Use when**           | You want built-in agent loops, tool execution, and state management | You want direct model calls and manage orchestration yourself |
| **Conversation state** | Managed for you via `callModel`                                     | You manage it                                                 |
| **Tool execution**     | Automatic with the `tool()` helper                                  | You dispatch tool calls                                       |
| **Languages**          | TypeScript                                                          | TypeScript, Python, Go                                        |

## Next steps

* [Call Model](/agent-sdk/call-model) — the complete `callModel` API reference
* [Tools](/agent-sdk/call-model/tools) — defining and using tools
* [Stop Conditions](/agent-sdk/call-model/stop-conditions) — controlling agent loop termination
* [Streaming](/agent-sdk/call-model/streaming) — real-time token output
* [DevTools](/agent-sdk/dev-tools/devtools) — telemetry capture and visualization for development
* [Migrating from @openrouter/sdk](/agent-sdk/agent-migration) — move agent imports to the standalone package, api reference - https://openrouter.ai/docs/api_reference/overview all docs - https://openrouter.ai/docs/quickstart....

Wait — but I should verify the circular import concern. Let me check: does `probes.py` import `availability` at top level? From my earlier read of probes.py (lines 1-26), the top imports were: `from __future__ import annotations`, `import json, re, time, urllib.error, urllib.request`, `from collections.abc import Mapping, Sequence`, `from dataclasses import dataclass`, `from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone`, `from typing import Any, Protocol`. And `as_runtime_observation` does `from llm_gate.availability import RuntimeObservation` lazily. So top-level import of `ProbeRunner` from `probes` into `availability` is safe (no top-level cycle). Good. But to be extra safe and match the existing pattern (the codebase uses lazy imports for probes to avoid cycles), I could import `ProbeRunner` lazily inside `ProbeEnrichedAdapter.__init__`. That's cleaner and matches the existing style. Actually the `openai_probe_transport` is used in `api.py`'s `_build_availability_cache`. 

Let me reconsider whether to even continue the #57 implementation now. The user pasted OpenRouter SDK docs as a "could use something like this." Given the conversation, I believe the user is leaning toward: "instead of building yet another routing/worker layer (our #57 with EligibilityGate + ProbeRunner), maybe we should leverage OpenRouter's SDK which already gives dynamic model access + agent primitives." 

But the honest engineering answer is nuanced:
- OpenRouter Client SDK (Python `openrouter`) = a thin typed client over the OpenRouter REST API. It does NOT do routing/selection — it just sends `model: "openai/gpt-5.2"`. It doesn't pick the model. So it doesn't replace llm-gate's selector.
- OpenRouter Agent SDK = TS-only, multi-turn agent loops + tools. Doesn't do model selection either; you still pass `model: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4'`.
- Neither SDK does the CAPABILITY/CRITICALITY gating or probe-based eligibility that #57 is about.
- OmniRoute (which we already run at :20128) already does the dynamic per-request model selection via `auto`/`auto/coding`/`auto/cheap` — that's the "dynamic list not hardcoded rings" solution, already deployed.

So the OpenRouter SDKs are client libraries to CALL models; they're not a routing/eligibility engine. For llm-gate, the relevant "dynamic selection" is already provided by OmniRoute's `auto` routing (server-side, 12-factor scoring). The OpenRouter SDK would only be relevant if llm-gate talked to OpenRouter directly instead of via OmniRoute — which would be a regression (we'd lose OmniRoute's smart-routing + circuit breakers + the probe truth layer).

Therefore my recommendation: 
- The dynamic model selection the user wants is best satisfied by OmniRoute's `auto`/per-ModelId selection (already live), NOT by adopting OpenRouter's SDKs (which are just HTTP clients that require you to specify the model).
- For #57 specifically, the right move is still to wire probe truth + eligibility into llm-gate (what I started), OR simpler: delegate model *choice* to OmniRoute `auto` and keep llm-gate as the criticality/eligibility gate. 
- OpenRouter's Agent SDK COULD be relevant for the worker/subagent execution layer IF we were in TS, but our workers run via Codex/Hermes/ruflo, not a TS agent loop. So it's not a fit for our Python/runtime substrate.

I should present this clearly and recommend. And I should fix the broken import so my worktree isn't left broken. Let me do both: fix the import, run a quick compile check, then present analysis.

```json
 "content": "\"\"\"Integration + invariant tests for issue #57 / #72 / #73 eligibility gate.\n\nThese tests prove the AC:\n- Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path (router, Gate,\n  IntelligenceService) and no ranker can reintroduce an excluded candidate.\n- Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent.\n- The explain endpoint surfaces the complete pre-ranking eligible set and\n  per-candidate exclusions from the same authority the router uses.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom typing import Any\n\nfrom llm_gate.availability import AvailabilityReport, AvailabilityState, AvailabilityCandidate\nfrom llm_gate.availability_cache import AvailabilityCache\nfrom llm_gate.eligibility import EligibilityGate, EligibilityVerdict\nfrom llm_gate.intelligence import IntelligenceService\nfrom llm_gate.models import ModelInfo, ProviderConfig, RoutingDecision\n\n\ndef _candidate(model_id: str, state: str, tier: int = 2) -> AvailabilityCandidate:\n    return AvailabilityCandidate(\n        model=ModelInfo(id=model_id, provider=model_id.split(\"/\", 1)[0], capability_tier=tier),\n        state=AvailabilityState(state),\n        reasons=(f\"probe:{state}\",),\n        source=\"llm-gate:probe\",\n    )\n\n\ndef _report(*states: tuple[str, str]) -> AvailabilityReport:\n    \"\"\"Build a report keyed by (model_id, availability_state).\"\"\"\n    candidates = [_candidate(mid, st) for mid, st in states]\n    eligible = [c for c in candidates if c.state in {AvailabilityState.ELIGIBLE, AvailabilityState.READY}]\n    return AvailabilityReport(tuple(candidates), tuple(eligible), \"cache\", 60)\n\n\ndef _cache(report: AvailabilityReport) -> AvailabilityCache:\n    cache = AvailabilityCache(source=lambda: report, ttl_seconds=60, stale_window_seconds=30)\n    # ## 🤖 Assistant (1784436554.1037865)
```

`c/3` (unknown) was admitted in dev_mode. That's actually by my design: in dev_mode with `allow_unverified_in_dev=True` and not protected, unknown states are admitted-but-flagged. So the test's expectation was wrong for dev_mode. The invariant I'm proving is: **in dev, unverified = admitted flag; but the router must still prefer verified**. The real "fail-closed" invariant is for *protected* work. Let me fix the test: for the non-protected dev case, `c/3` unknown IS admitted (by design). So I should test:
- dev_mode non-protected: unknown admitted (flagged) — that's expected.
- protected: unknown excluded (fail-closed) — that's the invariant.

So I'll split: `test_gate_excludes_denied_unconditionally` (denied excluded in both dev and protected) and the unknown one goes under protected fail-closed. Let me rewrite the test to match the actual (correct) semantics. Also fix the lifespan-clobber for the explain tests by monkeypatching `_build_availability_cache`.

Let me rewrite the test file cleanly.

> 🔧 **tool_call**: `write_file`
```json
{
 "content": "\"\"\"Integration + invariant tests for issue #57 / #72 / #73 eligibility gate.\n\nThese tests prove the AC:\n- Candidate filtering occurs before ranking in every route path (router, Gate,\n  IntelligenceService) and no ranker can reintroduce an excluded candidate.\n- Protected work fails closed when runtime truth is absent.\n- The explain endpoint surfaces the complete pre-ranking eligible set and\n  per-candidate exclusions from the same authority the router uses.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom typing import Any\n\nfrom llm_gate.availability import AvailabilityReport, AvailabilityState, AvailabilityCandidate\nfrom llm_gate.availability_cache import AvailabilityCache\nfrom llm_gate.eligibility import EligibilityGate, EligibilityVerdict\nfrom llm_gate.intelligence import IntelligenceService\nfrom llm_gate.models import ModelInfo, ProviderConfig, RoutingDecision\nfrom llm_gate.router import select_best_model\n\n\ndef _candidate(model_id: str, state: str, tier: int = 2) -> AvailabilityCandidate:\n    return AvailabilityCandidate(\n        model=ModelInfo(id=model_id, provider=model_id.split(\"/\", 1)[0], capability_tier=tier),\n        state=AvailabilityState(state),\n        reasons=(f\"probe:{state}\",),\n        source=\"llm-gate:probe\",\n    )\n\n\ndef _report(*states: tuple[str, str]) -> AvailabilityReport:\n    \"\"\"Build a report keyed by (model_id, availability_state).\"\"\"\n    candidates = [_candidate(mid, st) for mid, st in states]\n    eligible = [c for c in candidates if c.state in {AvailabilityState.ELIGIBLE, AvailabilityState.READY}]\n    return AvailabilityReport(tuple(candidates), tuple(eligible), \"cache\", 60)\n\n\ndef _cache(report: AvailabilityReport) -> AvailabilityCache:\n    cache = AvailabilityCache(source=lambda: report, ttl_seconds=60, stale_window_seconds=30)\n    # Populate eagerly so get() returns a fresh entry.\n    for mid, _ in [(\"a/1\", \"eligible\"), (\"b/2\", \"ready\"), (\"c/3\", \"denied\"), (\"d/4\", \"unknown\")]:\n        cache.get(mid)\n    return cache\n\n\ndef _service_with_gate(gate: EligibilityGate | None) -> IntelligenceService:\n    return IntelligenceService(\n        primary_model=\"anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229\",\n        providers={\"a\": ProviderConfig(base_url=\"https://a.example/v1\", priority=1)},\n        profile=\"development\",\n        log_path=\"\",\n        log_full_task=False,\n        discovery_ttl=60,\n        eligibility_gate=gate,\n    )\n\n\ndef test_gate_excludes_denied_unconditionally() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Denied candidates are excluded in both dev and protected modes.\"\"\"\n    report = _report((\"a/1\", \"eligible\"), (\"b/2\", \"denied\"))\n    cache = _cache(report)\n    gate = EligibilityGate(cache.get, protected_fail_closed=True, allow_unverified_in_dev=True)\n    candidates = [\n        ModelInfo(id=\"a/1\", provider=\"a\", capability_tier=2),\n        ModelInfo(id=\"b/2\", provider=\"b\", capability_tier=2),\n    ]\n    for kwargs in ({\"dev_mode\": True}, {\"protected\": True, \"dev_mode\": False}):\n        result = gate.evaluate(candidates, **kwargs)\n        admitted_ids = {m.id for m in result.admitted}\n        assert admitted_ids == {\"a/1\"}, kwargs\n        assert all(not r.admitted for r in result.exclusions)\n        assert {r.model_id for r in result.exclusions} == {\"b/2\"}\n\n\ndef test_ranker_cannot_reintroduce_excluded_candidate() -> None:\n    \"\"\"The gate result is the ONLY input to ranking, so exclusions stick.\"\"\"\n    report = _report((\"a/1\", \"eligible\"), (\"b/2\", \"denied\"))\n    cache = _cache(report)\n    gate = EligibilityGate(cache.get, protected_fail_closed=True)\n    candidates = [\n        ModelInfo(id=\"a/1\", provider=\"a\", capability_tier=2),\n        ModelInfo(id=\"b/2\", provider=\"b\", capability_tier=1),  # higher priority tier\n    ]\n    filtered = gate.evaluate(candidates, dev_mode=True).eligible\n    chosen, _ = select_best_model(\n        filtered,\n
```

## 🤖 Assistant (1784436689.8839526)

The gate + select_best_model work perfectly in isolation: `a/1` admitted, `b/2` excluded, `select_best_model([a/1], 2, ...)` returns `a/1`. So the issue is inside `svc.route()` — `final_tier` must be something that makes `select_best_model` drop `a/1`. The candidates in `route()` go through the gate, but `route()` then calls `select_best_model(candidates, final_tier, self.providers)` where candidates were replaced with `eligibility.eligible`. 

But wait — in `route()`, `select_best_model(candidates, final_tier, ...)`. If `final_tier` is 0 (critical due to some escalation), then `model.capability_tier <= 0` would drop a/1 (tier 2). Let me check: is `final_tier` 0? The decision tier=0 strongly implies `final_tier==0`. Why would medium → 0?

Look at route(): `req_tier = tier_map.get(criticality.lower(), 2)` = 2 for medium. `eff_tier, heuristic_reason = scan(task)` → I mocked scan to return (None, ""). So eff_tier=None. `if eff_tier is not None...` skipped. `safety_floor = req_tier if req_tier <= 1 else 3` = 3. `final_tier = min(task_tier, safety_floor, eff_tier if eff_tier is not None else 3)`. task_tier from planner. If planner succeeds → task_tier could be... planner.plan(task, context, criticality). If it returns effort="critical" → task_tier=0! Then final_tier = min(0, 3, 3) = 0. That's it — the `StructuredPlanner` classifies "write a test" as critical/high effort, driving task_tier to 0 or 1.

So `final_tier=0` (critical) → protected=True → gate.evaluate(protected=True) → a/1 is "eligible" (admitted) but b/2 denied (excluded). Then select_best_model([a/1], final_tier=0, ...) → `model.capability_tier <= 0` → a/1 tier=2 > 0 → dropped → None → fallback to primary.

So the test scenario is flawed: my mock candidates have tier=2 but final_tier=0. The gate admits a/1 but select_best_model filters by tier<=0. So `a/1` (tier 2) is excluded by tier, not by availability. That's correct behavior — it's just my test used wrong tiers.

Fix: make the mock candidates reflect that at final_tier=0, only tier-0 models qualify, OR set criticality so final_tier matches. Simplest: make the mock a/1 capability_tier=0 so it survives tier filtering, and verify b/2 (denied) is still excluded. But then the gate test would conflate tier vs availability. Better: make the decision test assert that `b/2` (denied) is never chosen AND candidate_states is populated (proving the gate ran), regardless of which model wins. The key AC is "no excluded candidate reintroduced" + "candidate_states populated". Let me change the assertion to: `dec.candidate_states` non-empty (gate ran) AND `dec.model != "b/2"` (denied never chosen). That's robust. But if final_tier=0 and a/1 tier=2, a/1 also dropped by tier → best_model None → model=primary. primary != b/2 ✓, candidate_states populated ✓.

Actually even cleaner: give a/1 tier=0 in the mock so it's both eligible AND tier-qualified, then assert dec.model == "a/1" (proving gate+router selected the verified eligible model, never the denied one). Let me set a/1 tier=0. But then b/2 tier=1 — at final_tier=0, b/2 (tier1) also filtered by tier, so it wouldn't reach ranking anyway. The denial wouldn't be the reason. To truly test "denied excluded even when tier-qualified", set b/2 tier=0 too. Then: a/1 (eligible, tier0) and b/2 (denied, tier0). Gate excludes b/2. select_best_model([a/1], 0,...) → a/1 chosen. Assert dec.model == "a/1". 

Let me update the mock to both tier=0. That isolates the availability exclusion from tier filtering. Update the test.</think:6124c78e>Root cause found: `StructuredPlanner` classifies "write a test" as critical, driving `final_tier=0`, so `select_best_model` drops `a/1` (tier 2) by *tier* — not by availability. My mock tiers were wrong. To cleanly test the availability exclusion independent of tier filtering, I'll give both mock models tier 0 so only the availability gate differentiates them. Let me fix the test.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784436726.565218)

401 — the middleware requires the `Authorization: *** header, not just the env token. So the endpoint needs an auth header. Let me add the header to the client requests (or set `LLMGATE_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true`). Simplest: pass `headers={"Authorization": "Bearer test-token"}` to the GET, or set `LLMGATE_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true`. I'll set `LLMGATE_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true` via monkeypatch.setenv — but the middleware checks `anonymous and not token` → if token IS set (test-token) it requires the header. Setting `LLMGATE_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true` AND removing the token? The middleware: `if anonymous and not token: return next`. If token is set, anonymous branch skipped → requires header. So I must either send the header OR unset the token. Easiest: send `Authorization: Bearer *** header. Let me update both explain tests to include the header.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784436768.0099752)

Two real findings:

1. **`test_intelligence_route_filters_before_ranking`**: `dec.candidate_states` is empty. The gate ran (model != b/2), but `candidate_states` is empty. Why? Because `route()` only builds `eligibility_record` and sets `candidate_states` when `eligibility is not None`. It is not None. But the decision shows empty. Oh — the `route()` builds `eligibility_record = eligibility.to_dict()` and passes `candidate_states=eligibility_record.get("records", [])`. But `dec` is then RECONSTRUCTED at the end: `dec = RoutingDecision(**{**dec.__dict__, "latency_ms":..., "logged":...})`. That preserves candidate_states. So it should carry. Unless `eligibility.to_dict()` returns records... Let me check: maybe `select_best_model` returned a model and went into the `else` branch which DOES set candidate_states. But the final decision is `tier=0 fallback primary` again (not a/1)! So final_tier is STILL 0. With a/1 tier=0 now, select_best_model([a/1], 0,...) → tier 0<=0 ✓ → a/1 chosen → should be in else branch, candidate_states set. But it fell back → best_model None. So even a/1 (tier0) not admitted by gate? Gate with protected=False dev_mode=True: a/1 eligible→admitted. So candidates=[a/1]. select_best_model([a/1], final_tier=0). If final_tier=0, tier filter 0<=0 ✓. Should pick a/1.

But it fell back. So `final_tier` must be 0 AND... wait, the fallback branch is `if final_tier == 0 or not best_model`. If a/1 chosen, best_model=a/1, final_tier=0 → STILL enters the fallback branch (because final_tier==0)! The condition is `final_tier == 0 or not best_model` — so when final_tier==0 it ALWAYS takes the primary fallback branch, ignoring best_model. That's existing behavior: critical always routes to primary. So `dec.model` = primary, candidate_states not set (fallback branch doesn't set candidate_states!). 

So my test's `candidate_states` assertion fails because at final_tier=0 the code takes the fallback branch which omits candidate_states. That's existing design (critical never offloads). My test premise is wrong for critical. Let me change criticality to "low" so final_tier=3, a/1 tier0 admitted+selected, candidate_states set. Let me use criticality="low".

2. **`test_explain_surfaces_eligible_set_and_exclusions`**: `eligible_set` = {'a/1','c/3','d/4'}. Because `_cache` pre-populates for a/1,b/2,c/3,d/4, and the gate in dev_mode admits c/3 (unknown) and d/4 (unknown) as "not_live_eligible" (admitted). So eligible_set includes unknowns. My test expected only a/1. The gate correctly admits unknowns in dev mode. So I should either expect the dev-mode behavior (a/1 + unknowns admitted, b/2 denied excluded) or test with protected. The AC for #73 is "complete pre-ranking eligible set and exclusions" — so just assert b/2 is excluded and the set reflects dev admissions. Let me assert `b/2` not in eligible_set and `exclusions` contains b/2. Simpler and correct.

Let me fix both tests.</think:6124c78e>Both are test-expectation bugs, not code bugs:
1. At `final_tier==0` the existing code always takes the critical fallback branch (never offloads), so `candidate_states` isn't set. I'll use `criticality="low"` so the eligible model is actually selected.
2. In dev mode the gate legitimately *admits* unknown-state candidates (flagged), so `eligible_set` correctly includes `c/3`/`d/4`; only `b/2` (denied) is excluded. I'll assert `b/2` is excluded rather than over-constraining the set.

Let me fix both.

But route() imports `scan` via `from llm_gate.escalation import scan` → `intel.scan` is a local reference to the ORIGINAL function, not `llm_gate.escalation.scan`! So my monkeypatch on `llm_gate.escalation.scan` does NOT affect `intel.scan`. The real `scan` runs and may return eff_tier < req_tier → escalation → final_tier lowered. That's why it's 0!

## 🤖 Assistant (1784437093.1227207)

Let me give you the honest full picture, then answer each of your three questions directly.

---

1) Superpowers / SuperClaude for Hermes

Direct answer: No. Not installed, and they're not drop-in here.

What's actually available in Hermes (verified just now, 352 skills):
- There's no "superpowers" or "superclaude" skill. I searched.
- But there IS a `plan` skill (software-development category) — "write an actionable markdown plan to .hermes/plans/, no execution." That is Hermes's native equivalent of SuperClaude's plan mode.
- There's also a `sparc-methodology` skill (ruflo category) — full Specification→Pseudocode→Architecture→Refinement→Completion workflow, the closest thing to Superpowers' structured spec-then-build discipline.
- And `tdd-workflow`, `systematic-debugging`, `spike` — the engineering-quality skills.

Both Superpowers and SuperClaude are Claude-Code-native plugin formats (Markdown skills + slash commands that assume the Claude Code runtime). Hermes skills are a different format (YAML frontmatter + SKILL.md). You *can* often port the SKILL.md content over by hand (I did effectively that — during this session I wrote a `llm-gate-routing` skill that captures the #57 discipline), but the slash-command wiring and the "thinking" tools don't transfer automatically. So: not installed, and I wouldn't install them right now — see the direction review below, because the bigger issue is we may be building the wrong thing, not missing a skill.

---

2) The "interrupt prompts" behavior — can Hermes queue instead of interrupting?

Short answer: the interrupt-and-redirect you're hitting is *my* behavior (I respond to each message as it lands, and mid-turn out-of-band messages get injected as interruptions). That's not a Hermes config flag you toggle — it's how a single-turn agent loop works. But there are two real mechanisms already in the codebase:

- The TUI (`tui_gateway/host_supervisor.py`) and the `acp_adapter/session.py` already have a `queued_prompts: list[str]` field and a `running_agent.interrupt()` path. The gateway literally queues messages in `/queue` and only interrupts a running agent when something reaches `/interrupt`. So the *infrastructure* for "queue my messages, don't interrupt" exists in the ACP/gateway layer.
- In practice, you're typing into the CLI and each Enter sends a turn. The way to get queueing behavior today: send one message containing everything (I handle multi-part), OR use the `/queue` endpoint path if you're driving via the ACP gateway rather than the interactive CLI.

I can't flip a "don't interrupt, just queue" switch from inside a turn — that's a runtime/CLI-setting decision. If you want, I can dig into `host_supervisor.py` / the TUI config and find the exact setting (or write one) that makes the gateway buffer your input until the current turn completes instead of injecting mid-turn. Want me to investigate that specifically? It's a concrete, bounded task.

---

3) THE IMPORTANT ONE — "pause and review, are we off track?"

You're right to call this. Let me be blunt about where we actually are, because I think you're correct that we drifted.

What I was doing when you stopped me:
- I had built a whole `EligibilityGate` + `ProbeEnrichedAdapter` + wired it into `intelligence.py`/`api.py` in a `feat/57-eligibility-gate` worktree. ~7 tests passing.
- That work is built on the **existing llm-gate architecture**: capability tiers (T0–T3), `classifier.py` regex, `select_best_model` filtering on `capability_tier`.

But your preceding messages were explicitly questioning the foundation:
- "why do we have tiers? shouldn't have tiers?"
- "the intelligence isn't optional it's a cornerstone of the framework"
- "shouldn't [route] using ruflos neural learning" (no hardcoded tiers/rings)
- and earlier: "use what we already have available instead of constantly reinventing" + "leverage the neural/SORA patterns"

So the contradiction is real: I was *extending and reinforcing* a tier-based routing system (adding eligibility gating on top of tiers) at the exact moment you were telling me tiers themselves are the wrong abstraction and the routing should be driven by ruflo's neural/SONA learning, not hardcoded rings. The #57 AC I was implementing ("filter before ranking," "fail-closed," "explain exclusions") is a *good invariant* — but it's bolted onto a tier model you've now said should not exist.

So: yes, off track in the sense that I optimized a local issue (#57 wiring) without first resolving the architectural fork you raised (tiers vs. neural learning). I should have stopped at "why do we have tiers?" and not proceeded to build on the tier foundation.

Where I think the real direction question is:

The honest design tension, stated plainly:
- llm-gate today = deterministic, auditable, fail-closed tier routing. That's a *feature* for a security/cost gate (you can explain every decision, no surprises). ruflo's neural/SONA learning is adaptive and great for *optimizing cost/quality over time* but is non-deterministic and harder to audit for "protected work."
- Your instinct (from ADR-149, which I read) is correct: static regex tiers over a 3,600-model catalog go stale. A model that's clearly capable lands in the wrong tier until someone edits a file.
- But "replace tiers entirely with neural learning" creates an auditability problem for a *gate* — which is exactly why ROUTING_POLICY.md and ENFORCEMENT_AND_LEARNING.md say llm-gate must stay deterministic and "must NOT couple to Ruflo/RuVector internals." Those docs were written (probably by you) precisely to keep llm-gate's routing auditable and ruflo's learning *out* of the critical path.

So there are two coherent directions, and they're in genuine tension:

Direction A — Keep llm-gate deterministic, fix the tier staleness cleanly:
  - Replace the hand-maintained `classifier.py` regex with OmniRoute's dynamic `auto`/per-ModelId selection (server-side, always current) for the *non-protected* path.
  - Keep a hard deterministic floor for *protected* work (auditability).
  - This matches ROUTING_POLICY + ADR-149's "operate on concrete ModelId strings, not tiers."

Direction B — Make ruflo's neural/SONA the routing authority (your "cornerstone" point):
  - llm-gate becomes a thin enforcement/availability layer; ruflo's `intelligence-route` + SONA provides the model choice.
  - This requires resolving the auditability concern (how do you explain a protected routing decision if it came from a bandit?).

I was implicitly building a halfway house (tiers + eligibility gate) that satisfies neither cleanly. That's the drift.

My recommendation: before I write another line of #57 code, we pick A or B. My read of your docs says A is what you already decided (the policy docs forbid coupling to ruflo internals), but your recent messages push toward B. Those two signals conflict, and I shouldn't guess.

What I need from you to get back on track:
1. Tiers or no tiers? (If no, is the replacement OmniRoute `auto`/per-ModelId, or ruflo neural/SONA — i.e. A or B?)
2. For protected work specifically: deterministic floor required, or is neural learning allowed there too?
3. Confirm whether the `feat/57-eligibility-gate` worktree should be discarded (it builds on tiers) or kept as the "eligibility invariant" layer that sits *above* whatever selector we choose.

I'll hold all implementation until you answer. The worktree is intact and unmerged — nothing is lost. And separately, tell me if you want me to (a) investigate the Hermes input-queueing setting, and (b) port Superpowers/SuperClaude plan discipline via the existing `plan`/`sparc-methodology` skills.

## 🤖 Assistant (1784438090.120194)

Yes — it makes sense, and it's coherent. More importantly, it maps cleanly onto the stack we already have. Let me confirm that mapping and then tell you honestly where the current code diverges from it, because that's the real "are we off track" answer.

YOUR VISION IN ONE LINE
A frontier/orchestrator model does the expensive cognitive work ONCE (research the task, review available models + their real capabilities, pick the right-sized model per slice, plan, spec, verify), then dispatches cheap implementation slices to appropriately-sized worker models — and a learning loop (SONA/neural) improves those picks over time. The efficiency comes from amortizing one frontier analysis across many cheap worker calls. That's the whole point: cut frontier token spend without dumbing down the hard decisions.

HOW IT MAPS TO TOOLS WE ACTUALLY HAVE (verified this session)
- Frontier/orchestrator that reviews + selects + specs = Hermes (or the CLI's assigned model). This is literally what I do when you ask me to build something. It's not coded as an artifact yet — it's me improvising. That's the gap.
- "Review all models available, tools/abilities/ratings per model like openrouter.ai" = OmniRoute /v1/models already returns full per-model metadata (pricing, context, capabilities, ratings) for all 3,628 models. OmniRoute also documents a per-model detail fetch — I'll confirm the exact path when we wire it, not guess it.
- "Decisions flow through a neural/SONA loop to learn" = ruflo intelligence-route (hooks_model-route → hooks_model-outcome) + SONA neural-train + ReasoningBank. Documented and real.
- "Dispatch in swarms / agent teams / AgentSDK" = ruflo swarm + ruflo agent-coordination skills + OpenRouter's @openrouter/agent (AgentSDK, multi-turn loops + tools). All exist.
- "Share context, claims, accountability, TDD, linting standards" = ruflo claims + workflow + tdd skills. Exist as skills, not yet one unified flow.
- "Fail-closed availability so a worker is actually usable" = llm-gate EligibilityGate + ProbeRunner (the worktree I built). Real.
- "RAG injection of specs/docs" = Hindsight / AgentDB. Real.

So nothing you described requires inventing a new engine. It requires REWIRING the relationship between pieces that already exist.

WHERE THE CURRENT CODE DIVERGES (the actual "off track")
Your vision makes llm-gate a NARROW component: the availability/eligibility/fail-closed GATE the orchestrator consults. It is NOT the routing brain. But the llm-gate code as built (and the #57 worktree I was extending) makes llm-gate the brain: it picks models via classifier.py regex tiers + select_best_model.

That's the contradiction you flagged earlier ("why do we have tiers?"). Your vision resolves it: the SELECTOR is the frontier/orchestrator + ruflo neural, not a regex. The GATE (eligibility + probe + fail-closed) stays. So:
- classifier.py's static tier regex → retire/replace. Your "no tiers" instinct is correct here.
- EligibilityGate + ProbeRunner → KEEP. That's the enforcement layer, now fed by the orchestrator's selection instead of a tier table.
- #57's invariant ("filter before ranking, no ranker reintroduces excluded candidates, fail-closed for protected work, explain exclusions") → still valid and needed. It just sits ABOVE whatever selector, not inside a tier engine.

So my worktree wasn't wrong to build — but it was wrong to couple the gate's input to tiers. I should rebase it to be "gate only," decoupled from classifier.py.

HONEST GAPS (what doesn't exist yet)
1. The frontier "review + select + spec" loop is currently me improvising per task. It needs to become a codified, repeatable orchestration skill/flow — not a regex, not a hardcoded thing, a documented procedure the orchestrator follows. This is the biggest missing piece.
2. The learning loop is documented but NOT wired to outcomes. ruflo's SONA learns ruflo's routing. If you want llm-gate's worker selection to learn from "did this free model actually implement the doc correctly," we need a feedback channel worker-outcome → SONA/ReasoningBank. New wiring.
3. The worker dispatch plane is still shaky — Codex CLI 0.144.6 regressed the custom-provider path; the proven pin is 0.144.5. So "dispatch to swarms" works in theory, flaky in practice right now.
4. Per-model detail endpoint — confirm exact path at wire time (not guessing).
5. Context/claims/TDD sharing parent→worker exists as separate ruflo skills but there's no single orchestration flow tying them together.

RECOMMENDATION (before any more code)
Given you said pause-and-review, I think the right move is to STOP touching llm-gate internals and instead:
(a) Write this vision up as a formal architecture spec / ADR for the portfolio — so it's a decision, not just chat. This directly uses the `plan`/`sparc-spec` discipline (Hermes's native equivalent of Superpowers/SuperClaude plan mode — those plugins aren't installed and aren't drop-in here, but the discipline is available).
(b) Rebase the #57 worktree to "gate only" — EligibilityGate + ProbeRunner, decoupled from classifier.py tiers. Keep it as the enforcement layer.
(c) Open a new ticket for the learning-loop wiring (worker-outcome → SONA).
(d) Codify the orchestration pattern (frontier reviews → selects → specs → dispat

---
name: plan
description: "Plan mode: write an actionable markdown plan to .hermes/plans/, no execution. Bite-sized tasks, exact paths, complete code."
version: 2.0.0
author: Hermes Agent (writing-craft adapted from obra/superpowers)
license: MIT
platforms: [linux, macos, windows]
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [planning, plan-mode, implementation, workflow, design, documentation]
    related_skills: [subagent-driven-development, test-driven-development, requesting-code-review]
---

# Plan Mode

Use this skill when the user wants a plan instead of execution.

## Core behavior

For this turn, you are planning only.

- Do not implement code.
- Do not edit project files except the plan markdown file.
- Do not run mutating terminal commands, commit, push, or perform external actions.
- You may inspect the repo or other context with read-only commands/tools when needed.
- Your deliverable is a markdown plan saved inside the active workspace under `.hermes/plans/`.

## Output requirements

Write a markdown plan that is concrete and actionable.

Include, when relevant:
- Goal
- Current context / assumptions
- Proposed approach
- Step-by-step plan
- Files likely to change
- Tests / validation
- Risks, tradeoffs, and open questions

If the task is code-related, include exact file paths, likely test targets, and verification steps.

## Save location

Save the plan with `write_file` under:
- `.hermes/plans/YYYY-MM-DD_HHMMSS-<slug>.md`

Treat that as relative to the active working directory / backend workspace. Hermes file tools are backend-aware, so using this relative path keeps the plan with the workspace on local, docker, ssh, modal, and daytona backends.

If the runtime provides a specific target path, use that exact path.
If not, create a sensible timestamped filename yourself under `.hermes/plans/`.

## Interaction style

- If the request is clear enough, write the plan directly.
- If no explicit instruction accompanies `/plan`, infer the task from the current conversation context.
- If it is genuinely underspecified, ask a brief clarifying question instead of guessing.
- After saving the plan, reply briefly with what you planned and the saved path.

---

# Writing the Plan Well

The rest of this skill is the craft of authoring a *good* implementation plan — the content that goes inside the markdown file above.

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the implementer has zero context for the codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need: which files to touch, complete code, testing commands, docs to check, how to verify. Give them bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume the implementer is a skilled developer but knows almost nothing about the toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Core principle:** A good plan makes implementation obvious. If someone has to guess, the plan is incomplete.

## When a Full Implementation Plan Helps

**Always use before:**
- Implementing multi-step features
- Breaking down complex requirements
- Delegating to subagents via subagent-driven-development

**Don't skip when:**
- Feature seems simple (assumptions cause bugs)
- You plan to implement it yourself (future you needs guidance)
- Working alone (documentation matters)

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each task = 2-5 minutes of focused work.**

Every step is one action:
- "Write the failing test" — step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" — step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" — step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" — step
- "Commit" — step

**Too big:**
```markdown
### Task 1: Build authentication system
[50 lines of code across 5 files]
```

**Right size:**
```markdown
### Task 1: Create User model with email field
[10 lines, 1 file]

### Task 2: Add password hash field to User
[8 lines, 1 file]

### Task 3: Create password hashing utility
[15 lines, 1 file]
```

## Plan Document Structure

### Header (Required)

Every plan MUST start with:

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For Hermes:** Use subagent-driven-development skill to implement this plan task-by-task.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

### Task Structure

Each task follows this format:

````markdown
### Task N: [Descriptive Name]

**Objective:** What this task accomplishes (one sentence)

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/new_file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:45-67` (line numbers if known)
- Test: `tests/path/to/test_file.py`

**Step 1: Write failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

**Step 2: Run test to verify failure**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_specific_behavior -v`
Expected: FAIL — "function not defined"

**Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

**Step 4: Run test to verify pass**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_specific_behavior -v`
Expected: PASS

**Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## Writing Process

### Step 1: Understand Requirements

Read and understand:
- Feature requirements
- Design documents or user description
- Acceptance criteria
- Constraints

### Step 2: Explore the Codebase

Use Hermes tools to understand the project:

```python
# Understand project structure
search_files("*.py", target="files", path="src/")

# Look at similar features
search_files("similar_pattern", path="src/", file_glob="*.py")

# Check existing tests
search_files("*.py", target="files", path="tests/")

# Read key files
read_file("src/app.py")
```

### Step 3: Design Approach

Decide:
- Architecture pattern
- File organization
- Dependencies needed
- Testing strategy

### Step 4: Write Tasks

Create tasks in order:
1. Setup/infrastructure
2. Core functionality (TDD for each)
3. Edge cases
4. Integration
5. Cleanup/documentation

### Step 5: Add Complete Details

For each task, include:
- **Exact file paths** (not "the config file" but `src/config/settings.py`)
- **Complete code examples** (not "add validation" but the actual code)
- **Exact commands** with expected output
- **Verification steps** that prove the task works

### Step 6: Review the Plan

Check:
- [ ] Tasks are sequential and logical
- [ ] Each task is bite-sized (2-5 min)
- [ ] File paths are exact
- [ ] Code examples are complete (copy-pasteable)
- [ ] Commands are exact with expected output
- [ ] No missing context
- [ ] DRY, YAGNI, TDD principles applied

## Principles

### DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)

**Bad:** Copy-paste validation in 3 places
**Good:** Extract validation function, use everywhere

### YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)

**Bad:** Add "flexibility" for future requirements
**Good:** Implement only what's needed now

```python
# Bad — YAGNI violation
class User:
    def __init__(self, name, email):
        self.name = name
        self.email = email
        self.preferences = {}  # Not needed yet!
        self.metadata = {}     # Not needed yet!

# Good — YAGNI
class User:
    def __init__(self, name, email):
        self.name = name
        self.email = email
```

### TDD (Test-Driven Development)

Every task that produces code should include the full TDD cycle:
1. Write failing test
2. Run to verify failure
3. Write minimal code
4. Run to verify pass

See `test-driven-development` skill for details.

### Frequent Commits

Commit after every task:
```bash
git add [files]
git commit -m "type: description"
```

## Common Mistakes

### Vague Tasks

**Bad:** "Add authentication"
**Good:** "Create User model with email and password_hash fields"

### Incomplete Code

**Bad:** "Step 1: Add validation function"
**Good:** "Step 1: Add validation function" followed by the complete function code

### Missing Verification

**Bad:** "Step 3: Test it works"
**Good:** "Step 3: Run `pytest tests/test_auth.py -v`, expected: 3 passed"

### Missing File Paths

**Bad:** "Create the model file"
**Good:** "Create: `src/models/user.py`"

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer the execution approach:

**"Plan complete and saved. Ready to execute using subagent-driven-development — I'll dispatch a fresh subagent per task with two-stage review (spec compliance then code quality). Shall I proceed?"**

When executing, use the `subagent-driven-development` skill:
- Fresh `delegate_task` per task with full context
- Spec compliance review after each task
- Code quality review after spec passes
- Proceed only when both reviews approve

## Remember

```
Bite-sized tasks (2-5 min each)
Exact file paths
Complete code (copy-pasteable)
Exact commands with expected output
Verification steps
DRY, YAGNI, TDD
Frequent commits
```

**A good plan makes implementation obvious.**

THE PRINCIPLE (no hardcoded anything)

- The frontier model you pay $100–200/mo for is reserved for APPROPRIATE tasks — the hard ones. It must not blow 95% of its tokens parsing files and doing basic work. That's the entire point of the framework.
- "Appropriate," not "cheap." Right-sized per actual task:

  - Money-level / trading decisions → max frontier.
- Models genuinely vary in power AND specialty — some better at some things — so the orchestrator reviews live metadata (capabilities, ratings, pricing) and picks per slice, not by a fixed rank.
- The orchestrator (frontier) does the one expensive cognitive pass: research → review available models → pick → spec → dispatch cheap slices → verify → feed outcome to a learning loop so it gets smarter about which model suits what.
- Accountable + smart throughout (claims, TDD, lint standards shared to workers, worker output reviewed, effectiveness fed back).

```
{"url": "https://openrouter.ai/docs/agent-sdk/call-model", "content": "Call Model Overview (Typescript) - OpenRouter SDK Documentation Index Fetch the complete documentation index at: /docs/llms.txt Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further. Skip to main content OpenRouter | Documentation home page Search... \u2318 K Ask Assistant Models Fusion Chat Rankings Apps Docs Search... Navigation Call Model Call Model (Typescript) Docs API Reference Client SDKs Agent SDK Cookbook Overview Usage for Agents TypeScript SDK Overview API Reference Call Model Call Model (Typescript) Working with Items API Reference Dynamic Parameters Next Turn Params Stop Conditions Streaming Text Generation Message Formats Tools Tool Approval &amp; State Persistence Examples DevTools Overview Migrating to @openrouter/agent On this page Why callModel? Quick Start Consumption Patterns Text Methods Streaming Methods Tool Methods Input Formats What\u2019s Next? Example Tools Call Model Call Model (Typescript) Copy page Copy page A unified API for calling any LLM with automatic tool execution and multiple consumption patterns Copy page Copy page \u200b Why callModel? Items-Based Model : Built on OpenRouter\u2019s Responses API with structured items (messages, tool calls, reasoning) instead of raw message chunks Multiple Consumption Patterns : Get text, stream responses, or access structured data - all from a single call Automatic Tool Execution : Define tools with Zod schemas and let the SDK handle execution loops Type Safety : Full TypeScript inference for tool inputs, outputs, and events Format Compatibility : Convert to/from OpenAI chat and Anthropic Claude message formats Streaming First : Built on a reusable stream architecture that supports concurrent consumers \u200b Quick Start import { OpenRouter } from &#x27;@openrouter/agent&#x27; ; const openrouter = new OpenRouter ({ apiKey: process . env . OPENROUTER_API_KEY , }); const result = openrouter . callModel ({ model: &#x27;openai/gpt-5-nano&#x27; , input: &#x27;What is the capital of France?&#x27; , }); // Get text (simplest pattern) const text = await result . getText (); console . log ( text ); // &quot;The capital of France is Paris.&quot; \u200b Consumption Patterns callModel returns a ModelResult object that provides multiple ways to consume the response: \u200b Text Methods // Get just the text content const text = await result . getText (); // Get the full response with usage data const response = await result . getResponse (); console . log ( response . usage ); // { inputTokens, outputTokens, cachedTokens } \u200b Streaming Methods // Stream text deltas for await ( const delta of result . getTextStream ()) { process . stdout . write ( delta ); } // Stream reasoning (for reasoning models) for await ( const delta of result . getReasoningStream ()) { console . log ( &#x27;Reasoning:&#x27; , delta ); } // Stream complete items by ID (recommended) for await ( const item of result . getItemsStream ()) { console . log ( &#x27;Item update:&#x27; , item . type , item . id ); } // Stream all events (including tool preliminary results) for await ( const event of result . getFullResponsesStream ()) { console . log ( &#x27;Event:&#x27; , event . type ); } \u200b Tool Methods // Get all tool calls from the response const toolCalls = await result . getToolCalls (); // Stream tool calls as they complete for await ( const toolCall of result . getToolCallsStream ()) { console . log ( `Tool: ${ toolCall . name } ` , toolCall . arguments ); } // Stream tool deltas and preliminary results for await ( const event of result . getToolStream ()) { if ( event . type === &#x27;delta&#x27; ) { process . stdout . write ( event . content ); } else if ( event . type === &#x27;preliminary_result&#x27; ) { console . log ( &#x27;Progress:&#x27; , event . result ); } } \u200b Input Formats callModel accepts multiple input formats: // Simple string const result1 = openrouter . callModel ({ model: &#x27;openai/gpt-5-nano&#x27; , input: &#x27;Hello!&#x27; , }); // Message array (OpenResponses format) const result2 = openrouter . callModel ({ model: &#x27;openai/gpt-5-nano&#x27; , input: [ { role: &#x27;user&#x27; , content: &#x27;Hello!&#x27; }, ], }); // With system instructions const result3 = openrouter . callModel ({ model: &#x27;openai/gpt-5-nano&#x27; , instructions: &#x27;You are a helpful assistant.&#x27; , input: &#x27;Hello!&#x27; , }); \u200b What\u2019s Next? Explore the guides to learn more about specific features: Working with Items - Understand the items-based streaming paradigm Text Generation - Input formats, model selection, and response handling Streaming - All streaming methods and patterns Tools - Creating typed tools with Zod schemas and multi-turn orchestration nextTurnParams - Tool-driven context injection for skills and plugins Message Formats - Converting to/from OpenAI and Claude formats Dynamic Parameters - Async functions for adaptive behavior Stop Conditions - Intelligent execution control API Reference - Complete type definitions and method signatures \u200b Example Tools Ready-to-use t", "chars": 5203, "source": "direct"}
```

{
