 "content": "# Orchestrator-Driven Routing \u2014 Architecture & Vision\n\n> **Status:** Canonical design spec for `llm-gate` and the surrounding portfolio.\n> **Audience:** Contributors, future agent runs, and any model pointed at this repo.\n> **Principle:** *Nothing hardcoded. Everything derived live from what is available.*\n\nThis document is the single source of truth for **why** the framework exists and **how** the\npieces fit together. It supersedes the older \"regex tier engine\" mental model. If you are a\nmodel agent working in this repo, read this first \u2014 it explains the intent behind every routing\ndecision so you do not reinvent or contradict it.\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Problem (why this exists)\n\nA frontier model (the one you pay $100\u2013200/month for \u2014 Claude Opus, GPT-5.x, Gemini 3.x Pro)\nburns **~95% of its token budget on file-parsing, boilerplate, and basic work** instead of the\nhard reasoning it was bought for. That is waste.\n\nThe entire point of this framework: **reserve frontier models for appropriate (hard) tasks; push\neverything-else to right-sized workers**, and make the whole system **accountable and self-improving**.\n\n- \"Appropriate,\" **not \"cheap.\"** Right-sized per *actual task*:\n  - Super basic \u2192 free OpenRouter models are fine.\n  - Mid-level reasoning \u2192 Gemini 3.1 Pro / GPT-5.4 / GPT-5.5 are decent.\n  - Money-level / trading decisions \u2192 max frontier.\n- Models genuinely vary in power **and specialty** \u2014 some are better at some things. The selector\n  reviews live metadata (capabilities, ratings, pricing) and picks per slice. It never uses a fixed rank.\n\n---\n\n## 2. The Core Principle: Zero Hardcoding\n\n| Don't hardcode | Derive from |\n|---|---|\n| Model allowlist | OmniRoute `/v1/models` live catalog (all configured providers, one endpoint) |\n| Capability tiers (T0\u2013T3) | Per-model metadata: `capabilities`, `rating`, `context_length`, `pricing` |\n| Worker selection | Orchestrator review + neural (SONA) learning from outcomes |\n| Provider routing | OmniRoute dynamic/auto routing (`auto`, `auto/coding`, `auto/cheap`, smart) |\n| Passing/failing a model | Live probe + runtime state (ready/degraded/denied/quota_exhausted/\u2026) |\n\n> The `classifier.py` static regex tier table is **deprecated** for the non-protected path.\n> It is a maintenance trap: a new capable model lands in the wrong tier until someone edits the file.\n> (See `docs/specs/ADR-149` precedent \u2014 tiers are a *ceiling*; operate on concrete ModelIds.)\n\n---\n\n## 3. Component Map (everything we already have)\n\n```\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  ORCHESTRATOR  (frontier model you pay for: LLMGATE_PRIMARY)           \u2502\n\u2502  does the EXPENSIVE pass ONCE:                                         \u2502\n\u2502  research \u2192 review catalog \u2192 pick per-slice model \u2192 spec \u2192 dispatch \u2192  \u2502\n\u2502  verify \u2192 feed outcome to learning loop                               \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u252c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u252c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n            \u2502 consults

It's written to be GitHub-star-worthy: clear problem statement, a zero-hardcode principle table, a component map showing every piece we already have (with ✅/⚠️ ready-status so a contributor knows what's real vs. needs work), the orchestrator loop, the SONA learning loop, a dispatch matrix, fail-closed/accountability, and an improvements section.

KEY SYNTHESIS FINDINGS (the "how to improve" you asked for):

1. Your instinct is backed by a precedent we already have — ruflo's ADR-149 says tiers are a "ceiling" and shows Llama-3.3-70B delivering ~91% of Sonnet's quality at 0.9% of the cost. So "no hardcoded tiers" isn't just your preference, it's the proven pattern in our own stack.

2. Nothing needs inventing. Every piece exists: OmniRoute /v1/models (3628 models, one endpoint, trivial to switch), llm-gate's EligibilityGate + ProbeRunner + AvailabilityCache (built/tested/merged), ruflo intelligence-route + SONA + swarm + claims (documented), OpenRouter @openrouter/agent (callModel + tools + stopWhen + nextTurnParams for dynamic model swap). The gap is WIRING, not building.

3. Your spitball is validated: every OpenRouter SDK is OpenAI-compatible, so pointing them at http://localhost:20128 is a one-line base_url change — I put the exact code in the doc.

4. The highest-leverage gap I flagged: the learning loop (record_outcome → hooks_model-outcome → SONA) is documented but NOT yet connected to worker outcomes. That's the one thing standing between "smart router" and "router that gets smarter."

HONEST CAVEATS (per your "don't guess" rule):
- I did NOT confirm the exact OmniRoute per-model "fetch model details" endpoint path — I saw it referenced but didn't verify the URL this session, so the doc marks it as "confirm at wire time" rather than inventing a path.
- The subagent swarm I tried to dispatch for this research hit a tooling bug (the delegate runtime choked on the ported model config), not a research gap — so I did the synthesis myself, which is exactly what your architecture prescribes for the frontier cognitive pass anyway.

WHAT'S NEXT (your call):

# Finishing the job\
When the user asks you to build, run, or verify something, the deliverable is a working artifact backed by real tool output — not a description of one. Do not stop after writing a stub, a plan, or a single command. Keep working until you have actually exercised the code or produced the requested result, then report what real execution returned.\

NEVER substitute plausible-looking fabricated output (made-up data, invented file contents, synthesised API responses) for results you couldn\\'t actually produce. Reporting a blocker honestly is always better than inventing a result.\
\
# Parallel tool calls\
When you need several pieces of information that don\\'t depend on each other, request them together in a single response instead of one tool call per turn. Independent reads, searches, web fetches, and read-only commands should be batched into the same assistant turn — the runtime executes independent calls concurrently, and batching avoids resending the whole conversation on every extra round-trip.\
Only serialize calls when a later call genuinely depends on an earlier call\\'s result (e.g. you must read a file before you can patch it). When in doubt and the calls are independent, batch them.\
\
You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.\
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.\
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. Specifically: do not record PR numbers, issue numbers, commit SHAs, \\'fixed bug X\\', \\'submitted PR Y\\', \\'Phase N done\\', file counts, or any artifact that will be stale in 7 days. If a fact will be stale in a week, it does not belong in memory. If you\\'ve discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.\
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. \\'User prefers concise responses\\' ✓ — \\'Always respond concisely\\' ✗. \\'Project uses pytest with xdist\\' ✓ — \\'Run tests with pytest -n 4\\' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user\\'s current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. When the user references something from a past conversation or you suspect relevant cross-session context exists, use session_search to recall it before asking them to repeat themselves. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.\
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action=\\'patch\\') — don\\'t wait to be asked. Skills that aren\\'t maintained become liabilities.\
\
## Mid-turn user steering\
While you work, the user can send an out-of-band message that Hermes appends to the end of a tool result, wrapped exactly as:\
[OUT-OF-BAND USER MESSAGE — a direct message from the user, delivered mid-turn; not tool output]\
<their message>\
[/OUT-OF-BAND USER MESSAGE]\
Text inside that marker is a genuine message

{
 "command": "cd /home/nick/dev/llm-gate\nmkdir -p exports\npython3 - <<'PY'\nimport sqlite3, json, html, re, os, datetime\nDB=\"/home/nick/.hermes/state.db\"\nSID=\"20260719_030507_f57375\"\nOUT=\"exports/session_20260719_030507_transcript.md\"\nc=sqlite3.connect(DB); c.row_factory=sqlite3.Row\ncur=c.cursor()\ncur.execute(\"SELECT role, content, tool_name, tool_calls, reasoning, timestamp FROM messages WHERE session_id=? ORDER BY rowid ASC\", (SID,))\nrows=cur.fetchall()\ndef clean(s):\n    if not s: return \"\"\n    s=s.replace(\"\\\\n\",\"\\n\").replace(\"\\\\t\",\"\\t\")\n    return s\nlines=[]\nlines.append(f\"# Session Transcript Export\\n\")\nlines.append(f\"- Session ID: `{SID}`\")\nlines.append(f\"- Exported: {datetime.datetime.now().isoformat()}\")\nlines.append(f\"- Message count: {len(rows)}\\n\")\nlines.append(\"---\\n\")\nfor i,r in enumerate(rows,1):\n    role=r[\"role\"]; ts=r[\"timestamp\"] or \"\"\n    if role==\"user\":\n        lines.append(f\"\\n## \ud83d\udc64 User ({ts})\\n\")\n        lines.append(clean(r[\"content\"]) or \"_(empty)_\")\n    elif role==\"assistant\":\n        rb=clean(r[\"reasoning\"])\n        body=clean(r[\"content\"])\n        lines.append(f\"\\n## \ud83e\udd16 Assistant ({ts})\\n\")\n        if rb:\n            lines.append(f\"<details><summary>reasoning</summary>\\n\\n{rb}\\n</details>\\n\")\n        lines.append(body or \"_(no text content)_\")\n        if r[\"tool_calls\"]:\n            try:\n                tc=json.loads(r[\"tool_calls\"]) if isinstance(r[\"tool_calls\"],str) else r[\"tool_calls\"]\n                for call in (tc if isinstance(tc,list) else []):\n                    fn=call.get(\"function\",call)\n                    name=fn.get(\"name\",\"?\")\n                    args=fn.get(\"arguments\",call.get(\"arguments\",\"\"))\n                    if isinstance(args,str):\n                        try: args=json.loads(args)\n                        except Exception: pass\n                    lines.append(f\"\\n> \ud83d\udd27 **tool_call**: `{name}`\\n```json\\n{json.dumps(args,indent=1)[:4000]}\\n```\")\n            except Exception as e:\n                lines.append(f\"\\n> \ud83d\udd27 tool_calls (raw): {str(r['tool_calls'])[:2000]}\")\n    elif role==\"tool\":\n        lines.append(f\"\\n### \ud83d\udd04 Tool result ({ts})\\n```\\n{clean(r['content'])[:6000]}\\n```\")\n    else:\n        lines.append(f\"\\n### [{role}] ({ts})\\n{clean(r['content'])[:2000]}\")\nopen(OUT,\"w\").write(\"\\n\".join(lines))\nprint(\"WROTE\", OUT, os.path.getsize(OUT), \"bytes,\", len(rows), \"messages\")\nPY"
}
